topics.title: Classification of Chemical Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Chemical Reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE)**, the **Classification of Chemical Reactions** is the systematic categorization of reactions based on their **thermodynamic nature**, **kinetic behavior**, **phase composition**, **molecularity**, **reaction order**, **mechanism**, and **operating mode**. This classification forms the foundational framework for selecting appropriate reactor types, deriving rate expressions, and modeling the performance of industrial reactors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Classification of chemical reactions forms the gateway concept of CRE. It directly dictates the choice of reactor (Batch, CSTR, PFR, PBR), the form of the rate equation, and the design strategy (integral vs. differential analysis). Every Module 1 question is anchored to this classification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of chemical reactions like **vehicles on a highway**:
- A **homogeneous reaction** is like all cars traveling in one lane (single phase) — easy to model because everything looks the same.
- A **heterogeneous reaction** is like cars crossing a bridge over a river — you now have to account for **two distinct phases** (cars and river surface), making things far more complex.
- A **first-order reaction** is like one car entering the highway per second (rate depends only on how many cars are already there).
- A **parallel reaction** is like a fork in the road — the same car (reactant) can take two different routes (two different products).
- A **series reaction** is like a relay race — the output of the first runner (intermediate) becomes the input of the next runner (final product).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Before designing any reactor, engineers *must* classify the reaction. A petroleum refinery cracking unit, a pharmaceutical batch reactor, and a wastewater treatment bioreactor all use the same fundamental rate equations but require entirely different reactor geometries because the *classification* differs.

## 1.3 Primary Basis of Classification (KTU Board Taxonomy)

The seven (7) universally accepted classification axes in the KTU 2024 CRE syllabus are:

| # | Classification Basis | Standard Metrics |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Phase (Physical State)** | Homogeneous / Heterogeneous |
| 2 | **Molecularity** | Uni / Bi / Termolecular |
| 3 | **Reaction Order** | Zero, 1st, 2nd, 3rd, n-th, Fractional, Pseudo |
| 4 | **Mechanism** | Elementary / Non-Elementary |
| 5 | **Reversibility** | Reversible / Irreversible |
| 6 | **Temperature Effect** | Exothermic / Endothermic |
| 7 | **Operating Mode & Complexity** | Batch / Flow, Series / Parallel / Complex |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Based on Phase (Number of Phases)

### 2.1.1 Homogeneous Reactions
- **Definition:** Reactions in which **all reactants and products exist in a single phase** (gas OR liquid OR solid).
- **Examples:** Gas-phase combustion of methane ($CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$); liquid-phase esterification.
- **Rate Modeling:** Simplified because only one phase resistance exists. Rate $= f(C_i)$ only.

### 2.1.2 Heterogeneous Reactions
- **Definition:** Reactions involving **two or more phases** (gas-solid, liquid-solid, gas-liquid, solid-solid).
- **Examples:** Catalytic cracking (gas-solid), absorption with reaction (gas-liquid), ore roasting.
- **Rate Modeling:** Must include **mass transfer resistances** across phase boundaries in addition to intrinsic kinetics. The overall rate is governed by the **slowest step** (rate-determining step, RDS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Heterogeneous reactions dominate the chemical industry (over 80% of industrial processes), so their classification into catalytic vs. non-catalytic is a high-weightage KTU topic.

## 2.2 Classification Based on Molecularity

**Molecularity** is the *exact number of molecules/atoms/ions* participating in a single elementary step.

- **Unimolecular (1):** $A \rightarrow Products$ — Example: radioactive decay, isomerization.
- **Bimolecular (2):** $A + B \rightarrow Products$ — Example: $H_2 + I_2 \rightarrow 2HI$.
- **Termolecular (3):** $A + B + C \rightarrow Products$ — Rare in practice; simultaneous three-body collisions have extremely low probability.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** *Molecularity* is NEVER confused with *order*. Molecularity is a **theoretical concept** applicable only to elementary reactions. Order is **experimental**.

## 2.3 Classification Based on Reaction Order

The **order** with respect to a reactant is the exponent of its concentration in the experimentally determined rate law:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^{\alpha} \cdot C_B^{\beta} \cdot \ldots$$

where the **overall order** $n = \alpha + \beta + \ldots$.

- **Zero-order:** Rate independent of concentration. Common in **surface-catalyzed** reactions where surface saturation occurs.
- **First-order:** Rate $\propto$ concentration. Common in unimolecular decompositions and radioactive decay.
- **Second-order:** Common in bimolecular reactions; can be (i) $A + A \rightarrow$, (ii) $A + B \rightarrow$.
- **Fractional order:** Indicates complex multi-step mechanisms (e.g., order = 1.5).
- **Pseudo-order:** When one reactant is present in vast excess (e.g., water in dilute aqueous solution), its concentration is essentially constant, and the rate reduces to lower order.

## 2.4 Classification Based on Mechanism

### 2.4.1 Elementary Reactions
- Occur in **a single step** as written in the stoichiometric equation.
- The rate law can be **written directly from the stoichiometry**.
- **Molecularity = Order** for elementary reactions.

### 2.4.2 Non-Elementary (Complex) Reactions
- Occur via **multiple elementary steps** (a mechanism).
- The overall stoichiometric equation is the *sum* of elementary steps, but the rate law **cannot be written directly from stoichiometry**.
- Examples: most gas-phase reactions like $H_2 + Br_2 \rightarrow 2HBr$ (Radical chain mechanism).

## 2.5 Classification Based on Reversibility

- **Irreversible Reactions:** Proceed essentially to completion in one direction. $A \rightarrow B$. Equilibrium constant $K \to \infty$.
- **Reversible Reactions:** Proceed in both forward and reverse directions simultaneously, reaching equilibrium. $A \rightleftharpoons B$ with $K = k_f / k_r$.

## 2.6 Classification Based on Temperature Effect (Reaction Enthalpy)

- **Exothermic:** Heat is released ($\Delta H < 0$). Examples: combustion, oxidation.
- **Endothermic:** Heat is absorbed ($\Delta H > 0$). Examples: cracking, decomposition of limestone.

Temperature dependence of the rate constant follows the **Arrhenius Equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E/RT}$$

where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E$ is the activation energy, $R$ is the universal gas constant (**8.314 J/(mol·K)**), and $T$ is the absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**.

## 2.7 Classification Based on Operating Mode & Reaction Complexity

### 2.7.1 Operating Mode
- **Batch:** All reactants charged initially, no inflow/outflow during reaction.
- **Semi-batch (Fed-batch):** One reactant added gradually, others present initially.
- **Continuous Flow (CSTR/PFR):** Steady inflow and outflow of reactants/products.

### 2.7.2 Reaction Network Complexity
- **Single (Simple) Reactions:** $A \rightarrow B$.
- **Multiple (Complex) Reactions:**
  - **Series (Consecutive):** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$
  - **Parallel (Competitive):** $A \rightarrow B$ and $A \rightarrow D$ simultaneously
  - **Series-Parallel (Mixed):** Combination of both
- **Autocatalytic Reactions:** Product catalyzes the reaction: $A + B \rightarrow 2B$ (B catalyzes its own formation).
- **Chain Reactions:** Involve initiation, propagation, and termination steps (free-radical mechanism).

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Boundary Condition | Units / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Rate Law | $-r_A = k \cdot C_A^{\alpha} C_B^{\beta}$ | $\frac{\text{mol}}{L \cdot s}$ |
| 2 | Arrhenius Equation | $k = A \cdot e^{-E/RT}$ | $E$ in J/mol, $T$ in K |
| 3 | Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| 4 | Zero-order rate | $-r_A = k$ | $k$ in mol/(L·s) |
| 5 | First-order rate | $-r_A = k C_A$ | $k$ in $s^{-1}$ |
| 6 | Second-order (same species) | $-r_A = k C_A^2$ | $k$ in L/(mol·s) |
| 7 | Second-order (diff. species) | $-r_A = k C_A C_B$ | L/(mol·s) |
| 8 | Reversible Equilibrium | $K = k_f / k_r$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Molecularity | $\text{Uni} / \text{Bi} / \text{Ter}$ | integer count |
| 10 | Overall order $n$ | $n = \alpha + \beta + \ldots$ | integer / fractional |
| 11 | Van't Hoff Rule | $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{RT^2}$ | $\Delta H$ in J/mol |
| 12 | Pseudo-order condition | $C_{\text{excess}} \approx$ constant | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the *core vocabulary* of every CRE problem in KTU ESE. Memorize units — KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks for missing units.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Petroleum Refining:** Heterogeneous catalytic cracking in fluidized-bed reactors (FCC).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Batch reactors for homogeneous liquid-phase synthesis with strict kinetic control.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Bioreactors using enzyme-catalyzed (homogeneous) reactions for wastewater treatment.
- **Polymer Industry:** Chain polymerization (free-radical mechanism) — series-parallel reactions.
- **Ammonia Synthesis (Haber-Bosch):** Reversible, exothermic, heterogeneous catalytic reaction in fixed-bed reactors.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Molecularity and Order for Elementary Reactions

For an elementary reaction, the rate law is written directly from the stoichiometric coefficients.

**Consider the elementary reaction:**

$$aA + bB \rightarrow \text{Products}$$

**Step 1:** The probability of a successful collision is proportional to the concentrations of the colliding species.

$$P_{\text{collision}} \propto C_A \cdot C_B$$

**Step 2:** Since $a$ molecules of $A$ and $b$ molecules of $B$ must simultaneously collide, the effective probability becomes:

$$P_{\text{reaction}} \propto C_A^{a} \cdot C_B^{b}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the rate of reaction is:

$$\begin{aligned}
-r_A &= k \cdot C_A^{a} \cdot C_B^{b} \\
\text{Order w.r.t. } A &= a \\
\text{Order w.r.t. } B &= b \\
\text{Overall order } n &= a + b
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion — For elementary reactions, **molecularity = order**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Arrhenius Equation (Temperature Dependence of Rate Constant)

The Arrhenius equation is empirically derived from the fraction of molecules possessing energy $\geq E$ (Boltzmann distribution).

**Step 1:** The fraction of molecules with energy $\geq E$ is given by the Maxwell-Boltzmann factor:

$$f = e^{-E/RT}$$

**Step 2:** The rate of reaction is proportional to this fraction:

$$k \propto e^{-E/RT}$$

**Step 3:** Introducing the proportionality constant $A$ (the pre-exponential factor):

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E/RT}$$

**Step 4:** Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 5:** This linear form is used to extract $E$ from experimental data by plotting $\ln k$ versus $1/T$.

**Step 6:** At two different temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln k_1 &= \ln A - \frac{E}{R \cdot T_1} \\
\ln k_2 &= \ln A - \frac{E}{R \cdot T_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7:** Subtracting:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

This is the **two-temperature form** used extensively in KTU problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Van't Hoff Equation (Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant)

For a reversible reaction, $K = k_f / k_r$. Both forward and reverse rate constants follow Arrhenius behavior.

**Step 1:** Write Arrhenius expressions for forward and reverse:

$$\begin{aligned}
k_f &= A_f \cdot e^{-E_f / RT} \\
k_r &= A_r \cdot e^{-E_r / RT}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** The equilibrium constant is:

$$K = \frac{k_f}{k_r} = \frac{A_f}{A_r} \cdot e^{-(E_f - E_r)/RT}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the heat of reaction is $\Delta H = E_f - E_r$ (energy difference between activated complexes).

$$K = \frac{A_f}{A_r} \cdot e^{-\Delta H / RT}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate with respect to $T$:

$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{RT^2}$$

This is the **Van't Hoff Equation**, linking thermodynamic $\Delta H$ with kinetic rate constants.

**Step 5: Le Chatelier's Principle** (KTU-correlated result):
- If $\Delta H > 0$ (endothermic), $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} > 0$ → $K$ increases with $T$.
- If $\Delta H < 0$ (exothermic), $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} < 0$ → $K$ decreases with $T$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pseudo-Order Approximation

Consider the elementary reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$ with intrinsic rate law:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B$$

**Step 1:** If $B$ is present in vast excess (e.g., $B$ is water in a dilute aqueous solution), its concentration remains nearly constant throughout the reaction:

$$C_B \approx C_{B,0} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2:** Define a new rate constant:

$$k' = k \cdot C_{B,0}$$

**Step 3:** The effective rate law becomes:

$$-r_A = k' \cdot C_A$$

**Step 4:** The reaction now exhibits **pseudo-first-order** behavior, even though the molecularity is bimolecular and intrinsic order is 2.

This derivation is fundamental for **enzyme kinetics** and **dilute aqueous chemistry**, both frequent KTU topics.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Reaction Classification Decision Logic

| Observed Property | Classification Test | Likely Category |
|---|---|---|
| All species in single phase | Visual inspection / phase diagram | Homogeneous |
| Two or more phases visible | Catalyst + reactant + product phases | Heterogeneous |
| Stoichiometric coefficients match rate-law exponents | Write rate law; compare exponents | Elementary |
| Reaction exhibits fractional order | Plot $\ln(-r_A)$ vs $\ln C_A$ | Non-elementary / Complex |
| One reactant in 1000× excess | Check $\Delta C_B / C_{B,0} < 1\%$ | Pseudo-order |
| Heat evolved; reactor temperature rises | Measure $\Delta T$; $\Delta H < 0$ | Exothermic |
| Reaction does not go to completion | Measure equilibrium $C$ values; $K$ finite | Reversible |
| Same reactant forms multiple products | HPLC / GC product analysis | Parallel / Competitive |
| Intermediate B appears and disappears | Concentration profile of B vs time | Series / Consecutive |
| Product accelerates its own formation | Rate increases as product accumulates | Autocatalytic |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    nodeMech --> nodeElem[Elementary Single Step]

    nodeRev --> nodeIrev[Irreversible A to B]
    nodeRev --> nodeRev2[Reversible A reversible B]

```

```mermaid
graph LR

    nodeQ1 --> nodeHetero2[Heterogeneous]

    nodeHomo2 --> nodeQ2{Kinetic Order}

    nodeOrder1 --> nodePFR[Select PFR Plug Flow Reactor]
    nodeOrder2 --> nodeBatch2[Select Batch or CSTR]

    nodeHetero2 --> nodeQ3{Catalyst Required}
    nodeQ3 --> nodeCatYes[Yes - Fixed Bed or Fluidized Bed]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeCatNo[No - Gas Liquid or Liquid Liquid Reactor]

    nodeCatYes --> nodePBR[Packed Bed Reactor PBR]
    nodeCatNo --> nodeAbs[Absorption Column or Mixer Settler]

    nodePFR --> nodeOut[Final Reactor Design Parameters]
    nodeBatch2 --> nodeOut
    nodePBR --> nodeOut
    nodeAbs --> nodeOut
```

## 4.3 Series-Parallel Reaction Network Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Reactant A]
    nodeA --> nodeR1[Reaction 1 k1]
    nodeA --> nodeR2[Reaction 2 k2]
    nodeR1 --> nodeB[Intermediate B]
    nodeR2 --> nodeD[Product D]
    nodeB --> nodeR3[Reaction 3 k3]
    nodeR3 --> nodeC[Final Product C]
    nodeB -. side reaction .-> nodeE[By Product E]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeStart2[Given Reaction]
    nodeStart2 --> nodeStep1[Step 1 Identify Elementary or Non Elementary]
    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2[Step 2 Write Stoichiometry]

    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4[Step 4 Express Overall Order]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5[Step 5 Verify with Experimental Data]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeOut2[Final Validated Rate Expression]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **molecularity** and **order of reaction**. Can a reaction have fractional molecularity? Justify.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Molecularity | Order |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Number of molecules colliding in an elementary step | Sum of exponents of concentration terms in rate law |
| Applicability | Only elementary reactions | All reactions (elementary + complex) |
| Determined | From reaction mechanism (theoretical) | From experimental data |
| Value type | Always a whole number (1, 2, 3) | Can be integer, zero, or fractional |

**Conclusion:** Molecularity is always a **whole number** because a fractional number of molecules is physically meaningless. Therefore, a reaction **cannot have fractional molecularity**, but it can certainly have a **fractional order** (e.g., order = 1.5 for some complex reactions).

---

### **Question 2**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What are **pseudo-order reactions**? Give an industrial example.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A pseudo-order reaction is one in which the true molecularity/order is higher, but due to one (or more) reactant being present in such large excess that its concentration remains essentially constant throughout the reaction, the effective observed order becomes lower.

**Mathematical Form:** For $A + B \rightarrow$ Products with $C_B \approx$ constant:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B = k' \cdot C_A \quad \text{where } k' = k C_B$$

**Industrial Example:** **Enzyme-catalyzed hydrolysis of sucrose** in dilute aqueous solution — water is in vast excess, so its concentration is effectively constant, making the reaction **pseudo-first-order** with respect to sucrose.

**Other Examples:** Acid-catalyzed reactions in aqueous buffer; oxidation of organic compounds by atmospheric oxygen.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Alternative 1)** — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a)** Classify chemical reactions in detail based on **(i) phase**, **(ii) molecularity**, and **(iii) mechanism**. Give **two examples** for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The rate constant of a reaction doubles when the temperature is raised from **300 K to 310 K**. Calculate the **activation energy** $E$ using the Arrhenius equation. Take $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**(i) Classification Based on Phase:**
- **Homogeneous reactions:** All reactants and products are in a single phase.
  - *Example 1:* Combustion of methane — $CH_{4(g)} + 2O_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{2(g)} + 2H_2O_{(g)}$
  - *Example 2:* Esterification in liquid phase — $CH_3COOH_{(l)} + C_2H_5OH_{(l)} \rightarrow CH_3COOC_2H_{5(l)} + H_2O_{(l)}$
- **Heterogeneous reactions:** Two or more phases are involved.
  - *Example 1:* Catalytic cracking — gas oil (liquid) over solid zeolite catalyst
  - *Example 2:* Roasting of zinc sulfide — $2ZnS_{(s)} + 3O_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2ZnO_{(s)} + 2SO_{2(g)}$

**(ii) Classification Based on Molecularity:**
- **Unimolecular:** $A \rightarrow P$ — *Example:* Radioactive decay of Uranium-238
- **Bimolecular:** $A + B \rightarrow P$ — *Example:* $H_2 + I_2 \rightarrow 2HI$
- **Termolecular:** $A + B + C \rightarrow P$ — *Example:* $2NO + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO_2$ (apparent termolecular, but actually proceeds via bimolecular mechanism)

**(iii) Classification Based on Mechanism:**
- **Elementary reactions:** Occur in a single step.
  - *Example:* $NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2$
- **Non-elementary (complex) reactions:** Occur via multiple steps.
  - *Example:* $H_2 + Br_2 \rightarrow 2HBr$ (radical chain mechanism with 5 elementary steps)

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 310$ K
- $k_2 / k_1 = 2$
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Formula (Two-temperature Arrhenius form):**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 1: Substitute known values.**

$$\ln(2) = \frac{E}{8.314} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{310}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the bracket term.**

$$\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{310} = \frac{310 - 300}{300 \times 310} = \frac{10}{93000} = 1.0753 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Solve for E.**

$$\begin{aligned}
0.6931 &= \frac{E}{8.314} \times 1.0753 \times 10^{-4} \\
E &= \frac{0.6931 \times 8.314}{1.0753 \times 10^{-4}} \\
E &= \frac{5.762}{1.0753 \times 10^{-4}} \\
E &= 53{,}590 \text{ J/mol} \\
E &\approx 53.59 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $E \approx 53.59$ kJ/mol

---

### **Question B (Alternative 2)** — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, classify **complex reactions** into series, parallel, and series-parallel types. Explain **autocatalytic reactions** and **chain reactions** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the elementary liquid-phase reaction $A + 2B \rightarrow C$ conducted in a solution where water is the solvent in 100-fold molar excess, derive the **rate law** and identify the **observed order**. The intrinsic rate constant is $k = 0.05$ L²/(mol²·s). If the initial concentration of $A$ is $C_{A,0} = 1.0$ mol/L and $C_{B,0} = 0.5$ mol/L, compute the **initial rate** of reaction. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Schematic of Complex Reaction Types:**

```
SERIES:       A --k1--> B --k2--> C
PARALLEL:     A --k1--> B       (B and D are products)
              A --k2--> D
SERIES-PARALLEL: A --k1--> B --k2--> D
                       \--k3--> C
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Series (Consecutive) Reactions:** The product of one reaction becomes the reactant for the next.
   - *Example:* $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$ where $B$ is an intermediate.
   - Real-world: Radioactive decay chains; sequential drug metabolism in pharmacokinetics.

2. **Parallel (Competitive) Reactions:** A single reactant undergoes two or more competing reactions simultaneously.
   - *Example:* $A \rightarrow B$ and $A \rightarrow D$
   - Real-world: Partial oxidation of ethylene producing both ethylene oxide and $CO_2$.

3. **Series-Parallel Reactions:** A combination of both networks — reactant forms intermediate, which can either continue or take a parallel path.

**Autocatalytic Reactions:** The product itself catalyzes the reaction.
- Form: $A + B \rightarrow 2B$ (where $B$ is both product and catalyst).
- *Example:* Acid-catalyzed ester hydrolysis; the produced acid accelerates further hydrolysis.
- *Characteristic:* Reaction rate is initially slow, rises to a maximum, then falls as $A$ depletes.

**Chain Reactions:** Free-radical-based reactions with three stages — initiation, propagation, termination.
- *Example:* $H_2 + Cl_2 \rightarrow 2HCl$ (photochemical chain reaction).
- Initiation: $Cl_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu} 2Cl\cdot$
- Propagation: $Cl\cdot + H_2 \rightarrow HCl + H\cdot$; $H\cdot + Cl_2 \rightarrow HCl + Cl\cdot$
- Termination: $Cl\cdot + Cl\cdot \rightarrow Cl_2$

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** Elementary reaction $A + 2B \rightarrow C$ in dilute aqueous solution.

**Step 1: Write the intrinsic rate law from stoichiometry.**

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B^2 \quad \text{where } k = 0.05 \text{ L}^2/(\text{mol}^2 \cdot \text{s})$$

**Step 2: Apply pseudo-order approximation.**

Water is the solvent in 100-fold molar excess relative to $B$. Although water is not directly in the stoichiometry, the *solution environment* is such that the dilute-solute assumption applies to $B$ only if $B$ were the limiting solute in solvent water. Given the wording, we treat $B$ as being the limited species in water-rich medium — but here the excess is water itself, which doesn't appear in the stoichiometry.

**Refined interpretation:** In many textbook treatments, when a *non-stoichiometric* species (like solvent water) is in vast excess, it acts as a constant. Here, water doesn't appear in rate law; hence intrinsic order remains **third order** ($1 + 2 = 3$).

However, if the problem intends the *solvent* to be a pseudo-reactant (rare but possible), the assumption becomes:

Since water is in 100-fold excess: $C_{H_2O} \approx$ constant.

If water were explicitly included: $-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B^2 \cdot C_{H_2O}$ → intrinsic 4th order.

For this problem, we stick to the **stoichiometric rate law**:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B^2 = 0.05 \cdot (1.0) \cdot (0.5)^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate initial rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
-r_{A,0} &= 0.05 \times 1.0 \times 0.25 \\
-r_{A,0} &= 0.0125 \text{ mol/(L·s)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Observed Order:** **Third order** (since $A$ and $B$ are the only stoichiometric species and $B$ is not in vast excess relative to $A$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in Classification Questions:**
>
> 1. **Molecularity ≠ Order Trap:** Students often state the molecularity equals the order for ALL reactions. **Half-mark deduction** if you don't qualify with "only for elementary reactions."
>
> 2. **Missing Units in Final Answer:** For Arrhenius problems, KTU examiners *strictly* require the unit **J/mol or kJ/mol**. Missing unit → **1-mark cut**.
>
> 3. **Confusing Exothermic/Endothermic with $\Delta H$ sign:** A wrong sign in Van't Hoff problems can flip the entire Le Chatelier conclusion. Always state **"$\Delta H < 0$ for exothermic"** explicitly.
>
> 4. **Pseudo-order Misapplication:** The excess species must be **at least 50×** (preferably 100×) larger to qualify as pseudo-order. State the magnitude in your answer.
>
> 5. **Examples Mandatory:** In classification questions, KTU expects *at least one example per category*. Generic answers without examples lose **2–3 marks** easily.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Chemical reactions are classified on **7 axes**: phase, molecularity, order, mechanism, reversibility, temperature effect, and operating mode.
- [ ] **Homogeneous** = single phase; **Heterogeneous** = multiple phases (involves mass transfer).
- [ ] **Molecularity** is theoretical (from mechanism); **Order** is experimental (from rate data).
- [ ] Molecularity is **always an integer**; order can be **integer, zero, or fractional**.
- [ ] For elementary reactions: **Molecularity = Order** (in terms of exponents matching stoichiometry).
- [ ] **Pseudo-order** arises when one reactant is in vast excess (≥50×); its concentration ≈ constant.
- [ ] **Arrhenius equation** $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ governs temperature dependence of rate constant.
- [ ] **Two-temperature form:** $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- [ ] **$R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)** is a constant in CRE problems.
- [ ] **Exothermic** ($\Delta H < 0$): heat released; **Endothermic** ($\Delta H > 0$): heat absorbed.
- [ ] **Van't Hoff equation:** $d \ln K / dT = \Delta H / RT^2$.
- [ ] Complex reactions: **Series** ($A \to B \to C$), **Parallel** ($A \to B$ and $A \to D$), **Series-Parallel** (mixed).
- [ ] **Autocatalytic reactions:** product catalyzes reaction ($A + B \to 2B$).
- [ ] **Chain reactions:** initiation, propagation, termination (free-radical mechanism).
- [ ] **Reversible** reactions have finite $K = k_f / k_r$; **Irreversible** have $K \to \infty$.
- [ ] Always **write units** in final numerical answers (KTU mandatory).
- [ ] Always **cite examples** for each classification category (KTU mandatory).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variables Affecting the Rate of Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Variables Affecting the Rate of Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE)**, the **rate of reaction** is defined as the change in the number of moles of a reactant (or product) per unit time per unit volume (for homogeneous systems) or per unit mass of catalyst (for heterogeneous systems). The mathematical expression is:

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt} = k \cdot f(C_A, C_B, \ldots, T, P, \text{catalyst}, \ldots)$$

The **variables affecting the rate of reaction** are those physical and chemical parameters whose variation causes a measurable change in $-r_A$ (the rate of disappearance of species A). These variables include:

1. **Concentration of reactants and products** $(C_i)$
2. **Temperature** $(T)$
3. **Pressure** $(P)$ — for gas-phase reactions
4. **Catalyst presence and type**
5. **Surface area of solid phases** (heterogeneous systems)
6. **Order of reaction** (intrinsic kinetic parameter)
7. **Solvent and ionic strength** (liquid-phase reactions)
8. **Radiation intensity** (photochemical reactions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** The rate constant $k$ is *not* a true constant; it is independent of concentration but is a strong function of temperature, catalyst, and solvent. Always remember that the **rate equation** $r_A$ depends on both $k$ (kinetic) and $f(C_i)$ (concentration-dependent terms).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍳 — The "Cooking Curry" Model

Imagine you are cooking a curry in your kitchen at home. The "reaction" is the formation of the cooked dish, and you want to **increase the rate of cooking**. What do you do?

| Curry Variable | Chemical Reaction Variable | Effect on Rate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 🔥 Increase flame / stove heat | **Increase Temperature ($T$)** | Molecules move faster → more collisions |
| 🥕 Add more vegetables | **Increase Reactant Concentration ($C_A$)** | More reactant molecules collide per unit volume |
| 🔒 Use a pressure cooker | **Increase Pressure ($P$)** | Compresses gas molecules → more collisions per unit volume |
| 🧂 Add a tenderizer (papain) | **Use a Catalyst** | Lowers activation energy → faster reaction |
| 🪵 Chop vegetables finely | **Increase Surface Area ($S$)** | More contact area between phases |
| 💧 Use less water | **Change Solvent / Medium** | Alters molecular interactions |

This analogy makes it instantly clear: **the rate of any chemical reaction is not magic** — it is governed by measurable, controllable variables that engineers tune to maximize yield and selectivity in industrial reactors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Board Exam:** **"T-P-C-S"** = **T**emperature, **P**ressure, **C**oncentration, **S**urface area & **Catalyst**. These four cover $\geq 90\%$ of all KTU Module 1 problems on this topic.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314 \ \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Standard Temperature** $T_{std} = 298.15 \ \text{K}$
- **Standard Pressure** $P_{std} = 1 \ \text{atm} = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$
- **Avogadro's Number** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Activation Energy** $E$: typically $40$ – $200 \ \text{kJ/mol}$ for industrial reactions
- **Pre-exponential Factor** $A$: units depend on order of reaction
- **Kinetic Rate Units:** $\text{mol} \cdot \text{L}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ (homogeneous) or $\text{mol} \cdot \text{g}_{\text{cat}}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ (heterogeneous)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Variables

The variables that affect the rate of reaction can be classified into two broad groups:

### Group A: Variables that affect the **rate constant $k$**
These do not change the form of $f(C_i)$, only the magnitude of the rate.

- **Temperature ($T$):** Governed by the **Arrhenius equation**
- **Catalyst:** Provides an alternative reaction pathway with lower $E$
- **Solvent:** Affects $A$ and $E$ in polar reactions
- **Ionic strength:** Affects kinetics in electrolyte solutions

### Group B: Variables that affect the **concentration-dependent term $f(C_i)$**
These change the driving force of the reaction.

- **Concentration of reactants** $(C_A, C_B, \ldots)$
- **Partial pressure** of gases $(p_A, p_B, \ldots)$
- **Surface area** of solid reactants
- **Order of reaction** (intrinsic to mechanism)
- **Presence of products** (inhibition, reversible reactions)

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Major Variable

### 🔹 2.2.1 Concentration
- **Why?** Rate of bimolecular collisions is proportional to the product of concentrations of reacting species (Maxwell-Boltzmann collision theory).
- **How?** For an elementary reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$:
$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B$$
For non-elementary reactions, the order is determined experimentally, and the rate law takes the power-law form:
$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^{\alpha} \cdot C_B^{\beta}$$
where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the orders with respect to A and B respectively (need not be integers).

### 🔹 2.2.2 Temperature
- **Why?** Increasing $T$ increases the average kinetic energy of molecules, so a larger fraction of molecules have energy $\geq E$ (the activation energy).
- **How?** Governed by the **Arrhenius equation**:
$$k = A \cdot e^{-E / RT}$$
- A rule of thumb: for many reactions, **rate doubles for every 10 °C rise** in temperature (this corresponds roughly to $E \approx 50 \ \text{kJ/mol}$ at room temperature).

### 🔹 2.2.3 Pressure
- **Why?** For gas-phase reactions, increasing $P$ effectively increases the molar concentration (via ideal gas law: $C = P / RT$).
- **How?** The pressure-dependence appears through:
$$-r_A = k \cdot \left(\frac{P_A}{RT}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot \left(\frac{P_B}{RT}\right)^{\beta}$$
- For liquid-phase reactions, pressure has negligible effect (liquids are nearly incompressible).

### 🔹 2.2.4 Catalyst
- **Why?** A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy barrier $E_{\text{cat}} < E$.
- **How?** Catalyst does **not** appear in the stoichiometry, does **not** change $\Delta G$, and does **not** shift equilibrium. It only speeds up the approach to equilibrium.
- **Effect on Arrhenius parameters:** Both $A$ and $E$ are altered. The new rate constant satisfies:
$$k_{\text{cat}} = A_{\text{cat}} \cdot e^{-E_{\text{cat}} / RT}$$

### 🔹 2.2.5 Surface Area
- **Why?** For heterogeneous (solid-fluid) reactions, the reaction occurs only at the interface.
- **How?** Rate is proportional to the available interfacial area $A_s$:
$$-r_A'' = k_s \cdot C_A \quad \text{(rate per unit surface area)}$$
$$-r_A = k_s \cdot A_s \cdot C_A \quad \text{(rate per unit volume)}$$

## 2.3 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize this table. It covers $\geq 95\%$ of numerical and conceptual questions on "Variables Affecting the Rate of Reaction" in KTU 2024 ESE and module tests.

| # | Variable | Governing Equation | Effect on Rate | KTU Common Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Concentration $C_i$ | $-r_A = k \prod C_i^{\alpha_i}$ | Increases monotonically | Power-law kinetics |
| 2 | Temperature $T$ | $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ | Exponential increase | Arrhenius plot |
| 3 | Two-Temp Comparison | $\ln(k_2/k_1) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Quantitative $T$ effect | Numerical KTU problems |
| 4 | Pressure $P$ (gas) | $C_i = P_i / RT$ | Increases with $P$ | Gas-phase kinetics |
| 5 | Catalyst | $E_{\text{cat}} < E$ | Large increase | Heterogeneous systems |
| 6 | Surface area $A_s$ | $-r_A = k_s A_s C_A$ | Linear in $A_s$ | Fluid-solid reactions |
| 7 | Order of reaction $\alpha$ | $-r_A = k C_A^{\alpha}$ | Determines $C$ sensitivity | Reaction design |
| 8 | Activation energy $E$ | $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ | Higher $E$ = more $T$-sensitive | Energy balance |
| 9 | Pre-exponential $A$ | $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ | Frequency factor | Collision theory |
| 10 | Liquid phase | Pressure-independent | $P$ has negligible effect | KTU 2-mark Q |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏭

| Industry | Variable Manipulated | Engineering Outcome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Haber-Bosch (NH₃ synthesis) | $T$, $P$, Fe catalyst | Optimal yield at $700$ K, $200$ atm |
| Catalytic cracking in petroleum | Zeolite catalyst, $T$ | Higher gasoline yield |
| Polymerization (Ziegler-Natta) | Catalyst type, $T$ | Controlled molecular weight |
| Pharmaceutical synthesis | Solvent, $T$ | Higher selectivity, fewer by-products |
| Food processing | $T$ (pasteurization) | Kill pathogens without denaturing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Connection:** Most industrial reactor design problems in KTU 2024 ask you to determine $k$ at a new $T$ given $E$ and $k$ at a reference $T$. Master the two-temperature Arrhenius form: $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Arrhenius Equation (Empirical Foundation)

### Starting Point — Collisions Must Have Sufficient Energy
According to the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, the fraction of molecular collisions that have energy greater than or equal to $E$ (the activation energy) is given by the Boltzmann factor:
$$f = e^{-E / RT}$$

If $Z_{AB}$ is the total collision frequency per unit volume per unit time between molecules A and B, then the *effective* collision frequency that leads to reaction is:
$$Z_{AB}^{\text{eff}} = Z_{AB} \cdot e^{-E / RT}$$

The rate of reaction is proportional to this effective collision frequency:
$$-r_A = p \cdot Z_{AB} \cdot e^{-E / RT}$$

where $p$ is the **steric factor** (orientation probability), typically $p \leq 1$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the rate constant as the proportionality factor that remains when concentration terms are extracted. For the bimolecular elementary reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$:
$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B$$

**Step 2:** From kinetic theory, the collision frequency $Z_{AB}$ per unit volume is:
$$Z_{AB} = N_A \cdot N_B \cdot \sigma_{AB} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu_{AB}}}$$

where $\sigma_{AB}$ is the collision cross-section, $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $\mu_{AB}$ is the reduced mass.

**Step 3:** Recognize that $Z_{AB}$ is proportional to the product of number densities (i.e., concentrations). Specifically, $Z_{AB} \propto C_A \cdot C_B$ at constant $T$.

**Step 4:** Substitute and identify the rate constant $k$:
$$-r_A = p \cdot Z_{AB}^{\text{total}} \cdot e^{-E / RT} = p \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{Z_{AB}^{\text{total}}}{C_A C_B}\right)}_{A(T)} \cdot e^{-E / RT} \cdot C_A C_B$$

**Step 5:** Define the pre-exponential factor:
$$A(T) = p \cdot \frac{Z_{AB}^{\text{total}}}{C_A C_B}$$

This $A$ is a weak function of $T$ (from the $T^{1/2}$ term in collision theory and possibly $T^{1/2}$ to $T^1$ from transition state theory).

**Step 6:** For the engineering approximation (and KTU exams), treat $A$ as constant over the temperature range of interest, yielding the **Arrhenius equation**:
$$k = A \cdot e^{-E / RT}$$

This is the cornerstone equation of Module 1.

**Step 7:** Taking natural log of both sides:
$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** when $\ln k$ is plotted against $1/T$ (the famous **Arrhenius plot**):
- **Slope** = $-E/R$
- **Intercept** = $\ln A$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Temperature Arrhenius Form (Most-Used in KTU Numericals)

We derive the formula that KTU examiners love to ask in Part B (14-mark) problems.

**Step 1:** Write the Arrhenius equation at two different temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:
$$k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E / R T_1}$$
$$k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E / R T_2}$$

**Step 2:** Divide $k_2$ by $k_1$:
$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E / R T_2}}{A \cdot e^{-E / R T_1}} = e^{-(E / R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)}$$

**Step 3:** Take natural log of both sides:
$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to the form preferred in KTU solutions:
$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

This is the **golden formula** for KTU numericals. It allows you to find $E$, $k_2$, or $T_2$ given the other three quantities.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rate Law from Reaction Order Definition

For a generic reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ with order $n$:
$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^{n}$$

**Step 1:** Express the rate in terms of conversion $X_A$. The concentration is:
$$C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the rate law:
$$-r_A = k \cdot [C_{A0}(1 - X_A)]^n = k \cdot C_{A0}^{n} \cdot (1 - X_A)^n$$

**Step 3:** For a batch reactor, the material balance is:
$$-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt} = C_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Equating and rearranging:
$$C_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dt} = k \cdot C_{A0}^{n} \cdot (1 - X_A)^n$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the time required to reach conversion $X_A$ by integration:
$$t = C_{A0}^{1-n} \cdot \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX}{(1-X)^n} \cdot \frac{1}{k}$$

This shows that the effect of concentration (and hence $C_{A0}$) on the rate depends on the order $n$ — justifying why "order of reaction" is itself a critical variable.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Variable — Quantitative Summary

| Variable | Direction of Change | Effect on $-r_A$ | Magnitude of Effect | Order of Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\uparrow C_A$ | Increase | Increase | Power-law: $\propto C_A^{\alpha}$ | Variable |
| $\uparrow T$ (with $E > 0$) | Increase | Exponential increase | $k$ doubles per $\sim 10$ K rise | 2–10× per 10 K |
| $\uparrow P$ (gas phase) | Increase | Increases via $C_i = P_i/RT$ | $r \propto P^{\sum \alpha_i}$ | Variable |
| Add catalyst | New pathway | Large increase | $E$ drops by $50$–$80\%$ | $10^2$ – $10^{10}$× |
| $\uparrow A_s$ (heterogeneous) | Increase | Linear increase | $r \propto A_s$ | Linear |
| Change solvent | Variable | Variable | $k$ changes $10^{-2}$ to $10^2$× | Variable |
| Add product (reversible) | Increase | Decreases forward rate | Per Le Chatelier | Variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The most-tested variable in KTU Module 1 numericals is **temperature**, because it couples the Arrhenius equation with reactor design. Practice solving 3–5 problems using the two-temperature form to ensure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Hierarchy of Variables Affecting the Rate of Reaction

The following Mermaid block provides a block-level functional architecture of how the variables interact to govern the reaction rate. This is exactly the type of diagram KTU examiners reward in Part A 3-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rate of Reaction -rA] --> B[Rate Constant k]
    A --> C[Concentration Function fCi]

    B --> D[Temperature T]
    B --> E[Catalyst Type]
    B --> F[Solvent Medium]
    B --> G[Ionic Strength]

    C --> H[Reactant Concentration CA]
    C --> I[Product Concentration CP]
    C --> J[Partial Pressure Pi for gases]
    C --> K[Surface Area As for solids]

    D --> D1[Arrhenius Equation k = A exp -E over RT]
    E --> E1[Lowers Activation Energy E]
    F --> F1[Alters Polar Transition State]
    G --> G1[Primary Salt Effect]

    H --> H1[Power Law rA = k CA to alpha]
    I --> I1[Equilibrium Shift Le Chatelier]
    J --> J1[Ideal Gas Law Ci = Pi over RT]
    K --> K1[Linear Proportionality rA = ks As CA]
```

## 4.2 Arrhenius Plot Visualization (ln k vs 1/T)

The Arrhenius plot is a straight line in $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ space. The diagram below shows how two reactions with different activation energies behave differently. **Higher $E$ → steeper negative slope** → more temperature-sensitive.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[Arrhenius Plot Domain]
        X[1 over T in K inverse] --> Y[ln k]
    end

    Y --> P1[Reaction 1: Low E = 50 kJ per mol]
    Y --> P2[Reaction 2: High E = 150 kJ per mol]

    P1 --> S1[Slope m1 = -E1 over R = -6013 K]
    P2 --> S2[Slope m2 = -E2 over R = -18040 K]

    S1 --> R1[Shallow Line: Less T-sensitive]
    S2 --> R2[Steep Line: Highly T-sensitive]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Effect of Catalyst on Activation Energy

A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy. The diagram below shows the energy profile for uncatalyzed vs catalyzed reactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reactants A + B] --> E1[Uncatalyzed Pathway]
    R --> E2[Catalyzed Pathway]

    E1 --> TS1[Transition State: E = 200 kJ per mol]
    E2 --> TS2[Transition State: E_cat = 80 kJ per mol]

    TS1 --> P1[Products: unchanged Delta G]
    TS2 --> P2[Products: unchanged Delta G]

    TS1 --> D1[High Barrier: Slow Reaction]
    TS2 --> D2[Lower Barrier: Fast Reaction]

    P1 --> EQ1[Same Equilibrium Position]
    P2 --> EQ2[Same Equilibrium Position]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing Variables in Reactor Design

KTU Module 1 often asks: "Which variable would you manipulate to increase the rate?" The decision tree below formalizes the answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the reaction gas phase or liquid phase?}
    Q1 -->|Gas Phase| Q2{Is there a change in moles delta n?}
    Q1 -->|Liquid Phase| Q3[Manipulate T, Catalyst, or Solvent]
    Q1 -->|Solid Gas| Q4[Manipulate Surface Area As]

    Q2 -->|Yes, delta n nonzero| A1[Pressure P is a key variable]
    Q2 -->|No, delta n zero| A2[Pressure has minimal effect]

    Q3 --> A3[Concentration via mixing]
    Q4 --> A4[Catalyst and Temperature are key]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Use in KTU Exam:** You are not required to draw Mermaid diagrams in your answer sheet. However, drawing a **clear, labeled block diagram** (boxes + arrows) for "Variables affecting rate of reaction" is highly recommended for 3-mark conceptual questions. It shows the examiner you have a structured understanding of the topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### 📝 Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"List any six variables that affect the rate of a chemical reaction. Briefly explain how temperature influences the rate constant."**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

The six main variables affecting the rate of reaction are:
1. **Concentration of reactants** $(C_i)$: Higher $C_i$ → more frequent molecular collisions → higher rate.
2. **Temperature** $(T)$: Higher $T$ → more molecules exceed activation energy $E$ → higher rate.
3. **Pressure** $(P)$: For gases, $P$ increases concentration via $C_i = P_i / RT$ → higher rate.
4. **Catalyst**: Lowers activation energy $E$ → exponential increase in rate constant.
5. **Surface area** $(A_s)$: For heterogeneous reactions, larger $A_s$ → higher rate.
6. **Order of reaction / Solvent / Radiation**: Influences sensitivity of $r$ to $C$, polarity, or photon flux.

**Effect of temperature on rate constant:** The rate constant $k$ depends on temperature through the **Arrhenius equation**:
$$k = A \cdot e^{-E / RT}$$
As $T$ increases, the exponential term $e^{-E/RT}$ increases (because $-E/RT$ becomes less negative), so $k$ rises **exponentially** with $T$. **(1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the explanation, 1 mark for the six variables.)**

---

### 📝 Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"A reaction rate doubles when the temperature is raised from 300 K to 310 K. Calculate the activation energy of the reaction."**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

**Given:** $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 310$ K, $k_2 / k_1 = 2$, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K

Using the two-temperature Arrhenius form:
$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Calculate the LHS:
$$\ln(2) = 0.6931$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the temperature term:
$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{310} = \frac{310 - 300}{300 \cdot 310} = \frac{10}{93000} = 1.0753 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $E$:
$$E = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = \frac{8.314 \times 0.6931}{1.0753 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E = \frac{5.7618}{1.0753 \times 10^{-4}} = 53{,}586 \ \text{J/mol} \approx 53.6 \ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Answer:** $E \approx 53.6 \ \text{kJ/mol}$ — typical of many chemical reactions.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions in Module 1 carry 14 marks each and have an **internal choice** (you answer either Choice A or Choice B). Each choice typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### 📝 Question B-A (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Discuss in detail the effect of the following on the rate of a chemical reaction: **(7 marks)**
**(i)** Concentration of reactants
**(ii)** Temperature
**(iii)** Pressure

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**(i) Effect of Concentration (2.5 marks):**
The rate of an elementary reaction is directly proportional to the product of the concentrations of the reacting species raised to their stoichiometric coefficients (Law of Mass Action). For the reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow \text{Products}$:
$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^{a} \cdot C_B^{b}$$
For non-elementary reactions, the rate law is empirical:
$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^{\alpha} \cdot C_B^{\beta}$$
where $\alpha, \beta$ are the orders (determined experimentally). Higher concentration → more collisions per unit time per unit volume → higher rate.

**(ii) Effect of Temperature (2.5 marks):**
Temperature affects the rate constant $k$ (not the concentration term) through the **Arrhenius equation**:
$$k = A \cdot e^{-E/RT}$$
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor)
- $E$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K

As $T$ increases, $k$ increases **exponentially**. Empirical rule: rate doubles per 10 K rise for many reactions. The Arrhenius plot ($\ln k$ vs $1/T$) is a straight line with slope $-E/R$.

**(iii) Effect of Pressure (2 marks):**
- **Gas-phase reactions:** Pressure affects concentration via $C_i = P_i / RT$. Increasing $P$ → increasing $C_i$ → increasing rate.
- **Liquid-phase reactions:** Pressure has negligible effect (liquids are nearly incompressible).
- **Effect on rate order:** For $aA + bB \rightarrow \text{Products}$ with rate law $-r_A = k C_A^{\alpha} C_B^{\beta}$:
$$-r_A = k \cdot \left(\frac{P_A}{RT}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot \left(\frac{P_B}{RT}\right)^{\beta}$$
- Reactions with $\Delta n \neq 0$ are most pressure-sensitive.

---

#### (b) The rate constant of a reaction is $3.46 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{min}^{-1}$ at 350 K and $6.91 \times 10^{-1} \ \text{min}^{-1}$ at 400 K. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
**(i)** The activation energy $E$
**(ii)** The pre-exponential factor $A$
**(iii)** The rate constant at 375 K

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**Given:** $k_1 = 3.46 \times 10^{-2}$ min$^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 350$ K; $k_2 = 6.91 \times 10^{-1}$ min$^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 400$ K.

**(i) Activation Energy (3 marks):**
Using the two-temperature Arrhenius form:
$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Calculate the LHS:
$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{6.91 \times 10^{-1}}{3.46 \times 10^{-2}} = 19.97$$
$$\ln(19.97) = 2.994$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the temperature term:
$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{400} = \frac{400 - 350}{350 \times 400} = \frac{50}{140000} = 3.571 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $E$:
$$E = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = \frac{8.314 \times 2.994}{3.571 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$E = \frac{24.891}{3.571 \times 10^{-4}} = 69{,}705 \ \text{J/mol} \approx 69.7 \ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**(ii) Pre-exponential Factor (2 marks):**
Using $k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E/RT_1}$:
$$A = k_1 \cdot e^{E/RT_1}$$

Calculate $E/RT_1$:
$$\frac{E}{RT_1} = \frac{69705}{8.314 \times 350} = \frac{69705}{2909.9} = 23.95$$

$$A = 3.46 \times 10^{-2} \times e^{23.95} = 3.46 \times 10^{-2} \times 2.51 \times 10^{10}$$
$$A \approx 8.69 \times 10^{8} \ \text{min}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Rate Constant at 375 K (2 marks):**
$$\ln\left(\frac{k_3}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_3}\right) = \frac{69705}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{375}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{375} = \frac{375 - 350}{350 \times 375} = \frac{25}{131250} = 1.905 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_3}{k_1}\right) = 8383.3 \times 1.905 \times 10^{-4} = 1.597$$

$$\frac{k_3}{k_1} = e^{1.597} = 4.94$$

$$k_3 = 3.46 \times 10^{-2} \times 4.94 = 0.1709 \ \text{min}^{-1}$$

**Final Answers:** $E = 69.7$ kJ/mol, $A = 8.69 \times 10^8$ min$^{-1}$, $k_{375} = 0.171$ min$^{-1}$.

---

### 📝 Question B-B (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the role of a catalyst in a chemical reaction. How does it differ from other variables affecting the rate? **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**Definition of Catalyst (1 mark):**
A catalyst is a substance that **increases the rate of a chemical reaction** without being consumed in the overall reaction, by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy $E_{\text{cat}} < E$.

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
- The catalyst forms intermediate complexes with the reactants.
- These intermediates have lower transition-state energy than the uncatalyzed pathway.
- The catalyst is regenerated at the end of the cycle (true catalyst, not consumed).
- The overall $\Delta G$ and equilibrium constant $K$ remain unchanged.

**Effect on Arrhenius Parameters (2 marks):**
- The catalyst **lowers $E$** (activation energy).
- It may also change the pre-exponential factor $A$.
- Net effect: huge increase in $k$, often $10^2$ to $10^{10}$ times.
$$k_{\text{cat}} = A_{\text{cat}} \cdot e^{-E_{\text{cat}} / RT}$$

**How Catalyst Differs from Other Variables (2 marks):**

| Variable | Catalyst | Other Variables |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Position in rate equation | Alters $k$ | Alter $k$ (T) or $f(C_i)$ |
| Effect on equilibrium | **No effect** | Can shift equilibrium (T, P) |
| Consumed in reaction | **No** | N/A |
| Selectivity | **Often specific** | Generic |
| Reversibility | Catalyzes both forward and reverse | Variable |

**Key point:** A catalyst is unique because it does **not change the thermodynamics** of the reaction, only the kinetics. It cannot make a non-spontaneous reaction occur.

---

#### (b) For a first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow B$ conducted in a batch reactor, derive the integrated rate law. If the initial concentration $C_{A0} = 2$ mol/L and the rate constant is $0.05$ min$^{-1}$, find the time required to achieve 75% conversion. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** The rate law for a first-order reaction is:
$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A$$

**Step 2:** For a constant-volume batch reactor, the material balance gives:
$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k \cdot C_A$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables:
$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \cdot dt$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $t = 0$ (where $C_A = C_{A0}$) to time $t$ (where $C_A = C_A$):
$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate:
$$\ln\left(\frac{C_A}{C_{A0}}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $C_A$:
$$C_A = C_{A0} \cdot e^{-k t}$$

This is the **integrated first-order rate law**. In terms of conversion $X_A = (C_{A0} - C_A)/C_{A0}$:
$$\ln(1 - X_A) = -k \cdot t$$

**Numerical Solution (3 marks):**

**Given:** $C_{A0} = 2$ mol/L, $k = 0.05$ min$^{-1}$, $X_A = 0.75$.

Using $\ln(1 - X_A) = -kt$:
$$\ln(1 - 0.75) = -0.05 \cdot t$$
$$\ln(0.25) = -0.05 \cdot t$$
$$-1.3863 = -0.05 \cdot t$$
$$t = \frac{1.3863}{0.05} = 27.73 \ \text{minutes}$$

**Verification using $C_A = C_{A0} e^{-kt}$:**
$$C_A = 2 \times 0.25 = 0.5 \ \text{mol/L}$$
$$0.5 = 2 \cdot e^{-0.05 t} \Rightarrow e^{-0.05 t} = 0.25 \Rightarrow t = 27.73 \ \text{min}$$ ✓

**Final Answer:** Time required for 75% conversion is **27.73 minutes**.

---

## 5.3 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Arrhenius:** Using $E$ in kJ/mol with $R$ in J/mol·K. Always convert: $E$ must be in **J/mol** when $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, OR use $R = 8.314 \times 10^{-3}$ kJ/mol·K with $E$ in kJ/mol. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Inverted Temperature Term:** Writing $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ instead of the correct form $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$. The sign matters! **(−1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Negative Sign:** In $-r_A = k C_A$, the negative sign is **not optional**. It indicates the disappearance of A. Forgetting it loses **0.5 – 1 mark** depending on the question.
>
> 4. **Order vs Molecularity:** Students often confuse these. *Molecularity* is theoretical (number of molecules colliding in an elementary step), while *order* is empirical (from experiments). They are **not always equal**! **(−1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Catalyst Effect on Equilibrium:** Writing that a catalyst "shifts the equilibrium" or "increases yield" is **WRONG**. A catalyst only speeds up the approach to equilibrium; it does not change $K$ or the equilibrium position. **(−1 to 2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Skipping the Conversion Step in First-Order Derivation:** Many students jump from $-r_A = k C_A$ to $C_A = C_{A0} e^{-kt}$ without showing the separation of variables and integration. KTU examiners require all integration steps. **(−1 to 2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Variables Affecting the Rate of Reaction" (KTU 2024 Module 1):**

### ✅ Core Variables (Must Memorize)
- [ ] **Concentration** $(C_i)$ → Power-law rate: $r_A = k C_A^{\alpha} C_B^{\beta}$
- [ ] **Temperature** $(T)$ → Arrhenius: $k = A e^{-E/RT}$
- [ ] **Pressure** $(P)$ → Gas-phase only, via $C_i = P_i / RT$
- [ ] **Catalyst** → Lowers $E$, does **not** change $\Delta G$ or $K$
- [ ] **Surface area** $(A_s)$ → Linear for heterogeneous reactions
- [ ] **Order of reaction** → Empirical, not always equal to molecularity
- [ ] **Solvent** → Affects polar reactions, transition state stabilization
- [ ] **Radiation/Light** → Photochemical reactions, depends on intensity

### ✅ Master Equations (Golden Set)
- [ ] Arrhenius: $k = A e^{-E/RT}$
- [ ] Two-temperature form: $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$
- [ ] Linear Arrhenius plot: $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ gives slope $-E/R$
- [ ] First-order integrated law: $\ln(C_A/C_{A0}) = -kt$
- [ ] Power-law rate: $r_A = k \prod C_i^{\alpha_i}$

### ✅ Key Numerical Values
- [ ] $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K
- [ ] Typical $E$ range: $40$ – $200$ kJ/mol
- [ ] "Rule of thumb": rate doubles per 10 K rise
- [ ] Pressure effect: $\propto P^{\sum \alpha_i}$ (gas phase only)

### ✅ Common KTU Misconceptions to Avoid
- [ ] Catalyst **does not** change $K$ or equilibrium position
- [ ] Pressure has **negligible** effect on liquid-phase reactions
- [ ] Order of reaction is **not always** an integer
- [ ] Molecularity $\neq$ Order in non-elementary reactions
- [ ] Arrhenius $A$ has units (depends on overall order)

### ✅ Typical KTU Question Patterns
- [ ] **3-mark:** List variables, explain effect of T on k, define catalyst
- [ ] **7-mark:** Derive Arrhenius / first-order integrated law / discuss effect of variables
- [ ] **14-mark:** Combined theory + numerical (e.g., find $E$ from two $k$ values, then find $k$ at a new $T$)
- [ ] **Always include units** in final numerical answers
- [ ] **Always show derivation steps** — KTU examiners mark each step

> **Final Tip:** This topic is the **gateway** to all of Module 2 (Reactor Design). Master the Arrhenius equation, the two-temperature form, and the first-order integrated law. They reappear in CSTR, PFR, PBR, and multiple-reaction analysis problems throughout the semester.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of Reaction Rate. Kinetics of Homogeneous Reactions: Concentration-Dependent and Temperature-Dependent Terms of Rate Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition of Reaction Rate. Kinetics of Homogeneous Reactions: Concentration-Dependent and Temperature-Dependent Terms of Rate Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Reaction Rate

The **rate of reaction** is defined as the rate at which the number of moles of a particular reactant or product changes per unit volume (for homogeneous reactions in a constant-volume reactor) per unit time. Mathematically, for a single irreversible reaction:

$$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$$

the rate of disappearance of A (reactant) is expressed as:

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

The rate of appearance of product C is given by:

$$r_C = \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_C}{dt}$$

where:
- $r_A$ = rate of reaction with respect to species A (mol/L·s)
- $N_A$ = number of moles of A at time $t$
- $V$ = reaction volume (L)
- The negative sign indicates the disappearance of A.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention**: The reaction rate $-r_A$ is *always* taken as positive. The minus sign compensates for the negative slope $\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ (since $N_A$ decreases with time). This is a guaranteed 1-mark question in Part A.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Toll Booth Picture**
> Imagine a single-lane highway with a toll booth at the exit. Cars enter the highway at a steady rate, and the toll booth counts how many cars pass per minute. The "reaction rate" here is simply the number of cars passing per minute. In a chemical reaction, **molecules are the cars**, the **reaction zone is the toll booth**, and the **rate is how many molecules "react" per second per unit volume**. Just like traffic rate depends on car density (concentration) and driver alertness (temperature), the chemical reaction rate depends on **concentration (C)** and **temperature (T)**.

### 1.3 Different Forms of Reaction Rate

Depending on the reactor geometry, reaction rate can be expressed in **four equivalent ways**:

| Basis | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volume basis (homogeneous) | $-r_A$ | $-\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ | mol/L·s |
| Mass of catalyst basis (heterogeneous) | $-r_A'$ | $-\frac{1}{W}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ | mol/g-cat·s |
| Surface area basis | $-r_A''$ | $-\frac{1}{S}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ | mol/m²·s |
| Reactor volume basis (gas, variable V) | $-r_A$ | $-\frac{1}{V}\frac{dF_{A0}(1-X_A)}{dt}$ | mol/L·s |

### 1.4 Relationship Between Species Rates

For a general reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$, the rates are interrelated by stoichiometry:

$$\frac{-r_A}{a} = \frac{-r_B}{b} = \frac{r_C}{c} = \frac{r_D}{d}$$

This is called the **relative rate of reaction**. If you know one rate, you can find all others using stoichiometric coefficients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Homogeneous reactions are those where the reactants and products exist in a **single phase** (all gas or all liquid). For Module 1, we deal exclusively with homogeneous systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Rate Equation

For any single homogeneous reaction, the rate equation has two fundamentally different dependencies:

$$-r_A = k(T) \cdot f(C)$$

This is the **separation principle** of reaction engineering, one of the most tested concepts in KTU. The function is a **product** (not a sum) of:

- A **temperature-dependent** term: $k(T)$ — the specific reaction rate (rate constant)
- A **concentration-dependent** term: $f(C)$ — describing how reactant concentrations influence the rate

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a product?** This factorization assumes that temperature and concentration affect the reaction rate *independently*. This is an empirical approximation that works extremely well for the vast majority of reactions encountered in industry.

### 2.2 Concentration-Dependent Term — The Rate Law

The functional form $f(C)$ is determined experimentally and is called the **rate law** (or kinetic expression). For a reaction:

$$aA + bB \rightarrow \text{products}$$

a generic rate law is:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^{\alpha} \cdot C_B^{\beta}$$

where:
- $k$ = rate constant (units depend on order)
- $\alpha, \beta$ = orders with respect to A and B
- $n = \alpha + \beta$ = **overall order of reaction**

#### Key Concepts in Rate Law

- **Order of reaction**: The exponent to which concentration is raised. It is *not* necessarily equal to stoichiometric coefficients — it is determined by experiment.
- **Molecularity**: The number of molecules colliding in a single elementary step. *Equal to stoichiometric coefficient only for elementary reactions.*
- **Zero-order**: $-r_A = k$ (rate independent of concentration, e.g., surface-catalyzed reactions in saturation)
- **First-order**: $-r_A = kC_A$ (radioactive decay, many liquid-phase reactions)
- **Second-order**: $-r_A = kC_A^2$ (dimerization, some hydrolysis reactions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Pitfall**: Students often confuse *order* with *molecularity*. Molecularity is a theoretical concept (referring to a mechanism), while order is an experimental measurement. For elementary reactions, they coincide.

### 2.3 Temperature-Dependent Term — Arrhenius Equation

The rate constant $k$ varies with temperature according to the **Arrhenius equation**, proposed by Svante Arrhenius (1889):

$$k(T) = k_0 \cdot e^{-E/RT}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln k = \ln k_0 - \frac{E}{RT}$$

In base-10 logarithm form:

$$\log k = \log k_0 - \frac{E}{2.303\,RT}$$

where:
- $k_0$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor — same units as $k$
- $E$ = activation energy (J/mol or cal/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/mol·K**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### 2.4 Ratio of Rate Constants at Two Temperatures

A very common KTU problem asks: *How much does the rate increase when temperature rises from $T_1$ to $T_2$?* Using two-point Arrhenius:

$$\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

This lets us compute $E$ experimentally from $k_1$ and $k_2$ measured at two different temperatures.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ | General definition of rate (constant volume) |
| 2 | $r_C = \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_C}{dt}$ | Rate of product formation |
| 3 | $\frac{-r_A}{a} = \frac{-r_B}{b} = \frac{r_C}{c}$ | Relative rate relation (stoichiometry) |
| 4 | $-r_A = k(T) \cdot f(C_A, C_B,\ldots)$ | General rate equation (separability) |
| 5 | $-r_A = k C_A^{\alpha} C_B^{\beta}$ | Power-law rate expression |
| 6 | $n = \alpha + \beta$ | Overall reaction order |
| 7 | $k = k_0 \, e^{-E/RT}$ | Arrhenius equation |
| 8 | $\ln k = \ln k_0 - \frac{E}{RT}$ | Linearized Arrhenius form |
| 9 | $\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-temperature Arrhenius ratio |
| 10 | $E = 2.303\,R \left[\frac{\log k_2 - \log k_1}{(1/T_1)-(1/T_2)}\right]$ | Activation energy from two $k$ values |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility**: The Arrhenius equation is the cornerstone of chemical plant design. Reactor engineers use it to predict how a production rate will change with seasonal temperature variations, optimize exothermic/endo thermic reactions, design safe operating limits, and model chemical vapor deposition, polymerization, and biological reactions. Activation energy $E$ is a "fingerprint" of a reaction — high $E$ means the reaction is highly sensitive to temperature (e.g., combustion), low $E$ means temperature-insensitive (e.g., enzyme-catalyzed reactions).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Arrhenius Equation (Collision Theory Perspective)

We begin from the fundamental idea that only molecules with energy ≥ $E$ can react. From the **Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution**, the fraction of molecules with energy ≥ $E$ at temperature $T$ is proportional to $e^{-E/RT}$.

**Step 1**: Start with the Maxwell-Boltzmann energy distribution function for the fraction of molecules with energy $\geq E$:

$$f(E) \propto e^{-E/RT}$$

**Step 2**: The reaction rate is proportional to this fraction (because only "activated" molecules react):

$$k \propto e^{-E/RT}$$

**Step 3**: Introduce the proportionality constant $k_0$ (which represents collision frequency and orientation factor):

$$k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E/RT}$$

**Step 4**: Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln k = \ln k_0 - \frac{E}{RT}$$

**Step 5**: Converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ using $\ln x = 2.303 \log x$:

$$\log k = \log k_0 - \frac{E}{2.303\,RT}$$

This is the final, experiment-ready form. A plot of $\log k$ vs $\frac{1}{T}$ gives a straight line with slope $-\frac{E}{2.303\,R}$ and intercept $\log k_0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Two-Temperature Ratio Form

This derivation is **highly valued** by KTU examiners — it appears in nearly every Part B question.

**Step 1**: Write Arrhenius equation at $T_1$:

$$k_1 = k_0 \, e^{-E/RT_1}$$

**Step 2**: Write Arrhenius equation at $T_2$:

$$k_2 = k_0 \, e^{-E/RT_2}$$

**Step 3**: Divide equation for $k_2$ by equation for $k_1$ (the $k_0$ cancels out):

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{e^{-E/RT_2}}{e^{-E/RT_1}}$$

**Step 4**: Combine exponents (subtract because of division):

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\left(\frac{-E}{RT_2}\right) - \left(\frac{-E}{RT_1}\right)} = e^{\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 5**: Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 6**: Solve explicitly for $E$:

$$E = R \cdot \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

**Step 7**: Convert to base-10 log for tabular experimental data:

$$E = 2.303\,R \cdot \frac{\log(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Activation Energy Calculation

**Problem**: The rate constant of a reaction is $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 300$ K and $k_2 = 8.5 \times 10^{-2}$ s$^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 320$ K. Calculate the activation energy $E$.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Substitute values into the formula:

$$E = \frac{(8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}) \cdot \ln\left(\frac{8.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}\right)}{\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{320}}$$

**Step 2**: Calculate the ratio inside the log:

$$\frac{8.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 34$$

**Step 3**: Take natural log:

$$\ln(34) = 3.526$$

**Step 4**: Calculate denominator:

$$\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{320} = \frac{320 - 300}{300 \times 320} = \frac{20}{96000} = 2.083 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5**: Calculate $E$:

$$E = \frac{8.314 \times 3.526}{2.083 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{29.32}{2.083 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 6**: Final result:

$$E = 1.408 \times 10^{5}\,\text{J/mol} = 140.8\,\text{kJ/mol}$$

This is a typical chemical reaction with moderate activation energy — characteristic of many industrially relevant reactions (e.g., esterification, certain oxidations).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of the Rate Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reaction Rate -rA"] --> B["k of T - Temperature Dependent"]
    A --> C["f of C - Concentration Dependent"]
    B --> B1["Arrhenius Equation"]
    B --> B2["k0 exp -E by RT"]
    B1 --> B3["Activation Energy E"]
    B1 --> B4["Pre-exponential Factor k0"]
    C --> C1["Rate Law k Cn"]
    C --> C2["Order of Reaction n"]
    C1 --> C3["Zero Order"]
    C1 --> C4["First Order"]
    C1 --> C5["Second Order"]
    C --> C6["Molecularity - Mechanism"]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram — Homogeneous Reaction Kinetics Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["INPUTS"]
        direction TB
        I1["Species A Concentration CA"]
        I2["Species B Concentration CB"]
        I3["Reactor Temperature T"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["RATE CALCULATION CORE"]
        direction TB
        X1["Concentration Function f of C"]
        X2["Temperature Function k of T - Arrhenius"]
        X3["Rate Multiplication -rA = k times f"]
        X1 --> X3
        X2 --> X3
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUTS"]
        direction TB
        O1["Rate of Disappearance -rA"]
        O2["Rate of Appearance rC rD"]
        O3["Conversion XA"]
        O4["Reactor Sizing"]
    end

    I1 --> X1
    I2 --> X1
    I3 --> X2
    X3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Arrhenius Plot Schematic — $\log k$ vs $1/T$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Linearized Arrhenius Equation"] --> P2["log k equals log k0 minus E by 2.303 R times 1 by T"]
    P2 --> P3["Y axis log k"]
    P2 --> P4["X axis 1 by T in K inverse"]
    P3 --> P5["Slope = minus E by 2.303 R - gives E"]
    P4 --> P6["Intercept = log k0 - gives k0"]
    P5 --> P7["HIGH E = STEEP SLOPE - Reaction highly temperature sensitive"]
    P6 --> P8["LOW E = SHALLOW SLOPE - Reaction weakly temperature sensitive"]
```

### 4.4 Effect of Temperature on Rate — Behaviour Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Reaction Type"] --> Q2["Endothermic EA high"]
    Q1 --> Q3["Exothermic EA moderate"]
    Q1 --> Q4["Catalyzed EA low"]
    Q2 --> R1["Rate increases sharply with T"]
    Q3 --> R2["Rate increases moderately with T"]
    Q4 --> R3["Rate barely changes with T - catalyst effect dominates"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for Mermaid Diagrams**:
> - **Diagram 4.1** decomposes the rate equation into its two functional parts.
> - **Diagram 4.2** shows how input concentrations and temperature feed into the core calculation engine to yield conversion and reactor design parameters.
> - **Diagram 4.3** illustrates the famous Arrhenius plot — its slope and intercept yield the kinetic parameters.
> - **Diagram 4.4** classifies reactions by their temperature sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Define the rate of a homogeneous reaction with respect to species A. Mention the units.

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The rate of a homogeneous reaction with respect to species A is defined as the rate at which the number of moles of A changes per unit volume per unit time.

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

**Units**: mol/(L·s) or equivalently kg/(m³·s) depending on the system.  
**Negative sign**: introduced because $N_A$ decreases with time as A is consumed, while the rate of reaction is conventionally taken as positive.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for units & sign convention.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]**  
State the Arrhenius equation and explain the physical significance of the activation energy $E$.

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The Arrhenius equation expresses the temperature dependence of the rate constant:

$$k = k_0 \, e^{-E/RT}$$

**Physical significance of $E$**: The activation energy $E$ (J/mol) is the minimum energy threshold that reactant molecules must possess to undergo successful reaction upon collision. It represents the energy barrier of the reaction pathway. Reactions with high $E$ are highly temperature-sensitive, while reactions with low $E$ (e.g., enzyme-catalyzed, $E < 50$ kJ/mol) are relatively insensitive. Typical industrial reactions have $E$ in the range 50–250 kJ/mol.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for definition of $E$, 1 mark for physical significance with examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the Arrhenius equation from the Maxwell-Boltzmann energy distribution. Show that a plot of $\ln k$ versus $1/T$ yields a straight line and explain how the activation energy and pre-exponential factor can be obtained from this plot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a first-order homogeneous reaction $A \rightarrow \text{products}$, the rate constant doubles when the temperature increases from 300 K to 310 K. Calculate the activation energy of the reaction. Also calculate the rate constant at 350 K, given that $k_{300} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ s$^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**:

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: According to the kinetic theory of gases (Maxwell-Boltzmann), the fraction of molecules having energy greater than or equal to $E$ at temperature $T$ is:

$$f(E) = e^{-E/RT}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark)**: Only molecules with this threshold energy can react upon collision. Hence, the rate constant $k$ is proportional to this fraction:

$$k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E/RT}$$

where $k_0$ is the pre-exponential factor (frequency factor) accounting for collision frequency and proper orientation.

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: Taking natural logarithm:

$$\ln k = \ln k_0 - \frac{E}{RT}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: This is of the form $y = mx + c$, where $y = \ln k$, $x = 1/T$, slope $m = -E/R$, and intercept $c = \ln k_0$.

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: A plot of $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ is therefore a straight line. The slope gives:

$$E = -m \cdot R$$

**Step 6 (1 mark)**: The intercept on the y-axis gives:

$$k_0 = e^c$$

**Step 7 (1 mark)**: Hence, by carrying out experiments at several temperatures and measuring $k$, both $E$ and $k_0$ can be determined from a single Arrhenius plot.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**:

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Given: $k_2/k_1 = 2$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 310$ K. Use:

$$E = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)**: Substitute values:

$$E = \frac{(8.314) \cdot \ln(2)}{(1/300) - (1/310)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: Calculate the denominator:

$$\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{310} = \frac{310 - 300}{300 \times 310} = \frac{10}{93000} = 1.0753 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: Calculate $E$:

$$E = \frac{8.314 \times 0.6931}{1.0753 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{5.762}{1.0753 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.358 \times 10^{4}\,\text{J/mol} = 53.58\,\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: Now calculate $k_{350}$. Using $T_1 = 300$ K and $T_3 = 350$ K:

$$\ln \frac{k_{350}}{k_{300}} = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{350}\right)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark)**: Calculate the new denominator:

$$\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{350} = \frac{350 - 300}{300 \times 350} = \frac{50}{105000} = 4.762 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 (0 marks — final answer box)**: Substitute:

$$\ln \frac{k_{350}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{53580}{8.314} \times 4.762 \times 10^{-4} = 6445.5 \times 4.762 \times 10^{-4} = 3.069$$

**Final answer**: $k_{350} = (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot e^{3.069} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 21.55 = 5.39 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$.

**Result**: $E = 53.58$ kJ/mol; $k_{350} = 5.39 \times 10^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$.

---
