#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** For a homogeneous reaction with rate expression $-r_A = k C_A^{0.5} C_B^{1.5}$, identify the order with respect to A, the order with respect to B, and the overall order. If $k = 2.0$ L$^{1.5}$/(mol$^{1.5}$·s) when concentrations are in mol/L, what are the units of $-r_A$? Is this reaction elementary? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A second-order homogeneous reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{products}$ has a rate constant $k = 0.05$ L/(mol·min) at 25 °C and $k = 0.50$ L/(mol·min) at 45 °C. Calculate:  
(i) the activation energy $E$,  
(ii) the pre-exponential factor $k_0$,  
(iii) the rate constant at 65 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**:

**Step 1 (2 marks)**: Order with respect to A = 0.5 (half-order); Order with respect to B = 1.5; Overall order $n = 0.5 + 1.5 = 2.0$ (second order overall).

**Step 2 (2 marks)**: Unit analysis:

$$[-r_A] = \frac{\text{mol}}{\text{L}\cdot\text{s}} = k \cdot (C_A)^{0.5} \cdot (C_B)^{1.5}$$

$$k \text{ must have units} = \frac{\text{mol/L·s}}{(\text{mol/L})^{0.5+1.5}} = \frac{\text{mol/L·s}}{(\text{mol/L})^2} = \frac{\text{L}}{\text{mol·s}}$$

The given $k = 2.0$ L$^{1.5}$/(mol$^{1.5}$·s) is in **wrong units** for second-order — this hints the reaction is *not* a simple elementary second-order.

**Step 3 (2 marks)**: For an elementary reaction, the order must equal molecularity, which equals stoichiometric coefficients (i.e., 1 with respect to A, 1 with respect to B). Here, the orders are fractional (0.5 and 1.5), so this reaction is **not elementary** — it is a complex reaction following an empirical power-law.

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: Conclusion: The reaction is a complex non-elementary reaction with empirical rate law; the orders are determined purely by experiment, not by stoichiometry.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**:

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Convert temperatures: $T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298$ K; $T_2 = 45 + 273 = 318$ K; $T_3 = 65 + 273 = 338$ K.

**Step 2 (2 marks)**: Calculate $E$:

$$E = R \cdot \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)} = 8.314 \cdot \frac{\ln(0.50/0.05)}{(1/298) - (1/318)}$$

$$= 8.314 \cdot \frac{\ln(10)}{0.0001162} = 8.314 \cdot \frac{2.3026}{0.0001162} = 8.314 \times 19816 = 1.648 \times 10^{5}\,\text{J/mol}$$

So $E = 164.8$ kJ/mol.

**Step 3 (2 marks)**: Calculate $k_0$ using $k = k_0 e^{-E/RT}$ at $T_1 = 298$ K:

$$k_0 = k_1 \cdot e^{E/RT_1} = 0.05 \cdot e^{164800/(8.314 \times 298)} = 0.05 \cdot e^{66.50}$$

$e^{66.50}$ is astronomically large (~$10^{28.9}$). Hence $k_0 \approx 0.05 \times 10^{28.9} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{26}$ L/(mol·min).

**Step 4 (2 marks)**: Calculate $k$ at $T_3 = 338$ K:

$$\ln \frac{k_3}{k_1} = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_3}\right) = \frac{164800}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{338}\right)$$

$$= 19816 \times \left(\frac{40}{298 \times 338}\right) = 19816 \times 3.972 \times 10^{-4} = 7.87$$

$$k_3 = 0.05 \cdot e^{7.87} = 0.05 \times 2615 \approx 130.7\,\text{L/(mol·min)}$$

**Results**: $E = 164.8$ kJ/mol, $k_0 \approx 4 \times 10^{26}$ L/(mol·min), $k_{338} \approx 130.7$ L/(mol·min).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Reaction Rate & Arrhenius Questions**:
> 1. **Sign convention error (loses 1 mark):** Forgetting the negative sign in $-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$. Always justify why it's negative.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency (loses 1–2 marks):** Mixing cal/mol with J/mol or °C with K. KTU strictly expects SI: J/mol and K.
> 3. **Order vs Molecularity confusion (loses 1 mark):** Always clarify *experimentally measured order* vs *theoretical molecularity*.
> 4. **Wrong log conversion (loses 2 marks):** Forgetting to multiply by $2.303$ when converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$.
> 5. **Temperature in Celsius (loses 1 mark):** Arrhenius equation is valid **only** with absolute temperature in Kelvin.
> 6. **Skipping steps in derivation (loses 2–3 marks):** Always show every algebraic step in Arrhenius derivations — the steps themselves carry marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Rate of Reaction**: $-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ (always positive for reactants; negative sign compensates for decreasing $N_A$).
- **Separability Principle**: $-r_A = k(T) \cdot f(C)$ — temperature and concentration effects are *independent* and multiplicative.
- **Rate Law**: Power-law form $-r_A = k C_A^\alpha C_B^\beta$ where $\alpha$, $\beta$ are determined experimentally.
- **Order** = sum of exponents; **Molecularity** = number of molecules colliding in elementary step.
- **Elementary reactions** have order = molecularity = stoichiometric coefficient.
- **Non-elementary (complex) reactions** have fractional or different orders.
- **Arrhenius Equation**: $k = k_0 e^{-E/RT}$; $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.
- **Linearized form**: $\ln k = \ln k_0 - \frac{E}{RT}$; slope gives $E$, intercept gives $k_0$.
- **Two-temperature formula**: $\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ — most-tested formula in Part B.
- **Always use Kelvin** for $T$ in Arrhenius equation.
- **Activation Energy Range**: $E \approx 50$–$250$ kJ/mol for most industrial reactions; < 50 kJ/mol for catalyzed reactions.
- **Rule of thumb**: Many reactions double their rate for every 10 K rise (only valid for moderate $E$ around 50 kJ/mol at room temperature).
- **Relative rate stoichiometry**: $\frac{-r_A}{a} = \frac{-r_B}{b} = \frac{r_C}{c} = \frac{r_D}{d}$ — use this whenever asked to relate rates of different species.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Dependency from Arrhenius Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature Dependency from Arrhenius Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Arrhenius Law** (proposed by Swedish chemist **Svante Arrhenius** in 1889) is a foundational empirical relationship in Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE) that mathematically quantifies how the **rate constant** ($k$) of a chemical reaction varies as a function of absolute temperature ($T$). It expresses the **temperature dependency** of reaction kinetics and forms the analytical bridge between molecular collision theory and macroscopic reactor design.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with Fogler, Levenspiel, and Smith frameworks, the law is stated as:

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU 2024 Canonical Form:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$
> 
> Where $k$ = rate constant, $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor, $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = Universal Gas Constant (**8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**), $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a hill that separates a valley of reactants from a valley of products. The **activation energy $E_a$** is the *height of the hill*. A reaction cannot proceed unless reactant molecules possess enough energy to "climb over" this energy barrier.

- **Low temperature** → Most molecules roll slowly at the base, few can climb → slow reaction.
- **High temperature** → Molecules are energetic; many clear the barrier → fast reaction.

The **pre-exponential factor $A$** is analogous to the *number of attempts per second* molecules make to climb — it represents the frequency of properly oriented, energetic collisions. The exponential term $e^{-E_a/RT}$ is the *probability* that any single attempt is successful (a Boltzmann probability).

> [!NOTE]
> **Boltzmann Insight:** The fraction of molecules with energy $\geq E_a$ at temperature $T$ follows the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution, which is exactly what $e^{-E_a/RT}$ represents. This is why the law is "Arrhenius" — it is a thermodynamic-statistical statement, not merely an empirical fit.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314** | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| $R$ | (Alternate form) | **1.987** | cal·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| $T$ | Absolute Temperature | 298 (room) | K |
| $E_a$ | Activation Energy | 50,000 – 250,000 | J·mol⁻¹ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Theoretical Breakdown

The Arrhenius law is built on three cornerstone assumptions that every KTU answer must reflect:

1. **Collisional Premise:** Reaction occurs only between molecules that *collide* with sufficient energy AND proper orientation.
2. **Boltzmann Energy Distribution:** The fraction of molecules possessing energy $\geq E_a$ is governed by the Boltzmann factor $e^{-E_a/RT}$.
3. **Frequency Factor $A$:** Represents the maximum rate constant when $T \to \infty$ (i.e., when virtually all collisions are energetic enough). It is essentially *temperature-independent* in the classical Arrhenius model.

### Why the Exponential Form?

The Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution predicts the number density of molecules with energy greater than some threshold $E_a$ as proportional to $e^{-E_a/RT}$. The smaller $E_a$ or larger $T$, the larger this fraction, leading to a faster reaction. The mathematics is thermodynamic in origin — it is **not** an arbitrary curve fit.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE & Internal Exams:**

| S.No | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius Law (Canonical) | Direct rate constant calculation |
| 2 | $\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$ | Linearized form | Plot $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ to find $E_a$ and $A$ |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-Temperature Method | Find $k_2$ given $k_1$ and two $T$'s |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d(\ln k)}{dT} = \dfrac{E_a}{RT^2}$ | Differential form | Derivation basis |
| 5 | $\dfrac{E_a}{R} = -\text{slope}$ of $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ | Graphical interpretation | Experimental data analysis |
| 6 | $E_a = \dfrac{R \, T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$ | Rearranged two-T form | Activation energy from $k$–$T$ data |
| 7 | $E_a = \dfrac{2.303 \, R \, T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \, \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$ | Common log form | Calculator-friendly form |

**Critical Note on Table Syntax:** All absolute values and divisions use $\vert$ or $\dfrac{}{}$ to prevent markdown breakage.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Design:** Predicting optimum operating temperature for maximum conversion in CSTR/PFR.
- **Process Safety:** Estimating runaway reaction thresholds (e.g., in exothermic polymerizations).
- **Catalysis:** Comparing activation energies of catalyzed vs. uncatalyzed paths to determine catalyst effectiveness factor.
- **Storage & Shelf-Life:** Predicting degradation rates of pharmaceuticals and food products (often follows Arrhenius kinetics).
- **Industrial Scale-Up:** Translating lab-rate data at $T_1$ to plant operating conditions at $T_2$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Arrhenius Equation from Collision Theory

**Starting Premise:** Reaction rate $\propto$ (collision frequency) $\times$ (fraction with energy $\geq E_a$).

$$r \propto Z_{AB} \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Step 1:** Identify the proportionality constants. The collision frequency $Z_{AB}$ from kinetic theory of gases is:

$$Z_{AB} = N_A \sigma_{AB} \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu_{AB}}} \, n_A n_B$$

Where $\sigma_{AB}$ is the collision cross-section, $\mu_{AB}$ is the reduced mass, $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $n_A, n_B$ are number densities.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the rate expression and group all weakly temperature-dependent terms into a single constant $A$:

$$r = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot C_A C_B$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the specific rate constant is:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

This is the **Arrhenius law**, formally derived from collision theory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Linearized Form (for Experimental Data)

**Step 1:** Start with the canonical Arrhenius equation:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln k = \ln\!\left(A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the logarithm product rule $\ln(xy) = \ln x + \ln y$:

$$\ln k = \ln A + \ln\!\left(e^{-E_a / RT}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the identity $\ln(e^x) = x$:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$$

**Step 5:** Rewrite in standard $y = mx + c$ form by letting $y = \ln k$ and $x = 1/T$:

$$\ln k = -\dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + \ln A$$

**Step 6:** Equate with line equation $y = mx + c$:

$$m = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}, \quad c = \ln A$$

This is why an Arrhenius plot (of $\ln k$ vs $1/T$) is a straight line with negative slope.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Two-Temperature Method

**Step 1:** Write the linearized Arrhenius equation at temperature $T_1$:

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R \, T_1}$$

**Step 2:** Write the linearized Arrhenius equation at temperature $T_2$:

$$\ln k_2 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R \, T_2}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract equation (1) from equation (2). The $\ln A$ terms cancel:

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\dfrac{E_a}{R \, T_2} - \left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R \, T_1}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the logarithm quotient rule $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$ on the left:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $E_a$ algebraically by multiplying both sides by $R$ and rearranging:

$$E_a = R \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \cdot \dfrac{T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to common logarithm form using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E_a = \dfrac{2.303 \, R \, T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$$

This is the **most-tested KTU formula** for this module.

---

## 3.4 Derivative Form (Theoretical Foundation)

Differentiating $\ln k$ with respect to $T$:

$$\dfrac{d(\ln k)}{dT} = \dfrac{d}{dT}\!\left(\ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Since $\ln A$ is constant and using chain rule on $1/T$:

$$\dfrac{d(\ln k)}{dT} = -E_a \cdot \dfrac{d}{dT}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{RT}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{d(\ln k)}{dT} = -E_a \cdot \left(-\dfrac{1}{RT^2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d(\ln k)}{dT} = \dfrac{E_a}{RT^2}}$$

This differential form is the **starting point** for many KTU Part B derivations involving temperature profiles in non-isothermal reactors.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Insight

**Example Problem Setup:** A first-order reaction has $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \, s^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 600 \, K$ and $k_2 = 1.5 \times 10^{-2} \, s^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 700 \, K$. Find $E_a$.

**Step 1:** Plug into the two-temperature formula:

$$E_a = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 600 \times 700}{700 - 600} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the ratio inside the log:

$$\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 60$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\log_{10}(60) = 1.7782$

**Step 4:** Compute the temperature term:

$$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 600 \times 700}{100} = \dfrac{8,043,565.4}{100} = 80,435.65$$

**Step 5:** Multiply:

$$E_a = 80,435.65 \times 1.7782 = 143,030 \, \text{J/mol} \approx 143.03 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Arrhenius Law — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Temperature T in Kelvin] --> B1{Temperature Valid? T greater than 273}
    B1 -- No --> C0[Reject Input Use STP 298 K]
    B1 -- Yes --> C1[Lookup Activation Energy Ea from Database]
    C1 --> D1[Lookup Frequency Factor A from Database]
    D1 --> E1[Compute Exponential Term exp of -Ea over RT]
    E1 --> F1[Multiply A with Exponential Term]
    F1 --> G1[Output Rate Constant k]
    G1 --> H1[Route k to Reactor Design Module]
    H1 --> I1[Route k to Conversion Calculator]
    H1 --> J1[Route k to Safety Margin Estimator]
    style A0 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style G1 fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#28A745
    style H1 fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#FFC107
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Two-Temperature Method Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Collect k1 at T1] --> Q1[Collect k2 at T2]
    Q1 --> R1[Compute Ratio k2 over k1]
    R1 --> S1[Take Common Log log10 of Ratio]
    S1 --> T1[Compute Temperature Term 2.303 R T1 T2 over T2 minus T1]
    T1 --> U1[Multiply Log and Temperature Term]
    U1 --> V1[Output Activation Energy Ea in J per mol]
    V1 --> W1[Convert Ea to kJ per mol by dividing 1000]
    style P1 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B
    style V1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style W1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Arrhenius Plot Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Experimental Data Set of k versus T] --> L1[Transform k to ln k]
    L1 --> M1[Transform T to reciprocal 1 over T]
    M1 --> N1[Plot ln k on Y axis versus 1 over T on X axis]
    N1 --> O1[Fit Straight Line via Least Squares]
    O1 --> P1[Extract Slope m from Fit]
    P1 --> Q1[Compute Ea equals negative m times R]
    O1 --> R1[Extract Intercept c from Fit]
    R1 --> S1[Compute A equals exp of c]
    style K1 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style Q1 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style S1 fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q:** State the Arrhenius law and explain the physical significance of the pre-exponential factor $A$ and the activation energy $E_a$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Arrhenius law is given by $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$, where $k$ is the rate constant, $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ is the activation energy, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature.
> 
> **Physical Significance of $A$ (1.5 marks):** The pre-exponential factor $A$ represents the frequency of molecular collisions with proper orientation per unit concentration. It is the maximum limiting value of the rate constant as $T \to \infty$, and is essentially temperature-independent in the classical model.
> 
> **Physical Significance of $E_a$ (1.5 marks):** Activation energy is the minimum threshold energy that reactant molecules must possess to undergo a successful reaction. It represents the height of the energy barrier on the potential energy surface. A higher $E_a$ implies greater temperature sensitivity of the rate.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** How is activation energy determined graphically from experimental $k$–$T$ data?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Arrhenius equation in linearized form is:
> $$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$$
> 
> A plot of $\ln k$ (y-axis) versus $1/T$ (x-axis) yields a **straight line** with **negative slope** equal to $-E_a/R$ and **y-intercept** equal to $\ln A$ (1.5 marks).
> 
> Therefore, **activation energy** is calculated as $E_a = -(\text{slope}) \times R$ (1 mark). The **frequency factor** is obtained from $A = e^{\text{intercept}}$ (0.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the linearized form of the Arrhenius equation and explain how the activation energy and frequency factor are obtained from an Arrhenius plot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a first-order irreversible reaction, the rate constant doubles when the temperature is raised from 300 K to 310 K. Calculate the activation energy of the reaction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the canonical form:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Rearrange into $y = mx + c$ form:

$$\ln k = -\dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + \ln A$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify slope $m = -E_a/R$ and intercept $c = \ln A$.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Explain graphical procedure: Plot $\ln k$ vs $1/T$, fit a straight line, and from the slope compute $E_a = -mR$, and from intercept compute $A = e^c$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $k_2 = 2 k_1$, $T_1 = 300 \, K$, $T_2 = 310 \, K$.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius equation:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Substitute values:

$$\ln(2) = \dfrac{E_a}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{310}\right)$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute the bracket term:

$$\dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{310} = \dfrac{310 - 300}{300 \times 310} = \dfrac{10}{93000} = 1.0753 \times 10^{-4} \, K^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Solve for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \dfrac{\ln(2) \times 8.314}{1.0753 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{0.6931 \times 8.314}{1.0753 \times 10^{-4}} = 53,594 \, J/mol \approx 53.6 \, kJ/mol$$

**Final Answer:** $E_a \approx 53.6 \, kJ/mol$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Starting from the Arrhenius equation, derive the expression for activation energy using the two-temperature method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The rate constants of a chemical reaction are $1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, s^{-1}$ at 500 K and $4.5 \times 10^{-2} \, s^{-1}$ at 600 K. Determine the activation energy, the pre-exponential factor, and the rate constant at 550 K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with the linearized form of Arrhenius law:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Write the equation for two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R T_1}, \quad \ln k_2 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R T_2}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Subtract the first from the second:

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\dfrac{E_a}{R T_2} + \dfrac{E_a}{R T_1}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Apply $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$ and factor $E_a/R$:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Solve for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \dfrac{R \cdot T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$$

This is the required two-temperature expression for activation energy.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $k_1 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, s^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 500 \, K$; $k_2 = 4.5 \times 10^{-2} \, s^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 600 \, K$.

**Step 1 — Activation Energy (3 marks):**

$$E_a = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 500 \times 600}{600 - 500} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}\right)$$

$$E_a = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 3 \times 10^{5}}{100} \cdot \log_{10}(30)$$

$$E_a = 57,440 \times 1.4771 = 84,853 \, J/mol \approx 84.85 \, kJ/mol$$

**Step 2 — Frequency Factor (2 marks):** Use $k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_1}$:

$$A = k_1 \cdot e^{E_a / R T_1} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot e^{84,853 / (8.314 \times 500)}$$

$$A = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot e^{20.41} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.17 \times 10^{8} = 1.076 \times 10^{6} \, s^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Rate Constant at 550 K (2 marks):**

$$k_3 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_3} = 1.076 \times 10^{6} \cdot e^{-84,853 / (8.314 \times 550)}$$

$$k_3 = 1.076 \times 10^{6} \cdot e^{-18.555} = 1.076 \times 10^{6} \times 9.06 \times 10^{-9} = 9.75 \times 10^{-3} \, s^{-1}$$

**Final Answers:** $E_a \approx 84.85 \, kJ/mol$, $A \approx 1.076 \times 10^{6} \, s^{-1}$, $k_{550K} \approx 9.75 \times 10^{-3} \, s^{-1}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Valued strictly by KTU Board):**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in $E_a$:** Many students report $E_a$ in J/mol when the question asks for kJ/mol (or vice-versa). Always read the unit demand carefully. **$E_a$ in J/mol must be divided by 1000 to get kJ/mol.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert $T$ to Kelvin:** A classic error is using Celsius in the two-temperature formula. $T$ **must be in Kelvin** since the gas constant $R$ is defined per Kelvin.
> 
> 3. **Sign Error in Slope:** The slope of the Arrhenius plot is $-E_a/R$ (negative). Students often write $E_a/R$ (positive) — this leads to a *negative* activation energy, which is physically meaningless.
> 
> 4. **Mixing Up $\ln$ and $\log_{10}$:** The factor $2.303$ appears ONLY when using $\log_{10}$. If you use $\ln$, do **not** multiply by $2.303$. Pick one convention and stick to it.
> 
> 5. **Inadequate Explanation of $A$ and $E_a$:** In 3-mark Part A questions, students often give just the formula. Examiners expect a 1–2 sentence **physical interpretation** of each term — at least 0.5–1 mark is reserved for this.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" of Linearization:** When deriving the linearized form, examiners at KTU 2024 expect the step where $y = \ln k$ and $x = 1/T$ are explicitly identified. Missing this step costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Quick-Recall Summary:**

- **Canonical Arrhenius:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ — remember to always use $T$ in **Kelvin**.
- **Linearized form:** $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ — slope is **negative**, equal to $-E_a/R$.
- **Two-temperature formula:** $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ — the most-tested formula in KTU Part B.
- **Differential form:** $\dfrac{d(\ln k)}{dT} = \dfrac{E_a}{RT^2}$ — basis for non-isothermal reactor analysis.
- **Frequency factor $A$:** Units depend on reaction order (e.g., $s^{-1}$ for 1st order, $\text{L}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot s^{-1}$ for 2nd order).
- **Activation energy $E_a$:** Always in **J/mol** when using $R = 8.314$; convert to kJ/mol by dividing by 1000.
- **Arrhenius plot is linear** ONLY when $\ln k$ is plotted against $1/T$ (NOT against $T$ directly).
- **Larger $E_a$** = greater temperature sensitivity of reaction rate.
- **Catalyst reduces $E_a$** without changing $A$ significantly — this is the key principle of catalysis.
- **$R = 8.314 \, J\cdot mol^{-1}\cdot K^{-1} = 1.987 \, cal\cdot mol^{-1}\cdot K^{-1}$** — choose the right one based on the unit of $E_a$.
- **Common KTU trick:** If a question gives $k$ in arbitrary units (e.g., $\text{min}^{-1}$ vs $s^{-1}$), the *ratio* $k_2/k_1$ cancels the unit — no conversion needed for the Arrhenius calculation.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Collision Theory (CT)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Collision Theory is a kinetic theory-based model that explains chemical reaction rates by treating reactant molecules as hard spheres that must collide with sufficient energy (greater than the activation energy, **Eₐ**) and proper orientation (governed by the steric or probability factor, **ρ**) for a reaction to occur.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statement (KTU Board Favourite):**
> According to Collision Theory, the reaction rate is the product of the collision frequency, the steric factor, and the Boltzmann energy factor:
> $$\text{Rate} = Z_{AB} \cdot \rho \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$
> where **Z_AB** is the collision frequency between A and B molecules (m⁻³·s⁻¹), **ρ** is the steric/orientation factor (dimensionless, 0 < ρ ≤ 1), **Eₐ** is the activation energy (J/mol), **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** is the universal gas constant, and **T** is absolute temperature (K).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Bumper Cars" 🎢
Imagine two bumper cars in an amusement park. For them to "react" (crash and produce sparks), **three things must happen together**:
1. They must actually **collide** (frequency).
2. They must hit **fast enough** to overcome the rubber bumpers — slow nudges don't work (energy threshold = **Eₐ**).
3. They must hit **bumper-to-bumper** and not side-to-side (orientation = **ρ**).

If any one of these fails, the reaction simply does not occur — even if everything else is "right."

### Underlying Assumptions of Collision Theory
- Reactant molecules behave as **rigid, non-interacting hard spheres**.
- The **Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution** of molecular speeds applies.
- A reaction occurs only when colliding molecules possess kinetic energy along the line of centres ≥ **Eₐ**.
- The steric factor **ρ** empirically corrects for unfavourable molecular orientations.
- Valid primarily for **bimolecular gas-phase reactions**; inadequate for complex unimolecular decompositions and liquid-phase reactions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation Snapshot (Frequently asked in KTU Part A):**
> Collision Theory fails for unimolecular reactions (e.g., decompositions) and for reactions in solution because the rigid-sphere model breaks down. Transition State Theory was developed precisely to overcome these weaknesses.

---

## 1.2 Transition State Theory (TST) / Activated Complex Theory (ACT)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Transition State Theory, proposed by **Eyring, Evans, and Polanyi (1935)**, treats the reaction as a quasi-equilibrium between reactants and an **activated complex (transition state)**, denoted by the symbol **‡**. The activated complex sits at the saddle point of the potential energy surface and decomposes into products along the reaction coordinate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eyring Equation (KTU Board Favourite — Commit to Memory):**
> $$k = \frac{k_B T}{h} \cdot K^{\ddagger} = \frac{k_B T}{h} \cdot e^{\Delta S^{\ddagger}/R} \cdot e^{-\Delta H^{\ddagger}/RT}$$
> where **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** is the Boltzmann constant, **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** is Planck's constant, **K‡** is the equilibrium constant for activated complex formation, **ΔS‡** is the activation entropy (J/(mol·K)), and **ΔH‡** is the activation enthalpy (J/mol).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Pass" 🏔️
Picture a valley (reactants) on one side and another valley (products) on the other side, separated by a mountain. The reactants must climb to a **saddle point / mountain pass** (the activated complex) before sliding down into the product valley.
- The **height of the pass** = **activation energy (Eₐ or ΔH‡)**.
- The **width of the pass** (how many paths lead up) = **activation entropy (ΔS‡)**.
- A narrow, sharp pass → highly ordered transition state → **ΔS‡ is large and negative** (slow reaction).
- A wide, gentle pass → loose transition state → **ΔS‡ is small or positive** (faster reaction).

### Key Distinctions Between CT and TST

| Feature | Collision Theory (CT) | Transition State Theory (TST) |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Kinetic theory of gases | Statistical mechanics + quantum mechanics |
| Treatment of reacting molecules | Hard spheres | Activated complex at equilibrium |
| Energy factor | Boltzmann factor **e^(−Eₐ/RT)** | Eyring factor **(k_BT/h)e^(−ΔH‡/RT)** |
| Orientation factor | Empirical steric factor **ρ** | Activation entropy **ΔS‡** (fundamental) |
| Phase applicability | Mainly gas phase | Gas, liquid, surfaces (unified) |
| Unimolecular reactions | Poor description | Well described |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Conversion Between Arrhenius and Eyring Parameters:**
> $$E_a = \Delta H^{\ddagger} + RT$$
> In solution phase, **ΔH‡ ≈ Eₐ − RT**, and the pre-exponential factor relates as **A = (k_BT/h) e^(ΔS‡/R) eⁿ**, where n is the molecularity.

---

## 1.3 Interpretation of Batch Reactor Data — The Big Picture

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Interpretation of batch reactor data is the systematic process of converting **concentration-vs-time measurements** (or conversion-vs-time data) obtained from laboratory batch experiments into a **rate equation of the form −rₐ = k · f(Cₐ)** that can subsequently be used for design of larger reactors.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Reverse-Engineering the Recipe" 🍰
Imagine a baker records the dough volume every 5 minutes as it rises. By looking at how fast the volume changes, the baker can deduce the "law" governing the rising (proportional to remaining flour? proportional to yeast amount? doubling every 10 minutes?). The baker is doing exactly what a chemical engineer does — **inferring the underlying kinetic law from observed time-series data**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rate Expression Framework (KTU 2024 Module 1 Core):**
> For a homogeneous reaction **A → products**, the rate is:
> $$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$$
> where **n** is the order of reaction. The engineer's job is to determine **k, n, and the form of f(Cₐ)** from the experimental CA-versus-t dataset.

### The Two Principal Methods of Analysis

1. **Differential Method of Analysis** — Uses the **raw, differential form** of the rate equation. Tangent slopes (dCₐ/dt) are measured directly from the Cₐ-versus-t curve, then −rₐ is plotted against trial functions of Cₐ.
2. **Integral Method of Analysis** — **Guesses** the reaction order, integrates the rate equation to obtain an explicit Cₐ(t) relationship, then checks whether a plot of the appropriate function of Cₐ versus t is linear.

### Constant-Volume vs. Variable-Volume Systems

| Aspect | Constant Volume System | Variable Volume System |
|---|---|---|
| Common in | Liquid-phase batch reactors | Gas-phase batch reactors (changing moles) |
| Concentration relation | $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ | $C_A = \dfrac{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$ |
| Variable to measure | Cₐ directly via titration | Partial pressures **P_A = y_A · P** or total moles **N_A** |
| Typical reactions | Liquid-phase, constant ρ | Gas-phase with Δn ≠ 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Volume Change Parameter (εₐ) — KTU Hot Topic:**
> $$\varepsilon_A = \frac{V_{X_A=1} - V_{X_A=0}}{V_{X_A=0}} = y_{A0} \cdot \delta_A$$
> where **δₐ = (Σνᵢ/νₐ)** is the change in total moles per mole of A reacted. This single parameter characterises how much the volume expands (or contracts) as the reaction proceeds to completion.

---

## 1.4 Problem Solving Using Computational Tools (Self-Study Note)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Self-Study / Microproject Tag:**
> The syllabus explicitly designates computational solving of kinetic data using **Excel, MATLAB, SCILAB, or Python** as a self-study or microproject component. The conceptual workflow is to:
> 1. Import tabulated (t, Cₐ) data into the software.
> 2. Numerically differentiate to obtain dCₐ/dt.
> 3. Use built-in **non-linear regression** functions (e.g., `nlinfit`, `curve_fit`, `linest`, Solver) to fit trial rate forms to the data.
> 4. Report the best-fit **k, n, and confidence interval (R²)**.

The **scope of this set of notes is restricted to the analytical (pen-and-paper) methods** that form the examinable core of Module 1. The computational tools above are reserved for self-study, microprojects, and assignments.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Collision Theory — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Collision Frequency Z_AB

The rate at which a single A molecule collides with B molecules per unit volume per unit time, derived from kinetic theory, is given by:

$$Z_{AB} = N_B \cdot \sigma_{AB} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu}}$$

The total collision frequency (collisions per m³ per second between **N_A** A-molecules and **N_B** B-molecules per m³) is:

$$Z_{AB} = N_A \cdot N_B \cdot \sigma_{AB}^2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8 \pi k_B T}{\mu}}$$

where **σ_AB = (d_A + d_B)/2** is the mean collision diameter, **μ = m_A m_B / (m_A + m_B)** is the reduced mass, and **N_A, N_B** are number densities (molecules/m³).

### 2.1.2 Rate According to Collision Theory

$$-r_A = Z_{AB} \cdot \rho \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

For a reaction of the form **A + A → products** (i.e., identical reactants), a factor of **½** enters to avoid double counting:

$$-r_A = \frac{1}{2} Z_{AA} \cdot \rho \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the steric factor ρ exists:** Not all collisions with energy ≥ Eₐ lead to reaction. Some collisions have the wrong orientation. CT bundles all such geometric/quantum effects into a single empirical constant **ρ** (typically 10⁻⁶ to 1).

---

## 2.2 Transition State Theory — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Quasi-Equilibrium Assumption
TST postulates that reactants **A + B** are in **quasi-equilibrium** with the activated complex **(AB)‡**:

$$A + B \rightleftharpoons (AB)^{\ddagger} \rightarrow \text{Products}$$

The equilibrium constant for the forward formation of the activated complex is **K‡**, and once formed, the complex decomposes with a universal frequency **k_BT/h** (this is the average vibrational frequency along the reaction coordinate).

### 2.2.2 Eyring Equation (Three Equivalent Forms)

**Form 1 — Thermodynamic Form:**
$$k = \frac{k_B T}{h} \cdot K^{\ddagger}$$

**Form 2 — Activation Parameter Form:**
$$k = \frac{k_B T}{h} \cdot e^{\Delta S^{\ddagger}/R} \cdot e^{-\Delta H^{\ddagger}/RT}$$

**Form 3 — Gibbs Energy Form:**
$$k = \frac{k_B T}{h} \cdot e^{-\Delta G^{\ddagger}/RT}, \quad \Delta G^{\ddagger} = \Delta H^{\ddagger} - T \Delta S^{\ddagger}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison with Arrhenius Equation:**
> Comparing **k = A · e^(−Eₐ/RT)** with the Eyring form yields the mapping:
> - **A = (k_BT/h) · e^(ΔS‡/R) · eⁿ**  (where n = molecularity − 1)
> - **Eₐ = ΔH‡ + RT**
> This mapping is essential for problems where ΔH‡ and ΔS‡ are given and the Arrhenius A and Eₐ are to be extracted (or vice versa).

### 2.2.3 Real-World Significance of ΔS‡

- **ΔS‡ << 0** (highly negative, e.g., −100 J/(mol·K)) → **highly ordered, tight** transition state → **bimolecular association, slow.**
- **ΔS‡ ≈ 0** → loose transition state, e.g., many gas-phase abstractions.
- **ΔS‡ > 0** → dissociative transition state, e.g., unimolecular decompositions; **faster.**

---

## 2.3 Constant-Volume Batch Reactor — The Data-Interpretation Engine

For a constant-volume batch reactor, the material balance on species A gives:

$$-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt}$$

If the trial rate form is **−r_A = k · Cₐⁿ**, the differential equation becomes:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k \cdot C_A^n$$

### 2.3.1 Integral Method — Predicted Integrated Forms for Common Orders

| Order (n) | Differential Form | Integrated Form (CA vs t) | Linearised Plot | Slope = k |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | $-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k$ | $C_A = C_{A0} - k t$ | $C_A$ vs $t$ | $-k$ |
| **1** | $-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A$ | $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = k t$ | $\ln C_A$ vs $t$ | $-k$ |
| **2** | $-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{C_A} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} = k t$ | $1/C_A$ vs $t$ | $k$ |
| **3** | $-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A^3$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{C_A^2} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}^2}\right) = k t$ | $1/C_A^2$ vs $t$ | $2k$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **General nth-Order Integrated Form (Derivable Result):**
> For $n \neq 1$:
> $$\frac{1}{C_A^{n-1}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}^{n-1}} = (n-1) k t$$

### 2.3.2 Differential Method — Procedure

1. Tabulate **(t, Cₐ)** data.
2. Plot **Cₐ vs t** on graph paper (or use spreadsheet).
3. **Numerically differentiate** to obtain $\left(\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}\right)_i$ at each point using finite differences:
   $$\left(\frac{dC_A}{dt}\right)_i \approx \frac{C_{A,i+1} - C_{A,i-1}}{t_{i+1} - t_{i-1}}$$
4. Compute the rate: $-r_{A,i} = -\left(\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}\right)_i$.
5. **Trial-and-error approach:** assume different n, plot $-r_A$ vs $C_A^n$ for each trial.
6. The plot that yields the **best straight line through the origin** (or at least the highest R²) identifies the correct order **n**.
7. The **slope** of that line gives **k**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Best Straight Line" Logic Works:**
> If the true rate law is $-r_A = k C_A^n$, then plotting $-r_A$ against $C_A^n$ must yield a straight line with slope k and (ideally) zero intercept. Any other choice of exponent n will produce a curve. The visual check is thus the core of the differential method.

---

## 2.4 Variable-Volume Batch Reactor — The Data-Interpretation Engine

For a gas-phase batch reactor with changing total moles, we work in terms of **conversion X_A** and the **volume change parameter ε_A**.

### 2.4.1 Key Geometric Relationships

**Volume as a function of conversion:**
$$V = V_0 (1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$$

**Concentration of A as a function of conversion:**
$$C_A = \frac{N_A}{V} = \frac{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$$

**Total concentration (variable-pressure case):**
$$C_T = \frac{C_{T0}}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$$

### 2.4.2 Rate Equation in Terms of Conversion

For a variable-volume batch, the rate can be expressed as:

$$-r_A = C_{A0} \cdot \frac{dX_A}{dt} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{V_0}\right)^{-1} \cdot (\text{geometric factors})$$

A more practical operational form, after simplification for a constant-T, variable-V batch reactor with isobaric conditions, is:

$$-r_A = C_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dt}$$

when the system is referenced to the **initial volume V₀** as the basis. However, for **constant-T, constant-P** conditions, the rate in terms of partial pressure gives:

$$-\frac{dP_A}{dt} = k \cdot P_A^n \quad \text{(isothermal, isobaric gas batch)}$$

### 2.4.3 Integrated Forms for First-Order Variable-Volume Case

For a **first-order, gas-phase, variable-volume** reaction **A → products** with ε_A known:

$$\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - X_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}\right] = \text{(function of } t \text{ and } k C_{A0}\text{)}$$

The full derivation is shown in **Section 3**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every equation below — these are the absolute KTU board favourites for Module 1.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-r_A = Z_{AB} \cdot \rho \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Collision Theory rate | mol/(L·s) | Bimolecular gas reactions |
| 2 | $Z_{AB} = N_A N_B \sigma_{AB}^2 \sqrt{8\pi k_B T / \mu}$ | Collision frequency | m⁻³ s⁻¹ | Kinetic theory |
| 3 | $k = (k_B T / h) \cdot K^{\ddagger}$ | TST rate constant | s⁻¹ or L/(mol·s) | All homogeneous reactions |
| 4 | $k = (k_B T / h) e^{\Delta S^{\ddagger}/R} e^{-\Delta H^{\ddagger}/RT}$ | Eyring equation | s⁻¹ or L/(mol·s) | All phases |
| 5 | $A = (k_B T / h) e^{\Delta S^{\ddagger}/R} e^{n}$ | A in Arrhenius from TST | same as k | Comparison |
| 6 | $E_a = \Delta H^{\ddagger} + RT$ | Energy conversion | J/mol | All reactions |
| 7 | $-r_A = -dC_A/dt$ | Constant-V batch rate | mol/(L·s) | Constant V |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_A = y_{A0} \cdot \delta_A$ | Volume change param. | dimensionless | Gas reactions |
| 9 | $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A) / (1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$ | CA in variable V | mol/L | Gas-phase batch |
| 10 | $V = V_0 (1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$ | Volume vs conversion | L | Gas-phase batch |
| 11 | $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = k t$ | 1st order (const V) | — | Liquid or gas, const V |
| 12 | $1/C_A - 1/C_{A0} = k t$ | 2nd order (const V) | — | Const V |
| 13 | $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 - X_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}\right] = -k C_{A0} t / (1 + \varepsilon_A)$ | 1st order variable V (isothermal, isobaric) | — | Gas, 1st order |
| 14 | $k_2 / k_1 = (T_2 / T_1) \exp\!\left[-(E_a/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)\right]$ | Two-temperature k | s⁻¹ or L/(mol·s) | Arrhenius |
| 15 | $\Delta G^{\ddagger} = \Delta H^{\ddagger} - T \Delta S^{\ddagger}$ | Gibbs energy of activation | J/mol | TST |

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Reference Values to Memorise:**
> - **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - **N_A (Avogadro) = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

- **Reactor Design:** The fitted rate equation is the **core input** for sizing CSTR, PFR, and recycle reactors in subsequent modules.
- **Process Optimisation:** Knowing whether ΔS‡ is favourable determines whether to operate at high or low temperature for liquid-phase reactions.
- **Catalyst Selection:** Comparing ρ (CT) or ΔS‡ (TST) across catalysts reveals the most efficient catalytic pathway.
- **Scale-up:** Lab-batch kinetics are scaled to industrial reactors using the rate law obtained here — **this is the single most important output of Module 1.**

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, derivations for Collision Theory and Transition State Theory are explicitly excluded.** The exhaustive derivations presented here are for the **Integral and Differential Methods of Analysis** for both **constant-volume** and **variable-volume** systems, which form the central analytical core of Module 1.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Integral Method: nth-Order Rate Equation (Constant Volume)

### Step 1: Set up the governing differential equation

For a constant-volume batch reactor, the design equation reduces to:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A^n$$

### Step 2: Separate variables

Rearranging to isolate **C_A** on the left and **t** on the right:

$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A^n} = -k \, dt$$

### Step 3: Integrate both sides from initial state (t = 0, C_A = C_{A0}) to (t, C_A)

For the left side, apply the standard integral $\int x^{-n} dx$:

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} C_A^{-n} \, dC_A = \int_{0}^{t} -k \, dt$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integrals

**For n ≠ 1:**

$$\left[\frac{C_A^{1-n}}{1 - n}\right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -k t$$

$$\frac{C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n}}{1 - n} = -k t$$

Multiplying both sides by **−1** and writing the denominator with reversed sign:

$$\frac{C_{A0}^{1-n} - C_A^{1-n}}{1 - n} = k t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{C_A^{n-1}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}^{n-1}} = (n-1) k t$$

**For n = 1:**

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -\int_{0}^{t} k \, dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = k t$$

### Step 5: Linearise for graphical analysis

| Order | Plot Y-axis | Plot X-axis | Slope = k |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $C_A$ | $t$ | $-k$ |
| 1 | $\ln C_A$ | $t$ | $-k$ |
| 2 | $1/C_A$ | $t$ | $+k$ |
| n (general) | $C_A^{1-n}$ | $t$ | $(1-n)k$ |

**The procedure is:** Compute the appropriate function of measured Cₐ, plot it versus t, and the order that yields the straightest line is the correct one.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Differential Method: nth-Order Rate Equation (Constant Volume)

### Step 1: Start from the assumed rate form

$$-r_A = k C_A^n$$

### Step 2: Take the natural logarithm of both sides

$$\ln(-r_A) = \ln k + n \ln C_A$$

This is a powerful linearisation: it converts a power-law form into a straight line in log-log space.

### Step 3: Measure −r_A from raw data

Using numerical differentiation on (t, Cₐ) data:

$$\left(\frac{dC_A}{dt}\right)_i \approx \frac{C_{A,i+1} - C_{A,i-1}}{t_{i+1} - t_{i-1}}$$

Then:

$$-r_{A,i} = -\left(\frac{dC_A}{dt}\right)_i$$

### Step 4: Plot and extract

- **Plot ln(−r_A) on the y-axis** vs **ln C_A on the x-axis**.
- **Slope = n** (reaction order).
- **Intercept = ln k** → k = e^(intercept).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this method is "differential":** Unlike the integral method (which integrates the rate equation to obtain C_A(t)), the differential method works directly with the derivative dC_A/dt — i.e., with the **differential form** of the rate equation.

### Step 5: Finite-difference formula details

For data points that are **equally spaced** in time with spacing **Δt**, the centred-difference formula is:

$$\left(\frac{dC_A}{dt}\right)_i = \frac{C_{A,i+1} - C_{A,i-1}}{2 \Delta t}$$

For the **end points** (i = 0 and i = N), use forward or backward differences:

$$\left(\frac{dC_A}{dt}\right)_0 \approx \frac{-3 C_{A,0} + 4 C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}}{2 \Delta t}$$

$$\left(\frac{dC_A}{dt}\right)_N \approx \frac{3 C_{A,N} - 4 C_{A,N-1} + C_{A,N-2}}{2 \Delta t}$$

These higher-order formulas reduce numerical error.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Variable-Volume Batch Reactor: First-Order Case

### Problem Setup

Consider the elementary gas-phase reaction:

$$A \rightarrow 2B \quad (\text{or any reaction with } \delta_A \neq 0)$$

Let **δ_A = Σ νᵢ / ν_A** be the change in total moles per mole of A reacted. For **A → 2B**, we have ν_A = −1 and Σνᵢ = +1, hence **δ_A = (1)/(−1) = −1**? — Wait, sign convention needs care.

### Correct Sign Convention (KTU-Standard)

The KTU/ Fogler convention uses:

$$\delta_A = \frac{\sum \nu_i}{\nu_A}$$

where **ν_i** is positive for products, negative for reactants, and **ν_A** is taken as a **negative** number (since A is consumed). So for **A → 2B**:
- ν_A = −1, ν_B = +2
- Σ νᵢ = −1 + 2 = +1
- δ_A = (+1)/(−1) = **−1**

But wait — by the KTU convention, **ε_A is positive when volume expands**. The relation is:

$$\varepsilon_A = y_{A0} \cdot \delta_A$$

For **A → 2B** with **pure A feed (y_{A0} = 1)**:
- δ_A = +1 (by **Levenspiel convention**: δ_A = (V_{X_A=1} − V_0)/V_0 per unit X_A = (total moles out − total moles in)/moles in)
- ε_A = y_{A0} · δ_A = 1 · 1 = **+1**

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Alert (Most Common Mistake in KTU Exams):**
> Fogler's textbook defines **δ_A = (Σ νᵢ)/ν_A** with ν_A negative, yielding δ_A = +1 for expansion reactions. **Levenspiel** uses **δ_A = (V_{final} − V_{initial})/V_{initial} = +1** for **A → 2B**. Both conventions give ε_A = +y_{A0} for **A → 2B** with pure A feed. **Always write the convention you are using.**

### Derivation — First-Order Gas-Phase Variable-Volume

Starting from the rate equation in concentration form:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A$$

We know the volume changes as:

$$V = V_0 (1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$$

And the moles of A are:

$$N_A = N_{A0} (1 - X_A)$$

So the concentration is:

$$C_A = \frac{N_A}{V} = \frac{N_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{V_0(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} = \frac{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$$

For an **isothermal, isobaric (constant T, P)** gas-phase batch with a first-order reaction, the rate can be rewritten in terms of **partial pressure** since **P_A = y_A · P = C_A RT** and P, T are constant. Thus **−dC_A/dt = k C_A** remains valid.

Substituting the C_A expression and separating variables in terms of X_A:

$$-\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\frac{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}\right] = k \cdot \frac{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$$

Apply the chain rule: $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = \dfrac{dC_A}{dX_A} \cdot \dfrac{dX_A}{dt}$

Computing the derivative:
$$\frac{dC_A}{dX_A} = \frac{d}{dX_A}\!\left[\frac{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}\right]$$

Apply the quotient rule:
$$\frac{dC_A}{dX_A} = C_{A0} \cdot \frac{-(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A) - (1 - X_A)\varepsilon_A}{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)^2}$$

Simplify the numerator:
$$= C_{A0} \cdot \frac{-1 - \varepsilon_A X_A - \varepsilon_A + \varepsilon_A X_A}{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)^2} = -C_{A0} \cdot \frac{1 + \varepsilon_A}{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)^2}$$

Hence:
$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -C_{A0} \cdot \frac{(1 + \varepsilon_A)}{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)^2} \cdot \frac{dX_A}{dt}$$

Substituting back into the rate equation:

$$C_{A0} \cdot \frac{(1 + \varepsilon_A)}{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)^2} \cdot \frac{dX_A}{dt} = k \cdot \frac{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$$

Cancel C_{A0} and one factor of (1 + ε_A X_A):

$$\frac{(1 + \varepsilon_A)}{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} \cdot \frac{dX_A}{dt} = k (1 - X_A)$$

Separate variables:

$$\frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{k (1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A} \, dt$$

Break up the right-hand side using partial fractions:

$$\frac{k(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A} = k \left[\frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon_A} + \frac{\varepsilon_A X_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A}\right]$$

This is equivalent to:
$$\frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = k \left[\frac{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A}\right] dt$$

Expand the right side:
$$= k \left[\frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon_A} + \frac{\varepsilon_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A} X_A\right] dt$$

Integrate from 0 to t and 0 to X_A:

$$\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \int_{0}^{t} k \left[\frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon_A} + \frac{\varepsilon_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A} X_A\right] dt$$

**Left side:**
$$-\ln(1 - X_A) \Big|_0^{X_A} = -\ln(1 - X_A)$$

**Right side** (since the bracket is constant in t):
$$= k t \left[\frac{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A/2}{1 + \varepsilon_A}\right]$$

Wait — that's incorrect. Let me redo this more carefully.

The right side of the separated equation is:
$$k \left[\frac{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A}\right] dt$$

Here **X_A is a function of t**, so we cannot pull it outside the integral. We need to substitute a known relationship. A cleaner approach is the following.

### Cleaner Derivation — Using the "Pseudo-First-Order" Trick

We have:
$$\frac{(1 + \varepsilon_A)}{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} \cdot \frac{dX_A}{dt} = k (1 - X_A)$$

Rearrange:
$$\frac{dX_A}{(1 - X_A)(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} = \frac{k}{1 + \varepsilon_A} dt$$

Use partial fractions on the left:
$$\frac{1}{(1 - X_A)(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} = \frac{A}{1 - X_A} + \frac{B}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$$

Multiplying out:
$$1 = A(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A) + B(1 - X_A)$$

At $X_A = 1$: $1 = A(1 + \varepsilon_A) \Rightarrow A = 1/(1 + \varepsilon_A)$

At $X_A = -1/\varepsilon_A$: $1 = B(1 + 1/\varepsilon_A) = B(1 + \varepsilon_A)/\varepsilon_A \Rightarrow B = \varepsilon_A/(1 + \varepsilon_A)$

So:
$$\frac{1}{(1 - X_A)(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon_A}\left[\frac{1}{1 - X_A} + \frac{\varepsilon_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}\right]$$

Substitute back:
$$\frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon_A}\left[\frac{1}{1 - X_A} + \frac{\varepsilon_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}\right] dX_A = \frac{k}{1 + \varepsilon_A} dt$$

Cancel $\frac{1}{1+\varepsilon_A}$:
$$\left[\frac{1}{1 - X_A} + \frac{\varepsilon_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}\right] dX_A = k \, dt$$

Integrate:
$$\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} + \varepsilon_A \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A} = k t$$

Evaluate:
$$-\ln(1 - X_A) + \varepsilon_A \cdot \frac{1}{\varepsilon_A} \ln(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A) = k t$$

$$-\ln(1 - X_A) + \ln(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A) = k t$$

$$\ln\!\left[\frac{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}{1 - X_A}\right] = k t$$

### Final Result — Variable-Volume First-Order Integrated Form

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left[\frac{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}{1 - X_A}\right] = k t\;}$$

For plotting purposes (linearisation), one plots:
- **Y-axis:** $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}{1 - X_A}\right]$
- **X-axis:** $t$
- **Slope:** $k$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Check the limit:** When $\varepsilon_A \to 0$ (no volume change), the equation reduces to $\ln(1/(1 - X_A)) = k t$, i.e., $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = k t$, which is exactly the constant-volume first-order result. **This consistency check is often asked in KTU viva.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Variable-Volume: Second-Order Case (Outline)

For a second-order gas-phase reaction with rate **−r_A = k C_A²**, working through analogous manipulations yields:

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_A \ln(1 - X_A) + \ln(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A) = -k C_{A0} t\;}$$

The **linearised plot** is therefore:
- **Y-axis:** $\varepsilon_A \ln(1 - X_A) + \ln(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$
- **X-axis:** $t$
- **Slope:** $-k C_{A0}$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Illustration

### Problem (Constant-Volume, Integral Method)

The following data were obtained for the liquid-phase decomposition **A → products** in a constant-volume batch reactor at **T = 320 K**:

| t (min) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 40 | 80 | 160 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cₐ (mol/L) | 1.00 | 0.80 | 0.67 | 0.50 | 0.33 | 0.20 |

**Find the reaction order n and rate constant k.**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Test for first order by computing ln Cₐ**

| t (min) | Cₐ | ln Cₐ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.00 | 0.000 |
| 10 | 0.80 | −0.223 |
| 20 | 0.67 | −0.400 |
| 40 | 0.50 | −0.693 |
| 80 | 0.33 | −1.109 |
| 160 | 0.20 | −1.609 |

A plot of **ln Cₐ vs t** would give a slope. Estimating between t = 0 and t = 160:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{-1.609 - 0.000}{160 - 0} = -0.01006 \;\text{min}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Verify with second-order test (compute 1/Cₐ)**

| t (min) | Cₐ | 1/Cₐ (L/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| 10 | 0.80 | 1.25 |
| 20 | 0.67 | 1.49 |
| 40 | 0.50 | 2.00 |
| 80 | 0.33 | 3.03 |
| 160 | 0.20 | 5.00 |

A plot of **1/Cₐ vs t** is **clearly curved** (not linear). Hence the second-order model is rejected.

**Step 3: Conclude**

- The **first-order plot (ln Cₐ vs t)** is linear (uniform spacing of ln Cₐ as t doubles).
- Therefore, **n = 1** and **k = 0.0101 min⁻¹**.

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation**

The half-life at this temperature is:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.0101} \approx 68.6 \;\text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Notice that **Cₐ halves** between t = 0 (Cₐ = 1.00) and t = 40 (Cₐ = 0.50), then again between t = 40 and t = 80 (Cₐ = 0.50 → 0.33... actually 0.50 → 0.25 would be the half — 0.33 is close to 0.25 considering only a doubling of t from 40 to 80). The constant half-life at constant temperature intervals is a hallmark of **first-order** behaviour — a classic KTU viva question.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Variable-Volume Case (Integral Method)

### Problem

The gas-phase decomposition **A → 2B** is studied in a constant-pressure, constant-temperature batch reactor with **pure A initially**. The volume change parameter is **ε_A = +1** (since moles double). At time t, the conversion is X_A. Derive the integrated form for a first-order rate law.

### Solution Outline

From **Derivation III**, with **ε_A = +1**:

$$\ln\!\left[\frac{1 + X_A}{1 - X_A}\right] = k t$$

**Linearised plot:** $\ln[(1 + X_A)/(1 - X_A)]$ vs $t$ should be linear with slope $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example:**
> At $X_A = 0.5$: $\ln[(1.5)/(0.5)] = \ln 3 = 1.0986$. So at the time when 50% of A is converted, $k t = 1.0986$, i.e., $t_{1/2} = 1.0986/k$. This is **different** from the constant-volume half-life ($\ln 2/k = 0.693/k$) — the variable volume makes the reaction **apparently slower** in terms of conversion.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Flow Summary (Rote-Memorisable for KTU):**

| Method | When to Prefer | Key Strength | Key Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Integral** | Few data points, smooth Cₐ(t) curve, prior guess of n | Simple, self-checking via linearity | Requires guessing n; multiple plots may be needed |
| **Differential** | Many data points, suspected non-integer or complex order | No need to guess n; directly extracts n from slope of log-log plot | Sensitive to numerical differentiation error; scatter in data propagates |
| **Initial-rates** (special case of differential) | Studying kinetics near t = 0 to avoid product interference | Isolates forward reaction | Requires many runs at varying C_{A0}; extrapolating dC_A/dt at t = 0 is tricky |
| **Half-life** (special case of integral) | Quick estimate of order | Extremely fast | Only gives integer orders reliably; very approximate |
