- **Cascade of $N$ equal MFRs (1st order):** $X_{N} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(1 + k\tau_{\text{total}}/N)^{N}}$.
- **Limit $N \to \infty$:** Recovers PFR result $X_{\text{PFR}} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$.
- **Comparison Ratio (1st order):** $\dfrac{V_{\text{MFR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} = \dfrac{-X}{(1-X)\,\ln(1-X)}$ — increases with $X$.
- **Optimum Split for 2 MFRs (2nd order):** Approximate $C_{A1}^{*} \approx \sqrt{C_{A0}\,C_{A2}}$ (geometric mean).
- **Engineering Rule:** For any positive-order reaction, $V_{\text{MFR}} \geq V_{\text{PFR}}$; **always** prefer PFR or MFR cascade for high conversion.
- **Real-World Footprint:** MFRs are used for liquid-phase polymerisations, fermentation, neutralisation, and slurry catalysis where mechanical agitation is feasible.
- **Valuation Safeguards:**
  1. Always write the perfect-mixing assumption (1 mark).
  2. Always evaluate $-r_A$ at $C_{A,\text{exit}}$ (avoid 3-mark penalty).
  3. State limiting cases $X \to 0$ and $X \to 1$ in derivations.
  4. Use $v_0$ consistently (constant density assumed unless stated).
  5. For cascades, derive the *recursion*; the final formula alone is insufficient for full marks.
- **Quick-Recall Cheat Line:** *"In an MFR, the rate of reaction is constant throughout the volume, equal to its value at the exit — that's why one MFR is always larger than one PFR for the same duty."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady State Plug Flow Reactor. Design for Single Reactions: Size Comparison of Single Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steady State Plug Flow Reactor. Design for Single Reactions: Size Comparison of Single Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Steady State Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)

### Formal KTU-Grade Definition

A **Steady State Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** is an ideal, continuous-flow tubular reactor in which:
- Fluid elements travel through the reactor as discrete "plugs" with **no back-mixing** (axial dispersion = 0) in the direction of flow.
- All fluid elements have **identical residence time** (τ) equal to the space time.
- Composition varies **continuously along the axial coordinate (z or V)**, but at any given axial position, the radial composition is uniform.
- Operating conditions (T, P, flow rate) do **not change with time** (steady state).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The PFR is treated as the second ideal reactor (after MFR) used as a building block for modeling real industrial reactors such as packed beds, tubular reactors, and long pipelines.

The generalized **mole balance** on a reactant **A** in a PFR (single reaction) is:

$$F_{A0} \frac{dX}{dV} = -r_A$$

where every variable has a fixed, KTU-conventional meaning (see Section 2 for the full nomenclature).

---

### Conceptual Analogy — "The One-Way Escalator"

Imagine a long **one-way escalator** in a metro station:

- Passengers (fluid elements) board at the entrance (inlet) and step off at the exit (outlet).
- No passenger ever walks **backwards** — this is the **no back-mixing** assumption.
- Every passenger spends **exactly the same time** on the escalator — this is the **uniform residence time**.
- The crowd is densest at the entrance (high reactant concentration) and thins out as you move toward the exit (lower concentration due to reaction).
- A passenger sitting at position $z=2\,\text{m}$ sees a *different* local crowd density than one sitting at $z=8\,\text{m}$ — this is the **axial composition gradient**.

A **CSTR (MFR)**, by contrast, behaves like a **giant elevator car** — everyone enters together, mixes instantly, and exits together with the *same average* concentration. Hence the dramatic size difference between PFRs and CSTRs for the same job (covered in §1.3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical constants / standard metrics used in this module:**
> - **Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$
> - **Standard conditions:** $T_0 = 273.15\,\text{K}$, $P_0 = 1\,\text{atm} = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$
> - **Space time:** $\tau = V/\nu_0$ **[s]**
> - **Space velocity:** $S = 1/\tau$ **[s⁻¹]**

---

## 1.2 What is "Single Reaction"?

A **single reaction** is a system in which **only one independent stoichiometric equation** is occurring. The reaction can be of any kinetic form (elementary, non-elementary, nth order, autocatalytic, etc.) but the network itself has a **single path** from reactant to product.

Examples:
- $A \rightarrow Products$ (irreversible)
- $A \leftrightarrow B$ (reversible)
- $A + B \rightarrow C$ (with constant $C_{B0} \gg C_{A0}$ — pseudo-homogeneous)

This is in contrast to **multiple reactions** (parallel, series, series-parallel) which is a higher-syllabus module.

---

## 1.3 Size Comparison of Single Reactors — The Big Idea

For a **single reaction of positive order** ($n > 0$), when an engineer must achieve a **specified conversion $X$** at a **given feed rate $F_{A0}$**, the question is: *"Should I use one PFR, one MFR, or a combination? And which is smaller?"*

The **key result (must be memorized for KTU exams):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Levenspiel's Theorem for Single Reactions:**
> For any **single reaction with rate $-r_A = k\,C_A^n$ where $n > 0$**:
> $$\boxed{V_{\text{PFR}} < V_{\text{MFR}}}$$
> **A single PFR is always smaller than a single MFR** to achieve the same conversion of a positive-order reaction.
>
> For $n = 0$ (zero-order): $V_{\text{PFR}} = V_{\text{MFR}}$.
> For $n < 0$ (negative-order): $V_{\text{PFR}} > V_{\text{MFR}}$ — the MFR is smaller.

Intuitive reasoning: In a PFR, the reactant enters at its highest concentration and the rate is **fastest where it matters most** (at the inlet). In a CSTR, the concentration drops *instantaneously* to the *lowest* value (the exit value), and the entire reactor operates at this poor, sluggish rate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PFR Assumptions (KTU Board-Exam Favorites)

The ideal PFR model rests on **four pillars** that examiners love to test:

1. **Steady state:** $\dfrac{\partial F_A}{\partial t} = 0$
2. **Plug flow (no axial mixing):** Every fluid element has identical residence time $\tau = V/\nu_0$.
3. **Radial uniformity:** No radial gradients in $T$, $C$, or $u$.
4. **Single fluid phase:** Homogeneous reaction (or pseudo-homogeneous for catalytic PFRs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** If asked to compare "ideal PFR" vs "real tubular reactor," the *only* deviation is **axial dispersion** (characterized by the Péclet number $Pe = uL/D_{ax}$). Real reactors approach PFR behavior as $Pe \to \infty$.

---

## 2.2 Derivation of the PFR Design Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

Starting from a **mole balance on A** over a differential slice $dV$ of the reactor:

| Step | Logic |
|------|-------|
| **Step 1** | In − Out + Generation = Accumulation |
| **Step 2** | At steady state, accumulation = 0 |
| **Step 3** | Define $F_{A0}$ = molar feed rate of A; define conversion $X = (F_{A0} - F_A)/F_{A0}$ |
| **Step 4** | $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$ → $dF_A = -F_{A0}\,dX$ |
| **Step 5** | Differential rate of disappearance of A in slice = $-r_A\,dV$ |
| **Step 6** | Combine: $F_{A0}\,dX = -r_A\,dV$ |
| **Step 7** | Rearrange to obtain the design equation (next section) |

The **integrated design equation** for an isothermal, constant-density PFR is:

$$\boxed{V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0} \int_{0}^{X} \frac{dX}{-r_A}}$$

For a **variable density system** (gas-phase with significant $\varepsilon_A$):

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0} \int_{0}^{X} \frac{(1 + \varepsilon_A X)}{-r_A}\,dX$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!WARNING]
> **Memorize this table — it is the single most-tested block in Module 2.** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (NOT bare `|`) inside tables to prevent Markdown breakage.

| **Reactor / Concept** | **Design Equation** | **Integrated Form (n ≠ 1, isothermal)** | **Integrated Form (n = 1)** |
|-----------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **PFR** (constant ρ) | $F_{A0}\dfrac{dX}{dV} = -r_A$ | $V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{X}\dfrac{dX}{k C_{A0}^n (1-X)^n}$ | $V_{\text{PFR}} = \dfrac{\nu_0}{k}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X}\right)$ |
| **MFR (CSTR)** | $V = \dfrac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A\big\vert_{X}}$ | $V_{\text{MFR}} = \dfrac{\nu_0 C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}^n (1-X)^n} = \dfrac{\nu_0 X}{k C_{A0}^{n-1}(1-X)^n}$ | $V_{\text{MFR}} = \dfrac{\nu_0 X}{k(1-X)}$ |
| **Size ratio** $V_{\text{PFR}}/V_{\text{MFR}}$ | — | $\dfrac{(1-X) - (1-X)^{1-n}}{n-1}\cdot\dfrac{1}{X}$ (for $n\neq 1$) | $-\dfrac{\ln(1-X)}{X}$ (for $n=1$) |
| **Zero order** ($n=0$) | — | $V_{\text{PFR}} = \dfrac{\nu_0 C_{A0} X}{k}$ | $V_{\text{MFR}} = \dfrac{\nu_0 C_{A0} X}{k}$ ⇒ **equal** |
| **Space time** | $\tau = V/\nu_0$ | $\tau_{\text{PFR}} = C_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X}\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ | $\tau_{\text{MFR}} = \dfrac{C_{A0} X}{-r_A\big\vert_{X}}$ |
| **Levenspiel Plot** | $\dfrac{1}{-r_A}$ vs $X$ | Area under curve $= \tau_{\text{PFR}}$ | Rectangle height $= \tau_{\text{MFR}}$ |

> **Nomenclature used in the table:**
> - $F_{A0}$ = inlet molar flow rate of A **[mol·s⁻¹]**
> - $X$ = fractional conversion **[dimensionless]**
> - $C_{A0}$ = inlet concentration **[mol·m⁻³]**
> - $k$ = rate constant (units depend on order)
> - $\nu_0$ = volumetric feed rate **[m³·s⁻¹]**
> - $\varepsilon_A = y_{A0}\,\delta$ (gas-phase fractional change)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Size Comparison

The size comparison is not just academic. Industrial implications include:

- **Capital cost (CAPEX):** A smaller PFR → less steel, less insulation, smaller plot area.
- **Catalyst volume:** For PFR-catalyst systems, volume directly dictates catalyst loading and operating cost.
- **Selection logic for negative-order reactions:** Unusual systems (e.g., certain polymerizations, microbial growth kinetics) prefer an MFR despite conventional wisdom.
- **Hybrid systems:** A series arrangement of MFR + PFR (or vice-versa) is often *even smaller* than either alone — but this is a higher-syllabus topic.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, real reactors are often **packed-bed reactors (PBR)** that are modeled as PFRs with the catalyst term $-r_A'$ (rate per unit catalyst mass) replacing $-r_A$ in the design equation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the PFR Mole Balance

We start with the **General Mole Balance Equation (GMBE)** for reactant A on a differential slice $dV$ of the reactor:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} &= \text{Accumulation} \\
F_A\big\vert_V - F_A\big\vert_{V+dV} + r_A\,dV &= \frac{dN_A}{dt}
\end{aligned}$$

At **steady state**, $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = 0$. Using the differential form:

$$-dF_A + r_A\,dV = 0$$

Recognizing that $r_A$ in the GMBE is the *generation* rate (negative for consumption), we substitute $r_A = -(-r_A)$:

$$-dF_A - (-r_A)\,dV = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad dF_A = -r_A\,dV$$

Now introduce **conversion** as the reaction progress variable:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_A &= F_{A0}(1 - X) \\
dF_A &= -F_{A0}\,dX
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back:

$$-F_{A0}\,dX = -r_A\,dV$$

Dividing both sides and rearranging:

$$\boxed{F_{A0}\,\frac{dX}{dV} = -r_A}$$

This is the **differential PFR design equation** in its KTU-board-canonical form. ✅

---

## 3.2 Integration for a First-Order Irreversible Reaction

Consider $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ with $-r_A = k C_A$ (first order) and constant density.

**Step 1:** Substitute $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)$ into the rate law:

$$-r_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X)$$

**Step 2:** Write the integrated form:

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0} \int_{0}^{X} \frac{dX}{k C_{A0}(1-X)}$$

**Step 3:** Pull constants out:

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \int_{0}^{X} \frac{dX}{1-X}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral $\displaystyle\int \frac{dX}{1-X} = -\ln(1-X)$:

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \Big[-\ln(1-X)\Big]_{0}^{X}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the limits:

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \big(-\ln(1-X) - 0\big)$$

**Step 6:** Final result:

$$\boxed{V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{\nu_0}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right)}$$

since $F_{A0}/C_{A0} = \nu_0$ (volumetric feed rate). ✅

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of the Size-Comparison Ratio for $n \neq 1$

For an $n$-th order reaction: $-r_A = k C_{A0}^n (1-X)^n$ (constant density, isothermal).

**MFR design equation** (algebraic, because CSTR is well-mixed):

$$V_{\text{MFR}} = \frac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A\big\vert_X} = \frac{F_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}^n (1-X)^n} = \frac{\nu_0 X}{k C_{A0}^{n-1}(1-X)^n}$$

**PFR design equation** (integral):

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}^n} \int_{0}^{X} \frac{dX}{(1-X)^n}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the standard integral $\displaystyle\int \frac{dX}{(1-X)^n} = \frac{(1-X)^{1-n} - 1}{n-1}$ for $n \neq 1$.

**Step 2:** Substitute limits:

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}^n} \cdot \frac{1 - (1-X)^{1-n}}{n-1}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize $F_{A0}/C_{A0}^n = \nu_0\,C_{A0}^{1-n}$:

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{\nu_0\,C_{A0}^{1-n}}{k} \cdot \frac{1 - (1-X)^{1-n}}{n-1}$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio $\dfrac{V_{\text{PFR}}}{V_{\text{MFR}}}$:

$$\frac{V_{\text{PFR}}}{V_{\text{MFR}}} = \frac{\dfrac{\nu_0 C_{A0}^{1-n}}{k}\cdot\dfrac{1 - (1-X)^{1-n}}{n-1}}{\dfrac{\nu_0 C_{A0}^{1-n}}{k}\cdot\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^n}}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel common factors:

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{\text{PFR}}}{V_{\text{MFR}}} = \frac{(1-X) - (1-X)^{1-n}}{X(n-1)} \quad \text{for } n \neq 1}$$

**Step 6:** Sanity check — verify $X \to 0$ limit gives $1$ (L'Hôpital):

$$\lim_{X\to 0}\frac{(1-X) - (1-X)^{1-n}}{X(n-1)} = \frac{0 - 0}{0} \xrightarrow{\text{L'H}} \frac{-1 - (1-n)(1-X)^{-n}\big\vert_0}{n-1} = \frac{-1 - (1-n)}{n-1} = 1\ \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This ratio is **always < 1** for $n > 0, X > 0$, which mathematically proves that $V_{\text{PFR}} < V_{\text{MFR}}$ for any positive-order single reaction.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** The liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ is first-order with $k = 0.5\,\text{min}^{-1}$. Find the volumes of a PFR and an MFR required to achieve $X = 0.80$ given $C_{A0} = 2\,\text{mol/L}$ and $\nu_0 = 50\,\text{L/min}$.

**Solution — PFR:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{PFR}} &= \frac{\nu_0}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right) \\
&= \frac{50}{0.5}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-0.80}\right) \\
&= 100 \times \ln(5) \\
&= 100 \times 1.6094 \\
&= 160.94\,\text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — MFR:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{MFR}} &= \frac{\nu_0 X}{k(1-X)} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 0.80}{0.5 \times 0.20} \\
&= \frac{40}{0.10} \\
&= 400\,\text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{V_{\text{PFR}}}{V_{\text{MFR}}} = \frac{160.94}{400} = 0.4024 \approx \frac{\ln 5}{4} = 0.4024\ \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The PFR is **2.48 times smaller** than the MFR for the same job.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PFR Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional architecture* of an ideal PFR — fluid elements enter as a "plug" and progress through differential slices where local conversion increases monotonically.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Stream: F_A0, nu_0, C_A0, T_0] --> B[Inlet Slice dV at z = 0]
    B --> C[Slice 1: X = dX, r_A high]
    C --> D[Slice 2: X = 2dX]
    D --> E[Slice 3: X = 3dX]
    E --> F[Slice k: X = kdX]
    F --> G[Outlet Slice at z = L]
    G --> H[Exit Stream: F_A, X_final, T_final]

    subgraph RC[Reaction Coordinate]
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style RC fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Comparison Architecture: PFR vs MFR Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PFR[Plug Flow Reactor]
        P1[Inlet: C_A0] --> P2[Continuous composition drop along z]
        P2 --> P3[Outlet: C_A = C_A0 1 - X]
    end

    subgraph MFR[Mixed Flow Reactor CSTR]
        M1[Inlet: C_A0] --> M2[Instantaneous mixing]
        M2 --> M3[Tank contents uniform at C_A = C_A0 1 - X]
        M3 --> M4[Outlet: same as tank]
    end

    style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style P3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style M1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style M3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Levenspiel Plot — Graphical Size Comparison

The plot of $\dfrac{1}{-r_A}$ vs $X$ is the **engineer's friend** for reactor sizing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LP[Levenspiel Plot Geometry]
        Xaxis[X axis: Conversion X from 0 to 1]
        Yaxis[Y axis: 1 by negative r_A]
        Curve[Curve 1 by negative r_A vs X for positive order reaction]
        AreaPFR[Area UNDER curve from 0 to X equals tau_PFR]
        RectMFR[Rectangle of HEIGHT 1 by negative r_A at X and WIDTH X equals tau_MFR]
    end

    Xaxis --- Curve
    Yaxis --- Curve
    Curve --- AreaPFR
    Curve --- RectMFR

    style AreaPFR fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style RectMFR fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Levenspiel plot:**
> - **Area under the curve** from $0$ to $X$ = $\tau_{\text{PFR}}$
> - **Rectangle area** with height $= \dfrac{1}{-r_A}\big\vert_{X}$ and width $= X$ = $\tau_{\text{MFR}}$
> - For $n>0$: curve is *concave up* (rate decreases with X), so the rectangle is **larger** than the area under the curve ⇒ $V_{\text{MFR}} > V_{\text{PFR}}$. ✅

## 4.4 Reactor Selection Decision Tree (For Exam Answers)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Identify reaction order n] --> Q1{Is n greater than 0?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> PFRwin[Use PFR smaller than MFR]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is n less than 0?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> MFRwin[Use MFR smaller than PFR]
    Q2 -- No, n equals 0 --> Equal[V_PFR equals V_MFR no preference]

    PFRwin --> Hybrid[Consider PFR followed by MFR for minimum total volume]
    MFRwin --> Hybrid2[Consider MFR followed by PFR for minimum total volume]

    style Start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style PFRwin fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style MFRwin fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style Equal fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. State the design equation of an ideal isothermal plug flow reactor (PFR) for a single reaction and define each term.

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

The mole balance on reactant A in a differential slice $dV$ of a PFR at steady state gives:

$$F_{A0}\,\frac{dX}{dV} = -r_A$$

where:
- $F_{A0}$ = molar feed rate of A entering the reactor **[mol·s⁻¹]**
- $X$ = fractional conversion of A
- $V$ = reactor volume measured from the inlet **[m³]**
- $-r_A$ = rate of disappearance of A per unit volume **[mol·m⁻³·s⁻¹]**

Integrated form:

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0}\int_{0}^{X} \frac{dX}{-r_A}$$

(1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for variable definitions, 1 mark for the integrated form.)

---

### Q2. For a single positive-order reaction, briefly justify why a PFR gives a smaller volume than an MFR for the same conversion.

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

In a **PFR**, reactant concentration is at its maximum $C_{A0}$ at the inlet and decreases gradually along the length. The reaction rate is therefore **highest where the reactant is most abundant**, leading to efficient use of reactor volume.

In an **MFR (CSTR)**, the incoming fluid is **instantly diluted** to the exit concentration $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$. The entire reactor operates at this **lowest, least reactive concentration**, making the volume inefficient.

Since rate is a monotonically increasing function of concentration for $n>0$, the PFR exploits the high-rate region, while the MFR wastes volume in the low-rate region. (1 mark for concentration profile, 1 mark for rate–concentration argument, 1 mark for volume comparison.)

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the design equation of an ideal PFR for a single reaction starting from a differential mole balance on reactant A. List all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the elementary gas-phase reaction $A \rightarrow 2B$ conducted isothermally in a PFR with $\varepsilon_A = -0.5$, derive the *variable-density* design equation and obtain the explicit reactor volume expression. State how the result simplifies when $\varepsilon_A \to 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Steady state operation
2. No axial or radial mixing (ideal plug flow)
3. Single homogeneous phase
4. Constant cross-sectional area
5. Uniform radial concentration (negligible radial gradients)

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Mole balance on A over a differential slice $dV$ at steady state:

$$F_A\big\vert_V - F_A\big\vert_{V+dV} + r_A\,dV = 0$$

Using the differential approximation and the convention $r_A = -(-r_A)$:

$$-dF_A = -r_A\,dV \quad\Longrightarrow\quad dF_A = -r_A\,dV$$

Express in terms of conversion: $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$, so $dF_A = -F_{A0}\,dX$. Substituting:

$$-F_{A0}\,dX = -r_A\,dV \quad\Longrightarrow\quad F_{A0}\,\frac{dX}{dV} = -r_A$$

Integrated form: $V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X}\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

For variable volumetric flow rate:

$$\nu = \nu_0(1 + \varepsilon_A X), \quad C_A = \frac{F_A}{\nu} = \frac{C_{A0}(1-X)}{1+\varepsilon_A X}$$

**Design equation (3 marks):**

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0} \int_{0}^{X} \frac{(1 + \varepsilon_A X)\,dX}{-r_A}$$

For a first-order $-r_A = k C_A$:

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0}\int_{0}^{X} \frac{(1+\varepsilon_A X)(1+\varepsilon_A X)\,dX}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}}\int_{0}^{X}\frac{(1+\varepsilon_A X)^2}{1-X}\,dX$$

**Explicit volume (3 marks):** The integral evaluates to:

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{\nu_0}{k}\Big[(1+\varepsilon_A)^2\ln\!\frac{1}{1-X} - \varepsilon_A^2 X - 2\varepsilon_A(1+\varepsilon_A)X\Big]$$

**Limiting case (1 mark):** As $\varepsilon_A \to 0$ (constant density):

$$V_{\text{PFR}} \to \frac{\nu_0}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right)$$

which recovers the standard first-order PFR result.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the help of the **Levenspiel plot** ($\dfrac{1}{-r_A}$ vs $X$), show graphically that a single PFR is always smaller than a single MFR for any positive-order single reaction at the same conversion $X$. State the conditions under which the opposite is true. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow B$ ($k = 0.07\,\text{min}^{-1}$) is to be carried out in an MFR and a PFR in parallel, each fed by $\nu_0 = 1000\,\text{L/min}$ of $C_{A0} = 0.5\,\text{mol/L}$ solution. Compare the volumes required at $X = 0.90$. What is the percentage saving in volume if a PFR is used instead of an MFR? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Levenspiel plot logic (5 marks):**

1. Plot $\dfrac{1}{-r_A}$ on the y-axis and $X$ on the x-axis. For a positive-order reaction, the rate $-r_A$ decreases as $X$ increases, so $\dfrac{1}{-r_A}$ is a **monotonically increasing, concave-up** curve.
2. **PFR volume:** $\tau_{\text{PFR}}$ = **area under the curve** from $0$ to $X$.
3. **MFR volume:** $\tau_{\text{MFR}}$ = **rectangle** with width $X$ and height $\dfrac{1}{-r_A}\big\vert_X$ (since the CSTR operates uniformly at the exit condition).
4. Since the curve is concave up, the rectangle *always* extends above the curve ⇒ **rectangle area > area under curve** ⇒ $\tau_{\text{MFR}} > \tau_{\text{PFR}}$ ⇒ $V_{\text{MFR}} > V_{\text{PFR}}$. ✅

**Conditions for reversal (2 marks):**
- **Negative-order reactions** ($n < 0$): Rate *increases* with $X$, curve becomes concave-down, and the MFR rectangle is *smaller*. ⇒ $V_{\text{MFR}} < V_{\text{PFR}}$.
- **Autocatalytic reactions** of the form $A \to B$ with rate $\propto C_A C_B$: a small amount of recycled MFR (or a small MFR upstream of a PFR) is optimal.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**PFR volume (3 marks):**

$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{\nu_0}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right) = \frac{1000}{0.07}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.10}\right) = 14285.7 \times 2.3026 = 32894\,\text{L}$$

**MFR volume (3 marks):**

$$V_{\text{MFR}} = \frac{\nu_0 X}{k(1-X)} = \frac{1000 \times 0.90}{0.07 \times 0.10} = \frac{900}{0.007} = 128571\,\text{L}$$

**Percentage saving (1 mark):**

$$\%\text{ saving} = \frac{V_{\text{MFR}} - V_{\text{PFR}}}{V_{\text{MFR}}} \times 100 = \frac{128571 - 32894}{128571}\times 100 = 74.4\,\%$$

The PFR is **74.4% smaller** than the MFR. ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units of $k$**: If $k$ is given in **min⁻¹** but $\nu_0$ in **L/s**, students lose 1–2 marks. Always check dimensional consistency.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_{A0}$ and $C_A$**: In the MFR design equation, $-r_A$ must be evaluated at the **exit** concentration $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$, not at $C_{A0}$.
> 3. **Dropping the $\varepsilon_A$ term silently**: Gas-phase reactions require the variable-density form. Writing the constant-density formula in a gas-phase problem costs full marks.
> 4. **Sign error in $\varepsilon_A$**: For $A \to 2B$ (moles increase), $\varepsilon_A$ is **negative** since $\delta = +1$ and $y_{A0} = 1$ give $\varepsilon_A = y_{A0}\delta = +1$ — wait, $\varepsilon_A = y_{A0}\,\delta$ where $\delta = \frac{2-1}{1} = +1$, so $\varepsilon_A = +1$. Know the formula cold.
> 5. **Levenspiel plot axis mistake**: The y-axis is $\dfrac{1}{-r_A}$ (not $-r_A$). This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss if mislabeled.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **PFR design eq (constant ρ):** $V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X}\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$
- ✅ **PFR design eq (variable ρ):** $V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X}\dfrac{(1+\varepsilon_A X)\,dX}{-r_A}$
- ✅ **MFR design eq (constant ρ):** $V_{\text{MFR}} = \dfrac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A\big\vert_X}$
- ✅ **Size ratio for $n$-th order ($n\neq 1$):** $\dfrac{V_{\text{PFR}}}{V_{\text{MFR}}} = \dfrac{(1-X) - (1-X)^{1-n}}{X(n-1)}$
- ✅ **Size ratio for $n=1$:** $\dfrac{V_{\text{PFR}}}{V_{\text{MFR}}} = -\dfrac{\ln(1-X)}{X}$
- ✅ **Key result:** $V_{\text{PFR}} < V_{\text{MFR}}$ for **all positive-order** single reactions.
- ✅ **Edge cases:** $n=0$ ⇒ equal; $n<0$ ⇒ MFR is smaller.
- ✅ **Levenspiel plot:** area under curve = PFR volume; rectangle = MFR volume.
- ✅ **First-order PFR:** $V = \dfrac{\nu_0}{k}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X}\right)$
- ✅ **First-order MFR:** $V = \dfrac{\nu_0 X}{k(1-X)}$
- ✅ **Assumptions of PFR:** steady state, no back-mixing, radial uniformity, single phase.
- ✅ **KTU Hot-Keywords:** space time $\tau$, space velocity $S=1/\tau$, Péclet number $Pe$, Levenspiel plot, $\varepsilon_A$, conversion $X$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Reactor Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Multiple Reactor Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multiple Reactor Systems** refer to the strategic arrangement (in **series**, **parallel**, or **mixed configurations**) of two or more ideal reactors — typically **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactors (CSTRs)** and **Plug Flow Reactors (PFRs)** — to achieve a desired conversion, selectivity, or production rate more efficiently than a single reactor of equivalent volume. In chemical reaction engineering, the performance of a reactor is governed by the interaction between **kinetics** (reaction rate) and **residence time distribution** (RTD). When the kinetic expression is **non-linear** (e.g., second-order, autocatalytic, exothermic with temperature rise), a single ideal reactor may not be the most economical design choice. Multiple reactor systems provide the flexibility to: - **Optimize volume** (minimize total reactor volume for a given conversion $X$). - **Control selectivity** in complex/consecutive reactions. - **Manage temperature profiles** by staging. - **Improve operability** and safety. The five canonical configurations studied in KTU Module 2 are: 1. **PFR followed by CSTR** (or vice versa) 2. **CSTRs in Series (n equal-sized CSTRs)** 3. **PFRs in Series (n equal-sized PFRs)** 4. **PFR with Recycle** 5. **Equal-volume CSTRs vs. Single CSTR comparison** > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 2 specifically emphasizes the **design equations**, **graphical interpretation** using the **Levenspiel plot** ($-r_A$ vs. $X$), and the **comparison of total volume** required to achieve a target conversion $X$ for various combinations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a long, exhausting queue at a single checkout counter in a supermarket. Now imagine the same queue split into **two counters in series** (you finish at one, then go to another) versus **two counters in parallel** (you choose either). - **PFR in Series with CSTR**: Like running on a treadmill at full speed (PFR — uniform progress) and then mixing in a holding tank (CSTR — thorough mixing). Each reactor handles a different "regime" of the reaction. - **CSTRs in Series**: Like a chain of ponds connected by small streams — the concentration in each pond equalizes (well-mixed), but each successive pond sees a lower concentration than the previous one, mimicking PFR-like behavior in the limit of infinite stages. - **Recycle Reactor**: Like a recycling bin where some of the output is sent back to the input — useful when intermediate products need more contact time or when feed pre-heating via product mixing is desired. > [!TIP] > **Key Intuition:** The *best* reactor arrangement depends on the **shape of the rate-conversion curve** $(1/-r_A)$ vs. $X$. PFRs are best when this curve is **concave up** (rate increases with X, e.g., autocatalytic), while CSTRs are best when the curve is **concave down** (rate decreases with X, e.g., first-order). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **$\tau$** = Space time (residence time) = **hours (h)** or **seconds (s)** - **$V$** = Reactor volume = **m³** or **L** - **$F_{A0}$** = Molar flow rate of A at inlet = **mol/s** - **$X$** = Fractional conversion of A (dimensionless, $0 \le X \le 1$) - **$-r_A$** = Rate of disappearance of A = **mol/(L·s)** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Design Equations (Recap) For an ideal reactor operating at steady state with single reaction $A \to products$: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{PFR:} \quad & V = F_{A0} \int_{0}^{X} \frac{dX}{-r_A} \\ \textbf{CSTR:} \quad & V = F_{A0} \cdot \frac{X}{-r_A} \Big|_{outlet} \end{aligned} $$ For a **recycle reactor (PFR with recycle)**, where $R$ is the recycle ratio (mass of recycle / mass of fresh feed), the design equation becomes: $$ V = F_{A0}(1+R) \int_{X_{in}}^{X_{out}} \frac{dX}{-r_A} $$ where $X_{in}$ (inlet to PFR) and $X_{out}$ (overall conversion) are related by: $$ X_{in} = \frac{R}{R+1} X_{out} $$ ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis of Each Configuration ### A. PFR Followed by a Single CSTR (or vice versa) This is the **most fundamental KTU question**. The total volume is the **sum** of individual volumes: $$ V_{total} = V_{PFR} + V_{CSTR} = F_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_1} \frac{dX}{-r_A} \; + \; F_{A0} \cdot \frac{X_2 - X_1}{-r_A}\Big|_{X_2} $$ where $X_1$ is the intermediate conversion at the junction of the two reactors, and $X_2$ is the final conversion. **Key Insight:** The *optimum* split point $X_1^*$ is found by setting $\dfrac{dV_{total}}{dX_1} = 0$, which gives: $$ \left( \frac{1}{-r_A} \right)_{X_1^*} = \left( \frac{1}{-r_A} \right)_{X_2} = \tau_{CSTR} $$ This means: at the optimum split, the reciprocal rate at $X_1^*$ (PFR exit) **equals** the reciprocal rate at $X_2$ (CSTR outlet). Graphically, the **PFR-rectangle and CSTR-rectangle areas** should be **equal below** the $1/-r_A$ curve. ### B. n CSTRs in Series (Equal Size) For *n* equal-volume CSTRs in series with total conversion $X$: $$ \tau_{total} = n\tau_i = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}^{n-1}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{X_i - X_{i-1}}{(1-X_i)^n} $$ For **first-order kinetics** ($n=1$), the total space time for *n* equal CSTRs is: $$ \tau_{total} = \frac{n}{k} \left[ \left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right)^{1/n} - 1 \right] $$ > [!TIP] > As $n \to \infty$, the CSTRs in series **approach PFR behavior** (volume approaches that of a single PFR). This is a key KTU viva question. ### C. n PFRs in Series (Equal Size) Multiple PFRs in series behave **identically** to a single PFR of the same total volume (since each is a PFR). No advantage or disadvantage — the total volume is the same: $$ V_{total,n\,PFR} = V_{single\,PFR} $$ ### D. PFR with Recycle Recycle ratio $R = \dfrac{\text{volumetric flow of recycle}}{\text{volumetric flow of fresh feed}}$. The recycle stream (often product or unreacted feed) is mixed with fresh feed before entering the PFR. This is especially useful for **autocatalytic reactions** $A + B \to 2B$ where some product B must be present to start the reaction. **Design equation:** $$ \frac{V}{F_{A0}} = (1+R) \int_{\frac{R}{R+1} X_f}^{X_f} \frac{dX}{-r_A} $$ For **autocatalytic kinetics** $-r_A = k C_A C_B = k C_{A0}^2 (1-X)(X_b + X)$, there exists an **optimum recycle ratio** $R^*$ that minimizes $V$. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes **all critical equations** for Module 2. Memorize these — they constitute 70% of Part B questions. | # | Configuration | Design Equation | Special Case / Note | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Single PFR | $V = F_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ | Best for $-r_A \propto C_A^n$, $n>0$ | | 2 | Single CSTR | $V = F_{A0} \dfrac{X}{-r_A}\big\vert_{outlet}$ | Best for $n=0$ (zero-order) | | 3 | PFR + CSTR (series) | $V = F_{A0}\left[\displaystyle\int_0^{X_1}\frac{dX}{-r_A} + \dfrac{X_2-X_1}{-r_A}\big\vert_{X_2}\right]$ | Optimum: $1/-r_A$ at $X_1^* = 1/-r_A$ at $X_2$ | | 4 | n equal CSTRs in series (1st order) | $\tau_{total} = \dfrac{n}{k}\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X}\right)^{1/n}-1\right]$ | $n \to \infty$ gives PFR | | 5 | n PFRs in series | $V_{total} = V_{single\,PFR}$ | No benefit, no loss | | 6 | PFR with recycle | $V = F_{A0}(1+R)\displaystyle\int_{\frac{RX_f}{R+1}}^{X_f}\frac{dX}{-r_A}$ | $R=0$: PFR; $R\to\infty$: CSTR | | 7 | Optimum $X_1^*$ for PFR+CSTR | $-r_A(X_1^*) = -r_A(X_f)$ | Intersection of curve with itself | | 8 | Autocatalytic optimum $R^*$ | $\dfrac{dV}{dR}=0$ condition | Plot $V$ vs. $R$ and find minimum | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **PFR + CSTR combination**: Used in **exothermic liquid-phase reactions** (e.g., nitration of aromatics) where high initial concentration drives the reaction in a tubular reactor, and a CSTR is used downstream for better temperature control and residence time uniformity. - **CSTRs in series**: Common in **polymerization reactors** (e.g., continuous stirred tank train in PVC production) and **fermentation cascades** for staged microbial growth phases. - **Recycle reactors**: Used in **autocatalytic processes** (e.g., fermentation where product inhibits its own growth) and in processes where feed pre-heating via hot product is desired (energy integration). - **Multiple Reactor Optimization**: Saves **capital cost** in large-scale plants. For a $1000 \, m^3$ reactor, even a 10% volume reduction translates to millions of dollars in savings. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Volume for n Equal CSTRs in Series (First-Order Kinetics) **Given:** $A \to products$, $-r_A = kC_A$, $C_{A0}$ at inlet, conversion $X$ after $n$th CSTR. **Step 1: Write the design equation for the $i$-th CSTR.** For a CSTR, the design equation is $V_i = F_{A0} \dfrac{X_i - X_{i-1}}{-r_{A,i}}$. Since $-r_{A,i} = kC_{A,i} = kC_{A0}(1-X_i)$: $$ V_i = \frac{F_{A0}(X_i - X_{i-1})}{k C_{A0}(1-X_i)} = \frac{F_{A0}}{kC_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_i - X_{i-1}}{1-X_i} $$ **Step 2: Divide by $F_{A0}$ to get space time $\tau_i$.** $$ \tau_i = \frac{V_i}{F_{A0}} = \frac{1}{kC_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_i - X_{i-1}}{1-X_i} $$ **Step 3: For equal-sized CSTRs, all $\tau_i$ are equal. Set $\tau_i = \tau$.** $$ \tau = \frac{1}{kC_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_i - X_{i-1}}{1-X_i} $$ **Step 4: Solve for $X_i$ in terms of $X_{i-1}$.** $$ \begin{aligned} k C_{A0} \tau (1-X_i) &= X_i - X_{i-1} \\ k C_{A0} \tau - k C_{A0} \tau X_i &= X_i - X_{i-1} \\ k C_{A0} \tau + X_{i-1} &= X_i + k C_{A0} \tau X_i \\ k C_{A0} \tau + X_{i-1} &= X_i (1 + k C_{A0} \tau) \end{aligned} $$ $$ X_i = \frac{X_{i-1} + kC_{A0}\tau}{1 + kC_{A0}\tau} $$ **Step 5: Let $a = kC_{A0}\tau$ (dimensionless). Then $X_i = \dfrac{X_{i-1} + a}{1+a}$.** **Step 6: Recursively expand for $n$ CSTRs starting from $X_0 = 0$.** - Reactor 1: $X_1 = \dfrac{0 + a}{1+a} = \dfrac{a}{1+a}$ - Reactor 2: $X_2 = \dfrac{X_1 + a}{1+a} = \dfrac{\frac{a}{1+a}+a}{1+a} = \dfrac{a(1+a) + a(1+a)}{(1+a)^2} = \dfrac{2a(1+a)}{(1+a)^2}$ Wait, let me redo this more carefully. $$ X_2 = \frac{X_1 + a}{1+a} = \frac{\frac{a}{1+a}+a}{1+a} = \frac{a + a(1+a)}{(1+a)^2} = \frac{a(2+a)}{(1+a)^2} $$ - Reactor 3: $X_3 = \dfrac{X_2 + a}{1+a}$ **Step 7: General formula (proved by induction).** $$ X_n = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+a)^n} \quad \text{where } a = kC_{A0}\tau $$ **Step 8: Solve for $\tau$ in terms of $X_n$ and $n$.** $$ \begin{aligned} 1 - X_n &= \frac{1}{(1+kC_{A0}\tau)^n} \\ (1-X_n)^{-1/n} &= 1 + kC_{A0}\tau \\ kC_{A0}\tau &= (1-X_n)^{-1/n} - 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 9: Total space time for $n$ equal CSTRs.** $$ \boxed{\tau_{total} = n\tau = \frac{n}{kC_{A0}}\left[(1-X_n)^{-1/n} - 1\right]} $$ For first-order kinetics where $C_{A0}$ appears in the rate law as $-r_A = kC_A$, we sometimes express this as: $$ \tau_{total} = \frac{n}{k}\left[\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right] $$ where the $C_{A0}$ cancels because $k$ is taken as a pseudo-first-order or because the formula is written in terms of $\tau = V/F_{A0}$ and the rate law is $-r_A = kC_A$ with $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$. Verification: $\tau = V/F_{A0}$, $-r_A = kC_{A0}(1-X)$, so $V = F_{A0} \cdot X/[kC_{A0}(1-X)]$, giving $\tau = X/[kC_{A0}(1-X)]$, which is consistent. ## 3.2 Derivation: Optimum Split Point $X_1^*$ for PFR + CSTR Combination **Problem Statement:** For a given overall conversion $X_f$, find the intermediate conversion $X_1$ (exit of PFR, inlet of CSTR) that minimizes $V_{total}$. **Step 1: Write the total volume expression.** $$ V_{total}(X_1) = F_{A0} \int_0^{X_1} \frac{dX}{-r_A(X)} + F_{A0} \cdot \frac{X_f - X_1}{-r_A(X_f)} $$ **Step 2: Differentiate $V_{total}$ with respect to $X_1$ and set to zero.** $$ \frac{dV_{total}}{dX_1} = F_{A0} \cdot \frac{1}{-r_A(X_1)} - F_{A0} \cdot \frac{1}{-r_A(X_f)} = 0 $$ **Step 3: Solve.** $$ \frac{1}{-r_A(X_1^*)} = \frac{1}{-r_A(X_f)} $$ $$ \boxed{X_1^* = X_f} $$ **Wait — this is a degenerate result!** Let me reconsider. The correct derivation requires recognizing that the **intermediate** reactor arrangement is PFR + CSTR, and we minimize over $X_1$ (the conversion at the junction). The CSTR has its volume determined by the *exit* condition only, so its derivative with respect to $X_1$ is just $-F_{A0}/(-r_A(X_f))$ (a constant). The PFR volume *increases* with $X_1$. Re-examining: $$ \frac{dV_{total}}{dX_1} = \frac{F_{A0}}{-r_A(X_1)} - \frac{F_{A0}}{-r_A(X_f)} = 0 $$ This gives $X_1^* = X_f$ only if the rate is monotonic. **Actually, the correct interpretation is different:** The proper condition for optimum is found by graphical construction on the **Levenspiel plot** ($1/-r_A$ vs. $X$): > [!IMPORTANT] > **Optimum Condition (Levenspiel Graphical Method):** The optimum $X_1$ is found by drawing a **rectangle** of area equal to the PFR area (under the curve from 0 to $X_1$), such that the **height of the rectangle** equals $1/-r_A$ at $X_f$. Equivalently, $X_1^*$ satisfies: > $$\boxed{\int_0^{X_1^*} \frac{dX}{-r_A} = \frac{X_f - X_1^*}{-r_A(X_f)}}$$ This means the **PFR area equals the CSTR rectangle area**, which is the graphical optimum criterion. ## 3.3 Derivation: Recycle Reactor Design Equation **Given:** A PFR with a recycle stream of ratio $R = F_R/F_0$. The fresh feed (flow $F_0$, conversion 0) mixes with recycle (flow $F_R$, conversion $X_f$) to give a combined feed to the PFR with conversion $X_{in}$. **Step 1: Component balance on the mixing point for species A.** $$ F_0 \cdot C_{A0} + F_R \cdot C_{A0}(1-X_f) = (F_0 + F_R) \cdot C_{A,in} $$ Dividing by $F_0$: $$ C_{A0} + R \cdot C_{A0}(1-X_f) = (1+R) C_{A,in} $$ $$ C_{A,in} = C_{A0} \cdot \frac{1 + R(1-X_f)}{1+R} $$ **Step 2: Convert to conversion at PFR inlet.** $$ 1 - X_{in} = \frac{C_{A,in}}{C_{A0}} = \frac{1 + R(1-X_f)}{1+R} $$ $$ 1 - X_{in} = \frac{1 + R - R X_f}{1+R} = 1 - \frac{R X_f}{1+R} $$ $$ X_{in} = \frac{R X_f}{1+R} $$ **Step 3: PFR design equation from $X_{in}$ to $X_f$.** $$ V = F_{A0} \cdot (1+R) \int_{X_{in}}^{X_f} \frac{dX}{-r_A} = F_{A0}(1+R) \int_{\frac{RX_f}{1+R}}^{X_f} \frac{dX}{-r_A} $$ **Step 4: Limiting cases.** - $R = 0$: $X_{in} = 0$, $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^{X_f} \frac{dX}{-r_A}$ → PFR. ✓ - $R \to \infty$: $X_{in} \to X_f$, $V \to \infty \cdot 0$ (use L'Hôpital) → CSTR. ✓ ## 3.4 Worked Example: PFR + CSTR Optimization **Problem:** A first-order liquid-phase reaction $-r_A = kC_A$ with $k = 0.5 \, min^{-1}$ and $C_{A0} = 2 \, mol/L$ is carried out in a PFR followed by a CSTR. The feed rate is $F_{A0} = 100 \, mol/min$. Target overall conversion $X_f = 0.80$. Find the **optimum split conversion** $X_1^*$ and the **minimum total volume**. **Solution:** **Step 1: PFR volume (from 0 to $X_1^*$).** $$ V_{PFR} = F_{A0} \int_0^{X_1^*} \frac{dX}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{F_{A0}}{kC_{A0}} \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X_1^*}\right) $$ **Step 2: CSTR volume (from $X_1^*$ to $X_f$).** $$ V_{CSTR} = F_{A0} \cdot \frac{X_f - X_1^*}{kC_{A0}(1-X_f)} $$ **Step 3: Total volume.** $$ V_{total} = \frac{F_{A0}}{kC_{A0}} \left[ -\ln(1-X_1^*) + \frac{X_f - X_1^*}{1-X_f} \right] $$ **Step 4: Apply optimum condition (PFR area = CSTR rectangle area).** $$ -\ln(1-X_1^*) = \frac{X_f - X_1^*}{1-X_f} $$ With $X_f = 0.80$, $1-X_f = 0.20$: $$ -\ln(1-X_1^*) = \frac{0.80 - X_1^*}{0.20} = 4 - 5X_1^* $$ This is a transcendental equation. Solve iteratively. Let $y = 1 - X_1^*$: $$ -\ln(y) = 4 - 5(1-y) = 5y - 1 $$ Trial: $y = 0.5$ → $-\ln(0.5) = 0.693$ vs. $5(0.5)-1 = 1.5$. LHS < RHS, need larger $y$. Trial: $y = 0.6$ → $-\ln(0.6) = 0.511$ vs. $5(0.6)-1 = 2.0$. Still LHS < RHS. Hmm, my equation setup might be off. Let me recheck. Actually for $X_1^* = 0.80$ (i.e., $y = 0.2$): $-\ln(0.2) = 1.609$ vs. $5(0.2)-1 = 0$. LHS > RHS. So the root is between 0.2 and 0.5. Trial: $y = 0.4$: $-\ln(0.4) = 0.916$ vs. $5(0.4)-1 = 1.0$. Close! Trial: $y = 0.38$: $-\ln(0.38) = 0.968$ vs. $5(0.38)-1 = 0.9$. LHS > RHS now. Trial: $y = 0.39$: $-\ln(0.39) = 0.942$ vs. $5(0.39)-1 = 0.95$. Very close! So $y \approx 0.39$, giving $X_1^* = 1 - 0.39 = 0.61$. **Step 5: Calculate minimum total volume.** $$ V_{total} = \frac{100}{0.5 \times 2} \left[ -\ln(0.39) + \frac{0.80 - 0.61}{0.20} \right] $$ $$ V_{total} = 100 \times [0.942 + 0.95] = 100 \times 1.892 = 189.2 \, L $$ > [!TIP] > **Compare with single PFR or single CSTR** for the same conversion: > - Single PFR: $V = 100 \times [-\ln(0.20)] = 100 \times 1.609 = 160.9 \, L$ > - Single CSTR: $V = 100 \times \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 400 \, L$ > - **Optimum PFR+CSTR: 189.2 L** — more than PFR (because first-order favors PFR), but less than CSTR. This is a key KTU result: **For first-order reactions, PFR alone is best; for higher-order reactions, CSTR mix helps; for autocatalytic, recycle is best.** # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Multiple Reactor System Configurations ```mermaid flowchart TB FEED["FRESH FEED

Define the recycle ratio R in a recycle reactor. What is the effect of $R \to \infty$ on the reactor behavior? [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The **recycle ratio $R$** is defined as the ratio of the volumetric flow rate of the recycle stream to the volumetric flow rate of the fresh feed: $$ R = \frac{F_R}{F_0} = \frac{\text{Recycle flow rate}}{\text{Fresh feed flow rate}} $$ **Effect of $R \to \infty$:** As $R \to \infty$, the composition of the inlet stream to the PFR approaches the composition of the product stream (i.e., the inlet conversion $X_{in} \to X_f$). Using L'Hôpital's rule on the recycle design equation, the reactor behaves as a **CSTR** (continuous stirred tank reactor) at this limit. In contrast, $R = 0$ corresponds to **pure PFR** behavior. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### **Q2. Why is a single PFR preferred over a single CSTR for first-order irreversible reactions? Justify with the design equation. [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** For a first-order reaction $-r_A = kC_A = kC_{A0}(1-X)$: - **PFR volume:** $V_{PFR} = \dfrac{F_{A0}}{kC_{A0}} \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X}\right)$ - **CSTR volume:** $V_{CSTR} = \dfrac{F_{A0}}{kC_{A0}} \cdot \dfrac{X}{1-X}$ The ratio is: $$ \frac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{X/(1-X)}{\ln[1/(1-X)]} = \frac{X}{(1-X)\ln[1/(1-X)]} $$ For $0 < X < 1$, this ratio is **always greater than 1**, meaning the CSTR always needs a larger volume than the PFR for the same conversion. This is because the PFR maintains a higher reactant concentration throughout (driving force is highest at inlet), while the CSTR immediately drops to the lowest concentration (exit condition throughout). Therefore, **PFR is preferred for positive-order reactions**. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks Each, Internal Choice]** ### **Question A: 14 Marks (Choice 1)** **(a)** A liquid-phase first-order reaction $A \to products$ with rate constant $k = 0.307 \, min^{-1}$ and inlet concentration $C_{A0} = 0.8 \, mol/L$ is carried out in **two CSTRs in series**. The total feed flow rate is $50 \, L/min$. Calculate the **total volume** required to achieve **80% overall conversion** in two equal-sized CSTRs. **Compare** with a single CSTR and a single PFR for the same conversion. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** For a **PFR followed by a single CSTR** handling the same first-order reaction, determine the **optimum intermediate conversion** $X_1^*$ and the **minimum total volume** for 80% overall conversion. Use the Levenspiel graphical method or the equal-area criterion. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A(a):** **Step 1: Apply the formula for $n$ equal CSTRs in series (first-order kinetics).** $$ \tau_{total} = \frac{n}{kC_{A0}}\left[(1-X_n)^{-1/n} - 1\right] $$ With $n = 2$, $X_n = 0.80$, $k = 0.307 \, min^{-1}$, $C_{A0} = 0.8 \, mol/L$: $$ kC_{A0} = 0.307 \times 0.8 = 0.2456 \, min^{-1} $$ $$ (1-X_n)^{-1/n} = (0.20)^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.20}} = \frac{1}{0.4472} = 2.236 $$ $$ \tau_{total} = \frac{2}{0.2456}(2.236 - 1) = \frac{2 \times 1.236}{0.2456} = \frac{2.472}{0.2456} = 10.06 \, min $$ **Step 2: Total volume.** $$ V_{total} = \tau_{total} \cdot F_{A0} = 10.06 \, min \times 50 \, L/min = 503 \, L $$ **Step 3: Comparison with single CSTR.** $$ V_{CSTR} = \frac{F_{A0}}{kC_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X}{1-X} = \frac{50}{0.2456} \times \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 203.6 \times 4 = 814.4 \, L $$ **Step 4: Comparison with single PFR.** $$ V_{PFR} = \frac{F_{A0}}{kC_{A0}} \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right) = \frac{50}{0.2456} \times \ln(5) = 203.6 \times 1.609 = 327.6 \, L $$ **Summary Table:** | Configuration | Volume (L) | % of PFR | |---|---|---| | Single PFR | 327.6 | 100% | | 2 Equal CSTRs | 503.0 | 154% | | Single CSTR | 814.4 | 249% | > **Conclusion:** PFR is the most efficient for first-order reactions; splitting into 2 CSTRs in series reduces volume by ~38% compared to a single CSTR. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A(b):** **Step 1: PFR volume from 0 to $X_1^*$.** $$ V_{PFR} = \frac{F_{A0}}{kC_{A0}} \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X_1^*}\right) = 203.6 \times \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X_1^*}\right) $$ **Step 2: CSTR volume from $X_1^*$ to $X_f = 0.80$.** $$ V_{CSTR} = \frac{F_{A0}}{kC_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_f - X_1^*}{1 - X_f} = 203.6 \times \frac{0.80 - X_1^*}{0.20} = 1018 \times (0.80 - X_1^*) $$ **Step 3: Apply equal-area optimum condition.** $$ \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X_1^*}\right) = \frac{X_f - X_1^*}{1-X_f} = \frac{0.80 - X_1^*}{0.20} $$ Let $y = 1 - X_1^*$: $$ -\ln(y) = \frac{0.80 - (1-y)}{0.20} = \frac{y - 0.20}{0.20} = 5y - 1 $$ Solve by iteration: - $y = 0.40$: $-\ln(0.40) = 0.916$ vs. $5(0.40) - 1 = 1.0$ → LHS < RHS - $y = 0.39$: $-\ln(0.39) = 0.942$ vs. $5(0.39) - 1 = 0.95$ → LHS ≈ RHS ✓ - $y = 0.38$: $-\ln(0.38) = 0.968$ vs. $5(0.38) - 1 = 0.90$ → LHS > RHS So $y \approx 0.39$, giving $X_1^* \approx 0.61$. **Step 4: Calculate minimum total volume.** $$ V_{PFR} = 203.6 \times \ln(1/0.39) = 203.6 \times 0.942 = 191.8 \, L $$ $$ V_{CSTR} = 1018 \times (0.80 - 0.61) = 1018 \times 0.19 = 193.4 \, L $$ $$ \boxed{V_{total,min} = 191.8 + 193.4 = 385.2 \, L} $$ > **Conclusion:** The optimum PFR+CSTR combination (385.2 L) is more than single PFR (327.6 L) but less than 2 equal CSTRs (503 L) for first-order kinetics. This confirms that for positive-order reactions, a single PFR remains the most efficient design. --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)** **(a)** Derive the **design equation for a recycle reactor** (PFR with recycle ratio $R$). Show the limiting cases $R = 0$ and $R \to \infty$ and identify the corresponding ideal reactor types. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** An **autocatalytic reaction** $A \to B$ with rate law $-r_A = k C_A C_B$ ($k = 0.05 \, L/(mol \cdot s)$, $C_{A0} = 1.0 \, mol/L$, $C_{B0} = 0$) is conducted in a recycle reactor. The target conversion is $X = 0.85$. If a small amount of product is initially present such that $C_{B0}/C_{A0} = 0.05$, find the **optimum recycle ratio $R^*$** that minimizes reactor volume. Plot qualitatively $V$ vs. $R$ to justify. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B(a):** **Step 1: Define the recycle ratio.** $$ R = \frac{F_R}{F_0} \quad \text{(recycle flow / fresh feed flow)} $$ **Step 2: Mixing point balance on species A.** Fresh feed carries $A$ at concentration $C_{A0}$ (conversion 0). Recycle carries $A$ at concentration $C_{A0}(1 - X_f)$. $$ F_0 C_{A0} + F_R C_{A0}(1-X_f) = (F_0 + F_R) C_{A,in} $$ Dividing by $F_0 C_{A0}$: $$ 1 + R(1-X_f) = (1+R)(1-X_{in}) $$ **Step 3: Solve for $X_{in}$.** $$ 1 - X_{in} = \frac{1 + R(1-X_f)}{1+R} $$ $$ X_{in} = 1 - \frac{1 + R(1-X_f)}{1+R} = \frac{R X_f}{1+R} $$ **Step 4: PFR design equation from $X_{in}$ to $X_f$.** The molar flow through the PFR is $F_0(1+R)$. Therefore: $$ V = F_0(1+R) \int_{X_{in}}^{X_f} \frac{dX}{-r_A} = F_{A0}(1+R) \int_{\frac{RX_f}{1+R}}^{X_f} \frac{dX}{-r_A} $$ **Step 5: Limiting cases.** - $R = 0$: $X_{in} = 0$, $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^{X_f}\frac{dX}{-r_A}$ → **PFR behavior** ✓ - $R \to \infty$: $X_{in} \to X_f$, the integrand range shrinks. Apply L'Hôpital or note that the high-recycle limit with large internal flow approximates the backmixing of a **CSTR** ✓ --- ### **Model Solution for Question B(b):** **Step 1: Write the rate law in terms of $X$.** With $C_B = C_{B0} + C_{A0} X$ and $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$: $$ -r_A = k C_{A0}(1-X) \cdot [C_{B0} + C_{A0}X] = k C_{A0}^2 (1-X)\left(\frac{C_{B0}}{C_{A0}} + X\right) $$ Let $\alpha = C_{B0}/C_{A0} = 0.05$: $$ -r_A = k C_{A0}^2 (1-X)(0.05 + X) $$ **Step 2: Substitute into the recycle design equation.** $$ V = F_{A0}(1+R) \int_{0.05R/(1+R)}^{0.85} \frac{dX}{(1-X)(0.05+X)} $$ **Step 3: Apply partial fractions.** $$ \frac{1}{(1-X)(0.05+X)} = \frac{A}{1-X} + \frac{B}{0.05+X} $$ Solving: $1 = A(0.05+X) + B(1-X)$. At $X = 1$: $1 = 1.05 A$ → $A = 1/1.05 \approx 0.952$. At $X = -0.05$: $1 = 1.05 B$ → $B = 1/1.05 \approx 0.952$. So: $$ \int \frac{dX}{(1-X)(0.05+X)} = 0.952 \left[ \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right) + \ln(0.05+X) \right] $$ **Step 4: Compute $V$ as a function of $R$ and find minimum.** The integral is a function of $R$ (through the lower limit). One can show that for autocatalytic reactions, $V$ vs. $R$ has a **minimum at a finite $R^*$** (typically $R^*$ between 1 and 5 for moderate conversions). For this problem: - $R = 0$ (PFR): Volume is large because reaction rate is very slow at low $X$. - $R \to \infty$ (CSTR): Volume is also large. - $R = R^*$: Volume is minimum. By numerical evaluation (typically done in KTU exams with table of integrals or iteration), the optimum $R^* \approx 2.5$ to $3.0$ for these parameters. > [!NOTE] > **Qualitative $V$ vs. $R$ plot for autocatalytic reactions:** > - At $R = 0$: Large $V$ (PFR is poor for autocatalytic because of slow start). > - $V$ **decreases** as $R$ increases. > - $V$ reaches a **minimum** at $R = R^*$. > - $V$ **increases** as $R \to \infty$ (pure CSTR also poor because concentration equalizes too quickly). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern 2024):** > > 1. **Forgetting $C_{A0}$ in the CSTR design equation** — Students often write $V = FX/(-r_A)$ instead of $V = F_{A0} X/(-r_A)$. The $C_{A0}$ factor in $-r_A$ does NOT cancel. **Always write the rate law first in terms of $C_A$ or $X$.** > > 2. **Confusing the recycle ratio formula** — Some students write $R = F_0/F_R$ (inverted). The correct definition is $R = F_R/F_0$ (recycle over fresh feed). Marks deducted: 1–2. > > 3. **Not showing units in final answer** — KTU evaluators **strictly deduct 0.5 mark** if the final numerical answer has no unit (L, m³, min, etc.). > > 4. **Skipping the graphical interpretation** — In "PFR + CSTR" questions, many students compute the volume but don't draw or describe the Levenspiel plot. The 2024 KTU pattern gives **2 marks for graphical reasoning** (equal-area criterion). Always include the plot description. > > 5. **Using $C_{A0} = 0$ for B in autocatalytic reactions** — When $C_{B0} = 0$, the reaction cannot start. Students must be careful: either a recycle is essential, or $C_{B0} > 0$ is given (as in our Question B(b)). > > 6. **Ignoring the $n \to \infty$ limit for CSTRs in series** — This is a **favourite 3-mark question** in viva. Always mention that $n$ equal CSTRs in series → PFR as $n \to \infty$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Multiple Reactor Systems (Module 2)** > > ✅ **Core Design Equations (must memorize):** > - PFR: $V = F_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^X \frac{dX}{-r_A}$ > - CSTR: $V = F_{A0} \cdot \dfrac{X}{-r_A}\big\vert_{outlet}$ > - n CSTRs (1st order): $\tau_{total} = \dfrac{n}{kC_{A0}}\left[(1-X)^{-1/n} - 1\right]$ > - Recycle: $V = F_{A0}(1+R)\displaystyle\int_{\frac{RX_f}{1+R}}^{X_f} \frac{dX}{-r_A}$ > > ✅ **Critical Principles:** > - PFR is **always best for $n$-th order with $n \geq 1$** (positive order). > - CSTR is **best for zero-order** reactions. > - **Recycle is essential for autocatalytic** reactions (e.g., fermentation, A+B→2B). > - **n CSTRs in series → PFR as $n \to \infty$** (fundamental RTD result). > - **n PFRs in series = single PFR** (no benefit, no loss in volume). > > ✅ **Optimum Split Criterion (PFR + CSTR):** > - Equal-area construction on Levenspiel plot. > - $\displaystyle\int_0^{X_1^*} \frac{dX}{-r_A} = \dfrac{X_f - X_1^*}{-r_A(X_f)}$ > > ✅ **Recycle Limits:** > - $R = 0$ → PFR > - $R \to \infty$ → CSTR > - $R = R^*$ → Optimum for autocatalytic > > ✅ **Quick Volume Comparison (for first-order, $X = 0.80$):** > - $V_{PFR} \approx 1.61 \times F_{A0}/kC_{A0}$ (minimum) > - $V_{2\,CSTR} \approx 2.47 \times F_{A0}/kC_{A0}$ > - $V_{CSTR} \approx 4.00 \times F_{A0}/kC_{A0}$ (maximum) > > ✅ **Graphical Tool — Levenspiel Plot:** > - PFR area = area under $1/-r_A$ vs. $X$ curve. > - CSTR area = rectangle with height = $1/-r_A$ at exit conversion. > - Use this to find optimum split for mixed configurations. > > ✅ **Real-World Applications:** > - CSTRs in series → PVC polymerization, fermentation cascades. > - PFR + CSTR → Nitration of aromatics, exothermic liquid-phase rxn. > - Recycle reactor → Fermentation, autocatalytic biochemical processes. > > ✅ **Frequently Tested Conversion Strategies:** > - First-order → PFR wins. > - Zero-order → CSTR wins (equal volumes regardless of split). > - Second-order → PFR still wins, but CSTR mix is acceptable. > - Autocatalytic → Recycle essential; find $R^*$.

topics.title: Plug Flow Reactor in Series and Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plug Flow Reactor in Series and Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)**
> A **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** is an ideal tubular reactor in which the fluid flows in *plug-like* fashion with no longitudinal mixing (no axial back-mixing), but complete radial mixing. All fluid elements have **identical residence time**, equal to the *space time* $\tau = V/\nu_0$.

The performance equation (mole balance on species A) of a single PFR is the differential form:

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}
$$

where $F_{A0}$ is the molar feed rate of the limiting reactant, $V$ is the reactor volume, and $-r_A$ is the rate of disappearance of A expressed as a function of conversion $X_A$.

## 1.2 Definition — PFR in Series and in Parallel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series Configuration (Cascade):** A train of $N$ PFRs connected end-to-end so that the **effluent of reactor $i-1$ becomes the feed of reactor $i$**. The total volume is $V_T = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i$ and conversion accumulates: $X_{A,0} < X_{A,1} < \dots < X_{A,N}$.
>
> **Parallel Configuration (Split-feed):** A feed stream is split into multiple branches, each feeding an independent PFR of volume $V_i$, and the effluents are recombined. The total feed $F_{A0}$ is divided as $F_{A0} = \sum F_{A0,i}$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Lane Toll Booth"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine cars entering a one-way toll road (a single PFR) versus a multi-lane toll plaza (PFRs in parallel).
>
> - **Single PFR = one long tunnel** — every car must travel the full length; exit order = entry order; identical travel time for all.
> - **PFRs in Series = linked subway carriages** — a passenger boards the first, exits the last. The journey is *continuous* and conversion builds progressively through each carriage.
> - **PFRs in Parallel = dual toll lanes merging at the end** — half the cars go through Lane 1, half through Lane 2. They never "see" each other; each lane behaves as an independent PFR. The combined output is the *volume-weighted average* of the individual outputs.

This is why a single PFR of volume $V$ and two parallel PFRs each of volume $V/2$ (equal split) deliver the **same overall conversion** — the residence time per element is unchanged.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V$ | Reactor volume | $\mathrm{m^3}$ |
| $\nu_0$ | Volumetric flow rate | $\mathrm{m^3 \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| $\tau$ | Space time | $\mathrm{s}$ |
| $F_{A0}$ | Molar feed rate of A | $\mathrm{mol \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| $C_{A0}$ | Inlet concentration of A | $\mathrm{mol \cdot m^{-3}}$ |
| $X_A$ | Fractional conversion of A | dimensionless |
| $-r_A$ | Rate of disappearance of A | $\mathrm{mol \cdot m^{-3} \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| $k$ | Reaction rate constant | units depend on order |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

### A. PFR in Series (Cascade of $N$ Reactors)

1. **Mole balance on each reactor $i$** assumes steady state, no axial mixing, and constant $\rho$ (for liquid) or corrected $F_T$ (for gas).
2. The **inlet to reactor $i$** is the **outlet of reactor $i-1$**, so the conversion at its entrance is $X_{A,i-1}$.
3. The **exit conversion** of reactor $i$ is $X_{A,i}$, and is the *intermediate* conversion for the cascade.
4. The design equation for *each* reactor in the series is:

$$
\frac{V_i}{F_{A0}} = \int_{X_{A,i-1}}^{X_{A,i}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}
$$

5. **Summing** all $N$ integrals from $i = 1$ to $N$ gives the *total* design equation:

$$
\frac{V_T}{F_{A0}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{V_i}{F_{A0}} = \int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}
$$

6. The right-hand side is a **definite integral over the cumulative conversion range** — it is path-independent, depending only on the *final* $X_{Af}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Favourite):** For any set of PFRs in series, the **total volume required to reach a given $X_{Af}$ is independent of how the total volume is distributed** among the individual reactors. This is the "**equal-volume theorem for PFRs in series**".

### B. PFRs in Parallel

1. The feed $F_{A0}$ is split into $F_{A0,1}, F_{A0,2}, \dots, F_{A0,N}$ with split fraction $\alpha_i = F_{A0,i}/F_{A0}$, where $\sum \alpha_i = 1$.
2. Each branch operates as an **independent** PFR with its own space time $\tau_i = V_i / \nu_{0,i}$.
3. Exit conversion of branch $i$ is obtained from its individual design equation:

$$
\tau_i = \frac{V_i}{F_{A0,i}} = \int_{0}^{X_{A,i}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}
$$

4. The **overall conversion** of the recombined exit stream is the *flow-weighted average*:

$$
\bar{X}_A = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \, X_{A,i}
$$

5. **For equal-volume PFRs in parallel with equal feed split** ($\alpha_i = 1/N$ and $V_i = V_T/N$), each branch has the same space time as a single PFR of volume $V_T$, hence each branch yields the same $X_{A,i}$, and therefore $\bar{X}_A$ = conversion in a single PFR of volume $V_T$.

### C. Optimum Split for PFRs in Parallel (Different Sizes)

When the parallel PFRs have **different volumes** (or the reaction rate is not linear), the split must be optimised. The general criterion is to make the *exit reaction rate* equal in all branches:

$$
\left. -r_{A}(X_{A,1}^{\text{exit}}) \right|_{\text{branch 1}} = \left. -r_{A}(X_{A,2}^{\text{exit}}) \right|_{\text{branch 2}}
$$

A more universally applicable statement, derivable from the optimisation of $\bar{X}_A$ subject to fixed total volume, is the **equal-area (Levenspiel) condition**:

$$
\int_{0}^{X_{A,1}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A / F_{A0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha_1} = \int_{0}^{X_{A,2}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A / F_{A0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha_2}
$$

subject to the **mixing condition** at the junction.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — these are the 12 equations that recur in every PFR-series/parallel university problem.**

| # | Configuration | Governing Equation | Notes / Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Single PFR | $\dfrac{V}{F_{A0}} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | First principles |
| 2 | PFR — $n$-th order, A $\to$ products | $\dfrac{V}{F_{A0}} = \dfrac{1}{kC_{A0}^{n-1}} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{(1+\varepsilon X_A)^{n-1}}{(1-X_A)^{n}}\, dX_A$ | Gas, $\varepsilon \neq 0$ |
| 3 | PFR, 1st order liquid | $V = \dfrac{\nu_0}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X_{Af}}\right)$ | $-r_A = kC_A$ |
| 4 | PFR, 2nd order liquid | $V = \dfrac{\nu_0}{kC_{A0}} \cdot \dfrac{X_{Af}}{(1-X_{Af})}$ | $2A \to P$ |
| 5 | PFR, 2nd order liquid | $V = \dfrac{\nu_0}{kC_{A0}} \cdot \dfrac{X_{Af}}{(1-X_{Af})}$ | $A + B \to P$, $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$ |
| 6 | $N$ PFRs in series, equal volume | $V_{\text{each}} = \dfrac{V_T}{N}$ | Conversion is *path-dependent* in intermediate $X_{A,i}$, not in $V_T$ |
| 7 | $N$ PFRs in series, total volume | $\dfrac{V_T}{F_{A0}} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | Same RHS as single PFR |
| 8 | $N$ equal PFRs in parallel, equal split | $\bar{X}_A = X_{A}^{\text{single PFR of } V_T}$ | Always equal conversion |
| 9 | PFRs in parallel, weighted split | $\bar{X}_A = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i X_{A,i}$ with $\sum \alpha_i = 1$ | General result |
| 10 | Split fraction, PFR parallel | $\alpha_i = \dfrac{F_{A0,i}}{F_{A0}} = \dfrac{\nu_{0,i}}{\nu_0}$ | Constant $\rho$ |
| 11 | 1st order, $N$ equal PFRs in series | $X_{Af} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+k\tau_{\text{each}})^{N}}$ | $V_{\text{each}} = V_T/N$ |
| 12 | Space time per element | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{\nu_0}$ | Equal for all elements in PFR |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymerisation tubular reactors** (LDPE, polystyrene) — large lengths necessitate *modelling as PFRs in series* for accurate residence-time distribution.
- **Catalytic converter monoliths** in automobiles — modelled as a series of differential PFR slices.
- **Cracking furnaces in petroleum refineries** — long coils simulated as a cascade of PFRs for cracking kinetics.
- **Pharmaceutical multi-step synthesis** — when each step has a different rate constant, multiple PFRs in series at different temperatures are used.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Design Equation for a Single PFR

Consider a tubular reactor of cross-section $S$ and length $L$. Take a differential slice of thickness $dz$ at position $z$ from the inlet.

**Step 1 — Molar flow at slice entrance and exit:**
Molar flow of A entering the slice: $F_A \vert_{z}$. Molar flow leaving the slice: $F_A \vert_{z+dz}$. By Taylor expansion for a thin slice:

$$
F_A \vert_{z+dz} \approx F_A \vert_{z} + \frac{dF_A}{dz} \, dz
$$

**Step 2 — Mole balance on the slice at steady state:**

$$
\text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Disappearance} = 0
$$

$$
F_A \big\vert_{z} - F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} - (-r_A)\, S\, dz = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Taylor expansion:**

$$
F_A \big\vert_{z} - \left(F_A \big\vert_{z} + \frac{dF_A}{dz} dz\right) - (-r_A)\, S\, dz = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $F_A \vert_z$ and solve for the gradient:**

$$
- \frac{dF_A}{dz} \, dz = (-r_A)\, S\, dz
$$

$$
\frac{dF_A}{dz} = -r_A \, S
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to conversion variable** $X_A = (F_{A0} - F_A)/F_{A0}$, so $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and $dF_A = -F_{A0}\, dX_A$:

$$
- F_{A0}\, \frac{dX_A}{dz} = -r_A \, S
$$

$$
F_{A0}\, \frac{dX_A}{dz} = r_A \, S
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from inlet ($z=0, X_A = 0$) to outlet ($z = L, X_A = X_{Af}$):**

$$
\int_{0}^{L} dz = \int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \frac{F_{A0}}{-r_A \, S}\, dX_A
$$

**Step 7 — Use $V = S \cdot L$ (volume = cross-section × length):**

$$
\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Derivation 1.** This is the single most-tested equation in the entire CRE syllabus.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two Unequal PFRs in Series for a Second-Order Reaction

**System:** Reaction $A + B \to P$ with $C_{A0} = C_{B0} = C_0$ (equimolar), liquid phase, elementary second-order, $-r_A = k C_A^2$. The reactor is split into two PFRs of volumes $V_1$ and $V_2$ in series, with intermediate conversion $X_{A,1}$ and final conversion $X_{Af}$.

**Step 1 — Mole balance on reactor 1 (PFR):**

$$
\frac{V_1}{F_{A0}} = \int_{0}^{X_{A,1}} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral (use $u = 1 - X_A$, $du = -dX_A$):**

$$
\int \frac{dX_A}{(1 - X_A)^2} = \frac{1}{1 - X_A} + C
$$

So,

$$
\frac{V_1 k C_{A0}^2}{F_{A0}} = \left[\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right]_{0}^{X_{A,1}} = \frac{1}{1 - X_{A,1}} - 1 = \frac{X_{A,1}}{1 - X_{A,1}}
$$

**Step 3 — Mole balance on reactor 2 (PFR):**

The inlet to reactor 2 has $X_{A,1}$ already achieved; balance the increment from $X_{A,1}$ to $X_{Af}$:

$$
\frac{V_2 k C_{A0}^2}{F_{A0}} = \left[\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right]_{X_{A,1}}^{X_{Af}} = \frac{1}{1 - X_{Af}} - \frac{1}{1 - X_{A,1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Total design equation for the cascade** (sum of step 2 and step 3):

$$
\frac{(V_1 + V_2) k C_{A0}^2}{F_{A0}} = \frac{X_{A,1}}{1 - X_{A,1}} + \frac{1}{1 - X_{Af}} - \frac{1}{1 - X_{A,1}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the right-hand side:**

$$
\frac{X_{A,1}}{1 - X_{A,1}} - \frac{1}{1 - X_{A,1}} + \frac{1}{1 - X_{Af}} = \frac{X_{A,1} - 1}{1 - X_{A,1}} + \frac{1}{1 - X_{Af}}
$$

Since $X_{A,1} - 1 = -(1 - X_{A,1})$:

$$
= \frac{-(1 - X_{A,1})}{1 - X_{A,1}} + \frac{1}{1 - X_{Af}} = -1 + \frac{1}{1 - X_{Af}} = \frac{X_{Af}}{1 - X_{Af}}
$$

**Step 6 — Final compact result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{V_T \, k \, C_{A0}^2}{F_{A0}} = \frac{X_{Af}}{1 - X_{Af}}\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Crucial Conclusion:**

The total volume $V_T = V_1 + V_2$ required to reach $X_{Af}$ for this second-order reaction in PFRs is **independent** of how $V_T$ is split between $V_1$ and $V_2$. The intermediate conversion $X_{A,1}$ will *adjust automatically* to match the volume $V_1$ chosen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This proves the **"PFR-Series Equal-Volume Theorem"**: for any reaction order, the *total* PFR volume in series to achieve a given $X_{Af}$ is fixed. Only the *intermediate* $X_{A,i}$ values change with the split. This theorem breaks down only when temperature varies between reactors.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimum Feed Split for Two PFRs in Parallel

**System:** Two PFRs of volumes $V_1$ and $V_2$ ($V_1 \neq V_2$) operate in parallel with a fraction $\alpha$ of the total feed $F_{A0}$ going to reactor 1, and $(1-\alpha)$ going to reactor 2. Find $\alpha$ that maximises the **overall conversion** $\bar{X}_A = \alpha X_{A,1} + (1-\alpha) X_{A,2}$.

**Step 1 — Write individual design equations.** Each reactor is a PFR; denote $g(X) \equiv \int_0^X dX_A/(-r_A)$. Then:

$$
g(X_{A,1}) = \frac{V_1}{\alpha F_{A0}}, \qquad g(X_{A,2}) = \frac{V_2}{(1-\alpha) F_{A0}}
$$

**Step 2 — Implicit expressions for $X_{A,1}$ and $X_{A,2}$** follow by inverting $g$:

$$
X_{A,1} = g^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_1}{\alpha F_{A0}}\right), \qquad X_{A,2} = g^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_2}{(1-\alpha) F_{A0}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Set up the optimisation.** $\bar{X}_A$ is to be maximised with respect to $\alpha$:

$$
\frac{d\bar{X}_A}{d\alpha} = X_{A,1} - X_{A,2} + \alpha \frac{dX_{A,1}}{d\alpha} - (1-\alpha) \frac{dX_{A,2}}{d\alpha} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate the implicit design equations** using $\frac{d}{d\alpha} g(X_{A,i}) = g'(X_{A,i}) \frac{dX_{A,i}}{d\alpha}$:

For reactor 1:
$$
\frac{1}{-r_A(X_{A,1})} \frac{dX_{A,1}}{d\alpha} = -\frac{V_1}{\alpha^2 F_{A0}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dX_{A,1}}{d\alpha} = \frac{V_1}{\alpha^2 F_{A0}} \cdot r_A(X_{A,1})
$$

For reactor 2:
$$
\frac{1}{-r_A(X_{A,2})} \frac{dX_{A,2}}{d\alpha} = \frac{V_2}{(1-\alpha)^2 F_{A0}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dX_{A,2}}{d\alpha} = -\frac{V_2}{(1-\alpha)^2 F_{A0}} \cdot r_A(X_{A,2})
$$

**Step 5 — Plug into step 3** and use the chain-rule identity:

$$
g(X_{A,1}) = \frac{V_1}{\alpha F_{A0}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{V_1}{\alpha^2 F_{A0}} = \frac{1}{\alpha} g(X_{A,1})
$$

So,
$$
\frac{dX_{A,1}}{d\alpha} = \frac{1}{\alpha} g(X_{A,1}) \cdot r_A(X_{A,1}) = -\frac{1}{\alpha} \int_0^{X_{A,1}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} \cdot r_A(X_{A,1})
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back and use the chain rule** with $g'(X) = 1/(-r_A)$:

After full substitution and simplification (this is the standard textbook result — Fogler, *Elements of Chemical Reaction Engineering*, 4th ed., Ch. 7):

$$
\boxed{\; -r_A(X_{A,1}) = -r_A(X_{A,2}) \quad \text{at optimum split} \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimum Criterion (KTU High-Yield):** The overall conversion is maximised when the *exit rate of reaction* (i.e., the rate evaluated at each branch's exit conversion) is **identical** in both parallel reactors. This is a *necessary* but not always *sufficient* condition; the second derivative must also be checked for a maximum.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PFR in Series vs Parallel

| Criterion | PFRs in Series (Cascade) | PFRs in Parallel (Split-Feed) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_T$ for given $X_{Af}$ | $V_T = $ value from $\int dX_A/(-r_A)$ | Depends on $\alpha_i$; minimised at optimum split |
| Intermediate conversions | $X_{A,i}$ accumulates monotonically | $X_{A,i}$ computed per branch |
| Equal-size, equal-split case | All $X_{A,i}$ equal only if $-r_A$ is constant | $X_{A,1} = X_{A,2} = X_{A,\text{single}}$ |
| Mixing between reactors | Continuous, no junction | Junction with stream re-mixing |
| Plug-flow assumption | Strictly holds within each tube | Holds within each branch |
| Application | Sequential reactions (A $\to$ B $\to$ C) | Multiple identical lines, redundancy |
| Optimisation variable | Volume distribution $V_i$ | Feed split $\alpha_i$ |
| Typical industry | Multi-zone tubular furnace | Multi-lane polymerisation lines |

## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem (from KTU 2018 Scheme, Q-pattern):** The elementary liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ is second-order with $k = 0.5\ \mathrm{L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot min^{-1}}$ and $C_{A0} = 2\ \mathrm{mol/L}$. The feed rate is $F_{A0} = 4\ \mathrm{mol/min}$. Calculate the volume of **two PFRs in series** to achieve $X_{Af} = 0.80$, if the intermediate conversion is $X_{A,1} = 0.50$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reactor 1, from $X_A = 0$ to $X_{A,1} = 0.5$:**

For a second-order reaction, $-r_A = k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2$.

$$
V_1 = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}^2} \cdot \frac{X_{A,1}}{1 - X_{A,1}}
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{4}{(0.5)(2)^2} \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.5} = \frac{4}{2} \cdot 1 = 2\ \mathrm{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Reactor 2, from $X_{A,1} = 0.5$ to $X_{Af} = 0.8$:**

$$
V_2 = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}^2} \left[\frac{1}{1 - X_{Af}} - \frac{1}{1 - X_{A,1}}\right]
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{4}{2} \left[\frac{1}{1 - 0.8} - \frac{1}{1 - 0.5}\right] = 2 \left[5 - 2\right] = 2 \cdot 3 = 6\ \mathrm{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Total volume:**

$$
V_T = V_1 + V_2 = 2 + 6 = 8\ \mathrm{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification using single-PFR formula:**

$$
V_T^{\text{single}} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}^2} \cdot \frac{X_{Af}}{1 - X_{Af}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8}{0.2} = 2 \cdot 4 = 8\ \mathrm{L}
$$

Both methods yield the **same $V_T = 8\ \mathrm{L}$**, confirming the series-equivalence theorem.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — PFR in Series (Cascade)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1([Feed F_A0, X_A = 0]) --> B1[PFR 1 Volume V1]
    B1 --> C1((Junction 1 X_A1))
    C1 --> D1[PFR 2 Volume V2]
    D1 --> E1((Junction 2 X_A2))
    E1 --> F1[PFR N Volume VN]
    F1 --> G1([Exit X_Af])
    
    style A1 fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1f6feb,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#ddf4dd,stroke:#2da44e,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#ddf4dd,stroke:#2da44e,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#ddf4dd,stroke:#2da44e,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#fff0f0,stroke:#cf222e,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#fff0f0,stroke:#cf222e,color:#000
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

- **Feed node** delivers molar flow $F_{A0}$ with $X_A = 0$ into the cascade.
- **Reactor 1** is the *primary* reaction zone where initial conversion $X_{A,1}$ is achieved; outlet streams retain $F_T = F_{A0} (1 + \varepsilon X_A)$ for gases (constant for liquids).
- **Junction nodes** are *ideal mixing points* that *do not* change conversion (they simply pass the stream to the next PFR).
- **Reactor 2, ..., Reactor N** continue to build conversion until the **final exit node** with $X_{Af}$.
- The total volume $V_T = \sum V_i$ satisfies the *single-PFR integral*, confirming path-independence.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — PFRs in Parallel (Split-Feed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0([Feed F_A0]) --> P1{Splitter}
    P1 -->|alpha F_A0| P2[PFR 1 Volume V1]
    P1 -->|1 minus alpha F_A0| P3[PFR 2 Volume V2]
    P2 --> P4((Junction))
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5([Exit X_A bar])
    
    style P0 fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1f6feb,color:#000
    P1_fill[/Split box fills here/]
    style P1 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#ddf4dd,stroke:#2da44e,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#ddf4dd,stroke:#2da44e,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#fff0f0,stroke:#cf222e,color:#000
    style P5 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

- **Splitter** divides the total molar feed $F_{A0}$ into $\alpha F_{A0}$ and $(1-\alpha)F_{A0}$ — analogous to a piping tee.
- **Each PFR branch** operates as an isolated tubular reactor with its own space time $\tau_i = V_i/(\alpha_i \nu_0)$.
- **Mixing Junction** recomposes the exit streams; the overall conversion is the *flow-weighted average* $\bar{X}_A = \alpha X_{A,1} + (1-\alpha) X_{A,2}$.
- The optimum $\alpha$ maximises $\bar{X}_A$ — the condition $-r_A(X_{A,1}) = -r_A(X_{A,2})$ (exit rates equal) is obtained from calculus, as derived in Section 3.3.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparison: Series vs Parallel Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Configuration} --> R1[Series Cascade]
    Q1 --> R2[Parallel Split]
    
    R1 --> S1[Conversion accumulates X_A0 less than X_A1 less than X_AN]
    R1 --> S2[Path-independent V_T for given X_Af]
    R1 --> S3[Best for sequential reactions]
    
    R2 --> T1[Independent branches X_A1 and X_A2]
    R2 --> T2[Weighted overall X_A bar]
    R2 --> T3[Optimum alpha satisfies equal exit rate]
    
    style Q1 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#ddf4dd,stroke:#2da44e,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#ddebf6,stroke:#1f6feb,color:#000
    style S1 fill:#f6f6f6,stroke:#555,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#f6f6f6,stroke:#555,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#f6f6f6,stroke:#555,color:#000
    style T1 fill:#f6f6f6,stroke:#555,color:#000
    style T2 fill:#f6f6f6,stroke:#555,color:#000
    style T3 fill:#f6f6f6,stroke:#555,color:#000
```
