## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:** Define "contacting pattern" in the context of multiple reactions. Why is it important in reactor design for parallel reactions?

**Model Answer:**
Contacting pattern refers to the way reactant molecules are brought together in a reactor, primarily characterized as either *mixed flow* (back-mix, CSTR/MFR) or *plug flow* (no axial mixing, PFR). In parallel reactions $A \to R$ and $A \to S$, the local concentration $C_A$ determines the instantaneous selectivity $S = r_R / r_S = (k_1/k_2) C_A^{a_1 - a_2}$. Since PFR and CSTR maintain different $C_A$ profiles, the *same conversion* yields *different product distributions*. The contacting pattern is thus critical for maximizing the yield of the desired product R. (3 marks — full marks for defining contacting pattern, explaining $C_A$ influence, and linking to selectivity.)

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:** Two parallel reactions $A \to R$ and $A \to S$ have orders $a_1 = 2$ and $a_2 = 1$ respectively. Which reactor — PFR or CSTR — gives a higher fractional yield of R at the same conversion? Justify in one sentence.

**Model Answer:**
**PFR gives a higher fractional yield of R.** Justification: Since the desired product R has a *higher order* ($a_1 = 2 > a_2 = 1$), the rate $r_R$ is favored at high $C_A$. PFR maintains a high $C_A$ profile along its length, whereas CSTR drops $C_A$ to the exit value everywhere, thereby suppressing $r_R$ relative to $r_S$. (3 marks — 1 for the answer, 2 for the justification involving order analysis.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Statement:** A feed containing $C_{A0} = 2 \; \text{mol/L}$ undergoes parallel reactions: $A \to R$ (desired, $k_1 = 0.4 \; \text{min}^{-1}$, order 1) and $A \to S$ (undesired, $k_2 = 0.25 \; \text{min}^{-1}$, order 1). Both reactions are first order. The required conversion is $X_A = 0.75$.

**(a)** Derive the design expressions for the fractional yield of R in a PFR and a CSTR. Show that the yields are identical for equal-order parallel reactions. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the exit concentration of R in both reactors. Also calculate the required residence time $\tau$ for each. Comment on whether the reactor choice matters here. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

For a PFR, the mole balance on R gives:

$$
\frac{dC_R}{dC_A} = \frac{r_R}{-r_A} = \frac{k_1 C_A}{k_1 C_A + k_2 C_A} = \frac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2}
$$

Integrating from $C_{A0}$ to $C_A$:

$$
C_R = \frac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2}(C_{A0} - C_A) = \frac{k_1 C_{A0} X_A}{k_1 + k_2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{C_R}{C_{A0}} = \frac{k_1 X_A}{k_1 + k_2} \quad \text{(PFR)}
$$

For a CSTR, the algebraic form is:

$$
\frac{C_R}{C_{A0}} = \frac{k_1 X_A}{k_1 + k_2} \quad \text{(CSTR)}
$$

Both expressions are **identical** because for equal orders, the ratio $r_R / (-r_A) = k_1 / (k_1 + k_2)$ is a constant independent of $C_A$. The integration in PFR yields the same result as the algebraic CSTR expression. ✓ (7 marks: 3 for PFR derivation, 2 for CSTR, 2 for the conclusion.)

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $C_{A0} = 2 \; \text{mol/L}$, $k_1 = 0.4 \; \text{min}^{-1}$, $k_2 = 0.25 \; \text{min}^{-1}$, $X_A = 0.75$.

Both reactors give the same $C_R$:

$$
\frac{C_R}{C_{A0}} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.75}{0.4 + 0.25} = \frac{0.3}{0.65} = 0.4615
$$

$$
C_R = 0.4615 \times 2 = 0.923 \; \text{mol/L}
$$

**Residence time:**

For PFR:

$$
\tau_{PFR} = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(k_1 + k_2) C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{1}{k_1 + k_2} \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A}
$$

$$
\tau_{PFR} = -\frac{1}{k_1 + k_2} \ln(1 - X_A) = -\frac{\ln(0.25)}{0.65} = \frac{1.3863}{0.65} = 2.133 \; \text{min}
$$

For CSTR:

$$
\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{(k_1 + k_2) C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{X_A}{(k_1 + k_2)(1 - X_A)} = \frac{0.75}{0.65 \times 0.25} = 4.615 \; \text{min}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since both reactors give the *same* $C_R = 0.923 \; \text{mol/L}$, the **choice of reactor is immaterial for product distribution**. However, the **CSTR requires a much larger volume** ($\tau_{CSTR}/\tau_{PFR} = 2.16$) for the same conversion. Therefore, the **PFR is preferred on economic grounds** (smaller volume), even though selectivity is identical. (7 marks: 2 for $C_R$ calculation, 3 for the two $\tau$ calculations, 2 for the conclusion.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Statement:** In a chemical process, reactant A undergoes two parallel reactions: $A \to R$ (desired, first order, $k_1 = 0.3 \; \text{min}^{-1}$) and $A \to S$ (undesired, second order, $k_2 = 0.15 \; \text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{min}^{-1}$). The feed concentration is $C_{A0} = 1 \; \text{mol/L}$.

**(a)** Without any calculation, state and justify which reactor (PFR or CSTR) gives a higher fractional yield of R. Mention the underlying principle. (7 marks)

**(b)** At conversion $X_A = 0.8$, calculate $C_R$ and the fractional yield of R in both PFR and CSTR. Verify your answer in part (a) numerically. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Qualitative Justification:**

**The CSTR gives a higher fractional yield of R.**

**Underlying Principle:** The instantaneous selectivity of R over S is:

$$
S_{R/S} = \frac{r_R}{r_S} = \frac{k_1 C_A}{k_2 C_A^2} = \frac{k_1}{k_2 C_A}
$$

Since the desired reaction (order 1) has a *lower* order than the undesired reaction (order 2), selectivity is *inversely proportional* to $C_A$. Lowering $C_A$ enhances the relative rate of R formation.

A **CSTR** operates at a single, *low* value of $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ throughout the reactor, which maximizes selectivity toward the lower-order R. A **PFR**, in contrast, begins at a high $C_{A0}$ where the second-order S is favored in the early portion of the reactor, reducing overall fractional yield. Hence, **CSTR is the preferred contacting pattern** for this system. (7 marks: 2 for stating CSTR, 3 for the selectivity analysis, 2 for the $C_A$-profile comparison.)

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $C_{A0} = 1 \; \text{mol/L}$, $k_1 = 0.3 \; \text{min}^{-1}$, $k_2 = 0.15 \; \text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{min}^{-1}$, $X_A = 0.8$.

Exit concentration: $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A) = 1 \times 0.2 = 0.2 \; \text{mol/L}$.

**PFR calculation** (using the Special Case II formula derived in Section 3.4):

$$
\frac{C_R}{C_{A0}} = \frac{k_1}{k_2 C_{A0}} \ln\!\left( \frac{k_1 + k_2 C_{A0}}{k_1 + k_2 C_A} \right)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\frac{C_R}{C_{A0}} = \frac{0.3}{0.15 \times 1} \ln\!\left( \frac{0.3 + 0.15 \times 1}{0.3 + 0.15 \times 0.2} \right) = 2 \times \ln\!\left( \frac{0.45}{0.33} \right) = 2 \times \ln(1.3636) = 2 \times 0.3102 = 0.6205
$$

Therefore $C_R^{PFR} = 0.6205 \times 1 = 0.6205 \; \text{mol/L}$.

**CSTR calculation** (using the general CSTR formula):

$$
\frac{C_R}{C_{A0}} = \frac{k_1 X_A}{k_1 + k_2 C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.8}{0.3 + 0.15 \times 1 \times 0.2} = \frac{0.24}{0.33} = 0.7273
$$

Therefore $C_R^{CSTR} = 0.7273 \times 1 = 0.7273 \; \text{mol/L}$.

**Fractional Yields:**

$$
\phi_R^{PFR} = \frac{C_R^{PFR}}{C_{A0} X_A} = \frac{0.6205}{0.8} = 0.776
$$

$$
\phi_R^{CSTR} = \frac{C_R^{CSTR}}{C_{A0} X_A} = \frac{0.7273}{0.8} = 0.909
$$

**Verification:** $\phi_R^{CSTR} = 0.909 > \phi_R^{PFR} = 0.776$, confirming the qualitative answer in part (a). The CSTR provides a **17.1% higher fractional yield** of R at the same conversion. (7 marks: 3 for PFR, 3 for CSTR, 1 for the fractional yields and verification.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign error in PFR integral:** Students often forget that $C_A$ is *decreasing* in a PFR, so the integral limits run from $C_{A0}$ down to $C_A$ and the natural log appears with a *flipped* sign. Always double-check the sign of $\ln[(k_1 + k_2 C_{A0})/(k_1 + k_2 C_A)]$.
> 2. **Conflating parallel and series rules:** For *parallel* reactions, **higher-order desired product → PFR**. For *series* reactions, the rule is *reversed*. Writing the series-reaction rule on a parallel-reaction problem costs full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting $C_{A0}$ normalization:** KTU demands $\phi_R$ (dimensionless) — dividing by $C_{A0} X_A$ is mandatory.
> 4. **Using the wrong special case formula:** When $a_1 = a_2$, students sometimes still integrate the PFR and obtain a non-constant — read the orders *first*.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** $k_1$ has units $\text{min}^{-1}$ for first-order, but $k_2$ has units $\text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{min}^{-1}$ for second-order. Mixing these up yields dimensionally wrong answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parallel reactions** are independent pathways: $A \to R$, $A \to S$, etc. There is **no intermediate**; both products are *primary*.
- The **instantaneous selectivity** is $S_{R/S} = (k_1/k_2) C_A^{a_1 - a_2}$. This formula alone decides the reactor choice.
- **Core reactor-selection rule (parallel reactions):**
  - $a_1 > a_2$ → **PFR** (maintain high $C_A$)
  - $a_1 < a_2$ → **CSTR** (low uniform $C_A$)
  - $a_1 = a_2$ → **Either** (selectivity is $C_A$-independent)
- **CSTR general formula** for fractional yield: $\phi_R = \dfrac{X_A}{1 + (k_2/k_1) C_A^{a_2 - a_1}}$.
- **PFR general formula** requires integration of $\dfrac{dC_R}{dC_A}$; tractable for integer orders.
- For **first-order in both** (i.e., $a_1 = a_2 = 1$), PFR and CSTR give the **same $C_R$**, but PFR needs less volume.
- The PFR result for $a_1 = 1, a_2 = 2$ is $\dfrac{C_R}{C_{A0}} = \dfrac{k_1}{k_2 C_{A0}} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{k_1 + k_2 C_{A0}}{k_1 + k_2 C_A} \right]$.
- The PFR result for $a_1 = 2, a_2 = 1$ is $\dfrac{C_R}{C_{A0}} = -X_A + \dfrac{k_2}{k_1 C_{A0}} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{k_1 C_{A0} + k_2}{k_1 C_A + k_2} \right]$.
- The selection rule for **parallel** reactions is the **opposite** of the rule for **series** reactions — KTU frequently tests this contrast.
- **Mixed reactors / recycle** are used when neither pure PFR nor pure CSTR is optimal, or when both high selectivity and high conversion are needed.
- **Batch reactor** behaves identically to a PFR in terms of product distribution (same $C_A$ vs. time trajectory), so all PFR conclusions transfer to batch.
- The **Levenspiel plot** of $1/(-r_A)$ vs. $C_A$ is the standard graphical tool used in KTU derivations for $V_{PFR}$ and $V_{CSTR}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Qualitative Discussion About Product Distribution of Series Reactions. Problem Solving in Ideal Reactors and Recycle Reactor Using Excel/MATLAB/SCILAB/Python etc. - Only for Self Study/Microproject/Assignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Qualitative Discussion About Product Distribution of Series Reactions. Problem Solving in Ideal Reactors and Recycle Reactor Using Excel/MATLAB/SCILAB/Python etc. - Only for Self Study/Microproject/Assignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Series (Consecutive) Reactions

In **Chemical Reaction Engineering**, a **series reaction** (also called a **consecutive reaction**) is a reaction network in which a reactant forms an intermediate product, which in turn reacts further to form an undesired final product. The general form studied in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 is:

$$
A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S
$$

where:
- **A** is the **limiting reactant** (feed species).
- **R** is the **desired intermediate product** (target molecule).
- **S** is the **waste / over-reacted byproduct**.
- **k₁** and **k₂** are the **rate constants** (units: **s⁻¹** or **time⁻¹**) for first-order processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Product distribution in series reactions is fundamentally governed by the **relative magnitudes of k₁ and k₂** and by the **residence time / space time** distribution inside the chosen ideal reactor (Batch, PFR, or CSTR).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Relay Race"

Imagine a relay race where runner **A** must hand the baton to runner **R**, who then hands it to runner **S**.

- If **A is fast** (high k₁) but **R is slow to pass the baton** (low k₂), then **R piles up** at the exchange zone — you maximize R.
- If **R runs too fast** (high k₂), the baton zooms past R and accumulates at **S** — you lose R.
- A **long, narrow track** (PFR) keeps all runners spread out: lots of A near the start, lots of S near the finish.
- A **big round mixing bowl** (CSTR) makes everyone run together instantly — bad for R because the average age of R is much higher, giving k₂ more time to destroy it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Because **CSTR has the broadest residence-time distribution**, it is the **worst** reactor for intermediate R when k₂ > k₁. Conversely, a **PFR (or Batch) with controlled short contact time** maximizes R selectivity.

### 1.3 The Recycle Reactor — Definition

A **recycle reactor** is a **plug flow reactor (PFR)** in which a fraction of the **exit stream** is routed back and mixed with the **fresh feed** at the inlet.

$$
\text{Recycle Ratio } \alpha = \frac{\text{Volume of fluid returned}}{\text{Volume of fresh feed}} = \frac{F_R}{F_0}
$$

- When **α → 0**, the recycle reactor behaves as a **pure PFR**.
- When **α → ∞**, the recycle reactor approaches the **CSTR limit** (complete back-mixing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a recycle reactor?** It allows the engineer to **continuously tune the effective residence-time distribution** between PFR-like and CSTR-like behavior by simply adjusting α. This is invaluable for **highly exothermic** or **autothermal** reactions, and for **selectivity optimization in series networks**.

### 1.4 Computational Tools — Scope in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Study / Microproject Note:** KTU lists Excel / MATLAB / SCILAB / Python as the permitted computational platforms. Numerical solution of the coupled ODEs governing series reactions in Batch and PFR, and the algebraic equations for CSTR, can be automated in any of these tools. The mathematics — not the syntax — is the focus of evaluation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Differential Equations for Series Reactions

For a **constant-density, isothermal, first-order series reaction** $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$, the species mole balances in a **differential (Batch or PFR) element** are:

$$
\frac{dC_A}{d\tau} = -k_1\, C_A
$$

$$
\frac{dC_R}{d\tau} = +k_1\, C_A - k_2\, C_R
$$

$$
\frac{dC_S}{d\tau} = +k_2\, C_R
$$

where $\tau$ is **residence time** (PFR) or **reaction time** (Batch). 

### 2.2 Why Product Distribution Depends on Reactor Type

- **Batch Reactor (BR) / PFR:** Each fluid element has the **same residence time τ**. The intermediate R builds up, reaches a maximum, then decays as it converts to S.
- **CSTR (MFR):** Fluid elements have a wide **exponential distribution of residence times**. Some fluid leaves almost immediately (low conversion of A) while some lingers (lots of S). The *time-averaged* CR is always **lower** than the PFR peak.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic — Maximum R Strategy

1. Compute $C_R(\tau)$ for Batch / PFR.
2. Differentiate $\dfrac{dC_R}{d\tau} = 0$ to find the **optimum contact time** $\tau_{\text{opt}}$.
3. Evaluate $C_{R,\max}$ at $\tau_{\text{opt}}$.
4. Compute **selectivity** $\Phi = \dfrac{C_R}{C_{A0} - C_A}$ and **yield** $Y = \dfrac{C_R}{C_{A0}}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Batch / PFR Equation | CSTR / MFR Equation | Units |
|---|----------|----------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $C_A$ | $C_{A0}\, e^{-k_1 \tau}$ | $\dfrac{C_{A0}}{1+k_1 \tau}$ | mol·m⁻³ |
| 2 | $C_R$ | $\dfrac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2-k_1}\left(e^{-k_1\tau}-e^{-k_2\tau}\right)$ | $\dfrac{k_1 C_{A0}\tau}{(1+k_1\tau)(1+k_2\tau)}$ | mol·m⁻³ |
| 3 | $C_S$ | $C_{A0}\!\left[1+\dfrac{k_1 e^{-k_2\tau}-k_2 e^{-k_1\tau}}{k_2-k_1}\right]$ | $\dfrac{k_1 k_2 C_{A0}\tau^2}{(1+k_1\tau)(1+k_2\tau)}$ | mol·m⁻³ |
| 4 | $\tau_{\text{opt}}$ (max R, PFR/BR) | $\dfrac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2-k_1}$ | — | s or min |
| 5 | $C_{R,\max}$ | $C_{A0}\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{k_2/(k_1-k_2)}$ | — | mol·m⁻³ |
| 6 | Recycle residence time | $\tau_{\text{eff}} = \tau\,(1+\alpha)$ | — | s |
| 7 | Recycle conversion of A | $X_A = \dfrac{k_1\tau_{\text{eff}}}{1+k_1\tau_{\text{eff}}}$ (1st order) | — | dimensionless |
| 8 | Selectivity $\Phi$ | $C_R/(C_{A0}-C_A)$ | same definition | dimensionless |
| 9 | Yield $Y$ | $C_R/C_{A0}$ | same definition | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Rule:** All formulas assume **constant density**, **isothermal** operation, and **first-order kinetics** in each step. Negative $\tau_{\text{opt}}$ simply means **no maximum exists** in the physically meaningful range — set $\tau_{\text{opt}} = 0$ instead.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical cracking:** Maximize gasoline (R) and minimize coke (S) — exactly a series-reaction selectivity problem solved by short-contact-time PFRs.
- **Pharmaceutical synthesis:** Protect an intermediate functional group; **stop R before it decomposes**.
- **Polymerization engineering:** Chain length is governed by $k_2/k_1$ ratios.
- **Recycle reactors:** Used in **autothermal reforming**, **adiabatic partial oxidation**, and **high-conversion ammonia synthesis loops**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Batch / PFR Solution for $C_R(\tau)$

**Step 1.** Solve the A balance first; it is decoupled:

$$
\frac{dC_A}{d\tau} = -k_1 C_A \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k_1\, d\tau
$$

**Step 2.** Integrate with initial condition $C_A(0) = C_{A0}$:

$$
\ln C_A - \ln C_{A0} = -k_1 \tau \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_A(\tau) = C_{A0}\, e^{-k_1 \tau}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into the R balance:

$$
\frac{dC_R}{d\tau} + k_2 C_R = k_1 C_{A0} e^{-k_1\tau}
$$

**Step 4.** Use the **integrating factor** $\mu(\tau) = e^{\int k_2 d\tau} = e^{k_2 \tau}$:

$$
\frac{d}{d\tau}\!\left[C_R\, e^{k_2\tau}\right] = k_1 C_{A0}\, e^{(k_2-k_1)\tau}
$$

**Step 5.** Integrate both sides from 0 to $\tau$ (with $C_R(0)=0$):

$$
C_R(\tau)\, e^{k_2\tau} - 0 = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2-k_1}\!\left[e^{(k_2-k_1)\tau} - 1\right]
$$

**Step 6.** Multiply by $e^{-k_2\tau}$:

$$
\boxed{\,C_R(\tau) = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2 - k_1}\!\left(e^{-k_1 \tau} - e^{-k_2 \tau}\right)\,}
$$

**Step 7.** The S balance: by **overall carbon balance** $C_A + C_R + C_S = C_{A0}$, so:

$$
C_S(\tau) = C_{A0} - C_A - C_R = C_{A0}\!\left[1 + \frac{k_1 e^{-k_2 \tau} - k_2 e^{-k_1 \tau}}{k_2 - k_1}\right]
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimum Contact Time for Maximum R

**Step 1.** Differentiate the closed-form $C_R$ with respect to $\tau$:

$$
\frac{dC_R}{d\tau} = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2-k_1}\!\left(-k_1 e^{-k_1\tau} + k_2 e^{-k_2\tau}\right) = 0
$$

**Step 2.** Simplify the numerator:

$$
k_2 e^{-k_2\tau} = k_1 e^{-k_1\tau} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{e^{-k_2\tau}}{e^{-k_1\tau}} = \frac{k_1}{k_2}
$$

**Step 3.** Take natural log of both sides:

$$
-(k_2-k_1)\tau_{\text{opt}} = \ln\!\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right) = -\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)
$$

**Step 4.** Solve:

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2-k_1}\,}
$$

**Step 5.** Substitute back to obtain $C_{R,\max}$:

$$
\boxed{\,C_{R,\max} = C_{A0}\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\!k_2/(k_1-k_2)}\,}
$$

**Validity Rule:** $C_{R,\max}$ is finite and meaningful **only if** $k_2 > k_1$ (R decomposes faster than it is formed). If $k_2 \le k_1$, $C_R$ is monotonically non-decreasing, so $\tau_{\text{opt}} = 0$ and $Y_R \to 1$ (100 % yield).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — CSTR Algebraic Solution

**Step 1.** Steady-state mole balance on A:

$$
F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{A0} - C_A - k_1 C_A \tau = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow C_A = \frac{C_{A0}}{1+k_1\tau}
$$

**Step 2.** Steady-state mole balance on R:

$$
0 - 0 + r_R V = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -k_1 C_A V + k_2 C_R V - F_R + F_{R0} = 0
$$

With $C_{R0}=0$ and $F_R = C_R v$:

$$
C_R = \frac{k_1 C_A \tau}{1+k_2\tau} = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}\tau}{(1+k_1\tau)(1+k_2\tau)}
$$

**Step 3.** Mass balance on S (or use total-balance shortcut):

$$
C_S = C_{A0} - C_A - C_R = \frac{k_1 k_2 C_{A0}\tau^2}{(1+k_1\tau)(1+k_2\tau)}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Recycle Reactor Equivalent Residence Time

**Step 1.** In a PFR with recycle ratio $\alpha$, the **total volumetric flow entering** the reactor is $F_0 + F_R = F_0(1+\alpha)$.

**Step 2.** The reactor volume is sized for this combined flow:

$$
\tau_{\text{reactor}} = \frac{V}{F_0+F_R} = \frac{V}{F_0(1+\alpha)}
$$

**Step 3.** The **fresh-feed-based space time** (used in design equations) is:

$$
\tau_{\text{design}} = \frac{V}{F_0} = (1+\alpha)\, \tau_{\text{reactor}}
$$

**Step 4.** For a first-order reaction $A \to \text{products}$, the conversion formula is **identical in form** to a PFR but with the design $\tau$:

$$
X_A = 1 - e^{-k_1 \tau_{\text{design}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial:** Although the conversion formula looks PFR-like, **each fluid element actually experiences a different trajectory** due to multiple recycles. With $\alpha \to \infty$, the system approaches a **CSTR**, not a PFR. This is a subtle but exam-important distinction.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Self-Study)

**Given:** $C_{A0} = 2$ mol·L⁻¹, $k_1 = 0.5$ min⁻¹, $k_2 = 0.2$ min⁻¹. Find $C_{R,\max}$ in a PFR and the corresponding $\tau_{\text{opt}}$.

**Solution:**

Compute $\tau_{\text{opt}}$:

$$
\tau_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln(0.2/0.5)}{0.2 - 0.5} = \frac{\ln(0.4)}{-0.3} = \frac{-0.9163}{-0.3} = 3.054 \text{ min}
$$

Compute $C_{R,\max}$:

$$
C_{R,\max} = 2 \times \left(\frac{0.2}{0.5}\right)^{0.2/(0.5-0.2)} = 2 \times (0.4)^{0.2/0.3} = 2 \times (0.4)^{0.6667}
$$

$$
C_{R,\max} = 2 \times 0.5430 = 1.086 \text{ mol·L}^{-1}
$$

**Yield:** $Y_R = 1.086 / 2 = 54.3\%$.

### 3.6 Computational Setup — Excel / MATLAB / SCILAB Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Excel Solver Approach:** (i) Create cells for $k_1$, $k_2$, $C_{A0}$. (ii) Build a column of $\tau$ values. (iii) Use the closed-form formulas in adjacent columns for $C_A$, $C_R$, $C_S$. (iv) Use **Goal Seek** or **Solver** to find $\tau_{\text{opt}}$ where $C_R$ is maximum. (v) For CSTR vs PFR comparison, plot $C_R$ vs $\tau$ on the same axes.

> [!NOTE]
> **MATLAB / SCILAB Approach:** Define an ODE function $f(y,\tau) = [-k_1 y(1);\, k_1 y(1) - k_2 y(2);\, k_2 y(2)]$. Call `ode45` (MATLAB) or `ode` (SCILAB) over $\tau = [0, 50]$ min. Plot $y(:,2)$ for $C_R(\tau)$. For CSTR, evaluate the algebraic closed-form expression directly.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reaction Pathway Diagram — Series Reaction with Selectivity Outcomes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFA[FRESH FEED STREAM A] --> nodeMIX[MIXING POINT]
    nodeRCY[RECYCLE STREAM EXIT R] --> nodeMIX
    nodeMIX --> nodePFR[PLUG FLOW REACTOR PFR]
    nodePFR --> nodeSPL[EXIT STREAM SPLITTER]
    nodeSPL --> nodePROD[PRODUCT R SEPARATION]
    nodeSPL --> nodeRCY
    nodePROD --> nodeSIDE[SIDE REACTION k2 BRANCH]
    nodeSIDE --> nodeWS[WASTE PRODUCT S]
    classDef feed fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1e88e5,color:#000
    classDef react fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#e76f51,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    classDef waste fill:#ffadad,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000
    class nodeFA feed
    class nodePFR react
    class nodePROD prod
    class nodeWS waste
```

### 4.2 Reactor Comparison Flowchart — Decision Logic for Series Reactions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START GOAL MAXIMIZE INTERMEDIATE R] --> nodeB{IS k2 LARGER THAN k1}
    nodeB -- YES R DECAYS FAST --> nodeC{WHAT REACTOR GIVES SHORT CONTACT TIME}
    nodeB -- NO R STABLE --> nodeD[ANY IDEAL REACTOR SUITS USE PFR FOR SIMPLICITY]
    nodeC --> nodeE[BATCH REACTOR WITH tau EQUAL TO tau opt]
    nodeC --> nodeF[PLUG FLOW REACTOR PFR WITH tau opt]
    nodeC --> nodeG[MULTIPLE CSTR IN SERIES GETS CLOSER TO PFR]
    nodeC --> nodeH{IS AUTOTHERMAL OR STRONG EXOTHERM}
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeI[RECYCLE REACTOR WITH alpha HIGH FOR BACKMIXING AND TEMPERATURE CONTROL]
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeF
    classDef start fill:#e0c3fc,stroke:#7b2cbf,color:#000
    classDef decide fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#e09f3e,color:#000
    classDef react fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    class nodeA start
    class nodeB nodeH decide
    class nodeF nodeI react
```

### 4.3 Recycle Reactor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF0[FRESH FEED F0] --> nodeMJ[MIXING JUNCTION]
    nodeFR[RECYCLE STREAM FR] --> nodeMJ
    nodeMJ --> nodeIN[INLET TO PFR V]
    nodeIN --> nodePFRV[IDEAL PFR VOLUME V]
    nodePFRV --> nodeOUT[OUTLET STREAM]
    nodeOUT --> nodeSEP[SEPARATOR KNOWN PRODUCT]
    nodeSEP --> nodeFIN[FINAL PRODUCT R PURIFIED]
    nodeSEP --> nodePURGE[PURGE STREAM TO AVOID INERT BUILDUP]
    nodeOUT --> nodeFR
    classDef feed fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1e88e5,color:#000
    classDef rcy fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#e76f51,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    class nodeF0 feed
    class nodeFR nodePFRV rcy
    class nodeFIN out
```

### 4.4 Concentration-vs-Time Profile Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0[TIME ZERO] --> nodeA[C_A MAX = C_A0]
    nodeA --> nodeDROP[C_A DECAYS EXPONENTIALLY]
    nodeDROP --> nodeR[C_R RISES TO PEAK THEN FALLS]
    nodeR --> nodeS[C_S MONOTONICALLY RISES]
    nodeS --> nodeEND[TIME INFINITY C_S APPROACHES C_A0]
    classDef peak fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    classDef drop fill:#ffadad,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000
    class nodeR peak
    class nodeDROP nodeS drop
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*For the series reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$, derive the condition for maximum concentration of R in a PFR.*

**Model Answer:**

Differentiate $C_R(\tau) = \dfrac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2-k_1}\!\left(e^{-k_1\tau} - e^{-k_2\tau}\right)$ with respect to $\tau$ and set $\dfrac{dC_R}{d\tau}=0$:

$$
-k_1 e^{-k_1\tau} + k_2 e^{-k_2\tau} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \tau_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2-k_1}
$$

The maximum concentration is $C_{R,\max} = C_{A0}\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{k_2/(k_1-k_2)}$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Compare qualitatively the selectivity of R in a single CSTR vs a PFR for the series reaction $A \to R \to S$ with $k_2 > k_1$. Justify with one sentence.*

**Model Answer:**

A **PFR** gives higher selectivity to R because every fluid element experiences the same short residence time $\tau_{\text{opt}}$, while in a **CSTR**, fluid elements have an exponential distribution of residence times and many molecules of R remain long enough to be converted to S. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign errors in $k_2 - k_1$** — students often flip numerator and denominator; verify with the limit $k_2 \gg k_1$ (then $C_{R,\max} \to 0$, no R accumulates).
> 2. **Confusing residence time and space time** in recycle reactors — clarify whether $\tau$ refers to **fresh feed basis** or **actual flow basis**.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply total mole balance** check $C_A + C_R + C_S = C_{A0}$ at the end — KTU examiners award marks for this verification.
> 4. **Units of rate constants** — must be consistent (s⁻¹, min⁻¹, or h⁻¹); mismatched units lose 1 mark per KTU guidelines.
> 5. **Recycle limit confusion** — $\alpha \to \infty$ does NOT give a PFR; it gives a CSTR.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

The liquid-phase series reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$ takes place in a PFR. Given $C_{A0} = 1.5$ mol·L⁻¹, $k_1 = 0.4$ min⁻¹, $k_2 = 0.1$ min⁻¹.

**(a)** Derive the closed-form expression for $C_R(\tau)$ in the PFR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine (i) $\tau_{\text{opt}}$ for maximum R, (ii) $C_{R,\max}$, (iii) the corresponding yield and selectivity of R. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Material balance on differential element:

$$
\frac{dC_A}{d\tau} = -k_1 C_A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_A = C_{A0} e^{-k_1 \tau} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

Material balance on R:

$$
\frac{dC_R}{d\tau} + k_2 C_R = k_1 C_{A0} e^{-k_1\tau} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

Using integrating factor $e^{k_2 \tau}$ and IC $C_R(0)=0$:

$$
C_R(\tau) = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1\tau} - e^{-k_2\tau}\right) \quad \text{(4 marks)}
$$

**(b)** (i) Optimum residence time:

$$
\tau_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln(0.1/0.4)}{0.1-0.4} = \frac{\ln(0.25)}{-0.3} = \frac{-1.3863}{-0.3} = 4.621 \text{ min} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

(ii) Maximum R:

$$
C_{R,\max} = 1.5 \times \left(\frac{0.1}{0.4}\right)^{0.1/(0.4-0.1)} = 1.5 \times (0.25)^{0.3333} = 1.5 \times 0.6300 = 0.945 \text{ mol·L}^{-1} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

(iii) Yield:

$$
Y_R = \frac{0.945}{1.5} = 63.0\%
$$

Selectivity:

$$
\Phi_R = \frac{C_R}{C_{A0}-C_A} = \frac{0.945}{1.5 - C_A(\tau_{\text{opt}})}
$$

$C_A(4.621) = 1.5\, e^{-0.4 \times 4.621} = 1.5 \times 0.1577 = 0.2366$ mol·L⁻¹.

$$
\Phi_R = \frac{0.945}{1.5 - 0.2366} = \frac{0.945}{1.2634} = 74.8\% \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary June 2024]**

The series reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$ is conducted in a CSTR. Given $C_{A0} = 2.0$ mol·L⁻¹, $k_1 = 0.3$ min⁻¹, $k_2 = 0.5$ min⁻¹.

**(a)** Write the steady-state mole balances for A, R, and S in the CSTR and derive $C_A$, $C_R$, $C_S$ as functions of $\tau$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the reaction is now carried out in a recycle reactor (PFR + recycle ratio $\alpha = 4$) with reactor volume giving $\tau_{\text{reactor}} = 2$ min based on combined flow, compute the conversion of A and qualitatively comment on whether the recycle reactor gives more or less R than the CSTR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Steady-state balance on A:

$$
F_{A0} - F_A - k_1 C_A V = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_A = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + k_1\tau} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

Balance on R:

$$
0 - 0 + (k_1 C_A - k_2 C_R)V = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_R = \frac{k_1 C_A \tau}{1+k_2\tau} = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}\tau}{(1+k_1\tau)(1+k_2\tau)} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

Total balance:

$$
C_S = C_{A0} - C_A - C_R = \frac{k_1 k_2 C_{A0}\tau^2}{(1+k_1\tau)(1+k_2\tau)} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(b)** Fresh-feed-based space time:

$$
\tau_{\text{design}} = (1+\alpha)\,\tau_{\text{reactor}} = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ min} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

First-order PFR conversion:

$$
X_A = 1 - e^{-k_1 \tau_{\text{design}}} = 1 - e^{-0.3 \times 10} = 1 - 0.0498 = 0.950 = 95.0\% \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Qualitative comment:** The recycle reactor at $\alpha = 4$ is approaching CSTR-like behavior but still retains partial plug-flow character. However, since $k_2 > k_1$ (R decomposes faster than it forms), even partial back-mixing favors destruction of R. So the recycle reactor will yield **less R** than a pure PFR operating at $\tau_{\text{design}}$, but conversion is high. A pure PFR without recycle would still be preferable for R selectivity. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Series Reactions & Recycle):**

- ✅ Series reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$ — R is the desired intermediate.
- ✅ **PFR/Batch** closed form: $C_R = \dfrac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2-k_1}\left(e^{-k_1\tau} - e^{-k_2\tau}\right)$.
- ✅ **CSTR** closed form: $C_R = \dfrac{k_1 C_{A0}\tau}{(1+k_1\tau)(1+k_2\tau)}$.
- ✅ **Optimum PFR contact time:** $\tau_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}$.
- ✅ **Maximum R concentration (PFR):** $C_{R,\max} = C_{A0}\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{k_2/(k_1-k_2)}$.
- ✅ CSTR **always gives lower $C_R$** than PFR when $k_2 > k_1$ (selectivity argument).
- ✅ For **selectivity optimization**: short contact time, PFR > CSTR.
- ✅ **Recycle reactor** $\alpha \to 0$ acts as PFR; $\alpha \to \infty$ acts as CSTR.
- ✅ **Design space time** for recycle: $\tau_{\text{design}} = (1+\alpha)\,\tau_{\text{reactor}}$.
- ✅ **Computational tools** (Excel / MATLAB / SCILAB / Python): use closed-form formulas (CSTR) or ODE solver `ode45` (PFR / Batch) for $C_R(\tau)$.
- ✅ **Verification rule:** always check $C_A + C_R + C_S = C_{A0}$ at the end.
- ✅ **Units of $k_1, k_2$** must be consistent with $\tau$ — common pitfall.
- ✅ For $k_2 \le k_1$, no finite $\tau_{\text{opt}}$ exists; $Y_R \to 100\%$ at large $\tau$.
- ✅ Recycle reactors are favored for **autothermal / highly exothermic** systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature and Pressure Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temperature and Pressure Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Temperature and pressure effects** in Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE) describe the quantitative and qualitative influence of these two critical thermodynamic state variables on the **rate of reaction** ($r_A$), the **rate constant** ($k$), the **equilibrium constant** ($K$), the **conversion** ($X_A$), and the **selectivity** of complex reacting systems. They are central to reactor design, optimization, and control in heterogeneous and homogeneous reaction systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Anchor Concept:**
> - **Temperature** primarily affects the **rate constant ($k$)** via the **Arrhenius equation**.
> - **Temperature** affects the **equilibrium constant ($K$)** via the **van't Hoff equation**.
> - **Pressure** primarily affects the **concentration of gaseous reactants** and hence the rate of gas-phase reactions.
> - The interplay between $k(T)$ and $K(T)$ defines the **optimum temperature** for exothermic reversible reactions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cooking Pressure Cooker Analogy:**
> Imagine you are cooking rice at home. If you **increase the temperature** (heat), the rice cooks faster because molecules vibrate more vigorously and collide with higher energy. If you **increase the pressure** (by sealing the pot), the steam pushes water molecules closer together, increasing their concentration and forcing them to react/cook faster. This is precisely why chemical engineers study these two variables — *temperature changes the intrinsic molecular activity, while pressure changes the molecular crowding*. For an exothermic reversible reaction (like $A \rightleftharpoons B$, where forward reaction releases heat), increasing temperature speeds up forward reaction **but also shifts equilibrium backward**. A balance point exists — the **optimum temperature** — where net rate of product formation is maximum.

## 1.3 Standard Constants and Metrics (Bolded)

- **Universal Gas Constant ($R$)**: **$8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$** or **$1.987 \text{ cal/(mol·K)}$**.
- **Standard Reference Temperature ($T_{ref}$)**: Typically **$298.15 \text{ K}$**.
- **Standard Pressure ($P^0$)**: **$1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**.
- **Activation Energy ($E$)**: Typically in **kJ/mol**.
- **Heat of Reaction ($-\Delta H_r$)**: Positive for endothermic, negative for exothermic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effect of Temperature on Rate Constant — The Arrhenius Law

The **Arrhenius equation** is the single most important temperature-rate relationship in CRE. It was empirically proposed by **Svante Arrhenius (1889)** and later derived from collision theory and transition state theory.

### 2.1.1 Forms of the Arrhenius Equation

**Form 1 — Exponential form:**

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E / RT}$$

**Form 2 — Two-temperature evaluation form:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = -\frac{E}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right) = \frac{E}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

**Form 3 — Differentiated form (Temperature Sensitivity):**

$$\frac{d(\ln k)}{dT} = \frac{E}{RT^2}$$

**Form 4 — Arrhenius plot (Linear form):**

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

This gives a straight line when $\ln k$ is plotted vs. $1/T$, with **slope = $-E/R$** and **intercept = $\ln A$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of Each Term:**
> - $A$ = **Pre-exponential factor (Frequency factor)** — units match those of $k$; represents the frequency of molecular collisions with the correct orientation.
> - $E$ = **Activation Energy (J/mol)** — the minimum energy threshold that reacting molecules must possess to react upon collision.
> - $e^{-E/RT}$ = **Boltzmann factor** — the fraction of molecules possessing energy $\geq E$ at temperature $T$.

### 2.1.2 Classification of Reactions Based on Temperature Sensitivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Reactions are classified by the magnitude of $E$, which determines the **temperature sensitivity**:
>
> | Reaction Type | Activation Energy $E$ | Temperature Sensitivity | Example |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Unimolecular** | $E < 80 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Low | Decomposition, Isomerization |
> | **Biomolecular** | $80 \leq E \leq 200 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Moderate | $A + B \rightarrow P$ |
> | **Trimolecular** | $E > 200 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | High | Termolecular recombinations |
>
> A **rule of thumb** in industry: *For every 10 °C rise, reaction rate approximately doubles* (valid for $E \approx 50 \text{ kJ/mol}$ near 300 K).

### 2.1.3 Non-Arrhenius Behavior (Advanced Insight)

Some reactions show **deviations** from the linear Arrhenius plot:
- **Curved Arrhenius plots** — common in complex reactions, enzyme catalysis, and diffusion-limited reactions.
- **Negative activation energy** — observed in reactions with a pre-equilibrium (e.g., $A + B \rightleftharpoons I \rightarrow P$, where $K$ decreases sharply with $T$).
- **Modified Arrhenius / Kooij equation**: $k = A \cdot T^n \cdot e^{-E/RT}$, where $n$ is an empirical exponent.

## 2.2 Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant — The van't Hoff Equation

The **van't Hoff equation** relates the equilibrium constant $K$ to temperature:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{RT^2}$$

**Integrated form (assuming $\Delta H^0$ is constant over the temperature range):**

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_2}{K_1} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^0}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Le Chatelier's Principle in Equation Form:**
> - For **endothermic** reactions ($\Delta H^0 > 0$): Increasing $T$ **increases** $K$ (shifts equilibrium forward).
> - For **exothermic** reactions ($\Delta H^0 < 0$): Increasing $T$ **decreases** $K$ (shifts equilibrium backward).

## 2.3 Effect of Pressure on Reaction Rate

For **gas-phase reactions**, pressure alters concentration through the **ideal gas law**:

$$C_i = \frac{y_i \cdot P}{RT} = \frac{P_i}{RT}$$

Therefore, the rate equation transforms as:

$$r_A = k \cdot C_A^{\alpha} \cdot C_B^{\beta} = k \left( \frac{y_A P}{RT} \right)^{\alpha} \left( \frac{y_B P}{RT} \right)^{\beta}$$

Grouping all $y_i$, $R$, $T$ constants into $k'$:

$$r_A = k' \cdot P^{(\alpha + \beta)} = k' \cdot P^{n}$$

where $n = \alpha + \beta$ is the **overall order of reaction** with respect to total pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Pressure has **no effect** on liquid-phase reactions (where concentrations are essentially incompressible) and on the rate constant $k$ itself. Pressure only modifies the **concentration** of gaseous species.

## 2.4 Optimum Temperature Progression (Exothermic Reversible Reactions)

For an **exothermic reversible reaction** $A \rightleftharpoons B$ with:
- Forward rate: $r_1 = k_1 C_A$
- Reverse rate: $r_2 = k_2 C_B$
- Net rate: $r_{net} = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_B$

Both $k_1$ and $k_2$ increase with $T$ (Arrhenius), but $k_1/k_2 = K$ **decreases** with $T$ (van't Hoff, exothermic). Hence, a temperature exists where $r_{net}$ is maximum — the **optimum temperature ($T_{opt}$)**.

$$T_{opt} = \frac{T_1 \cdot T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \cdot \ln \left[ \frac{k_1(T_1) \cdot E_1}{k_2(T_2) \cdot E_2} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an **adiabatic PFR** for exothermic reactions, temperature rises along the reactor length, so the **optimum temperature progression (OTP)** profile must be followed to maintain maximum rate. A typical OTP curve looks like a hyperbolic decay from high to low temperature as conversion increases.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Temperature & Pressure Effects**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arrhenius (exponential) | $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ | $A$: freq. factor; $E$: J/mol; $R$: J/(mol·K); $T$: K | Find $k$ at temperature $T$ |
| 2 | Arrhenius (two-T) | $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E/R)[(1/T_1)-(1/T_2)]$ | Same as above | Find $k_2$ given $k_1$ at $T_1$ |
| 3 | Arrhenius (linear) | $\ln k = \ln A - (E/R)(1/T)$ | — | Graphical $E$ and $A$ from data |
| 4 | van't Hoff (differential) | $d(\ln K)/dT = \Delta H^0/(RT^2)$ | $\Delta H^0$: J/mol | Local sensitivity of $K$ |
| 5 | van't Hoff (integrated) | $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta H^0/R)[(1/T_2)-(1/T_1)]$ | Same | Find $K_2$ given $K_1$ |
| 6 | Ideal gas concentration | $C_i = y_i P / (RT)$ | $C_i$: mol/m³; $P$: Pa | Concentration from partial pressure |
| 7 | Pressure rate law | $r_A = k' P^n$ | $n = \sum \nu_i$ | Total-pressure effect on rate |
| 8 | Optimum temperature | $T_{opt}$ from $dr_{net}/dT = 0$ | K | Design of reversible exothermic reactors |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Arrhenius Equation from Collision Theory (Exhaustive)

**Objective:** Derive $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ starting from the kinetic theory of gases.

### Step 1 — Define the rate constant via collision frequency

According to **collision theory**, the rate constant for a bimolecular reaction is:

$$k = Z_{AB} \cdot p \cdot e^{-E/RT}$$

where $Z_{AB}$ is the collision frequency per unit volume, and $p$ is the **steric (orientation) factor**.

### Step 2 — Express the collision frequency $Z_{AB}$

For two spherical molecules $A$ and $B$ with masses $m_A$, $m_B$ and diameters $d_A$, $d_B$:

$$Z_{AB} = N_A N_B \sigma_{AB} \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu_{AB}}}$$

where:
- $N_A$, $N_B$ = number densities (molecules/volume)
- $\sigma_{AB} = \pi \left( \frac{d_A + d_B}{2} \right)^2$ = collision cross-section
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $\mu_{AB} = m_A m_B / (m_A + m_B)$ = reduced mass

### Step 3 — Identify temperature-dependent terms

The square-root term in $Z_{AB}$ depends on $\sqrt{T}$, while the Boltzmann factor depends on $e^{-E/RT}$. For most engineering ranges, the $\sqrt{T}$ dependence is weak compared to the exponential, so it is grouped into the pre-exponential factor $A$:

$$\sqrt{T} \cdot e^{-E/RT} \quad \longrightarrow \quad e^{-E/RT} \quad \text{(dominant term)}$$

### Step 4 — Define the pre-exponential factor

Combining all weakly temperature-dependent terms (collision frequency, steric factor, geometric considerations) into a single constant:

$$A = p \cdot Z_{AB}^{\text{baseline}} \cdot (\text{geometry factors})$$

### Step 5 — Final Arrhenius form

$$\boxed{k = A \cdot e^{-E/RT}}$$

**Conclusion:** The exponential temperature dependence dominates; the $T^{1/2}$ (or $T^n$) correction is often absorbed into $A$ for engineering purposes, but can be made explicit using the **Kooij modification** $k = A T^n e^{-E/RT}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Temperature Arrhenius Form (Universally Tested)

**Given:** $k_1$ at $T_1$ and $k_2$ at $T_2$, find $E$ or unknown $k_2$.

**Starting equations:**

$$k_1 = A e^{-E / (R T_1)} \quad \quad (i)$$

$$k_2 = A e^{-E / (R T_2)} \quad \quad (ii)$$

**Step 1 — Take the natural logarithm of each equation:**

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \frac{E}{R T_1}$$

$$\ln k_2 = \ln A - \frac{E}{R T_2}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract (i) from (ii) to eliminate $\ln A$:**

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E}{R T_2} + \frac{E}{R T_1}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using logarithm property $\ln(a) - \ln(b) = \ln(a/b)$:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \frac{E}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for $E$:**

$$E = R \cdot \ln \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right) \cdot \left[ \frac{T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (Engineering Intuition):**
> - If $T_2 > T_1$, then $1/T_1 > 1/T_2$, so $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2) > 0$, and for $k_2 > k_1$, $\ln(k_2/k_1) > 0$. Both sides positive — consistent. ✓
> - If $E$ is very large, the rate is **highly temperature sensitive** — small $T$ change → large $k$ change. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Derivation of the van't Hoff Equation

**Starting point — Fundamental thermodynamic relation:**

$$\Delta G^0 = -RT \ln K$$

**Step 1 — Gibbs–Helmholtz equation:**

$$\left[ \frac{\partial (\Delta G^0 / T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H^0}{T^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta G^0 = -RT \ln K$ into the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation:**

$$\left[ \frac{\partial (-R \ln K)}{\partial T} \right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H^0}{T^2}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the left side with respect to $T$:**

$$-R \cdot \frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^0}{T^2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $-1$ and divide by $R$:**

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{RT^2}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate assuming $\Delta H^0$ is constant:**

$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d(\ln K) = \frac{\Delta H^0}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate both integrals:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_2}{K_1} \right) = \frac{\Delta H^0}{R} \left[ -\frac{1}{T} \right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\ln \left( \frac{K_2}{K_1} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^0}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pressure Effect on Gas-Phase Reaction Rate

**Consider:** $A + B \rightarrow P$ (gas phase, elementary, second-order overall)

**Step 1 — Standard rate law in terms of concentrations:**

$$r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal gas law for each species:**

$$C_A = \frac{P_A}{RT} = \frac{y_A \cdot P_{total}}{RT} \quad ; \quad C_B = \frac{y_B \cdot P_{total}}{RT}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the rate equation:**

$$r_A = k \cdot \left( \frac{y_A P}{RT} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{y_B P}{RT} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to group the pressure terms:**

$$r_A = \frac{k \cdot y_A \cdot y_B}{(RT)^2} \cdot P^2$$

**Step 5 — Define a new rate constant in terms of pressure:**

$$k' = \frac{k \cdot y_A \cdot y_B}{(RT)^2}$$

**Step 6 — Final pressure-rate law:**

$$r_A = k' \cdot P^n$$

where $n = 2$ for this second-order reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalization:** For $aA + bB \rightarrow P$, the apparent order with respect to total pressure is $n = a + b$ (sum of stoichiometric coefficients for elementary reactions).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Optimum Temperature for Reversible Exothermic Reactions

**Reaction:** $A \rightleftharpoons B$, exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$)
**Rate equations:** $r_1 = k_1 C_A$ (forward), $r_2 = k_2 C_B$ (reverse)

**Step 1 — Net rate of formation of $B$:**

$$r_B = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_B$$

**Step 2 — Apply Arrhenius to both $k_1$ and $k_2$:**

$$k_1 = A_1 e^{-E_1/RT} \quad ; \quad k_2 = A_2 e^{-E_2/RT}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $r_B$ with respect to $T$ and set to zero for maximum:**

$$\frac{d r_B}{dT} = \frac{d k_1}{dT} C_A - \frac{d k_2}{dT} C_B = 0$$

**Step 4 — Using $dk/dT = k \cdot (E/RT^2)$:**

$$k_1 C_A \cdot \frac{E_1}{RT^2} = k_2 C_B \cdot \frac{E_2}{RT^2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify and solve for $C_A/C_B$ at optimum:**

$$\frac{C_B}{C_A} = \frac{k_1 E_1}{k_2 E_2}$$

**Step 6 — This is the equilibrium-like condition giving $T_{opt}$ implicitly.** For adiabatic operation with feed $C_{A0}$, the OTP is obtained by coupling:

$$T_{opt}(X_A) = T_0 + \frac{(-\Delta H_{rxn})}{\sum \Theta_i C_{p,i}} \cdot X_A \quad \text{(adiabatic)}$$

where $\Theta_i = F_{i0} / F_{A0}$.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):**
> The first-order rate constant for the decomposition of $N_2O_5$ is $k_1 = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 318 \text{ K}$ and $k_2 = 1.4 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 338 \text{ K}$. Find the activation energy $E$ and the pre-exponential factor $A$. What is $k$ at $350 \text{ K}$?

**Solution — Step 1: Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius formula:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \frac{E}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $k_2/k_1$:**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^{-2}}{4.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 31.111$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\ln(31.111)$:**

$$\ln(31.111) = 3.4377$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{318} - \frac{1}{338} = 0.003145 - 0.002959 = 1.86 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E$:**

$$E = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = \frac{8.314 \times 3.4377}{1.86 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$E = \frac{28.580}{1.86 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.5365 \times 10^5 \text{ J/mol} = 153.65 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 7 — Find $A$ using $k_1$ at $T_1$:**

$$A = k_1 \cdot e^{E / (R T_1)} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot e^{153650 / (8.314 \times 318)}$$

**Step 8 — Compute $E/(R T_1)$:**

$$\frac{E}{R T_1} = \frac{153650}{8.314 \times 318} = \frac{153650}{2643.85} = 58.12$$

**Step 9 — Compute $A$:**

$$A = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot e^{58.12} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 2.65 \times 10^{25} = 1.19 \times 10^{22} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 10 — Find $k$ at $T = 350 \text{ K}$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{350} = 0.003145 - 0.002857 = 2.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{k}{k_1} \right) = \frac{E}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{350} \right) = \frac{153650}{8.314} \times 2.88 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{k}{k_1} \right) = 18481 \times 2.88 \times 10^{-4} = 5.323$$

$$\frac{k}{k_1} = e^{5.323} = 205.4$$

$$k = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 205.4 = 9.24 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Answer:** $E = 153.65 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $A = 1.19 \times 10^{22} \text{ s}^{-1}$, $k(350 \text{ K}) = 0.0924 \text{ s}^{-1}$.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Reaction Behavior Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight Table:**

| Reaction Type | $\Delta H$ | Effect of $\uparrow T$ on $K$ | Effect of $\uparrow P$ on $r$ | Optimum $T$? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Irreversible endothermic | $> 0$ | N/A ($K = \infty$) | Yes (gases) | None (max $T$ preferred) |
| Irreversible exothermic | $< 0$ | N/A | Yes (gases) | None (intermediate $T$) |
| Reversible endothermic | $> 0$ | Increases | Yes (gases) | None (high $T$ preferred) |
| Reversible exothermic | $< 0$ | Decreases | Yes (gases) | **YES — Critical** |
| Reversible with $\Delta n \neq 0$ | Any | As above | Strong (shifts $K$) | Combined $T, P$ optima |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Temperature Effect Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactor Operating Conditions] --> B{Temperature Changed?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Arrhenius Equation Applies]
    C --> D[k = A exp(-E/RT)]
    D --> E{Reaction Reversible?}
    E -- No --> F[Rate Increases Monotonically with T]
    E -- Yes --> G{Exothermic dH less than 0?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Optimum Temperature Exists Topt]
    G -- No --> I[Higher T always beneficial]
    H --> J[OTP Profile in PFR]
    I --> J
    B -- No --> K[Pressure Changed?]
    K -- Yes --> L[Gas Phase Reaction?]
    L -- Yes --> M[Concentration Changes C = yP/RT]
    L -- No --> N[No Effect on Liquid Phase]
    M --> O[Rate Law: r = k prime P to power n]
    O --> P{Reaction Reversible?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Pressure Shifts Equilibrium K]
    P -- No --> R[Rate increases with P]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Arrhenius Plot Construction

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Experimental Data T vs k] --> B[Compute 1/T and ln k]
    B --> C[Plot ln k vs 1/T]
    C --> D{Slope Linear?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Slope equals negative E over R]
    D -- No --> F[Non-Arrhenius Behavior]
    E --> G[Intercept equals ln A]
    E --> H[E equals slope times negative R]
    G --> I[Compute k at any new T]
    H --> I
    F --> J[Use Modified Arrhenius: k = A T^n exp negative E over RT]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Pressure Effect on Gas-Phase Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas-Phase Reaction: A plus B goes to P] --> B[Write Rate Law in terms of C]
    B --> C[r equals k times C_A to power a times C_B to power b]
    C --> D[Apply Ideal Gas: C_i equals y_i P over RT]
    D --> E[Substitute: r equals k prime times P to power n]
    E --> F[n equals a plus b = overall order]
    F --> G{Doubling P?}
    G -- n equals 1 --> H[Rate Doubles]
    G -- n equals 2 --> I[Rate Quadruples]
    G -- n equals 0 --> J[Rate Unchanged]
    G -- n less than 0 --> K[Rate Decreases]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture for Reactor Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Parameters]
        A1[Feed Composition F_A0]
        A2[Inlet Temperature T_0]
        A3[Operating Pressure P]
    end

    subgraph ReactionKinetics[Reaction Kinetics Module]
        B1[Arrhenius: k = A exp negative E over RT]
        B2[van't Hoff: K = K_1 exp negative dH over R times 1 over T minus 1 over T_1]
        B3[Pressure Effect: r = k prime P to power n]
    end

    subgraph ReactorModel[Reactor Model]
        C1[PFR Design Equation]
        C2[CSTR Design Equation]
        C3[Heat Balance: Adiabatic vs Isothermal]
    end

    subgraph OptimizationLayer[Optimization Layer]
        D1[Optimum Temperature Topt]
        D2[Optimum Pressure Popt]
        D3[Optimum Temperature Progression OTP]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Performance]
        E1[Conversion X_A]
        E2[Selectivity S]
        E3[Yield Y]
    end

    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D3
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
    E1 --> E3
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP) Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet: T_high X_A equals 0] --> B[Maximum Rate at Inlet]
    B --> C[As X_A increases, T must decrease]
    C --> D[Follow OTP curve for max rate]
    D --> E[Exit: T_low X_A equals final]
    E --> F{Adiabatic Operation?}
    F -- Yes --> G[OTP coincides with adiabatic T rise curve]
    F -- No --> H[External heat exchange maintains OTP]
    G --> I[Maximum Conversion Achieved]
    H --> I
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State and explain the Arrhenius equation. Discuss how the activation energy is obtained graphically.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Arrhenius equation is given by:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E/RT}$$

where $k$ is the rate constant, $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E$ is activation energy, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**Graphical method (2 marks):** Taking natural log:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

A plot of $\ln k$ versus $1/T$ yields a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** = $-E/R$ → $E$ is computed as $E = -R \times \text{slope}$.
- **Intercept** = $\ln A$ → $A$ is computed as $A = e^{\text{intercept}}$.

The magnitude of the slope gives a direct measure of the temperature sensitivity of the reaction.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain with a suitable equation how temperature affects the equilibrium constant of a reversible reaction. What is the consequence for an exothermic reaction?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **van't Hoff equation** gives:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{RT^2}$$

Or in integrated form:

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_2}{K_1} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^0}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$

For an **exothermic reaction** ($\Delta H^0 < 0$), the right-hand side becomes **negative** when $T_2 > T_1$, which means $K_2 < K_1$. Hence, increasing temperature **decreases the equilibrium constant** and shifts equilibrium toward the **reactants** (Le Chatelier's principle).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style 2024]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the two-temperature form of the Arrhenius equation starting from $k = A e^{-E/RT}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The rate constant of a first-order reaction is $3.46 \times 10^{-3} \text{ min}^{-1}$ at $350 \text{ K}$ and $6.17 \times 10^{-2} \text{ min}^{-1}$ at $400 \text{ K}$. Calculate the activation energy $E$ and the pre-exponential factor $A$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Starting from the exponential Arrhenius equation:

$$k_1 = A e^{-E/(RT_1)} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$k_2 = A e^{-E/(RT_2)} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Divide equation (2) by equation (1):

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{A e^{-E/(RT_2)}}{A e^{-E/(RT_1)}}$$

The pre-exponential factor $A$ cancels:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{-E/(RT_2) + E/(RT_1)} = e^{(E/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \frac{E}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

This is the two-temperature form, which allows us to:
- Compute $E$ if $k_1, k_2, T_1, T_2$ are known.
- Compute $k_2$ if $E, k_1, T_1, T_2$ are known.

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Substitute into the two-temperature form.

$$\ln \left( \frac{6.17 \times 10^{-2}}{3.46 \times 10^{-3}} \right) = \frac{E}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{400} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio and its logarithm.

$$\frac{6.17 \times 10^{-2}}{3.46 \times 10^{-3}} = 17.832 \quad ; \quad \ln(17.832) = 2.880$$

**Step 3:** Compute the temperature term.

$$\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{400} = 0.002857 - 0.002500 = 3.571 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $E$.

$$E = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = \frac{8.314 \times 2.880}{3.571 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E = \frac{23.944}{3.571 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.704 \times 10^4 \text{ J/mol} = 67.04 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $A$ using $k_1$ and $T_1$.

$$A = k_1 \cdot e^{E/(RT_1)} = 3.46 \times 10^{-3} \cdot e^{67040 / (8.314 \times 350)}$$

$$\frac{E}{RT_1} = \frac{67040}{2909.9} = 23.04$$

$$A = 3.46 \times 10^{-3} \cdot e^{23.04} = 3.46 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.012 \times 10^{10} = 3.50 \times 10^7 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer:** $E = 67.04 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $A = 3.50 \times 10^7 \text{ min}^{-1}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style 2024]**
>
> **(a)** What is the effect of pressure on the rate of a gas-phase reaction? Derive the pressure-rate relationship for a second-order gas-phase reaction $A + B \rightarrow P$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The elementary gas-phase reaction $2A + B \rightarrow P$ is carried out at $600 \text{ K}$ and $2 \text{ atm}$ with a feed of $50\% A$, $50\% B$ (molar). The rate constant is $k = 0.05 \text{ L}^2 / (\text{mol}^2 \cdot \text{s})$. Calculate the initial rate of reaction. If the pressure is doubled to $4 \text{ atm}$, what is the new rate? **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Theory and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):** Pressure affects the rate of a gas-phase reaction by altering the **concentration** of the gaseous reactants. According to the ideal gas law, $C_i = y_i P / (RT)$. Hence, increasing $P$ increases the molar concentration of each species proportionally, accelerating the reaction. The **rate constant $k$ itself is not affected** by pressure; only the concentration-driven rate changes.

**Derivation (4 marks):** For the elementary reaction $A + B \rightarrow P$, the rate law in terms of concentration is:

$$r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B$$

Apply the ideal gas law:

$$C_A = \frac{y_A P}{RT} \quad , \quad C_B = \frac{y_B P}{RT}$$

Substitute:

$$r_A = k \cdot \left( \frac{y_A P}{RT} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{y_B P}{RT} \right) = \frac{k \cdot y_A \cdot y_B}{(RT)^2} \cdot P^2$$

Define:

$$k' = \frac{k \cdot y_A \cdot y_B}{(RT)^2}$$

Then:

$$\boxed{r_A = k' \cdot P^2}$$

For an elementary reaction, the apparent order with respect to total pressure equals the **sum of stoichiometric coefficients** ($1 + 1 = 2$).

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute initial concentrations using $C_i = y_i P / (RT)$.

For $P = 2 \text{ atm} = 2 \times 101325 = 202650 \text{ Pa}$, $T = 600 \text{ K}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$:

$$C_A = \frac{y_A P}{RT} = \frac{0.5 \times 202650}{8.314 \times 600} = \frac{101325}{4988.4} = 20.31 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

$$C_B = 20.31 \text{ mol/m}^3 \quad (\text{since } y_A = y_B = 0.5)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the rate law for third-order overall reaction $2A + B$.

$$r_A = k \cdot C_A^2 \cdot C_B$$

$$r_A = 0.05 \times (20.31)^2 \times 20.31 = 0.05 \times 8380.3 = 419.0 \text{ mol/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}$$

**Step 3:** Find rate at $P = 4 \text{ atm}$ (doubled).

Concentrations double when $P$ doubles (at constant $T$):

$$C_A' = C_B' = 2 \times 20.31 = 40.62 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Apply the rate law again.

$$r_A' = k \cdot (C_A')^2 \cdot C_B' = 0.05 \times (40.62)^2 \times 40.62$$

$$r_A' = 0.05 \times 67035 = 3351.8 \text{ mol/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the ratio $r_A'/r_A$.

$$\frac{r_A'}{r_A} = \frac{3351.8}{419.0} = 8.00$$

**Step 6:** Verify using pressure-rate proportionality.

For a third-order reaction, $r \propto P^3$:

$$\frac{r_A'}{r_A} = \left( \frac{P'}{P} \right)^3 = 2^3 = 8 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** Initial rate = **419 mol/(m³·s)**; New rate at 4 atm = **3351.8 mol/(m³·s)**; Rate increases **8-fold** when pressure is doubled.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $E$:** If $R$ is taken in cal/(mol·K), $E$ comes out in cal/mol — must convert to kJ/mol for final answer.
> 2. **Temperature in °C instead of K:** Always convert to Kelvin before substituting into Arrhenius or van't Hoff.
> 3. **Forgetting the natural log:** Some students write $k_2/k_1$ instead of $\ln(k_2/k_1)$ — this is a **fatal error** and costs full marks.
> 4. **Sign errors in van't Hoff:** For exothermic reactions ($\Delta H < 0$), increasing $T$ gives a negative $\ln(K_2/K_1)$, meaning $K_2 < K_1$. Mix-up of signs is the most common mistake.
> 5. **Pressure effect confused with $k$ change:** Pressure does NOT change the rate constant $k$; it only changes concentrations. Many students incorrectly state that "pressure affects $k$."
> 6. **Forgetting the unit of $C$:** $C$ should be in mol/m³ (or mol/L consistently). Mixing units in gas-law calculations is a frequent source of numerical error.
> 7. **OTP misunderstanding:** Students often state that exothermic reversible reactions should be run at the highest $T$. The correct answer is the **optimum temperature progression**, not a single high $T$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Temperature & Pressure Effects**
>
> - [ ] **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ — memorize all 3 forms (exponential, two-T, linear).
> - [ ] **van't Hoff Equation:** $d\ln K/dT = \Delta H^0 / (RT^2)$ — exothermic means $K$ decreases with $T$.
> - [ ] **Activation Energy $E$:** Slope of $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ plot multiplied by $-R$.
> - [ ] **Pre-exponential Factor $A$:** Intercept of $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ plot exponentiated.
> - [ ] **Pressure effect on rate:** $r \propto P^n$ for gas-phase reactions, $n = $ overall order.
> - [ ] **Pressure does NOT change $k$** — only the concentrations.
> - [ ] **Liquid-phase rates are independent of pressure** (incompressible).
> - [ ] **Optimum Temperature (T_opt):** Exists for **exothermic reversible reactions only**; determined by setting $dr_{net}/dT = 0$.
> - [ ] **Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP):** Hyperbolic decreasing $T$ profile in adiabatic PFR for exothermic reversible reactions to maintain maximum rate.
> - [ ] **Temperature Sensitivity Rule:** Higher $E$ → higher temperature sensitivity; large $E$ reactions are "steep" on Arrhenius plots.
> - [ ] **Constant Values:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) or $1.987$ cal/(mol·K); $T$ must always be in Kelvin.
> - [ ] **Kooij Modification:** $k = A T^n e^{-E/RT}$ for non-linear Arrhenius behavior.
> - [ ] **Steric Factor ($p$):** Orientation probability in collision theory ($0 \leq p \leq 1$).
> - [ ] **Le Chatelier's Principle:** Increasing $T$ favors endothermic direction; increasing $P$ favors side with fewer gas moles.
> - [ ] **For gas-phase reactions, $C_i = y_i P / (RT)$** — the bridge between pressure and rate.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧪 SINGLE REACTIONS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Reaction (KTU Definition):**
> A *single reaction* is one in which **only one stoichiometric equation** proceeds, yielding a single set of products from the given reactants. The reactor design problem reduces to finding the **reactor volume (V)** or **residence time (τ)** required to achieve a specified **fractional conversion (X_A)** of the limiting reactant A.

Mathematically, for a generic single reaction:

$$A \rightarrow \text{Products}$$

The fractional conversion is defined as:

$$X_A = \frac{\text{Moles of A reacted}}{\text{Moles of A fed}} = \frac{N_{A0} - N_A}{N_{A0}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for Single Reactions:**
> - **Conversion (X_A):** Dimensionless, range [0, 1]
> - **Space Time (τ):** Defined as the **time required to process one reactor volume of feed** under specified conditions, units: **seconds (s)**
> - **Space Velocity (SV):** Reciprocal of space time, units: **s⁻¹**

Formally:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} \quad \text{and} \quad SV = \frac{v_0}{V} = \frac{1}{\tau}$$

where $v_0$ is the **volumetric flow rate of feed** (m³/s) and $V$ is the reactor volume (m³).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition 🏭

> **Real-World Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model:**
> Imagine three types of restaurant kitchens (reactors) preparing one dish (single product) from raw ingredients (reactant A):
>
> 1. **🍳 Batch Kitchen (Batch Reactor):** All ingredients are loaded, cooking proceeds for a fixed time, then the entire dish is removed. You can run batch cycles repeatedly.
>
> 2. **🛤️ Assembly-Line Kitchen (Plug Flow Reactor, PFR):** Ingredients enter at one end, get cooked progressively as they move through a long tunnel (like a conveyor oven), and come out fully cooked at the other end. Each "slice" of fluid doesn't mix with neighboring slices.
>
> 3. **🌀 Big-Pot Kitchen (Mixed Flow Reactor, MFR / CSTR):** All ingredients are dumped into one giant continuously-stirred pot. Anything entering instantly mixes with everything already inside. The exit stream has the same composition as the contents of the pot.
>
> For a *single reaction*, the goal is simply: **"Which kitchen cooks the dish fastest/cheapest for a given recipe?"** The answer depends on the *order* of the recipe (reaction order), and KTU expects you to derive it mathematically.

---

## 1.3 Why Single Reactions Matter in CRE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Viewpoint:**
> Single reactions form the **conceptual foundation** for ALL reaction engineering. Before tackling complex multiple/parallel/series reactions, you MUST master:
> - Deriving the **design (performance) equation** for ideal reactors.
> - Comparing volumes of PFR vs MFR for the **same duty**.
> - Handling **variable density systems** (gas-phase, ε_A ≠ 0).
> - Recognizing the **autocatalytic** special case.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):**
> - **R** = 8.314 J/(mol·K) — Universal Gas Constant
> - **C_A0** = Initial/Feed Concentration of A (mol/m³ or kmol/m³)
> - **τ** = **V / v₀** — Space Time (s)
> - **SV = 1/τ** — Space Velocity (s⁻¹)
> - **F_A0** = Molar Feed Rate of A (mol/s)
> - **ε_A** = Expansion Factor (dimensionless, fractional change in volume per mole of A converted)

---

## 1.4 Classification of Single Reactions by Order

| Reaction Order | Rate Law $-r_A$ (constant density) | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|
| Zero Order | $k$ | Rate is **independent** of concentration |
| First Order | $k C_A$ | Most common in KTU problems |
| Second Order | $k C_A^2$ | Strong concentration dependence |
| $n^{th}$ Order | $k C_A^n$ | Generalized comparison |
| Autocatalytic | $k C_A C_C$ | Product C **catalyzes** the reaction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Mole Balance Framework

The **starting point** for every ideal reactor in KTU's Module 3 is the **General Mole Balance**:

$$\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

For reactant A under steady-state, no accumulation:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{A0} X_A = -r_A V$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Universal" Reactor Performance Equation for Single Reactions:**
>
> $$\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \frac{\tau}{C_{A0}} = \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$
>
> This integral form is the **PFR/Batch design equation** (steady-state, no dispersion). For the **MFR**, the integrand collapses to a single algebraic evaluation at the exit.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-memorize** for the KTU Board Exam. Units and conditions are stated explicitly to avoid valuation pitfalls.

| Reactor Type | Design Equation (Constant Density) | Design Equation (Variable Density, $\varepsilon_A$) | Conversion Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | $t = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $t = N_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $X_A = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_{A0}}$ |
| **PFR** | $\tau = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $\tau = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} \dfrac{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)\, dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ |
| **MFR (CSTR)** | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}\bigg\vert_{X_A}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A \big(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A\big)}\bigg\vert_{X_A}$ | Same as above |
| **Recycle PFR** | $\tau = (1 + R)C_{A0} \int_{X_{Ai}}^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $X_{Ai} = \dfrac{R\, X_{Af}}{1 + R}$ | $R = \dfrac{\text{Recycle flow}}{\text{Fresh feed}}$ |

### Conversion Relations (Gas-Phase, Variable Density)

| Relation | Formula |
|---|---|
| Concentration of A | $C_A = \dfrac{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$ |
| Total concentration | $C_T = \dfrac{C_{T0}}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$ |
| Volumetric flow rate | $v = v_0(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$ |
| Expansion factor | $\varepsilon_A = \dfrac{y_{A0} \cdot \delta}{\sum y_i} = \dfrac{V_{X_A=1} - V_{X_A=0}}{V_{X_A=0}}$ |

where $\delta$ = stoichiometric coefficient change.

### Space Time & Space Velocity Relations

| Symbol | Definition | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau$ | Space Time | $\tau = V / v_0$ | s |
| $SV$ | Space Velocity | $SV = v_0 / V$ | s⁻¹ |
| $t_{\text{res}}$ | Mean Residence Time | $t_{\text{res}} = \tau$ (for liquids) | s |

### Rate Laws for Common Single Reactions

| Order | Rate Law $-r_A$ | Integrated Form (Constant Density) | $\tau_{PFR}$ | $\tau_{MFR}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zero | $k$ | $C_A = C_{A0} - kt$ | $C_{A0} X_A / k$ | $C_{A0} X_A / k$ |
| First | $k C_A$ | $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = kt$ | $(1/k) \ln\!\big[1/(1-X_A)\big]$ | $(1/k)[X_A/(1-X_A)]$ |
| Second | $k C_A^2$ | $1/C_A - 1/C_{A0} = kt$ | $(1/kC_{A0})[X_A/(1-X_A)]$ | $(1/kC_{A0})[X_A/(1-X_A)^2]$ |
| $n^{th}$ | $k C_A^n$ | — | Use integral form | $C_{A0}^{1-n} X_A / [k(1-X_A)^n]$ |

### Autocatalytic Reaction: A → R, with $-r_A = k C_A C_R$

| Quantity | Expression |
|---|---|
| Concentration of R (product) | $C_R = C_{R0} + C_{A0} X_A$ |
| Concentration of A | $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ |
| PFR $\tau$ | $\tau = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{k C_A C_R}$ |
| MFR $\tau$ | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1-X_A)(C_{R0} + C_{A0} X_A)}$ |
| Optimum $\tau$ in MFR | $\tau_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{1}{k C_{A0}} \left[\sqrt{\dfrac{C_{R0}}{C_{A0}}} + \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{R0}}{C_{A0}} + X_{A,\text{opt}}}\,\right]^2 \cdot \dfrac{(1-X_{A,\text{opt}})}{1}$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Reactor Sizing:** Single-reaction design equations allow chemical engineers to size commercial reactors for processes like *saponification*, *isomerization*, *enzyme catalysis*, and *acid-base neutralization*.
> - **Cost Optimization:** PFR vs MFR comparison directly determines capital expenditure (CAPEX). For positive-order reactions, **PFR is smaller → cheaper**, but MFR is preferred when mixing issues dominate (e.g., viscous liquids, autocatalytic fermentations).
> - **Process Control:** Space time $\tau$ is the key control variable; space velocity $SV$ is the metric reported in catalyst literature.

---

## 2.4 Reactor Comparison Logic (KTU Gold)

> [!NOTE]
> **General Rules for Single Reactions (Positive Order, n > 0):**
> 1. For the **same conversion**, the volume of an MFR is **always larger** than (or equal to) the volume of a PFR:
>
> $$V_{MFR} \geq V_{PFR}$$
>
> 2. For **zero-order** reactions, both reactors give **identical** performance.
> 3. The volume ratio $V_{MFR} / V_{PFR}$ **increases with reaction order** and with target conversion $X_A$.
> 4. For **negative-order** reactions, an MFR may give a **smaller** volume than a PFR.
