# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Performance Equation for a Batch Reactor (Single Reaction)

**Starting Point:** Mole balance on reactant A for a well-mixed batch system.

**Step 1:** Write the general rate of disappearance:

$$\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A V$$

**Step 2:** Convert to fractional conversion. We have $N_A = N_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, so:

$$dN_A = -N_{A0}\, dX_A$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the mole balance:

$$-N_{A0}\frac{dX_A}{dt} = r_A V$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$N_{A0}\frac{dX_A}{dt} = -r_A V$$

**Step 5:** Separate variables and integrate from $t = 0$ (where $X_A = 0$) to time $t$ (where $X_A = X_{A,f}$):

$$N_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_{A,f}} dX_A = \int_{0}^{t} (-r_A)\, V\, dt'$$

**Step 6:** For a **constant-volume batch** ($V = $ const), $-r_A$ depends only on $X_A$ (or equivalently on time). The volume can be taken outside the integrand on the right-hand side only if $-r_A$ is expressed in terms of $X_A$. Writing $-r_A$ as a function of $X_A$ and integrating:

$$\frac{N_{A0}}{V}\int_{0}^{X_{A,f}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} = \int_{0}^{t} dt' = t$$

**Step 7:** Recognize $N_{A0}/V = C_{A0}$ (for constant volume):

$$\boxed{\,t = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}\,} \quad \text{— Batch Reactor Design Equation}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for a batch reactor under constant-volume, single-reaction conditions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Performance Equation for a Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)

**Step 1:** Take a differential slice of the PFR at position $z$ with volume $dV$. Mole balance for steady flow, no axial mixing:

$$F_A \big\vert_z - F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} + r_A\, dV = 0$$

**Step 2:** Recognize $F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} - F_A \big\vert_z = dF_A$, so:

$$-dF_A + r_A\, dV = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad dF_A = r_A\, dV$$

**Step 3:** Express $F_A$ in terms of conversion: $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, so $dF_A = -F_{A0}\, dX_A$:

$$-F_{A0}\, dX_A = r_A\, dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{A0}\, dX_A = -r_A\, dV$$

**Step 4:** Separate and integrate from inlet ($X_A = 0$, $V = 0$) to outlet ($X_A = X_{A,f}$, $V = V$):

$$F_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_{A,f}} dX_A = \int_{0}^{V} (-r_A)\, dV'$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $F_{A0}$, and let $\tau = V/v_0$. For a constant-density system, $C_{A0} = F_{A0}/v_0$:

$$\boxed{\,\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}\,} \quad \text{— PFR Design Equation}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The PFR equation has the **same form** as the Batch equation! The space time $\tau$ in a PFR is the *time-equivalent* of holding time in a batch reactor. This is one of KTU's most-tested equivalences.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Performance Equation for a Mixed Flow Reactor (MFR / CSTR)

**Step 1:** Apply the steady-state mole balance to the entire well-mixed vessel:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:

$$F_{A0} - F_{A0}(1 - X_A) + r_A V = 0$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$F_{A0} X_A = -r_A V$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $v_0$ (recognizing $\tau = V/v_0$ and $C_{A0} = F_{A0}/v_0$):

$$\boxed{\,\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A \big\vert_{\text{exit}}}\,} \quad \text{— MFR Design Equation}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference:** The MFR equation is **algebraic** (no integral), evaluated at the **exit conditions** only. The PFR equation is **integral** over the conversion range. This is the #1 reason MFR volumes are larger for positive-order reactions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Variable Density Extension (ε_A ≠ 0)

**Step 1:** When the reaction occurs in the gas phase with a change in total moles, the volumetric flow rate changes:

$$v = v_0(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$$

**Step 2:** The concentration of A becomes:

$$C_A = \frac{F_A}{v} = \frac{F_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{v_0(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} = C_{A0}\frac{(1 - X_A)}{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)}$$

**Step 3:** Re-derive the PFR performance equation by NOT pulling the volumetric flow out of the integral:

$$\tau = \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{F_{A0}\, dX_A}{v\,(-r_A)} = \int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{F_{A0}\, dX_A}{v_0(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)(-r_A)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $C_{A0} = F_{A0}/v_0$:

$$\boxed{\,\tau = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)\, dX_A}{-r_A}\,} \quad \text{— PFR with Variable Density}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step substitution chain for the MFR (variable density):**
>
> $$\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{v_0(-r_A)} \bigg\vert_{\text{exit}}$$
>
> Since $v_{\text{exit}} = v_0(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$ and $C_A$ contains the $(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$ factor, the relation becomes:
>
> $$\boxed{\,\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A (1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} \bigg\vert_{\text{exit}}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Recycle Reactor Performance Equation

**Setup:** A fraction $R$ of the exit stream is recycled back to the inlet. The total feed to the reactor becomes $F_{A0}(1 + R)$, and the inlet conversion is no longer zero.

**Step 1:** Define the recycle ratio:

$$R = \frac{\text{Volume recycled}}{\text{Volume of fresh feed}}$$

**Step 2:** The inlet conversion (after mixing of fresh feed + recycle) is:

$$X_{A,i} = \frac{R \cdot X_{A,f}}{1 + R}$$

**Step 3:** The PFR integral runs from $X_{A,i}$ to $X_{A,f}$:

$$V = (1 + R) F_{A0}\int_{X_{A,i}}^{X_{A,f}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

**Step 4:** Equivalently, in terms of space time:

$$\boxed{\,\tau_{\text{recycle}} = (1 + R) C_{A0}\int_{X_{A,i}}^{X_{A}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}\,}$$

**Step 5:** **Limiting cases:**
- $R = 0 \Rightarrow$ plain PFR, $X_{A,i} = 0$.
- $R \to \infty \Rightarrow$ behaves as MFR.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Reactor Volume Ratios ($V_{MFR} / V_{PFR}$) for Different Orders

This is a **high-yield KTU comparison table** that examiners love to ask about:

| Reaction Order | $\tau_{PFR}$ (or $t_{PFR}$) | $\tau_{MFR}$ | $\tau_{MFR} / \tau_{PFR}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zero | $\dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k}$ | $\dfrac{C_{A0} X_A}{k}$ | $1$ (always) |
| Half | $\dfrac{1}{k}\left[1 - (1-X_A)^{0.5}\right]\dfrac{1}{C_{A0}^{0.5}}$ | $\dfrac{X_A}{k \sqrt{C_{A0}}(1-X_A)^{0.5}}$ | $\dfrac{X_A}{(1-X_A)^{0.5}[1-(1-X_A)^{0.5}]}$ |
| First | $\dfrac{1}{k}\ln\!\big[1/(1-X_A)\big]$ | $\dfrac{X_A}{k(1-X_A)}$ | $\dfrac{X_A}{(1-X_A)\ln\!\big[1/(1-X_A)\big]}$ |
| Second | $\dfrac{X_A}{kC_{A0}(1-X_A)}$ | $\dfrac{X_A}{kC_{A0}(1-X_A)^2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{(1-X_A)}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern recognition (KTU key insight):**
> For **positive-order** reactions, the ratio $V_{MFR} / V_{PFR}$ is **always > 1** and grows with both $X_A$ and $n$. This is why industries prefer **PFRs** for high-conversion gas-phase reactions (e.g., cracking, ammonia synthesis).

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ideal Reactor Types for Single Reactions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Fresh Feed FA0, CA0, v0] --> M1{Mixing Point}
    M1 --> R1[Reactor Vessel]
    R1 --> P1[Product Stream FA, XAf]
    R1 -. Recycle R .-> M1
    R2[Reactor Type Selector] --> R1
    R2 --> BR[Batch Reactor]
    R2 --> PFR[Plug Flow Reactor PFR]
    R2 --> MFR[Mixed Flow Reactor MFR CSTR]
    R2 --> RPR[Recycle PFR]
    BR --> EQ1[tau equals CA0 times integral of dX by minus rA]
    PFR --> EQ1
    MFR --> EQ2[tau equals CA0 XA over minus rA evaluated at exit]
    RPR --> EQ3[tau equals 1 plus R times CA0 times integral from XAi to XAf]
```


## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Reactor for Which Reaction Order?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Single Reaction Given] --> Q1{Determine Order n}
    Q1 -->|n equals 0| ZERO[Both PFR and MFR identical - choose by mixing needs]
    Q1 -->|n greater than 0| POS[Positive Order - PFR preferred for high XA]
    Q1 -->|n less than 0| NEG[Negative Order - MFR preferred for high XA]
    Q1 -->|Autocatalytic| AUTO[Use Recycle PFR or MFR with optimum tau]
    POS --> Q2{Target Conversion}
    Q2 -->|Low XA under 0.5| LO[PFR marginal - MFR may be competitive]
    Q2 -->|High XA above 0.8| HI[PFR strongly preferred - large V savings]
    AUTO --> Q3{Calculate tau opt}
    Q3 --> OPTR[Select recycle ratio R so that XAi matches XAopt]
    OPTR --> FINAL[Design Recycle PFR with R for minimum total volume]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Variable Density vs Constant Density Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Reaction Phase] --> B{Gas Phase with delta not zero}
    B -->|Yes| C[Compute epsilon A from stoichiometry]
    C --> D[Use modified design equations with 1 plus epsilon A XA term]
    B -->|No - Liquid| E[Assume constant density]
    E --> F[Use standard design equations without epsilon correction]
    D --> G[Apply PFR or MFR formula with variable density form]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute tau and V required]
    H --> I[Compare V_PFR versus V_MFR for given XA]
```

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> Define **space time (τ)** and **space velocity (SV)** for a flow reactor. How are they related? Mention their SI units.

**Model Answer:**

> Space time $\tau$ is defined as the time required to process **one reactor volume of feed** measured at inlet conditions:
>
> $$\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} \quad \text{(units: seconds, s)}$$
>
> Space velocity $SV$ is the **reciprocal** of space time, representing the number of reactor volumes of feed processed per unit time:
>
> $$SV = \frac{v_0}{V} = \frac{1}{\tau} \quad \text{(units: s}^{-1}\text{)}$$
>
> **Relation:** $SV \cdot \tau = 1$.
>
> **Significance:** Space time is the **fundamental design variable** in KTU reactor design; it directly links the reactor volume to the desired throughput.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

> Compare the performance of a **PFR and an MFR** for an **autocatalytic reaction** of the form $A \rightarrow R$ with rate law $-r_A = k C_A C_R$. Which one is preferred and why?

**Model Answer:**

> For an autocatalytic reaction, the rate is **zero at the start** (no R) and **zero at the end** (no A), reaching a **maximum at an intermediate conversion**. The standard PFR equation:
>
> $$\tau_{PFR} = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k C_A C_R}$$
>
> forces the reaction through the slow region near $X_A = 0$. An MFR, by contrast, **starts at the feed composition**, where the rate is also low, but allows the bulk to operate near the **optimum** composition. Hence:
>
> $$\tau_{MFR} \quad \text{can be less than} \quad \tau_{PFR} \quad \text{for } X_A > X_{A,\text{opt}}$$
>
> **Preferred choice:** A **Recycle PFR** with an appropriately chosen recycle ratio $R$, so that the inlet conversion $X_{A,i}$ lies on the **optimum operating line**. As $R \to \infty$, the recycle PFR **degenerates into an MFR**, confirming the MFR is the natural choice for autocatalytic single reactions at high conversion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read Before Attempting!):**
> - **Forgetting the $(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$ factor** in variable-density problems is the **#1 cause of full-mark losses** in KTU paper valuation. Always check whether the problem specifies "constant volume/density" or "gas-phase with change in moles."
> - **Mixing up "$\tau$" and "$t$"** for batch vs flow reactors. The Batch equation gives *holding time $t$*, while PFR/MFR give *space time $\tau$*. They are equivalent in magnitude but conceptually different.
> - **Not specifying reaction order** in the rate law before integration. Examiners will **deduct 1–2 marks** if you write $-r_A = k C_A^n$ without stating $n$.
> - **Algebraic slip in second-order MFR derivation:** The denominator is $(1 - X_A)^2$, not $(1 - X_A)$.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks) — Option A** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]

> The liquid-phase first-order reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ is to be carried out in a flow system.
> **Fresh feed** concentration $C_{A0} = 4$ kmol/m³ enters at a flow rate $v_0 = 0.05$ m³/min. The rate constant is $k = 0.2$ min⁻¹.
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Calculate the **space time and volume** required to achieve **75% conversion** in (i) a PFR and (ii) an MFR.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] If the same conversion is required in a **single MFR followed by a PFR** (series combination), determine the optimum split that minimizes the total volume. Compare the total volume to a single PFR.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Given:** $C_{A0} = 4$ kmol/m³, $v_0 = 0.05$ m³/min, $k = 0.2$ min⁻¹, $X_A = 0.75$, first order.

**Rate law:** $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A) = 0.2 \cdot 4 \cdot (1 - X_A) = 0.8(1 - X_A)$ kmol/(m³·min).

**(i) PFR Volume:**

$$\tau_{PFR} = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{1}{k}\int_{0}^{X_A}\frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A}$$

$$= \frac{1}{k}\big[-\ln(1 - X_A)\big]_{0}^{0.75} = \frac{1}{0.2}\big[-\ln(0.25)\big]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.2} \cdot (1.3863) = 6.9315 \text{ min}$$

$$V_{PFR} = \tau_{PFR} \cdot v_0 = 6.9315 \times 0.05 = 0.3466 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) MFR Volume:**

$$\tau_{MFR} = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = \frac{X_A}{k(1 - X_A)} = \frac{0.75}{0.2 \cdot 0.25} = 15 \text{ min}$$

$$V_{MFR} = 15 \times 0.05 = 0.75 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Volume ratio:** $V_{MFR}/V_{PFR} = 0.75/0.3466 = 2.164$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Optimum Split for MFR-then-PFR Series:**

For a series combination, an **MFR followed by a PFR** gives the **smallest total volume** when the MFR is sized to operate at the **point where the local MFR design chord is parallel to the PFR curve**. For first-order kinetics, the optimum exit of the MFR is:

$$X_{A1}^* = 1 - \sqrt{1 - X_A}$$

Substituting $X_A = 0.75$:

$$X_{A1}^* = 1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.75} = 1 - \sqrt{0.25} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

**Step 1 — MFR from 0 to 0.5:**

$$\tau_{MFR,1} = \frac{X_{A1}}{k(1 - X_{A1})} = \frac{0.5}{0.2 \cdot 0.5} = 5 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — PFR from 0.5 to 0.75:**

$$\tau_{PFR,2} = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - X_{A1}}{1 - X_A}\right] = \frac{1}{0.2}\ln\!\left[\frac{0.5}{0.25}\right] = \frac{1}{0.2}\ln(2) = \frac{0.6931}{0.2} = 3.466 \text{ min}$$

**Total space time:** $\tau_{\text{total}} = 5 + 3.466 = 8.466$ min

**Total volume:** $V_{\text{total}} = 8.466 \times 0.05 = 0.4233$ m³

**Comparison with single PFR ($V_{PFR} = 0.3466$ m³):**

$$\frac{V_{\text{total, MFR+PFR}}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{0.4233}{0.3466} = 1.221$$

> **Conclusion:** The optimum-split MFR+PFR series gives a total volume **22% larger** than a single PFR, but **44% smaller** than a single MFR. **A single PFR is the optimal choice** for this first-order single reaction.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Option B** [KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]

> A **gas-phase elementary reaction** $2A \rightarrow B + C$ is carried out in an MFR. Pure A is fed at $T = 600$ K, $P = 5$ atm with $F_{A0} = 10$ kmol/min. The rate law is $-r_A = k C_A^2$ with $k = 0.05$ m³/(kmol·min).
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Determine the **expansion factor $\varepsilon_A$** and derive the MFR design equation. Calculate the **space time and volume** required for **80% conversion**.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Now suppose the same reaction is conducted **isothermally in a PFR** under identical conditions. Compute the **PFR volume** for 80% conversion and compare. Comment on the result.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometry and Expansion Factor:**

Reaction: $2A \rightarrow B + C$

- Moles of A reacted per mole of A reacted: 1
- Moles of products produced per mole of A reacted: $\delta = 1$ (B) $+ 1$ (C) $- 2$ (A) $= 0$

For pure A feed: $y_{A0} = 1$, total moles initially per mole of A: 1.

$$\varepsilon_A = \frac{y_{A0} \cdot \delta}{\sum y_i} = \frac{1 \cdot 0}{1} = 0$$

> **Note:** $\varepsilon_A = 0$ because the total number of moles is unchanged ($2 \to 2$). The system therefore behaves as **constant density**, and the standard formulas apply without modification.

**Step 2 — Rate law in terms of conversion:**

For constant density, $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$. So:

$$-r_A = k C_{A0}^2(1 - X_A)^2$$

**Step 3 — Initial concentration:**

$$C_{A0} = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{5 \text{ atm}}{0.0821 \text{ L·atm/(mol·K)} \times 600 \text{ K}}$$

$$= \frac{5}{49.26} = 0.1015 \text{ mol/L} = 0.1015 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — MFR Design Equation:**

$$\tau_{MFR} = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}^2(1 - X_A)^2} = \frac{X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)^2}$$

Substituting $X_A = 0.80$, $k = 0.05$, $C_{A0} = 0.1015$:

$$\tau_{MFR} = \frac{0.80}{0.05 \times 0.1015 \times (0.20)^2} = \frac{0.80}{0.05 \times 0.1015 \times 0.04}$$

$$= \frac{0.80}{0.000203} = 3940.9 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5 — MFR Volume:**

$$V_{MFR} = \tau_{MFR} \cdot v_0$$

We need $v_0$. From $F_{A0} = C_{A0} v_0$:

$$v_0 = \frac{F_{A0}}{C_{A0}} = \frac{10}{0.1015} = 98.52 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

$$V_{MFR} = 3940.9 \times 98.52 = 388{,}221 \text{ m}^3 \approx 3.88 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3$$

(Equivalent to $\approx 6.47$ km³ — clearly impractical, illustrating why **very slow second-order gas-phase reactions** are challenging industrially.)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**PFR Design Equation (Second Order):**

$$\tau_{PFR} = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}^2(1 - X_A)^2} = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}}\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(1 - X_A)^2}$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(1 - X_A)^2} = \left[\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right]_{0}^{X_A} = \frac{1}{1 - X_A} - 1 = \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\tau_{PFR} = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{1}{0.05 \times 0.1015} \cdot \frac{0.80}{0.20} = \frac{1}{0.005075} \cdot 4 = 197.04 \times 4 = 788.18 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — PFR Volume:**

$$V_{PFR} = 788.18 \times 98.52 = 77{,}644 \text{ m}^3 \approx 7.76 \times 10^4 \text{ m}^3$$

**Comparison:**

$$\frac{V_{MFR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{1}{(1 - X_A)} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.0$$

> **Conclusion:** For this **second-order** reaction at 80% conversion, the MFR requires **5 times the volume of the PFR**. This matches the general rule:
>
> $$\frac{V_{MFR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{1}{1 - X_A} \quad \text{(second order)}$$
>
> The PFR is **dramatically superior** for high-conversion, high-order single reactions. Industry would prefer a **tubular PFR with high surface area** (e.g., a multi-tubular catalytic converter) to handle this duty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save Before Exam):**

- ✅ **Batch Reactor (BR):** $t = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} dX_A / (-r_A)$ — gives **time**, not volume.
- ✅ **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR):** $\tau = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_A} dX_A / (-r_A)$ — *identical* to batch formula, but represents **space time**.
- ✅ **Mixed Flow Reactor (MFR/CSTR):** $\tau = C_{A0} X_A / (-r_A) \big\vert_{X_A}$ — *algebraic*, evaluate at **exit** only.
- ✅ **Variable density gas-phase:** Always include the $(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$ correction in **both** rate expression and reactor formula.
- ✅ **Expansion factor formula:** $\varepsilon_A = y_{A0} \cdot \delta / \sum y_i$, where $\delta = $ (moles of products) $-$ (moles of reactants).
- ✅ **Reactor volume ranking for positive order:** $V_{MFR} \geq V_{PFR}$ always, with equality only at $X_A \to 0$ or $n = 0$.
- ✅ **Autocatalytic reactions:** Use **Recycle PFR** or **MFR** — plain PFR is inefficient because the rate is zero at the inlet.
- ✅ **Space time** = $V/v_0$ (feed conditions); **Residence time** = $V/v$ (actual in-reactor conditions). They differ for variable-density systems.
- ✅ **Optimum split for MFR-then-PFR series:** For $n^{th}$-order reaction, set $X_{A,1} = 1 - (1 - X_A)^{1/n}$ (KTU-favorite derivation).
- ✅ **Recycle ratio $R$ rule:** $R = 0 \Rightarrow$ PFR; $R \to \infty \Rightarrow$ MFR. $X_{A,i} = R X_A / (1 + R)$.
- ✅ **For first-order reactions:** $\tau_{PFR} = (1/k) \ln[1/(1-X_A)]$ and $\tau_{MFR} = X_A / [k(1 - X_A)]$.
- ✅ **For zero-order reactions:** Both reactors require $V = v_0 C_{A0} X_A / k$ — **same volume**.
- ✅ **Volume ratio $V_{MFR}/V_{PFR}$ grows with conversion and reaction order** — critical for KTU Board exam reasoning questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat of Reaction from Thermodynamics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat of Reaction from Thermodynamics

---

# 🔥 Heat of Reaction from Thermodynamics — KTU Premier Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

The **Heat of Reaction** (also termed *Enthalpy of Reaction* under constant pressure) is defined as the quantity of energy evolved (exothermic, negative sign) or absorbed (endothermic, positive sign) when a chemical reaction proceeds from reactants to products under conditions of either **constant pressure** or **constant temperature**, with all reactants and products existing in their standard reference states.

Mathematically, for a generalized stoichiometric reaction:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nu_i A_i = 0$$

where $\nu_i$ are the **stoichiometric coefficients** (negative for reactants, positive for products) and $A_i$ represents the chemical species. The heat of reaction at temperature $T$ is:

$$\Delta H_r (T) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nu_i \cdot H_i (T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition Required Verbatim:**
> *"The Heat of Reaction is the change in enthalpy of a system when one mole of a reaction (as written in the stoichiometric equation) has proceeded to completion, with all substances being in their standard states at a specified temperature."*

### 1.2 Two Variants — Know the Distinction (CRITICAL for KTU 2-Markers)

| Variant | Symbol | Condition | Common Use |
|---------|--------|-----------|------------|
| **Heat of Reaction at Constant Pressure** | $\Delta H$ | $P$ = constant | Most industrial reactors (open systems) |
| **Heat of Reaction at Constant Volume** | $\Delta U$ | $V$ = constant | Batch reactors (closed systems) |

The thermodynamic relationship bridging them is the **First Law identity**:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta (PV)$$

For ideal gases, applying $PV = nRT$, this reduces to:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_{gas} \cdot RT$$

where $\Delta n_{gas}$ represents the change in the number of gaseous moles per stoichiometric reaction.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Energy Bank Account" 🏦

Imagine a chemistry class project where students are *atoms*. The heat of reaction is simply the **net withdrawal or deposit** from the chemical "energy bank account" of the molecules.

- **Bonds in reactants** = money deposited in the bank (high energy account).
- **Bonds in products** = money remaining in the bank (low energy account).
- **Heat of reaction** = the *difference* — the energy released as heat (exothermic) or absorbed (endothermic) during the molecular "restructuring."

If the products (post-restructuring account) hold **less energy** than the reactants, the **excess energy exits the system as heat** — this is an **exothermic** reaction (e.g., combustion of methane, $\Delta H < 0$).

If the products hold **more energy** than the reactants, the system must **withdraw energy from the surroundings** to fund the bond rearrangement — this is an **endothermic** reaction (e.g., photosynthesis, $\Delta H > 0$).

### 1.4 Standard States and Reference Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conventions (per IUPAC & KTU Textbooks):**
> - **Temperature:** $25^\circ C = 298.15\,K$ (often approximated as $298\,K$)
> - **Pressure:** $1\,atm = 101.325\,kPa$
> - **Concentration:** $1\,M$ for dissolved species
> - **Phase:** Most stable allotrope of the element (e.g., $O_2(g)$, $C_{(graphite)}$, $H_2(g)$)

The superscript $\circ$ (e.g., $\Delta H^\circ$) denotes "standard state conditions." KTU problems typically specify whether they require standard ($\Delta H^\circ_{298}$) or general ($\Delta H_T$) values.

### 1.5 Physical Constants Recurring in KTU Problems

| Constant | Symbol | Numerical Value | Units |
|----------|--------|------------------|-------|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | $J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$ |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **1.987** | $cal \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$ |
| Standard Temperature | $T_{std}$ | **298.15** | $K$ |
| Standard Pressure | $P_{std}$ | **101.325** | $kPa$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Heat of Reaction (Foundational Knowledge)

KTU 2024 Module 3 specifically tests students on the *calculation methodologies* for $\Delta H_r$. These methodologies are built upon three cornerstone definitions.

#### 🧱 Pillar 1: Standard Heat of Formation ($\Delta H^\circ_f$)

The **standard heat of formation** of a compound is the enthalpy change when **one mole** of the compound is formed from its constituent **elements** in their standard states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cardinal Rule:** The standard heat of formation of any element in its standard state is **ZERO** by convention.
> Examples: $\Delta H^\circ_f (O_2, g) = 0$; $\Delta H^\circ_f (C, graphite) = 0$; $\Delta H^\circ_f (H_2, g) = 0$.

**Engineering Utility:** Heat of formation data forms the basis of *free-energy minimization* calculations used in equilibrium studies, combustion modeling, and process simulator databases (Aspen Plus, HYSYS).

#### 🧱 Pillar 2: Standard Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H^\circ_c$)

The **standard heat of combustion** is the enthalpy change when **one mole** of a substance undergoes **complete combustion** with pure oxygen at standard conditions, yielding stable, fully oxidized products ($CO_2(g)$, $H_2O(l)$, $SO_2(g)$, $N_2(g)$ as applicable).

**Engineering Utility:** Critical for sizing **waste heat recovery boilers**, designing **Fired Heaters**, and computing **calorific values** of fuels in petrochemical and power generation industries.

#### 🧱 Pillar 3: Standard Heat of Reaction ($\Delta H^\circ_r$)

The **standard heat of reaction** is computed from formation data using the **Law of Additivity of Heats of Reaction** (formal thermodynamic statement of Hess's Law):

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum_{products} \nu_i \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f,i} - \sum_{reactants} \nu_j \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f,j}$$

Or, equivalently, using combustion data:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum_{reactants} \nu_j \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{c,j} - \sum_{products} \nu_i \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{c,i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Mastery (Frequently Tested):**
> - When using **formation** data: $\Delta H_r = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_f$ (products) **$-$** $\sum \nu_j \Delta H_f$ (reactants)
> - When using **combustion** data: $\Delta H_r = \sum \nu_j \Delta H_c$ (reactants) **$-$** $\sum \nu_i \Delta H_c$ (products)
> - The signs are **OPPOSITE** in the two methods. Confusing this is the #1 cause of negative marks in KTU exams.

### 2.2 Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation

**Statement:** *The total enthalpy change accompanying a chemical reaction is independent of the path by which the reaction is carried out, provided the initial and final states are the same.*

**Why does it work?** Because enthalpy is a **state function** — its differential $dH$ is an *exact differential* satisfying:

$$\oint dH = 0$$

Engineering Implication: Engineers can compute heats of reaction for reactions that are difficult or impossible to study experimentally (e.g., formation of complex hydrocarbons) by routing the reaction through a series of well-known thermochemical steps (typically formation or combustion intermediates).

### 2.3 Temperature Dependence — Kirchhoff's Law (HIGH-YIELD MODULE 3 TOPIC)

In real reactors, the temperature of operation is rarely $298\,K$. The heat of reaction varies with temperature due to the differing heat capacities of reactants and products. **Kirchhoff's Law** quantifies this:

$$\frac{d(\Delta H_r)}{dT} = \Delta C_p$$

where $\Delta C_p$ is the change in heat capacity per mole of reaction as written:

$$\Delta C_p = \sum_{products} \nu_i C_{p,i} - \sum_{reactants} \nu_j C_{p,j}$$

The **integrated form** of Kirchhoff's Law, assuming $C_p$ is temperature-independent (a common KTU assumption):

$$\Delta H_r (T_2) = \Delta H_r (T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

For the special case $T_1 = 298\,K$:

$$\Delta H_r (T) = \Delta H^\circ_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Subtlety:** If $\Delta C_p$ is treated as a constant over the integration range, the integral collapses to $\Delta C_p (T - 298)$. If $C_p$ has a temperature dependence of the form $C_p = \alpha + \beta T + \gamma T^2$, the integration must be performed term-by-term — this is a common 7-mark derivation question.

### 2.4 The Engineering Reality — Why Heat of Reaction Matters in CRE

In chemical reaction engineering, the heat of reaction directly influences:

- **Reactor Temperature Profiles:** Highly exothermic reactions (e.g., oxidations, polymerizations) risk **thermal runaway** if heat removal is inadequate. The Damköhler number and temperature rise are linked through $\Delta H_r$.
- **Reactor Sizing & Heat Transfer Area:** Heat duty $Q = \dot{n}_A \cdot (-\Delta H_r)$ determines whether a heat exchanger jacket or internal coil is required.
- **Adiabatic Temperature Rise:** $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)}{C_{p,mix}}$ — the maximum temperature a reaction mixture can reach in the absence of heat exchange.
- **Equilibrium Conversions:** Via the van't Hoff equation, $\Delta H_r$ governs the temperature-dependence of the equilibrium constant, $K_p$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|----------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_{gas} \cdot RT$ | $H$-$U$ relation for ideal gases | 3-mark direct questions |
| 2 | $\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum \nu_i \Delta H^\circ_{f,i} - \sum \nu_j \Delta H^\circ_{f,j}$ | Heat of reaction from formation data | 7-mark problems (very common) |
| 3 | $\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum \nu_j \Delta H^\circ_{c,j} - \sum \nu_i \Delta H^\circ_{c,i}$ | Heat of reaction from combustion data | 7-mark problems |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d(\Delta H_r)}{dT} = \Delta C_p$ | Kirchhoff's Law (differential form) | Derivation-based questions |
| 5 | $\Delta H_r (T) = \Delta H_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | Kirchhoff's Law (integrated form) | 14-mark application problems |
| 6 | $\Delta C_p = \sum \nu_i C_{p,i} - \sum \nu_j C_{p,j}$ | Heat capacity change of reaction | Sub-step in integrated form |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_r}{\sum \nu_i C_{p,i}}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise | Conceptual 2-markers |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A: Kirchhoff's Law from the First Principles of Thermodynamics

**Starting Premise:** For any species $i$, the temperature dependence of its molar enthalpy is given by:

$$dH_i = C_{p,i} \, dT$$

This is the definition of the heat capacity at constant pressure for an ideal gas (or incompressible substance).

**Step 1 — Enthalpy of Reaction Definition:**

For a reaction with products (P) and reactants (R), the heat of reaction at any temperature is:

$$\Delta H_r (T) = \sum_{P} \nu_i H_i (T) - \sum_{R} \nu_j H_j (T)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to Temperature:**

Taking the derivative of both sides with respect to $T$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta H_r)}{dT} = \sum_{P} \nu_i \frac{dH_i}{dT} - \sum_{R} \nu_j \frac{dH_j}{dT}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Heat Capacity Definition:**

Since $\dfrac{dH_i}{dT} = C_{p,i}$, we substitute:

$$\frac{d(\Delta H_r)}{dT} = \sum_{P} \nu_i C_{p,i} - \sum_{R} \nu_j C_{p,j}$$

**Step 4 — Define the Heat Capacity Change of Reaction:**

By definition, $\Delta C_p = \sum \nu_i C_{p,i}$ (products) $- \sum \nu_j C_{p,j}$ (reactants). Therefore:

$$\frac{d(\Delta H_r)}{dT} = \Delta C_p$$

This is the **differential form of Kirchhoff's Law**. ∎

**Step 5 — Integrate Between Two Temperature Limits:**

Assuming $C_{p,i}$ are temperature-dependent and defined as $C_{p,i} = \alpha_i + \beta_i T + \gamma_i T^2$:

$$\int_{\Delta H_r (T_1)}^{\Delta H_r (T_2)} d(\Delta H_r) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

$$\Delta H_r (T_2) = \Delta H_r (T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \left[ \Delta \alpha + \Delta \beta T + \Delta \gamma T^2 \right] dT$$

where $\Delta \alpha = \sum \nu_i \alpha_i - \sum \nu_j \alpha_j$, and similarly for $\Delta \beta$, $\Delta \gamma$.

**Step 6 — Perform the Integration Term by Term:**

$$\Delta H_r (T_2) = \Delta H_r (T_1) + \Delta \alpha (T_2 - T_1) + \frac{\Delta \beta}{2} (T_2^2 - T_1^2) + \frac{\Delta \gamma}{3} (T_2^3 - T_1^3)$$

This is the **final, exam-ready form** of Kirchhoff's integrated equation. ∎

---

### 3.2 Derivation B: Linking Standard Heat of Formation to Standard Heat of Reaction (Hess's Law Proof)

**Reaction of Interest:**

$$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$$

**Strategy:** Construct two paths from the *same initial state* (constituent elements at standard state) to the *same final state* (products at standard state).

**Path 1 (Direct):**

Elements → Reactants (A, B) → Products (C, D)

The enthalpy change is the sum of the formation steps of reactants (positive, since we're going element → compound) **reversed** plus the formation steps of products:

$$\Delta H_{Path\,1} = -\sum \nu_R \Delta H^\circ_{f,R} + \sum \nu_P \Delta H^\circ_{f,P}$$

**Path 2 (Hess's Composite Path):**

Elements → Reactants → Products (single combined step)

The enthalpy change is the standard heat of reaction:

$$\Delta H_{Path\,2} = \Delta H^\circ_r$$

**Apply Hess's Law** ($\Delta H_{Path\,1} = \Delta H_{Path\,2}$):

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum_{P} \nu_P \Delta H^\circ_{f,P} - \sum_{R} \nu_R \Delta H^\circ_{f,R}$$

∎ This is the **thermodynamic proof** of the formation-based formula for heat of reaction.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem Statement:**

Calculate the **standard heat of reaction** at $298\,K$ for the following gas-phase reaction:

$$C_2H_4 (g) + H_2O (g) \rightarrow C_2H_5OH (g)$$

Given the standard heats of formation at $298\,K$:

| Species | $\Delta H^\circ_f$ (kJ/mol) |
|---------|------------------------------|
| $C_2H_4 (g)$ | +52.30 |
| $H_2O (g)$ | -241.80 |
| $C_2H_5OH (g)$ | -235.30 |

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify products and reactants from the stoichiometric equation.

- Products (P): $C_2H_5OH$ with $\nu = +1$
- Reactants (R): $C_2H_4$ with $\nu = -1$ (implicitly, the magnitude is 1), $H_2O$ with $\nu = -1$

**Step 2:** Apply the formation-based formula:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[ \nu_{C_2H_5OH} \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f, C_2H_5OH} \right] - \left[ \nu_{C_2H_4} \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f, C_2H_4} + \nu_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f, H_2O} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values (magnitude of stoichiometric coefficient is used):

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = [(1)(-235.30)] - [(1)(+52.30) + (1)(-241.80)]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = [-235.30] - [52.30 - 241.80]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = [-235.30] - [-189.50]$$

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = -235.30 + 189.50 = -45.80 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.

- The **negative sign** indicates the reaction is **exothermic** at standard conditions.
- The magnitude ($45.80\,kJ/mol$) is the energy released as heat per mole of ethylene hydrated.

**Step 5:** Verify dimensional consistency and physical intuition.

- The reaction converts a C=C double bond (high-energy unsaturated state) and water into an alcohol (lower-energy saturated state). A modest exothermicity is consistent with the small energy difference between unsaturated and saturated hydrocarbon bonding.

**Final Answer:** $\Delta H^\circ_r = -45.80\,kJ/mol$ (exothermic hydration of ethylene to ethanol vapor). ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

| Situation in KTU Problem | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---------------------------|--------------------|--------|
| All species are simple, well-known compounds (CO₂, H₂O, CH₄, etc.) | **Heats of Formation** | Data is universally tabulated |
| Organic compounds with unknown formation data but known combustion data | **Heats of Combustion** | Combustion data more readily measured for complex organics |
| Problem asks for $\Delta H$ at $T \neq 298\,K$ | **Kirchhoff's Law** | Temperature correction is required |
| Reaction involves only gases at constant volume | **$\Delta U$ via $\Delta H$** | Open/closed system distinction matters |
| Adiabatic reactor temperature rise | **Energy balance with $\Delta H_r$** | Reactor design problem |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Flow of Heat of Reaction Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Given Reaction Stoichiometry"] --> B{"Data Available?"}
    B -- Formation Data --> C["Apply Hess's Law via Formation"]
    B -- Combustion Data --> D["Apply Hess's Law via Combustion"]
    B -- Both --> E["Cross-verify Both Methods"]
    C --> F["Sum Products minus Sum Reactants"]
    D --> G["Sum Reactants minus Sum Products"]
    F --> H["Intermediate Result DeltaH_298"]
    G --> H
    H --> I{"Target Temperature 298K?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Final Answer DeltaH_r_298"]
    I -- No --> K["Apply Kirchhoff's Law"]
    K --> L["Compute Delta Cp from C_p_i data"]
    L --> M["Integrate Delta Cp from 298K to T_target"]
    M --> N["Add Integral to DeltaH_298"]
    N --> O["Final Answer DeltaH_r_T"]
    J --> P["Report with Sign and Units"]
    O --> P
    E --> H
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Kirchhoff's Law Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Identify Reactants and Products"] --> S2["Compute Delta Cp = Sum Cp_products - Sum Cp_reactants"]
    S2 --> S3{"Cp function known?"}
    S3 -- Constant --> S4["Integral = Delta Cp times T minus 298"]
    S3 -- Linear: alpha + beta T --> S5["Integral = Delta alpha T plus half Delta beta T squared"]
    S3 -- Polynomial --> S6["Term-by-Term Integration to T"]
    S4 --> S7["Add to DeltaH_298 to get DeltaH_T"]
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8["Output Final DeltaH_r at T"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Flow in an Exothermic Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reactants at T_initial"] --> B["Chemical Bonds Broken and Reformed"]
    B --> C["Products at T_final"]
    C -- Heat Evolved out --> D["Surroundings or Heat Exchanger"]
    A -- Energy IN --> B
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In an exothermic reaction, the system releases energy to the surroundings. The *direction* of energy flow is critical: chemical potential energy of bonds (input side) is converted to thermal energy (output side). In an endothermic reaction, this flow is *reversed*.

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Standard Reference State Convention

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Elements_StdState
    Elements_StdState --> Compound_Product: Formation Step
    Elements_Std_State --> Compound_Reactant: Formation Step
    Compound_Reactant --> Compound_Product: Reaction Step
    Compound_Product --> [*]
    note right of Compound_Product: DeltaH_f = 0 for elements
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define the standard heat of formation of a compound. State the convention for the standard heat of formation of an element in its standard state.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **standard heat of formation** of a compound is defined as the enthalpy change accompanying the formation of **one mole** of the compound from its constituent elements, with all reactants and products in their **standard states** (typically at $25^\circ C$ and $1\,atm$ pressure).

**Convention:** The standard heat of formation of an element in its **most stable allotropic form** at standard conditions is assigned a value of **zero** by definition. Examples include: $\Delta H^\circ_f (O_2, g) = 0$, $\Delta H^\circ_f (H_2, g) = 0$, $\Delta H^\circ_f (C, graphite) = 0$.

This convention provides a consistent reference baseline for computing heats of formation of all compounds via Hess's Law. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State and explain Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation. Why is it a consequence of enthalpy being a state function?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** *The total enthalpy change accompanying a chemical reaction is independent of the path taken; it depends only on the initial and final states of the system.*

**Explanation with Example:** To determine the heat of formation of CO from elements (which is experimentally difficult), we construct a thermochemical cycle:

$$C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta H_1} CO \quad (\text{direct, unknown})$$

$$C + O_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta H_2} CO_2 \quad (\text{combustion of C, known})$$

$$CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta H_3} CO_2 \quad (\text{combustion of CO, known})$$

By Hess's Law: $\Delta H_2 = \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_3$, hence $\Delta H_1 = \Delta H_2 - \Delta H_3$.

**Thermodynamic Basis:** Since enthalpy $H$ is a state function, its change $\Delta H$ depends only on the initial and final states, not on the path. Mathematically, the cyclic integral of $dH$ is zero: $\oint dH = 0$. (3 marks)

---

### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 Marks = 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign convention errors** account for ~40% of marks lost in heat-of-reaction problems. Always explicitly write the formula *before* plugging in numbers.
> - **Units mismatch** (kJ vs. J, mol vs. kmol) can cost 1–2 marks. State units in every step.
> - **Kirchhoff's Law problems** are often solved incorrectly by *forgetting to subtract* the heat capacities of reactants. Remember: $\Delta C_p = \sum_P \nu_i C_{p,i} - \sum_R \nu_j C_{p,j}$.
> - **Temperature limits:** When integrating Kirchhoff's Law, ensure the lower limit is the temperature at which $\Delta H_r$ is known, and the upper limit is the target temperature. Reversed limits yield the wrong sign.

---

#### **Option A: Question from Formation Data + Kirchhoff's Law (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** Calculate the **standard heat of reaction** at $298\,K$ for the following reaction:

$$4NH_3 (g) + 5O_2 (g) \rightarrow 4NO (g) + 6H_2O (g)$$

Given the standard heats of formation at $298\,K$:

| Species | $\Delta H^\circ_f$ (kJ/mol) |
|---------|------------------------------|
| $NH_3 (g)$ | -46.20 |
| $O_2 (g)$ | 0.00 |
| $NO (g)$ | +90.37 |
| $H_2O (g)$ | -241.80 |

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the formula using stoichiometric coefficients from the balanced equation:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum_P \nu_i \Delta H^\circ_{f,i} - \sum_R \nu_j \Delta H^\circ_{f,j}$$

**Step 2:** Expand for each species:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[ 4 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f,NO} + 6 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f,H_2O} \right] - \left[ 4 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f,NH_3} + 5 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_{f,O_2} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values (note: $O_2$ contributes 0 as it is an element):

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[ 4 \cdot (+90.37) + 6 \cdot (-241.80) \right] - \left[ 4 \cdot (-46.20) + 5 \cdot (0) \right]$$

**Step 4:** Compute the products sum:

$$\sum_P = (4)(90.37) + (6)(-241.80) = 361.48 + (-1450.80) = -1089.32 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the reactants sum:

$$\sum_R = (4)(-46.20) + 0 = -184.80 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 6:** Final calculation:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = -1089.32 - (-184.80) = -1089.32 + 184.80 = -904.52 \text{ kJ}$$

**Answer:** $\Delta H^\circ_{r,298} = -904.52\,kJ$ per 4 moles of $NH_3$ oxidized. The reaction is **strongly exothermic**, consistent with the well-known Ostwald process for nitric acid manufacture. ✓ (7 marks)

---

**(b)** The above reaction is to be carried out in a reactor at $T = 800\,K$. The mean molar heat capacities (in $J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$) over the range $298$–$800\,K$ are:

| Species | $C_p$ ($J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$) |
|---------|----------------------------------------|
| $NH_3 (g)$ | 35.06 |
| $O_2 (g)$ | 29.37 |
| $NO (g)$ | 29.84 |
| $H_2O (g)$ | 34.31 |

Using **Kirchhoff's Law**, compute the heat of reaction at $800\,K$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate $\Delta C_p$ for the reaction:

$$\Delta C_p = \sum_P \nu_i C_{p,i} - \sum_R \nu_j C_{p,j}$$

**Step 2:** Products side:

$$\sum_P C_{p,i} = 4 \cdot C_{p,NO} + 6 \cdot C_{p,H_2O} = 4 \cdot 29.84 + 6 \cdot 34.31$$

$$= 119.36 + 205.86 = 325.22 \text{ J/K}$$

**Step 3:** Reactants side:

$$\sum_R C_{p,j} = 4 \cdot C_{p,NH_3} + 5 \cdot C_{p,O_2} = 4 \cdot 35.06 + 5 \cdot 29.37$$

$$= 140.24 + 146.85 = 287.09 \text{ J/K}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\Delta C_p$:

$$\Delta C_p = 325.22 - 287.09 = 38.13 \text{ J/K}$$

**Step 5:** Apply Kirchhoff's Law (integrated form, constant $\Delta C_p$):

$$\Delta H_{800} = \Delta H_{298} + \Delta C_p \cdot (T - 298)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute values (watch the units — $\Delta C_p$ in $J/K$, $\Delta H_{298}$ in $kJ$):

$$\Delta H_{800} = (-904.52 \text{ kJ}) + (38.13 \text{ J/K}) \cdot (800 - 298) \text{ K} \cdot \left( \frac{1 \text{ kJ}}{1000 \text{ J}} \right)$$

$$\Delta H_{800} = -904.52 + (38.13)(502)(0.001)$$

$$\Delta H_{800} = -904.52 + 19.14 = -885.38 \text{ kJ}$$

**Answer:** $\Delta H_{r,800} = -885.38\,kJ$ per 4 mol of $NH_3$. The heat of reaction becomes *less exothermic* at higher temperatures because $\Delta C_p > 0$ (products have greater heat capacity than reactants in this case). ✓ (7 marks)

---

#### **Option B: Alternative Question from Combustion Data (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** The standard heats of combustion at $298\,K$ are given as:

| Compound | $\Delta H^\circ_c$ (kJ/mol) |
|----------|------------------------------|
| $C_2H_4 (g)$ (ethylene) | -1411.0 |
| $H_2 (g)$ | -285.8 |
| $C_2H_6 (g)$ (ethane) | -1559.7 |

Calculate the **standard heat of reaction** at $298\,K$ for the hydrogenation of ethylene to ethane:

$$C_2H_4 (g) + H_2 (g) \rightarrow C_2H_6 (g)$$

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the combustion-based formula:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum_R \nu_j \Delta H^\circ_{c,j} - \sum_P \nu_i \Delta H^\circ_{c,i}$$

**Step 2:** Identify reactants and products:

- Reactants: $C_2H_4$ ($\nu = 1$), $H_2$ ($\nu = 1$)
- Products: $C_2H_6$ ($\nu = 1$)

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[ (1)(-1411.0) + (1)(-285.8) \right] - \left[ (1)(-1559.7) \right]$$

**Step 4:** Compute reactants sum:

$$\sum_R = -1411.0 + (-285.8) = -1696.8 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = -1696.8 - (-1559.7) = -1696.8 + 1559.7 = -137.1 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Answer:** $\Delta H^\circ_{r,298} = -137.1\,kJ/mol$ (exothermic hydrogenation). The negative sign is consistent with the fact that hydrogenation of a C=C double bond releases energy as it converts the high-energy unsaturated bond to a lower-energy saturated C–C single bond. ✓ (7 marks)

---

**(b)** Compare the above result with the value obtained from the **heat of formation** method using the data below, and comment on the agreement.

| Species | $\Delta H^\circ_f$ (kJ/mol) |
|---------|------------------------------|
| $C_2H_4 (g)$ | +52.30 |
| $H_2 (g)$ | 0.00 |
| $C_2H_6 (g)$ | -84.70 |

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the formation-based formula:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \sum_P \nu_i \Delta H^\circ_{f,i} - \sum_R \nu_j \Delta H^\circ_{f,j}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = \left[ (1)(-84.70) \right] - \left[ (1)(+52.30) + (1)(0) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Calculate:

$$\Delta H^\circ_r = -84.70 - 52.30 = -137.00 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4:** Compare with combustion method:

- Combustion method: $-137.1\,kJ/mol$
- Formation method: $-137.0\,kJ/mol$
- **Discrepancy:** $0.1\,kJ/mol$ ($\sim 0.07\%$ relative error)

**Step 5:** Comment:

The two methods yield results that are in **excellent agreement**, with a negligible discrepancy attributable to **rounding errors** in the tabulated thermochemical data and minor variations in standard-state conditions across different reference sources. This consistency **validates Hess's Law** — the heat of reaction is path-independent and can be computed via multiple thermochemical routes, yielding equivalent results within experimental uncertainty. ✓ (7 marks)

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Heat of Reaction from Thermodynamics**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Heat of reaction = enthalpy change for one mole of reaction as written, at specified $T$ and $P$ (or $V$).
- ⭐ **Two Variants:** $\Delta H$ (constant $P$) and $\Delta U$ (constant $V$). They differ by $\Delta n_{gas} RT$ for ideal gases.
- ⭐ **Three Pillar Quantities:** Standard heats of *formation* ($\Delta H_f^\circ$), *combustion* ($\Delta H_c^\circ$), and *reaction* ($\Delta H_r^\circ$).
- ⭐ **Formation Formula:** $\Delta H_r^\circ = \sum_P \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ - \sum_R \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ$
- ⭐ **Combustion Formula:** $\Delta H_r^\circ = \sum_R \nu_j \Delta H_{c,j}^\circ - \sum_P \nu_i \Delta H_{c,i}^\circ$ (signs **reversed** relative to formation method!)
- ⭐ **Hess's Law:** Enthalpy is a state function; $\Delta H$ is path-independent. $\oint dH = 0$.
- ⭐ **Kirchhoff's Law (Differential):** $\dfrac{d(\Delta H_r)}{dT} = \Delta C_p$
- ⭐ **Kirchhoff's Law (Integrated):** $\Delta H_r(T_2) = \Delta H_r(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$
- ⭐ **Standard State Convention:** Elements in their stable allotropic form have $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$.
- ⭐ **Sign Convention Mastery:** Combustion is *always* exothermic ($\Delta H_c^\circ < 0$); unstable compounds have *positive* $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (e.g., $NO$, $C_2H_4$).
- ⭐ **Units Vigilance:** Convert $J \to kJ$ and $mol \to kmol$ consistently. Most KTU answers expect $kJ/mol$ or $kJ$ per stoichiometric equation.
- ⭐ **Engineering Linkages:** $\Delta H_r$ is the foundation for energy balances, adiabatic temperature rise, equilibrium constant temperature dependence (van't Hoff), and reactor heat-transfer design.
- ⭐ **Frequent Examiner Trap:** Forgetting to **include the stoichiometric coefficients** as multipliers when summing formation/combustion contributions.
- ⭐ **Memory Aid — "FoRP / CoRP":**
  - **F**ormation → Reactants subtracted from **P**roducts (FoRP)
  - **C**ombustion → Products subtracted from **R**eactants (CoRP)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat of Reaction and Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat of Reaction and Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heat of Reaction (ΔH_r) — The Core Definition

The **heat of reaction** (also called the **enthalpy of reaction**, denoted $\Delta H_r$) is defined as the amount of heat energy that is either released or absorbed when a chemical reaction proceeds at a specified temperature and pressure, expressed per mole of a chosen reference species (usually the limiting reactant or a key product).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCHT503, Module 3):**
> *"The heat of reaction is the enthalpy change accompanying a chemical reaction. It is positive for endothermic reactions (heat absorbed) and negative for exothermic reactions (heat released)."*

Formally, for a generic reaction:

$$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$$

The heat of reaction at temperature $T$ is:

$$\Delta H_r(T) = \sum_i \nu_i H_i^{\text{products}} - \sum_i \nu_i H_i^{\text{reactants}}$$

where $\nu_i$ is the **stoichiometric coefficient** (negative for reactants, positive for products) and $H_i$ is the **specific molar enthalpy** of species $i$.

---

## 1.2 Two Faces of Energy — Exothermic vs Endothermic

| Reaction Type | Sign of ΔH_r | Energy Flow | Industrial Example |
|---------------|--------------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Exothermic** | $\Delta H_r < 0$ | Heat **released** to surroundings | Ammonia synthesis, Sulfuric acid production, Polymerization |
| **Endothermic** | $\Delta H_r > 0$ | Heat **absorbed** from surroundings | Steam reforming of methane, Cracking of hydrocarbons, Calcination of limestone |

The standard convention used in KTU examinations:
- **Exothermic**: $\Delta H_r$ is **negative** (e.g., $\Delta H_r = -92.4$ kJ/mol for the water-gas shift reaction at 298 K).
- **Endothermic**: $\Delta H_r$ is **positive** (e.g., $\Delta H_r = +206.1$ kJ/mol for steam reforming of methane at 298 K).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bank Account" of Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: Reactions as Financial Transactions**
>
> Imagine a chemical reaction as a **bank transaction**:
> - **Reactants** = Money going OUT of the account (you "spend" the chemical bonds of the reactants).
> - **Products** = Money coming INTO the account (the new chemical bonds formed).
> - **Heat of reaction ($\Delta H_r$)** = The NET BALANCE of the transaction.
> - If products are "richer" in energy than reactants → the bank must **RELEASE** money (heat) to the surroundings → **Exothermic** (ΔH_r < 0).
> - If products are "poorer" in energy than reactants → the bank must **ABSORB** money (heat) from the surroundings → **Endothermic** (ΔH_r > 0).
>
> This is why understanding heat of reaction is the **first step** in designing reactor cooling/heating systems — it's essentially the energy ledger for the entire process.

---

## 1.4 Key Thermodynamic Quantities at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Conditions (IUPAC / KTU Convention):**
> - **Standard State**: Pure substance at **298.15 K** (often approximated as 298 K) and **1 atm** (**101.325 kPa**).
> - **Standard Heat of Formation ($\Delta H_f^\circ$)**: Enthalpy change when 1 mole of a compound is formed from its elements in their standard states.
> - **Standard Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c^\circ$)**: Enthalpy change when 1 mole of a substance undergoes complete combustion with O₂ at standard conditions.
> - **Gas Constant** $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)** (must be used in consistent SI units).

---

## 1.5 The Role of Temperature in Reaction Engineering

Temperature is the **single most influential operating variable** in any chemical reactor. It simultaneously affects:

1. **Reaction Rate** through the **Arrhenius equation** (kinetic effect).
2. **Equilibrium Conversion** through the **van't Hoff equation** (thermodynamic effect).
3. **Physical Properties** like density, viscosity, heat capacity, and diffusivity.
4. **Selectivity** in complex (parallel/consecutive) reaction networks.

This is why Module 3 of PCCHT503 dedicates an entire sub-topic to the interplay between **heat effects** and **temperature** in reactor design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Calculation of Heat of Reaction — Hess's Law Pathway

Since the heat of reaction cannot be measured directly for every reaction, KTU examiners expect students to apply **Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation**:

> *"The total enthalpy change in a chemical reaction is independent of the pathway taken; it depends only on the initial and final states."*

The general working formula used in KTU problems is:

$$\Delta H_r^\circ (298\,\text{K}) = \sum_i \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ \,(\text{products}) - \sum_i \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ \,(\text{reactants})$$

For example, for the reaction:
$$CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)$$

$$\Delta H_r^\circ = \left[\Delta H_f^\circ(CO_2) + 2\Delta H_f^\circ(H_2O,l)\right] - \left[\Delta H_f^\circ(CH_4) + 2\Delta H_f^\circ(O_2)\right]$$

Since $\Delta H_f^\circ(O_2) = 0$ (element in standard state):
$$\Delta H_r^\circ = \left[(-393.5) + 2(-285.8)\right] - \left[(-74.8) + 0\right] = -890.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

---

## 2.2 Temperature Dependence of Heat of Reaction — Kirchhoff's Equation

The heat of reaction at any temperature $T$ can be calculated from its value at a reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}}$ (usually 298 K) using **Kirchhoff's Law**:

$$\Delta H_r(T) = \Delta H_r(T_{\text{ref}}) + \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

where the change in heat capacity is:

$$\Delta C_p = \sum_i \nu_i C_{p,i}^{\text{products}} - \sum_i \nu_i C_{p,i}^{\text{reactants}}$$

**Why this matters in engineering:** Most data tables provide $\Delta H_f^\circ$ at 298 K, but real reactors operate at 500–1000 K. Kirchhoff's equation allows us to **scale** thermodynamic data to actual operating temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Kirchhoff's Equation:**
> - **Reactor Design**: Accurate energy balance calculations for high-temperature processes (e.g., ethylene cracking at 1100 K).
> - **Safety Analysis**: Predicting adiabatic temperature rise in exothermic runaway reactions.
> - **Process Optimization**: Determining optimal feed preheat temperatures for endothermic reactors.

---

## 2.3 Arrhenius Equation — The Master Key to Temperature-Dependent Kinetics

The Swedish chemist **Svante Arrhenius (1889)** discovered that the rate constant of virtually all chemical reactions follows an exponential dependence on temperature:

$$k = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $k$ | Rate constant | (varies, e.g., s⁻¹, L/(mol·s)) |
| $A$ | Pre-exponential (frequency) factor | Same as $k$ |
| $E$ | Activation energy | **J/mol** (sometimes kJ/mol) |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **K (Kelvin)** |

**The "Two-Finger" Rule for KTU problems:**
1. **Two temperatures** given → use the **two-point form** to find $E$ or $k$.
2. **One temperature + E** given → use the **one-point form** to find $k$.

**Two-point form** (logarithmic form — the form 99% of KTU problems demand):

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

---

## 2.4 Van't Hoff Equation — Temperature Effect on Equilibrium

For a reaction at equilibrium, the equilibrium constant $K$ varies with temperature according to the **van't Hoff equation**:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{RT^2}$$

**Integrated form** (assuming $\Delta H_r^\circ$ is approximately constant over the temperature range):

$$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Le Chatelier's Principle — The Memory Aid:**
> - **Exothermic reactions** ($\Delta H_r^\circ < 0$): As $T$ **increases**, $K$ **decreases** → equilibrium shifts to **reactants** (lower conversion).
> - **Endothermic reactions** ($\Delta H_r^\circ > 0$): As $T$ **increases**, $K$ **increases** → equilibrium shifts to **products** (higher conversion).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Heat of reaction (formation) | $\Delta H_r^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ$ (products) $- \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ$ (reactants) | KTU 3-mark and 14-mark problems |
| 2 | Kirchhoff's equation | $\Delta H_r(T) = \Delta H_r(298) + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | High-T reactor design |
| 3 | Arrhenius equation | $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ | Rate constant vs temperature |
| 4 | Two-point Arrhenius | $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ | Find $E$ or $k$ at new $T$ |
| 5 | van't Hoff (differential) | $d(\ln K)/dT = \Delta H_r^\circ / (RT^2)$ | Effect of T on equilibrium |
| 6 | van't Hoff (integrated) | $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta H_r^\circ / R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | Find $K$ at new $T$ |
| 7 | Adiabatic temperature rise | $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = -\Delta H_r \, X \, C_{A0} / (\rho \, C_p)$ | Safety analysis |
| 8 | Energy balance (adiabatic PFR) | $dT/d\tau = (-\Delta H_r) \cdot r_A / (\rho C_p)$ | Reactor temperature profile |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU formula sheet equations, absolute value bars are written using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to ensure compatibility with markdown table syntax. For example, $\vert \Delta H_r \vert$ rather than $\lvert \Delta H_r \rvert$.

---

## 2.6 Adiabatic vs Non-Adiabatic Reactors — The Energy Management Dichotomy

| Reactor Mode | Heat Transfer | Energy Balance Simplification | KTU Relevance |
|--------------|---------------|------------------------------|---------------|
| **Adiabatic** | $Q = 0$ (perfectly insulated) | All reaction heat → temperature change | Maximum 14-mark problems on temperature profiles |
| **Non-adiabatic (isothermal)** | Perfect heat removal/addition | $T = T_0$ = constant | Used to find pure kinetic parameters |
| **Non-adiabatic (intercooled)** | $Q \neq 0$ controlled by $UA$ | $UA(T_a - T) = (-\Delta H_r) F_{A0} dX$ | Multi-stage reactor design |

---

## 2.7 The Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP) — KTU Favorite

For **reversible exothermic reactions** (e.g., SO₂ oxidation, ammonia synthesis), there exists a unique temperature profile that maximizes the **overall rate of reaction** $r_A = k(C_A, C_B, \ldots) \cdot (1 - \frac{1}{K})$ along the reactor length.

**Key Insight (KEEP THIS FOR 14-MARK QUESTIONS):**
- As conversion $X$ increases, equilibrium conversion decreases → $T$ must be **lowered** to compensate.
- The **optimum temperature** is the temperature at which the reaction rate is maximum for a given conversion.

The OTP is found by solving:

$$\left(\frac{\partial r_A}{\partial T}\right)_X = 0$$

subject to the material balance and the van't Hoff relation between $K$ and $T$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Integrated Van't Hoff Equation

Starting from the differential form:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{RT^2}$$

**Step 1: Separate variables**

We rewrite the equation by multiplying both sides by $dT$:

$$d(\ln K) = \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{RT^2} \, dT$$

**Step 2: Define the integration limits**

Let the equilibrium constant be $K_1$ at temperature $T_1$ and $K_2$ at temperature $T_2$.

**Step 3: Integrate both sides (assuming $\Delta H_r^\circ$ is independent of $T$)**

$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d(\ln K) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{RT^2} \, dT$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the left-hand side**

$$\ln K \Big|_{K_1}^{K_2} = \ln K_2 - \ln K_1 = \ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right)$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the right-hand side**

Since $\Delta H_r^\circ$ and $R$ are constants with respect to $T$:

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{R T^2} \, dT = \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^{-2} \, dT = \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{R} \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

**Step 6: Simplify the bracket**

$$-\frac{1}{T}\Big|_{T_1}^{T_2} = -\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}$$

**Step 7: Combine both sides — FINAL RESULT**

$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This equation tells us that for an **exothermic** reaction ($\Delta H_r^\circ < 0$), increasing $T_2 > T_1$ makes the right-hand side **negative**, so $K_2 < K_1$ — equilibrium shifts backward. This is **Le Chatelier's principle** in mathematical form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Two-Point Arrhenius Form (from First Principles)

Starting from the Arrhenius equation:

$$k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1: Take natural logarithm of both sides**

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E}{RT}$$

**Step 2: Write the same equation at two different temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$**

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \frac{E}{RT_1}$$

$$\ln k_2 = \ln A - \frac{E}{RT_2}$$

**Step 3: Subtract the first equation from the second**

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E}{RT_2} - \left(-\frac{E}{RT_1}\right)$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

This is the **most-tested form** in KTU Module 3 problems. Memorize it!

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Arrhenius Calculation (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** The rate constant for the decomposition of nitrogen pentoxide doubles when the temperature is raised from 300 K to 310 K. Calculate the activation energy $E$.

**Given:**
- $k_2 / k_1 = 2$
- $T_1 = 300$ K
- $T_2 = 310$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Solution using the two-point form:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 1: Compute the LHS**

$$\ln(2) = 0.6931$$

**Step 2: Compute the temperature bracket**

$$\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{310} = \frac{310 - 300}{300 \times 310} = \frac{10}{93000} = 1.0753 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange for $E$**

$$E = R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1) \cdot \frac{T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1}$$

**Step 4: Substitute values**

$$E = 8.314 \times 0.6931 \times \frac{300 \times 310}{10}$$

**Step 5: Compute final answer**

$$E = 8.314 \times 0.6931 \times 9300 = 5.358 \times 10^{4} \text{ J/mol} \approx 53.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Interpretation:** A typical chemical reaction has $E \approx 50$–250 kJ/mol, so this result is reasonable.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Adiabatic Temperature Rise in a CSTR

For a single irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow B$ in a **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** operating adiabatically, the steady-state energy balance gives:

$$F_{A0} X (-\Delta H_r) = F_T C_{p,\text{avg}} (T - T_0)$$

**Step 1: Define the total molar flow rate**

For a mixture with feed molar flow $F_{A0}$ and initial mole fraction of $A$ as $y_{A0}$:

$$F_T = \frac{F_{A0}}{y_{A0}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the energy balance**

$$F_{A0} X (-\Delta H_r) = \frac{F_{A0}}{y_{A0}} C_{p,\text{avg}} (T - T_0)$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T$**

$$T = T_0 + \frac{y_{A0} X (-\Delta H_r)}{C_{p,\text{avg}}}$$

**Step 4: Define the adiabatic temperature rise**

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = T - T_0 = \frac{y_{A0} (-\Delta H_r) X}{C_{p,\text{avg}}}}$$

**Step 5: Maximum possible temperature rise (at $X = 1$)**

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad,max}} = \frac{y_{A0} (-\Delta H_r)}{C_{p,\text{avg}}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the **worst-case scenario** used in safety relief valve sizing. For highly exothermic reactions (e.g., organic peroxide decompositions), $\Delta T_{\text{ad,max}}$ can exceed 1000 K — a critical runaway scenario.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU High-Yield Differences

| Concept | Heat of Reaction | Activation Energy | Equilibrium Constant |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Symbol** | $\Delta H_r$ | $E$ | $K$ |
| **Domain** | Thermodynamics | Kinetics | Thermodynamics |
| **Dependence on T** | Weak (via Kirchhoff) | Strong (Arrhenius) | Strong (van't Hoff) |
| **Sign convention** | Exo: negative; Endo: positive | Always positive | Exo: decreases with T; Endo: increases with T |
| **Units** | kJ/mol | J/mol or kJ/mol | Dimensionless (or with activities) |
| **KTU Typical Question** | "Calculate $\Delta H_r$ from formation data" | "Find $E$ given two $k$–$T$ pairs" | "Predict effect of T on conversion" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Students often confuse $\Delta H_r$ (thermodynamic) with $E$ (kinetic). Remember: $\Delta H_r$ is the **net energy** difference between products and reactants, while $E$ is the **energy barrier** that reactants must overcome to become products.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Profile Diagram (Reaction Coordinate Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1["REACTANTS H_R"] --> T1["TRANSITION STATE E_a forward"]
    T1 --> P1["PRODUCTS H_P"]
    R2["REACTANTS H_R"] --> T2["TRANSITION STATE E_a reverse"]
    T2 --> P2["PRODUCTS H_P"]

    T1 -. "Activation Energy Forward E_a_f" .- R1
    T2 -. "Activation Energy Reverse E_a_r" .- P2
    P1 -. "Heat of Reaction DELTA H_r" .- R1
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The vertical axis represents **Potential Energy** (in kJ/mol).
- The horizontal axis is the **Reaction Coordinate** (progress of reaction).
- $E_{a,f}$ is the **forward activation energy** (reactants → transition state).
- $E_{a,r}$ is the **reverse activation energy** (products → transition state).
- $\Delta H_r = E_{a,f} - E_{a,r}$ — the **net** heat of reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** For an **exothermic** reaction, $\Delta H_r < 0$ and $E_{a,f} < E_{a,r}$. For an **endothermic** reaction, $\Delta H_r > 0$ and $E_{a,f} > E_{a,r}$. Drawing this diagram correctly in your answer sheet is worth **2 easy marks** in a 14-mark problem.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Flow in an Industrial Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["FEED STREAM F_A0 T_0 C_p"] --> B1["REACTOR VOLUME V"]
    B1 --> C1["PRODUCT STREAM F_A T C_p"]
    B1 --> D1["COOLING JACKET T_a UA"]

    B1 -. "Heat Generated Q_gen DELTA H_r times r_A times V" .-> B1
    D1 -. "Heat Removed Q_rem UA times T minus T_a" .-> B1
    B1 -. "Net Energy Accumulation dE_dt rho C_p dT_dt" .-> B1

    subgraph ENERGY_BALANCE["ENERGY BALANCE NODE"]
        EB1["Q_gen minus Q_rem equals Accumulation"]
    end

    B1 --- ENERGY_BALANCE
```

**Operational Logic:**
- **Feed Stream** enters with enthalpy $F_{A0} C_p T_0$.
- **Heat Generated** $Q_{\text{gen}} = (-\Delta H_r) \cdot r_A \cdot V$ from the reaction.
- **Heat Removed** $Q_{\text{rem}} = UA(T - T_a)$ via the cooling jacket.
- **Net Balance**: $Q_{\text{gen}} - Q_{\text{rem}} = \rho C_p \, dT/dt$ (for transient) or 0 at steady state.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Effect of Temperature on Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Reaction Type?"] --> B1{"Exothermic or Endothermic"}
    B1 -->|"Exothermic"| C1["As T increases"]
    B1 -->|"Endothermic"| C2["As T increases"]

    C1 --> D1["K decreases van_t Hoff"]
    D1 --> E1["Equilibrium Conversion DECREASES"]

    C2 --> F1["K increases van_t Hoff"]
    F1 --> G1["Equilibrium Conversion INCREASES"]

    E1 --> H1["OPTIMUM: Lower T better but rate slows"]
    G1 --> H1

    H1 --> I1["Optimum Temperature Progression OTP Analysis"]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP) for Exothermic Reversible Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OTP_DIAGRAM["TEMP vs X AXIS"]
        X1["X 0 percent INLET"] --> X2["X 50 percent MIDDLE"]
        X2 --> X3["X 100 percent EXIT"]

        T1["T HIGH initial"] -.-> X1
        T2["T MEDIUM"] -.-> X2
        T3["T LOW final"] -.-> X3
    end

    OTP_DIAGRAM -. "Highest rate at each X" .- OTP_DIAGRAM
```

**Physical Interpretation:**
- Near the inlet (low $X$), the mixture is far from equilibrium → **high temperature** maximizes the rate constant (Arrhenius effect).
- Near the exit (high $X$), the mixture is close to equilibrium → **low temperature** keeps $K$ large (Le Chatelier effect).
- The OTP curve gives the **maximum overall conversion** for a given reactor size.

---

## 4.5 Classification Flow — Types of Heat Effects in Reactors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["REACTOR ENERGY MODE"] --> B0{"Is Heat Transferred?"}
    B0 -->|"No Q equals 0"| C0["ADIABATIC"]
    B0 -->|"Yes UA nonzero"| D0["NON ADIABATIC"]

    C0 --> C1["All heat changes T"]
    C0 --> C2["Profile T vs X monotonic"]

    D0 --> D1{"Is T held constant?"}
    D1 -->|"Yes"| E0["ISOTHERMAL"]
    D1 -->|"No"| F0["NON ISOTHERMAL"]

    E0 --> E1["Used for kinetic studies"]
    F0 --> F1["Industrial reality most reactors"]
    F0 --> F2["Requires OTP analysis"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual Question
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *"Differentiate between the heat of reaction and the activation energy of a reaction. Why are both important in Chemical Reaction Engineering?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute 1 mark per key point):**

The **heat of reaction** ($\Delta H_r$) is a **thermodynamic** quantity that represents the net enthalpy change between the products and reactants at a given temperature. It determines the **direction of spontaneity** (combined with entropy) and the **magnitude of heat effects** that must be managed in reactor design. For example, $\Delta H_r = -92.4$ kJ/mol for the water-gas shift reaction indicates a strongly exothermic process.

The **activation energy** ($E$) is a **kinetic** quantity that represents the minimum energy barrier that reactant molecules must overcome to form products. It appears in the Arrhenius equation $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ and governs how **sensitively the reaction rate responds to temperature changes**.

Both are essential: $\Delta H_r$ dictates the **energy balance** and cooling/heating requirements, while $E$ dictates the **rate-temperature sensitivity** and optimal operating temperature.

---

### Question 2: Short-Answer Definitional Question
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *"State the Arrhenius equation and explain the physical significance of the pre-exponential factor 'A' and the activation energy 'E'."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Arrhenius equation** is:

$$k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

- **Pre-exponential factor $A$**: Represents the **frequency of molecular collisions** (or, more precisely, the number of times per second reactant molecules approach the transition state configuration with the proper orientation). It has the same units as the rate constant $k$ and is **weakly dependent on temperature**.

- **Activation energy $E$**: Represents the **minimum kinetic energy** that reactant molecules must possess to undergo a successful reaction upon collision. Higher $E$ means a stronger temperature dependence. Typical values range from **50 to 250 kJ/mol** for most chemical reactions.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Arrhenius + Heat of Reaction Problem

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
> *(a)* The standard heats of formation at 298 K are given as:
> - $\Delta H_f^\circ(CH_4, g) = -74.8$ kJ/mol
> - $\Delta H_f^\circ(O_2, g) = 0$ kJ/mol
> - $\Delta H_f^\circ(CO_2, g) = -393.5$ kJ/mol
> - $\Delta H_f^\circ(H_2O, l) = -285.8$ kJ/mol
>
> Calculate the standard heat of combustion of methane at 298 K. Classify the reaction as exothermic or endothermic. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* For a first-order reaction, the rate constant is $k_1 = 3.5 \times 10^{-2}$ min⁻¹ at 350 K and $k_2 = 8.5 \times 10^{-2}$ min⁻¹ at 370 K. Calculate the activation energy $E$ and the pre-exponential factor $A$. What will be the rate constant at 400 K? (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Write the balanced combustion reaction** (1 mark)

$$CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)$$

**Step 2: Apply the standard heat of formation formula** (2 marks)

$$\Delta H_r^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ (\text{products}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ (\text{reactants})$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values** (2 marks)

$$\Delta H_r^\circ = \left[(-393.5) + 2(-285.8)\right] - \left[(-74.8) + 2(0)\right]$$

$$\Delta H_r^\circ = \left[-393.5 - 571.6\right] - \left[-74.8\right] = -965.1 + 74.8$$

**Step 4: Final answer and classification** (2 marks)

$$\boxed{\Delta H_r^\circ = -890.3 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

Since $\Delta H_r^\circ < 0$, the reaction is **exothermic** (heat is released to the surroundings).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Use the two-point Arrhenius form** (2 marks)

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the LHS** (1 mark)

$$\ln\left(\frac{8.5 \times 10^{-2}}{3.5 \times 10^{-2}}\right) = \ln(2.4286) = 0.8873$$

**Step 3: Compute the temperature bracket** (1 mark)

$$\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{370} = \frac{370 - 350}{350 \times 370} = \frac{20}{129500} = 1.5444 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $E$** (1 mark)

$$E = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = \frac{8.314 \times 0.8873}{1.5444 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.776 \times 10^4 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 47.76 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 5: Find $A$ using $k_1$** (1 mark)

$$A = k_1 \exp(E/RT_1) = 3.5 \times 10^{-2} \times \exp\left(\frac{47760}{8.314 \times 350}\right)$$

$$A = 3.5 \times 10^{-2} \times \exp(16.41) = 3.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.34 \times 10^7 = 4.69 \times 10^5 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

**Step 6: Find $k$ at 400 K** (1 mark)

$$k(400) = A \exp(-E/RT) = 4.69 \times 10^5 \times \exp\left(\frac{-47760}{8.314 \times 400}\right)$$

$$k(400) = 4.69 \times 10^5 \times \exp(-14.36) = 4.69 \times 10^5 \times 5.81 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{k(400) \approx 0.273 \text{ min}^{-1}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Adiabatic Reactor + Temperature Progression Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> *(a)* Derive the integrated form of the van't Hoff equation. Use it to determine the equilibrium constant for the endothermic reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$ at 500 K, given that $K = 0.05$ at 300 K and $\Delta H_r^\circ = +40$ kJ/mol. (Assume $\Delta H_r^\circ$ is temperature-independent.) (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* A liquid-phase exothermic reaction $A \rightarrow B$ is carried out adiabatically in a CSTR. The feed conditions are: $F_{A0} = 10$ mol/s, $C_{A0} = 2$ mol/L, $T_0 = 350$ K, $-\Delta H_r = 80$ kJ/mol, $C_p = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), $\rho = 900$ kg/m³. If the conversion is $X = 0.65$, calculate the operating temperature $T$ and the maximum possible adiabatic temperature rise $\Delta T_{\text{ad,max}}$. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Start with the differential van't Hoff equation** (1 mark)

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{RT^2}$$

**Step 2: Separate variables and integrate** (2 marks)

$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d(\ln K) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate** (1 mark)

$$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_r^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute given values** (1 mark)

Given: $K_1 = 0.05$ at $T_1 = 300$ K; $T_2 = 500$ K; $\Delta H_r^\circ = +40000$ J/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

$$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{0.05}\right) = -\frac{40000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{300}\right)$$

**Step 5: Compute the bracket** (1 mark)

$$\frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{300} = \frac{3 - 5}{1500} = -\frac{2}{1500} = -1.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6: Final computation** (1 mark)

$$\ln(K_2/0.05) = -\frac{40000}{8.314} \times (-1.333 \times 10^{-3}) = 4811 \times 1.333 \times 10^{-3} = 6.414$$

$$K_2/0.05 = e^{6.414} = 610.4 \implies \boxed{K_2 \approx 30.5}$$

**Interpretation:** As expected for an **endothermic** reaction, $K$ increases significantly (from 0.05 to 30.5) when temperature increases from 300 K to 500 K.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Convert units for consistency** (1 mark)

$\rho = 900$ kg/m³ = 0.9 kg/L.
$C_p = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Step 2: Maximum adiabatic temperature rise formula** (1 mark)

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad,max}} = \frac{C_{A0} (-\Delta H_r)}{\rho C_p}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values** (1 mark)

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad,max}} = \frac{2 \text{ mol/L} \times 80000 \text{ J/mol}}{0.9 \text{ kg/L} \times 3500 \text{ J/(kg·K)}}$$

$$= \frac{160000}{3150} = 50.79 \text{ K}$$
