$$\boxed{\Delta T_{\text{ad,max}} \approx 50.8 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 4: Actual adiabatic temperature rise at $X = 0.65$** (1 mark)

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{C_{A0} (-\Delta H_r) X}{\rho C_p} = 50.79 \times 0.65 = 33.01 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Operating temperature** (1 mark)

$$T = T_0 + \Delta T_{\text{ad}} = 350 + 33.01 = 383.01 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6: Conversion to °C for practical use** (1 mark)

$$T \approx 110 ^\circ C$$

**Safety Note:** The maximum temperature achievable under adiabatic conditions is $T_{\text{max}} = 350 + 50.8 = 400.8$ K ≈ 128 °C. This is the **worst-case runaway temperature** for the system.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Lose 2-3 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error**: Writing $\Delta H_r$ as positive for exothermic reactions. **Always use the IUPAC convention**: Exothermic → $\Delta H_r < 0$.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in Arrhenius**: Using $E$ in kJ/mol but $R$ in J/(mol·K) without converting. **Either keep both in SI (J) or both in kJ consistently.**
>
> 3. **Inverting the Temperature Bracket**: Writing $(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ instead of $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ in the two-point Arrhenius form. **Always subtract in the order: $T_1$ first, $T_2$ second.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting van't Hoff's Sign Convention**: Using $\Delta H_r^\circ$ as positive for exothermic in van't Hoff equation. **The formula requires the actual signed value of $\Delta H_r^\circ$**.
>
> 5. **Omitting the Energy Balance for Adiabatic Reactors**: A 14-mark CSTR/PFR problem that involves temperature must include BOTH the material balance AND the energy balance for full credit.
>
> 6. **Not Stating Assumptions**: Failing to mention that $C_p$ is constant, or that $\Delta H_r$ is temperature-independent. **Always state your assumptions explicitly** in derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Heat of Reaction and Temperature**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - Heat of reaction $\Delta H_r$: **exothermic** (negative) vs **endothermic** (positive).
> - Heat of formation $\Delta H_f^\circ$: refers to forming 1 mole from elements in standard states.
> - Standard state: **298 K, 1 atm**.
> - Gas constant: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
>
> **Key Equations (Memorize These!):**
> - $\Delta H_r^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ$ (products) $- \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ$ (reactants)
> - Kirchhoff: $\Delta H_r(T) = \Delta H_r(298) + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$
> - Arrhenius: $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$
> - Two-point Arrhenius: $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$
> - van't Hoff integrated: $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta H_r^\circ/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$
> - Adiabatic CSTR: $T = T_0 + y_{A0}(-\Delta H_r)X / C_p$
>
> **Effect of Temperature:**
> - **Exothermic reactions**: Higher $T$ → **lower** $K$ → **lower** equilibrium conversion (Le Chatelier).
> - **Endothermic reactions**: Higher $T$ → **higher** $K$ → **higher** equilibrium conversion.
> - For all reactions: Higher $T$ → **higher** $k$ (Arrhenius) → **higher** rate (kinetic effect).
>
> **Reactor Energy Modes:**
> - **Adiabatic** ($Q=0$): Simple, no heat exchanger; temperature changes monotonically.
> - **Isothermal** ($T$ = constant): Used in lab-scale kinetic studies.
> - **Non-isothermal, non-adiabatic**: Industrial norm; requires full energy balance.
>
> **Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP):**
> - Applies to **reversible exothermic** reactions only.
> - $T$ should be **high at inlet** (low $X$) and **low at exit** (high $X$).
> - Found by setting $(\partial r_A / \partial T)_X = 0$.
>
> **Practical Engineering Insights:**
> - **Safety**: $\Delta T_{\text{ad,max}}$ is critical for relief valve sizing in runaway-prone systems.
> - **Economics**: Higher $T$ increases rate but decreases equilibrium conversion for exothermic reactions → **optimum exists**.
> - **Catalysis**: Lowers $E$ (kinetic) but does **not** affect $\Delta H_r$ (thermodynamic).
> - **Endothermic reactions** almost always require external heating (furnace, fired heater, or electric heating).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium Constants from Thermodynamics. General Graphical Design Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium Constants from Thermodynamics. General Graphical Design Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Equilibrium Constant — Formal KTU Definition

The **thermodynamic equilibrium constant** ($K$) of a chemical reaction is a dimensionless quantity (or dimensioned when expressed in terms of activities) that characterizes the ratio of product activities to reactant activities at equilibrium, each raised to the power of their stoichiometric coefficients.

For a general reaction:
$$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$

The thermodynamic equilibrium constant is:
$$K = \frac{a_C^c \, a_D^d}{a_A^a \, a_B^b}$$

where $a_i$ is the activity of species $i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** At KTU level, students must distinguish between $K_p$ (pressure-based), $K_c$ (concentration-based), and $K_x$ (mole-fraction-based). For ideal gases: $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n}$.

## 1.2 Connection Between Equilibrium Constant and Gibbs Free Energy

The fundamental thermodynamic relationship linking equilibrium to free energy is:
$$\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$$

where:
- $\Delta G°$ = Standard Gibbs free energy change (**J/mol**)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (**K**)
- $K$ = Equilibrium constant (dimensionless)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important?** This single equation allows the chemical engineer to predict equilibrium conversion **without performing laboratory experiments**, purely from tabulated thermodynamic data.

## 1.3 General Graphical Design Procedure — Formal Definition

The **General Graphical Design Procedure** is a powerful, equipment-independent method, originally formalized by **Octave Levenspiel**, used to determine the **optimal temperature progression** in a series of reactors (or within a single reactor) for **reversible exothermic reactions**. It works on an $X_A$ vs $T$ plot where contours of constant rate $(-r_A)$ are drawn, and the operating path is constructed to give the minimum reactor volume.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are hiking from the base camp (zero conversion) to a mountain peak (desired conversion $X_A$).** The mountain represents the rate of reaction ($-r_A$). For an **exothermic reversible** reaction, the mountain has a peculiar shape:
> - At **low temperature**: the mountain is short (low rates) — the reaction is "lazy."
> - At **high temperature**: the mountain starts shrinking again because equilibrium shifts backward (Le Chatelier).
>
> **Question:** What is the fastest route to reach the peak? The **General Graphical Design Procedure** gives the answer: it traces the **path of steepest ascent** along the ridge of maximum rates — this corresponds to the **shortest residence time** (smallest reactor volume).

For an **endothermic reaction**, the rate keeps increasing with temperature — so the strategy is simply: **use the highest allowable temperature throughout**.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Reference temperature | $T_{ref}$ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Standard pressure | $P°$ | **1 atm = 101.325 kPa** |
| Conversion target | $X_{A,f}$ | Problem-specific |
| Reaction enthalpy (typical) | $\Delta H°$ | **−50 to −200 kJ/mol** (exothermic) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundations of Equilibrium

### 2.1.1 The Three Pillars

The equilibrium constant is governed by **three thermodynamic state functions**:

1. **Gibbs Free Energy Change ($\Delta G°$)**: Tells us *whether* the reaction proceeds and *how far* (position of equilibrium).
2. **Enthalpy Change ($\Delta H°$)**: Tells us *how strongly* $K$ depends on temperature (heat effect).
3. **Entropy Change ($\Delta S°$)**: Tells us the *magnitude* of $K$ at any reference temperature.

The master equation:
$$\boxed{\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T \Delta S°}$$

Combining with $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$:
$$-RT \ln K = \Delta H° - T \Delta S°$$

Rearranging:
$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H°}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S°}{R}$$

### 2.1.2 The van't Hoff Equation — Why It Works

Differentiating $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$ with respect to $T$ at constant pressure:

Starting point:
$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta G°}{RT}$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$:
$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = -\frac{1}{R}\frac{d(\Delta G°/T)}{dT}$$

Applying the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation**:
$$\frac{d(\Delta G°/T)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H°}{T^2}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H°}{RT^2}}$$

This is the **differential form of the van't Hoff equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** 
> - If $\Delta H° < 0$ (exothermic): $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} < 0$ → $K$ **decreases** with temperature. Higher $T$ shifts equilibrium backward.
> - If $\Delta H° > 0$ (endothermic): $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} > 0$ → $K$ **increases** with temperature. Higher $T$ favors forward reaction.

## 2.2 Integrated Forms of the van't Hoff Equation

### 2.2.1 Case 1: $\Delta H°$ is approximately **constant** with $T$

Integrating between $T_1$ and $T_2$:
$$\int_{\ln K_1}^{\ln K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H°}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\boxed{\ln \frac{K_2}{K_1} = \frac{\Delta H°}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

### 2.2.2 Case 2: $\Delta H°$ varies with $T$ as $\Delta H° = \Delta H_0° + \Delta a\,T + \frac{\Delta b}{2}T^2 + \cdots$

The integrated form becomes more complex and requires numerical integration in practice.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gibbs → K relation | $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$ | Always valid |
| 2 | Gibbs decomposition | $\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$ | Always valid |
| 3 | van't Hoff (differential) | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H°}{RT^2}$ | $\Delta H°$ = const. |
| 4 | van't Hoff (integrated) | $\ln \dfrac{K_2}{K_1} = \dfrac{\Delta H°}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | $\Delta H°$ = const. |
| 5 | Linear form (y = mx + c) | $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H°}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta S°}{R}$ | Plot $\ln K$ vs $1/T$ |
| 6 | Equilibrium conversion | $K(T)$ used to solve material balance for $X_{Ae}$ | Gas/liquid phase |
| 7 | Design Equation (any reactor) | $\tau = \dfrac{F_{A0}\,dX_A}{-r_A}$ | Basis of graphical procedure |
| 8 | PFR operating line | $\dfrac{1}{-r_A} = \tau_X + \int \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | Numerical integration |
| 9 | CSTR operating line | $\dfrac{1}{-r_A}\vert_{exit} = \dfrac{\tau}{X_{Af}}$ | Algebraic |
| 10 | Optimum path for exothermic reversible | Follow ridge of maximum rates on $X_A$–$T$ plot | The graphical procedure |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ammonia Synthesis (Haber Process):** $\Delta H° = -91.8$ kJ/mol. Increasing $T$ lowers $K_{NH_3}$. Industrial plants use **quench cooling** between catalyst beds to maximize per-pass conversion.
- **Sulfuric Acid Contact Process:** $\Delta H° = -98$ kJ/mol. Optimum temperature decreases with conversion.
- **Methanol Synthesis:** Highly exothermic; multi-bed reactors with inter-stage cooling match the graphical optimum path.
- **Catalytic Cracking:** Endothermic; operate at the highest temperature consistent with catalyst stability.
- **Polymerization Reactions:** Even when not equilibrium-limited, the heat of reaction drives temperature profile selection.

## 2.5 Linking $K$ to Equilibrium Conversion $X_{Ae}$

For a gas-phase reaction with the stoichiometry shown above, applying the material balance at equilibrium yields a relationship of the form:
$$K_{eq}(T) = f(X_{Ae}, P, \varepsilon, y_{A0})$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the fractional change in moles. For an elementary case:
$$K = \frac{X_{Ae}^2 (1+\varepsilon X_{Ae})^2 \cdot P^{-\Delta n}}{4(1-X_{Ae})^2 (1+\varepsilon X_{Ae})^{-2}}$$

Solving this polynomial gives $X_{Ae}(T)$ — the **equilibrium curve** on the $X_A$–$T$ plot.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From First Law to van't Hoff Equation

**Starting point:** The definition of Gibbs free energy for a reacting system.
$$G = H - TS$$

For the reaction as a whole at standard state:
$$\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S° \qquad \text{(Eq. A)}$$

**Step 1:** At equilibrium, the chemical potentials satisfy:
$$\sum \nu_i \mu_i = 0$$

This leads directly to:
$$\Delta G° = -RT \ln K \qquad \text{(Eq. B)}$$

**Step 2:** Equate Eq. A and Eq. B:
$$-RT \ln K = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $-RT$:
$$\ln K = \frac{\Delta S°}{R} - \frac{\Delta H°}{RT}$$

This is the **linear form** (Eq. C). The slope is $-\Delta H°/R$ and intercept is $\Delta S°/R$.

**Step 4:** Differentiate Eq. C with respect to $T$, noting $\Delta H°$ and $\Delta S°$ are assumed constant:
$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H°}{R T^2}$$

This is the **van't Hoff differential equation** (Eq. D).

**Step 5:** Separate variables and integrate between $T_1, K_1$ and $T_2, K_2$:
$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H°}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

The right-side integral evaluates as:
$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2} = \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}$$

Therefore:
$$\ln\frac{K_2}{K_1} = \frac{\Delta H°}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) \qquad \text{(Eq. E)}$$

This is the **integrated van't Hoff equation** — valid when $\Delta H°$ is approximately constant over the temperature range.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equilibrium Conversion $X_{Ae}$ from $K$ for Gas-Phase Reaction

**Reaction:** $A \rightleftharpoons B$ (gas phase, isothermal, isobaric)

**Stoichiometry and material balance** at equilibrium with fractional change $\varepsilon$ (note: here $\varepsilon = y_{A0}$ for this simple stoichiometry, but generally depends on feed):

| Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $F_{A0}$ | $-F_{A0}X_{Ae}$ | $F_{A0}(1-X_{Ae})$ |
| $B$ | 0 | $+F_{A0}X_{Ae}$ | $F_{A0}X_{Ae}$ |
| Total | $F_{T0}$ | $F_{A0}X_{Ae}$ | $F_{T0}+F_{A0}X_{Ae}$ |

Mole fractions:
$$y_A = \frac{1-X_{Ae}}{1+\varepsilon X_{Ae}}, \quad y_B = \frac{X_{Ae}}{1+\varepsilon X_{Ae}}$$

For this stoichiometry, $\Delta n = 0$ and $\varepsilon = y_{A0}$.

Equilibrium relation:
$$K = \frac{y_B}{y_A} = \frac{X_{Ae}}{1-X_{Ae}}$$

Solving:
$$X_{Ae} = \frac{K}{1+K}$$

**For $A \rightleftharpoons 2B$ (gas phase, $\Delta n = 1$):**

Using the more general derivation with $\varepsilon = y_{A0}$:

$$\boxed{X_{Ae} = \frac{\sqrt{K P}}{1 + \sqrt{K P}}}$$

(when feed is pure $A$)

**Derivation in detail:**
Equilibrium partial pressures:
$$p_A = y_A P = \frac{(1-X_{Ae}) P}{1+\varepsilon X_{Ae}}, \quad p_B = y_B P = \frac{2 X_{Ae} P}{1+\varepsilon X_{Ae}}$$

Equilibrium constant:
$$K_p = \frac{p_B^2}{p_A} = \frac{[2X_{Ae}/(1+\varepsilon X_{Ae})]^2 \cdot P^2}{(1-X_{Ae})P/(1+\varepsilon X_{Ae})}$$

Simplifying:
$$K_p = \frac{4 X_{Ae}^2 P}{(1-X_{Ae})(1+\varepsilon X_{Ae})}$$

Solving the quadratic in $X_{Ae}$ yields the closed-form expressions commonly tabulated in Fogler/Levenspiel.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: General Graphical Design Procedure Logic

**Objective:** For a given conversion $X_{A,f}$, find the temperature progression $T(X_A)$ that minimizes the reactor volume.

**Step 1 — Reactor Design Equation (General):**
For any reactor type:
$$\tau = \int_{X_{A,in}}^{X_{A,out}} \frac{F_{A0}\,dX_A}{V_r (-r_A)} = \int_{X_{A,in}}^{X_{A,out}} \frac{dX_A}{(-r_A)\cdot F_A / F_{A0}}$$

Or more conveniently:
$$\boxed{\frac{V_r}{F_{A0}} = \int \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}}$$

**Step 2 — Reciprocal Rate Plot:**
The integral $\int dX_A / (-r_A)$ is the **area under the $1/(-r_A)$ vs $X_A$ curve** at the chosen temperature sequence.

**Step 3 — Temperature Dependence of Rate:**
For an elementary reversible reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$ with forward rate constant $k_1$ and reverse rate constant $k_2 = k_1/K$:
$$-r_A = k_1 \left(C_A - \frac{C_B}{K}\right)$$

Both $k_1$ and $K$ are functions of $T$:
- $k_1$ increases with $T$ (Arrhenius).
- $K$ decreases with $T$ for exothermic reactions.

**Step 4 — Rate Contours on $X_A$–$T$ Plot:**
On a plane with $X_A$ on the horizontal axis and $T$ on the vertical axis, plot curves of constant $(-r_A)$. These contours form **closed loops** for exothermic reversible reactions — a hill-like structure.

**Step 5 — Optimal Path:**
To minimize $\int dX_A/(-r_A)$, we need the **highest possible rates** at every $X_A$. The locus of maximum rates is the **ridge of the hill**. Following this ridge gives the optimal temperature sequence.

**Step 6 — Operating Line:**
For a PFR, every point $(X_A, T)$ on the path satisfies that the **area to the left** equals the residence time. The path is constructed by:
1. Drawing the locus of maximum rates (the ridge).
2. Connecting $(X_{A,in}, T_{in})$ to the ridge at the shortest temperature-time equivalent distance.
3. Following the ridge to $(X_{A,f}, T_{f})$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** For the gas-phase reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$, $\Delta H° = -75$ kJ/mol, $\Delta S° = -180$ J/(mol·K). Find $X_{Ae}$ at $T = 500$ K and 700 K (pure $A$ feed, 1 atm).

**Step 1:** Calculate $\ln K$ at each temperature.

$$\ln K = \frac{\Delta S°}{R} - \frac{\Delta H°}{R T}$$

At $T = 500$ K:
$$\ln K = \frac{-180}{8.314} - \frac{-75000}{8.314 \times 500}$$

$$\ln K = -21.65 + 18.04 = -3.61 \Rightarrow K = 0.0271$$

At $T = 700$ K:
$$\ln K = \frac{-180}{8.314} - \frac{-75000}{8.314 \times 700}$$

$$\ln K = -21.65 + 12.89 = -8.76 \Rightarrow K = 1.57 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2:** For $A \rightleftharpoons B$, $X_{Ae} = K/(1+K)$.

At 500 K: $X_{Ae} = 0.0271/1.0271 = \mathbf{0.0264}$ (2.64%)
At 700 K: $X_{Ae} = 1.57\times 10^{-4}/1.000157 \approx \mathbf{1.57\times 10^{-4}}$ (0.016%)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing temperature from 500 K to 700 K **decreased** equilibrium conversion by a factor of ~170. This is the textbook signature of an exothermic reversible reaction, and exactly why **the graphical design procedure** is needed — to pick the right temperature at each conversion level.

This Mermaid block maps the iterative decision-making process an engineer follows when applying the graphical procedure.

    decision1 -->|Endothermic| path2["Endothermic Branch"]
    path1 --> draw1["Plot XA vs T, contours of constant -rA"]
    path2 --> draw2["Plot XA vs T, contours of constant -rA"]
    draw2 --> endo1["Find maximum allowable T"]

## 4.3 Figure 3: Rate Contour Topology on the $X_A$–$T$ Plane (Schematic)

Because Mermaid cannot render 3D rate hills, we represent the rate-contour map as a hierarchical block diagram showing the closed-loop structure of rate contours for an exothermic reversible reaction.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph XA_T_Plane["XA vs T Plane with Rate Contours"]
        direction TB
        feedpoint["Feed: XA = 0, T = T0"]
        lowrate["Outer Contour: LOW rate region"]
        midrate["Middle Contour: MEDIUM rate"]
        highrate["Inner Contour: HIGH rate ridge"]
        peakrate["PEAK RATE: -rA maximum point"]
        targetpoint["Target: XA = XAf, T = Tf"]
        feedpoint --> lowrate
        lowrate --> midrate
        midrate --> highrate
        highrate --> peakrate
        peakrate --> highrate
        highrate --> targetpoint
    end
    note1["The ridge passes through the peak rate point"]
    note2["Optimal path goes: feed -> ascend ridge -> descend to target"]
    feedpoint -.follows.-> peakrate
    peakrate -.descent to.-> targetpoint
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The concentric structure represents **closed-loop rate contours** characteristic of exothermic reversible reactions. The rate increases as you approach the ridge from outside and decreases again beyond it. For an endothermic reaction, the contours would be **monotonic in T** — no closed loops — and the optimal path is simply at the highest allowable temperature.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**State and explain the van't Hoff equation. Discuss how it helps in predicting the effect of temperature on the equilibrium constant.**

**Model Answer:**
The van't Hoff equation (differential form) is:
$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H°}{RT^2}$$

It expresses how the equilibrium constant $K$ varies with absolute temperature $T$ in terms of the standard enthalpy of reaction $\Delta H°$. For **exothermic reactions** ($\Delta H° < 0$), increasing temperature **decreases** $K$, shifting equilibrium toward reactants. For **endothermic reactions** ($\Delta H° > 0$), increasing temperature **increases** $K$, shifting equilibrium toward products. The integrated form, assuming $\Delta H°$ is constant, is:
$$\ln\frac{K_2}{K_1} = \frac{\Delta H°}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

This equation allows prediction of $K$ at any temperature using one reference value of $K$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Explain the General Graphical Design Procedure for finding the optimum temperature progression in exothermic reversible reactions.**

**Model Answer:**
The General Graphical Design Procedure (developed by Levenspiel) determines the temperature progression that minimizes reactor volume for a given conversion. The procedure is:

1. **Plot the rate contours** on an $X_A$ vs $T$ plane for the exothermic reversible reaction. These contours form **closed loops** because the rate first increases with $T$ (faster kinetics) and then decreases (lower equilibrium conversion).
2. **Identify the ridge** of maximum rates — the locus of points with the highest rate at each $X_A$.
3. **Construct the operating path** from the feed condition $(X_{A,in}, T_{in})$ that stays as close to the ridge as possible while reaching the target $(X_{A,f}, T_f)$.
4. **Compute the volume** from the area under the $1/(-r_A)$ vs $X_A$ curve along this optimal path.

For endothermic reactions, the ridge is at the maximum allowable temperature throughout.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse $K_p$ with $K_c$ in gas-phase problems. Always check whether the question states **pressure units or concentration units** and use the conversion $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}$.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that the **van't Hoff equation** assumes $\Delta H°$ is constant over the temperature range — students lose marks for not stating this assumption explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In the graphical procedure, students draw the rate contours correctly but forget that the **operating path** must also satisfy the **reactor design equation** (mass balance). The path is not just the ridge — it must connect feed to product.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Mixing up signs. $\Delta G°$ is **negative** for spontaneous reactions at the given $T$, giving $K > 1$. For endothermic reactions above the crossover temperature, $K > 1$; below it, $K < 1$.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** In multi-part problems, students sometimes use **wrong equilibrium expressions** by mis-applying stoichiometric coefficients. Always write the balanced equation first.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the elementary gas-phase reaction $A \rightleftharpoons 2B$ taking place in a flow reactor with pure $A$ feed at 1 atm, derive the relationship between the equilibrium constant $K_p$ and the equilibrium conversion $X_{Ae}$. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The standard enthalpy change for the reaction is $\Delta H° = -80$ kJ/mol and $\Delta S° = -100$ J/(mol·K). Calculate the equilibrium conversion at $T = 600$ K and at $T = 800$ K. Comment on the temperature effect.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** State the equilibrium expression.
$$K_p = \frac{p_B^2}{p_A}$$

**Step 2:** Material balance. Let $F_{A0}$ = 1 mol/s for simplicity. Feed is pure $A$, so $F_{B0} = 0$, $F_{T0} = 1$.

| Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 1 | $-X_{Ae}$ | $1-X_{Ae}$ |
| $B$ | 0 | $+2X_{Ae}$ | $2X_{Ae}$ |
| Total | 1 | $+X_{Ae}$ | $1+X_{Ae}$ |

**Step 3:** Mole fractions.
$$y_A = \frac{1-X_{Ae}}{1+X_{Ae}}, \quad y_B = \frac{2X_{Ae}}{1+X_{Ae}}$$

**Step 4:** Partial pressures ($P$ = total pressure).
$$p_A = y_A P = \frac{(1-X_{Ae})P}{1+X_{Ae}}, \quad p_B = y_B P = \frac{2X_{Ae}P}{1+X_{Ae}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into $K_p$ expression.
$$K_p = \frac{[2X_{Ae}/(1+X_{Ae})]^2 \cdot P^2}{(1-X_{Ae})P/(1+X_{Ae})}$$

$$K_p = \frac{4 X_{Ae}^2 \cdot P}{(1-X_{Ae})(1+X_{Ae})} = \frac{4 P X_{Ae}^2}{1-X_{Ae}^2}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{K_p = \frac{4 P X_{Ae}^2}{1-X_{Ae}^2}}$$

**Assumptions stated:**
1. Ideal gas behavior.
2. Isothermal and isobaric operation.
3. Pure $A$ feed, $\varepsilon = y_{A0} = 1$.
4. Equilibrium is established.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Calculate $\ln K$ at each temperature using:
$$\ln K = \frac{\Delta S°}{R} - \frac{\Delta H°}{RT}$$

Compute common factor:
$$\frac{\Delta S°}{R} = \frac{-100}{8.314} = -12.028$$

**At $T = 600$ K:**
$$\frac{\Delta H°}{RT} = \frac{-80000}{8.314 \times 600} = \frac{-80000}{4988.4} = -16.038$$

$$\ln K_{600} = -12.028 - (-16.038) = -12.028 + 16.038 = 4.010$$

$$K_{600} = e^{4.010} = 55.15$$

**At $T = 800$ K:**
$$\frac{\Delta H°}{RT} = \frac{-80000}{8.314 \times 800} = \frac{-80000}{6651.2} = -12.028$$

$$\ln K_{800} = -12.028 - (-12.028) = 0$$

$$K_{800} = e^0 = 1.000$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $X_{Ae}$ from the derived relation $K_p = \frac{4 P X_{Ae}^2}{1-X_{Ae}^2}$, with $P = 1$ atm:

**At $T = 600$ K, $K_p = 55.15$:**
$$55.15 (1-X_{Ae}^2) = 4 X_{Ae}^2$$
$$55.15 - 55.15 X_{Ae}^2 = 4 X_{Ae}^2$$
$$55.15 = 59.15 X_{Ae}^2$$
$$X_{Ae}^2 = 0.9324$$
$$X_{Ae} = 0.9656 \text{ or } 96.56\%$$

**At $T = 800$ K, $K_p = 1.000$:**
$$1.000 (1-X_{Ae}^2) = 4 X_{Ae}^2$$
$$1 - X_{Ae}^2 = 4 X_{Ae}^2$$
$$1 = 5 X_{Ae}^2$$
$$X_{Ae} = \sqrt{0.2} = 0.4472 \text{ or } 44.72\%$$

**Step 3: Comment.**
The equilibrium conversion **drops dramatically from 96.56% to 44.72%** as temperature rises from 600 K to 800 K. This is consistent with the **Le Chatelier principle** for an **exothermic reaction** ($\Delta H° < 0$). Hence, **lower temperatures favor higher conversion**, but at the cost of slower rates. This trade-off is precisely what the **General Graphical Design Procedure** resolves by choosing an optimal temperature sequence.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the integrated form of the van't Hoff equation from the differential form. State clearly all assumptions and the form of the equation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the ammonia synthesis reaction $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$, the equilibrium constant $K_p = 6.8 \times 10^4$ atm$^{-2}$ at 700 K and $\Delta H° = -92.4$ kJ/mol. Calculate $K_p$ at 800 K using the van't Hoff equation. Also find $\Delta S°$ at 700 K.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Start from differential form.
$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H°}{RT^2}$$

**Step 2:** State the assumption: $\Delta H°$ = constant (independent of $T$).

**Step 3:** Separate variables.
$$d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H°}{R}\cdot\frac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate between limits $(T_1, K_1)$ and $(T_2, K_2)$.
$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H°}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln\frac{K_2}{K_1} = \frac{\Delta H°}{R}\left[\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Equivalent logarithmic form.
$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H°}{R T} + \frac{\Delta S°}{R}$$

This is the **linear form** valid when $\Delta H°$ and $\Delta S°$ are constant.

**Assumptions:**
1. $\Delta H°$ is independent of temperature (valid over moderate ranges, ~100 K).
2. The reaction mixture behaves ideally (activities = mole fractions × fugacity coefficients).
3. Pressure is low enough for ideal-gas behavior.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Calculate $K_p$ at 800 K using van't Hoff.

Given: $K_1 = 6.8 \times 10^4$ at $T_1 = 700$ K; $T_2 = 800$ K; $\Delta H° = -92.4$ kJ/mol = $-92400$ J/mol.

$$\ln\frac{K_2}{K_1} = \frac{\Delta H°}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Compute:
$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{700} - \frac{1}{800} = \frac{800 - 700}{700 \times 800} = \frac{100}{560000} = 1.7857 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{\Delta H°}{R} = \frac{-92400}{8.314} = -11113.8 \text{ K}$$

$$\ln\frac{K_2}{K_1} = (-11113.8)(1.7857 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.985$$

$$\frac{K_2}{K_1} = e^{-1.985} = 0.1375$$

$$K_2 = 0.1375 \times 6.8 \times 10^4 = 9350$$

$$\boxed{K_p(800\text{ K}) = 9.35 \times 10^3 \text{ atm}^{-2}}$$

**Step 2:** Find $\Delta S°$ at 700 K using $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K = \Delta H° - T \Delta S°$.

$$\Delta G°_{700} = -(8.314)(700)\ln(6.8 \times 10^4)$$
$$= -5819.8 \times (\ln 6.8 + 4 \ln 10)$$
$$= -5819.8 \times (1.9169 + 9.2103)$$
$$= -5819.8 \times 11.1272$$
$$= -64{,}758 \text{ J/mol} = -64.76 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Now:
$$\Delta S° = \frac{\Delta H° - \Delta G°}{T} = \frac{-92.4 - (-64.76)}{700} = \frac{-27.64}{700}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S°_{700} = -0.0395 \text{ kJ/(mol·K)} = -39.5 \text{ J/(mol·K)}}$$

**Comment:** $K_p$ **decreased** from $6.8 \times 10^4$ to $9.35 \times 10^3$ when temperature increased from 700 K to 800 K — consistent with the exothermic nature of ammonia synthesis. This is why industrial Haber plants operate at moderate temperatures (~700 K) and use very high pressures to compensate for the lower equilibrium constant.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield KTU Points**

**Equilibrium Constants from Thermodynamics:**
- ☐ Master equation: $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$ — **memorize this.**
- ☐ Decomposition: $\ln K = \dfrac{\Delta S°}{R} - \dfrac{\Delta H°}{R T}$ — linear in $1/T$.
- ☐ Slope of $\ln K$ vs $1/T$ plot gives $-\Delta H°/R$.
- ☐ van't Hoff differential: $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H°}{RT^2}$.
- ☐ van't Hoff integrated: $\ln\dfrac{K_2}{K_1} = \dfrac{\Delta H°}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$.
- ☐ **Exothermic** ($\Delta H°<0$): $K$ **decreases** with $T$.
- ☐ **Endothermic** ($\Delta H°>0$): $K$ **increases** with $T$.
- ☐ Gas-phase conversion needs $\varepsilon$ and $\Delta n$ in the material balance.
- ☐ For $A \rightleftharpoons 2B$ with pure $A$: $K_p = \dfrac{4 P X_{Ae}^2}{1-X_{Ae}^2}$.
- ☐ For $A \rightleftharpoons B$ with pure $A$: $X_{Ae} = K/(1+K)$.

**General Graphical Design Procedure:**
- ☐ Applies to **reversible reactions** where equilibrium limits conversion.
- ☐ Plots $X_A$ (x-axis) vs $T$ (y-axis) with **rate contours** $(-r_A)$.
- ☐ **Exothermic reversible**: rate contours form **closed loops**; the ridge is the optimal path.
- ☐ **Endothermic**: operate at the **maximum allowable temperature** throughout.
- ☐ The optimal path goes from feed $(X_{A,in}, T_{in})$ to $(X_{A,f}, T_f)$ staying on the ridge.
- ☐ Reactor volume $V_r/F_{A0} = \int dX_A/(-r_A)$ = area under $1/(-r_A)$ curve along the path.
- ☐ For PFR, the path is **continuous** along the ridge.
- ☐ For CSTRs in series, choose $T$ for each tank to be as close to the ridge as possible.
- ☐ Multi-bed PFR with inter-stage cooling approximates the optimal path.
- ☐ **Engineering principle**: optimum balances **kinetics** (favors high $T$) and **equilibrium** (favors low $T$ for exothermic).

**Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ Always **convert ΔH°** to J/mol when using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- ☐ Check units of $K_p$ — they depend on the **change in moles** for gas reactions.
- ☐ In van't Hoff, do not forget the **sign** of $\Delta H°$.
- ☐ For graphical procedure, always **state the assumptions**: ideal gas, well-mixed, etc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimum Temperature Progression. Heat Effects: Adiabatic Operations and Non-Adiabatic Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Optimum Temperature Progression. Heat Effects: Adiabatic Operations and Non-Adiabatic Operations # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP) ### Formal Definition **Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP)** is the locus of temperatures, one corresponding to each conversion level, at which the instantaneous rate of a single reversible reaction is maximized. For an **exothermic reversible reaction**, the OTP is a **monotonically decreasing function of conversion** (Xₐ), while for an **endothermic reversible reaction**, the OTP is a **monotonically increasing function of conversion**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCHT503 — Module 3):** > The OTP concept is central to the design of reactors for exothermic reversible reactions such as SO₂ oxidation, ammonia synthesis, and water-gas shift. The student must clearly distinguish between reversible and irreversible kinetics while locating the temperature that maximizes the rate at a *given* conversion. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sailing Tacking" Analogy Imagine you are sailing a boat from point A to point B **against the wind**. You cannot sail directly into the wind — you must *tack* (zig-zag). The OTP concept is similar: - **Rate of reaction** = boat speed (you want to maximize this). - **Equilibrium conversion** = how close you are to destination B. - **Temperature** = angle of your sail. - For an exothermic reversible reaction, at *low* Xₐ, "speed" (rate) matters most — sail wide (high T). At *high* Xₐ, "destination" (equilibrium) matters — sail close to the wind (lower T). ### Mathematical Statement of the Problem For the reversible elementary reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$ with forward and reverse rate constants $k_1$ and $k_2$, the OTP is found by solving: $$\left( \frac{\partial r_A}{\partial T} \right)_{X_A,\,P} = 0$$ at each fixed conversion $X_A$. --- ## 1.2 Adiabatic Operations ### Formal Definition An **adiabatic operation** is one in which **no heat is transferred** between the reacting mixture and the surroundings. All the heat released (exothermic) or absorbed (endothermic) by the reaction accumulates within the reaction mass, causing a temperature change. > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Condition for Adiabatic Operation:** $U A (T - T_a) = 0$, where $U$ is the overall heat-transfer coefficient, $A$ is the heat-transfer area, and $T_a$ is the ambient/coolant temperature. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Sealed Thermos Flask Picture a reaction mixture inside a perfectly insulated thermos flask (a vacuum-walled, silvered container). Heat has nowhere to escape, so the temperature inside rises (for an exothermic reaction) as the reaction proceeds. The flask's contents get progressively hotter — this is exactly how an adiabatic reactor behaves. --- ## 1.3 Non-Adiabatic Operations ### Formal Definition A **non-adiabatic operation** is one in which heat is deliberately exchanged between the reacting mixture and an external heating/cooling medium (coolant or heating fluid) through a heat-transfer surface (jacket, coil, or external heat exchanger). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Non-adiabatic operation allows the engineer to *follow* the OTP curve closely, dramatically increasing conversion and selectivity for exothermic reversible reactions. Industrial examples include multi-tubular catalytic reactors (e.g., methanol synthesis, ammonia converters). ### Conceptual Analogy — The Controlled Cooker A pressure cooker with a temperature regulator is non-adiabatic. The heater element adds heat, and a controlled valve (or cooling coil) removes excess heat to *maintain* the temperature at the optimum value for each cooking stage. The reactor engineer does the same with a coolant jacket. --- ## 1.4 Why Temperature Progression Matters > [!NOTE] > **Effect of Temperature on Reaction Rate and Equilibrium:** > - **Irreversible reactions:** Always favor higher T (Arrhenius). No upper bound except material limits. > - **Reversible Exothermic:** A trade-off exists — higher T accelerates rate but depresses $K_{eq}$, lowering equilibrium conversion. > - **Reversible Endothermic:** Higher T is *always* better (both rate and $K_{eq}$ increase). | Reaction Type | Rate with ↑T | $K_{eq}$ with ↑T | Net Optimum | |---|---|---|---| | Irreversible | ↑ | — | Highest allowable T | | Reversible Exothermic | ↑ then ↓ | ↓ | **OTP** (decreasing) | | Reversible Endothermic | ↑ | ↑ | Highest allowable T | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Temperature Effects — Two Fundamental Laws ### 2.1.1 Arrhenius Law (Kinetic Effect) $$k(T) = A \, \exp\!\left( -\frac{E}{R T} \right)$$ - $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor), units depend on reaction order - $E$ = activation energy (J/mol or cal/mol) — **standard metric: kJ/mol** - $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ — universal gas constant - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) **Why it matters:** Differentiating $k$ with respect to $T$ gives the *temperature sensitivity* of the rate constant: $$\frac{1}{k}\frac{dk}{dT} = \frac{E}{R T^{2}}$$ Reactions with large $E$ are *more* sensitive to temperature changes. ### 2.1.2 Van't Hoff Equation (Thermodynamic Effect) $$\frac{d \ln K_{eq}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}}$$ - For **exothermic** ($\Delta H^\circ < 0$): $K_{eq}$ *decreases* with $T$. - For **endothermic** ($\Delta H^\circ > 0$): $K_{eq}$ *increases* with $T$. ### 2.2 Mathematical Form of the Rate for $A \rightleftharpoons B$ $$r_A = k_1 \, C_A - k_2 \, C_B = k_1\!\left( C_A - \frac{C_B}{K_{eq}} \right)$$ with $K_{eq} = k_1 / k_2 = A_1/A_2 \cdot \exp\!\left[ -(E_1 - E_2)/RT \right]$. ### 2.3 Energy Balance Coupling In any reactor, mass and energy balances are *coupled*. For a **PFR**: **Mass balance:** $$\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$$ **Energy balance (general):** $$\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} \, \frac{dT}{dV} = -r_A \, \Delta H_{Rx} - U A \, (T - T_a)$$ For an **adiabatic** PFR (UA = 0): $$\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} \, \frac{dT}{dV} = -r_A \, \Delta H_{Rx}$$ For a **CSTR (non-adiabatic):** $$\sum_i F_{i,0} C_{p_i} (T - T_0) + U A \, (T_a - T) = -r_A \, V \, \Delta H_{Rx}$$ --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Equation | Meaning / When to Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ | Arrhenius rate constant | | 2 | $\frac{d \ln K_{eq}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ | Van't Hoff (equilibrium) | | 3 | $K_{eq}(T) = K_{eq}(T_1) \exp\!\left[ \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T} \right) \right]$ | Integrated Van't Hoff | | 4 | $T = T_0 + \frac{-\Delta H_{Rx} \cdot X_A}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}$ | **Adiabatic T–X relationship** (PFR) | | 5 | $T_{ad,\max} = T_0 + \frac{-\Delta H_{Rx}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}$ | **Adiabatic temperature rise** at $X_A = 1$ | | 6 | $\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{-\Delta H_{Rx} \cdot F_{A0}}{\sum_i F_{i,0} C_{p_i}}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise (absolute form) | | 7 | $T_{opt}(X_A) = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{R \ln\!\left[ \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1-X_A} \right]}$ | **OTP for A ⇌ B** | | 8 | $\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} \frac{dT}{dV} = -r_A \Delta H_{Rx} - UA(T - T_a)$ | PFR energy balance (general) | | 9 | $\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} \frac{dT}{dF_A} = -\Delta H_{Rx} - \frac{UA(T-T_a)}{r_A}$ | PFR energy balance in X-form | | 10 | $\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$ | CSTR space time | | 11 | $\tau_{PFR} = \int_0^{X_A} \frac{F_{A0}\, dX_A}{-r_A}$ | PFR space time | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Formula (4) is the workhorse for adiabatic reactor sizing. Formula (7) is the OTP and is the basis of all industrial catalytic converter designs (e.g., SO₂ → SO₃ in sulfuric acid plants). --- ## 2.5 Graphical Picture of OTP (Qualitative) - **Rate vs T** at fixed $X_A$ for exothermic reversible: a **convex curve** with a single maximum. - The locus of these maxima (one per $X_A$) traces the **OTP curve**. - The OTP curve **always lies below the equilibrium curve** in the $T$–$X_A$ plane. - The area between OTP and equilibrium is the *kinetically feasible* operating region. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP) ### Starting Point — Reversible Elementary Reaction Consider the elementary reversible reaction: $$A \rightleftharpoons B$$ with rate expression (per unit volume): $$r_A = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_B$$ where $$k_1 = A_1 \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_1}{RT} \right), \qquad k_2 = A_2 \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_2}{RT} \right)$$ The thermodynamic equilibrium constant is: $$K_{eq} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} = \frac{A_1}{A_2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_1 - E_2}{RT} \right)$$ **Step 1: Rewrite the rate in terms of conversion.** For constant-density batch or plug-flow with $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and $C_B = C_{A0} X_A$: $$r_A = k_1 C_{A0} (1 - X_A) - k_2 C_{A0} X_A$$ $$\boxed{\, r_A = C_{A0} \left[ k_1 (1 - X_A) - k_2 X_A \right] \,}$$ **Step 2: Apply the OTP condition.** We seek $T$ that maximizes $r_A$ at *fixed* $X_A$: $$\left( \frac{\partial r_A}{\partial T} \right)_{X_A} = 0$$ **Step 3: Differentiate.** Since $C_{A0}$ and $X_A$ are held constant: $$\frac{\partial r_A}{\partial T} = C_{A0} \left[ \frac{\partial k_1}{\partial T}(1 - X_A) - \frac{\partial k_2}{\partial T} X_A \right] = 0$$ $$\frac{\partial k_1}{\partial T}(1 - X_A) = \frac{\partial k_2}{\partial T} X_A$$ **Step 4: Use Arrhenius temperature dependence.** Recall that: $$\frac{d k_i}{d T} = k_i \cdot \frac{E_i}{R T^{2}}$$ Therefore: $$k_1 \frac{E_1}{R T^{2}} (1 - X_A) = k_2 \frac{E_2}{R T^{2}} X_A$$ The $\frac{1}{RT^2}$ factor cancels on both sides: $$k_1 E_1 (1 - X_A) = k_2 E_2 X_A$$ **Step 5: Rearrange to isolate $k_1 / k_2$.** $$\frac{k_1}{k_2} = \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$$ But $k_1 / k_2 = K_{eq}$, so: $$\boxed{\, K_{eq}(T_{opt}) = \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A} \,}$$ This is a **powerful result**: at the OTP for a given $X_A$, the *kinetic* equilibrium constant equals a *stoichiometric* expression scaled by the activation-energy ratio. **Step 6: Convert to an explicit formula for $T_{opt}$.** Substituting $K_{eq} = \frac{A_1}{A_2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_1 - E_2}{RT} \right)$: $$\frac{A_1}{A_2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_1 - E_2}{R T_{opt}} \right) = \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$$ Rearranging the exponential: $$\exp\!\left( \frac{E_2 - E_1}{R T_{opt}} \right) = \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$$ Taking the natural logarithm: $$\frac{E_2 - E_1}{R T_{opt}} = \ln\!\left[ \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A} \right]$$ Solving for $T_{opt}$: $$\boxed{\, T_{opt}(X_A) = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{R \, \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A} \right]} \,}$$ **Step 7: Interpretation of the result.** For an **exothermic reaction** ($E_1 < E_2$, i.e., $E_2 - E_1 > 0$): as $X_A \to 1$, the argument of the logarithm grows without bound, so $\ln(\cdots)$ grows. Hence $T_{opt}$ **decreases** with $X_A$. ✔ For an **endothermic reaction** ($E_1 > E_2$): the sign of $E_2 - E_1$ flips, and the optimum temperature becomes complex/meaningless — the engineer simply uses the maximum allowable temperature. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Adiabatic T–X Relationship ### Setup — Adiabatic PFR Take the **mass balance**: $$\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -F_{A0}\, dX_A = r_A \, dV$$ Take the **energy balance** (adiabatic, $UA = 0$): $$\sum_i F_i C_{p_i}\, \frac{dT}{dV} = -r_A \, \Delta H_{Rx}$$ **Step 1: Divide the energy balance by the mass balance.** $$\frac{\sum_i F_i C_{p_i}\, dT}{dV} \cdot \frac{dV}{-F_{A0}\, dX_A} = \frac{-r_A \, \Delta H_{Rx}}{r_A}$$ **Step 2: Simplify.** $$\frac{\sum_i F_i C_{p_i}\, dT}{-F_{A0}\, dX_A} = -\Delta H_{Rx}$$ Multiply both sides by $-F_{A0}$: $$\sum_i F_i C_{p_i}\, dT = F_{A0}\, \Delta H_{Rx}\, dX_A$$ **Step 3: Introduce dimensionless heat-capacity group.** Define $\Theta_i = F_{i,0}/F_{A0}$ (ratio of inlet flow of species $i$ to inlet flow of A). For a single reaction $A \to B$ with constant molar flow (e.g., $\varepsilon = 0$): $$F_i = F_{i,0} + \nu_i F_{A0} X_A$$ If heat capacities are approximately constant and we assume $F_T$ is constant (valid for $\varepsilon = 0$ or liquid-phase), then: $$\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} = F_{A0} \sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}$$ Substituting: $$F_{A0} \left( \sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i} \right) dT = F_{A0}\, \Delta H_{Rx}\, dX_A$$ $$dT = \frac{\Delta H_{Rx}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}\, dX_A$$ **Step 4: Integrate from $T_0$ to $T$ and $0$ to $X_A$.** $$\int_{T_0}^{T} dT = \int_0^{X_A} \frac{\Delta H_{Rx}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}\, dX_A$$ Assuming $\Delta H_{Rx}$ is approximately constant (or evaluated at a reference temperature): $$T = T_0 + \frac{\Delta H_{Rx} \cdot X_A}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}$$ $$\boxed{\, T = T_0 + \frac{X_A \, \Delta H_{Rx}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}} \,}$$ Since $\Delta H_{Rx} < 0$ for exothermic reactions, $T > T_0$ — temperature *rises* with conversion, as physically expected. **Step 5: Special case — Adiabatic Flame / Equilibrium Temperature.** At $X_A = 1$ (full conversion), the maximum adiabatic temperature is: $$T_{ad,\max} = T_0 + \frac{\Delta H_{Rx}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Non-Adiabatic Energy Balance (PFR) **Step 1: Start from the general energy balance.** $$\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} \, \frac{dT}{dV} = -r_A \, \Delta H_{Rx} - U A\, (T - T_a)$$ **Step 2: Use the chain rule to convert to $X_A$ form.** Since $dV = -F_{A0}\, dX_A / r_A$ (from mass balance): $$\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} \, \frac{dT}{-F_{A0}\, dX_A / r_A} = -r_A \, \Delta H_{Rx} - U A\, (T - T_a)$$ **Step 3: Simplify.** $$-\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} \cdot \frac{r_A}{F_{A0}} \cdot \frac{dT}{dX_A} = -r_A \, \Delta H_{Rx} - U A\, (T - T_a)$$ Divide both sides by $-r_A$: $$\frac{\sum_i F_i C_{p_i}}{F_{A0}} \cdot \frac{dT}{dX_A} = \Delta H_{Rx} + \frac{U A\, (T - T_a)}{r_A}$$ Recognizing $\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} / F_{A0} \approx \sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}$ (for dilute mixtures or constant $F_T$): $$\boxed{\, \frac{dT}{dX_A} = \frac{\Delta H_{Rx} + \dfrac{U A\, (T - T_a)}{r_A}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}} \,}$$ **Step 4: Limiting cases check.** - **Adiabatic:** $UA = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{dT}{dX_A} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{Rx}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}$ ✔ (matches §3.2) - **Isothermal:** $\dfrac{dT}{dX_A} = 0 \Rightarrow U A\, (T - T_a) = -r_A \, \Delta H_{Rx}$ ✔ (the heat removal exactly balances heat generation) --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Operating Mode | Heat Removal Term | Governing T–X Relationship | Practical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Adiabatic** | $UA(T - T_a) = 0$ | Linear: $T = T_0 + X_A \Delta H_{Rx} / \sum \Theta_i C_{p_i}$ | Simple, no control | | **Non-Adiabatic (PFR)** | $UA(T - T_a) \neq 0$ | Non-linear ODE (coupled with mass balance) | Industrial reactors | | **Isothermal** | $UA(T - T_a) = -r_A \Delta H_{Rx}$ | $T = $ const. | Lab-scale, sensitive reactions | | **Adiabatic CSTR** | $UA = 0$ | Algebraic: $T - T_0 = -\Delta H_{Rx} \tau / \sum \Theta_i C_{p_i}$ | Tank reactors | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reactor Block Architectures ### 4.1.1 Adiabatic vs Non-Adiabatic PFR (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ADIABATIC[Adiabatic PFR Configuration] A1[Feed: T0, FA0, XA0=0] --> A2[Reactor Tube
UA equals zero
Insulated shell] A2 --> A3[Product Stream:
T greater than T0
XA equals XA_final] A2 -. No heat exchange .-> A4[Surroundings] end subgraph NONADIABATIC[Non-Adiabatic PFR Configuration] N1[Feed: T0, FA0] --> N2[Reactor Tube
with jacket] N2 --> N3[Product Stream:
T can follow OTP
XA increased] N4[Coolant Inlet Ta_in] --> N5[Jacket Annulus] N5 --> N6[Coolant Outlet Ta_out] N5 -. Heat exchange UA .-> N2 end ``` ### 4.1.2 OTP Trajectory & Reactor Operating Path (Adiabatic vs Non-Adiabatic) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Identify Reaction Type and Inlet Conditions] --> Q1{Reversible
Exothermic} Q1 -- Yes --> Path1[Compute OTP curve using T_opt formula] Q1 -- No --> Path2[Use highest allowable T] Path1 --> Q2{Operating Mode} Q2 -- Adiabatic --> A1[Linear T-X path
from T0 to T_ad_max] Q2 -- Non-Adiabatic --> A2[Engineered T-X path
tracking OTP closely] A1 --> End1[Intersection with equilibrium
gives X_A_adiabatic_eq] A2 --> End2[Reactor approximates OTP
Maximizes X_A and rate] Path2 --> End3[Isothermal or rising T] ``` ### 4.1.3 Multiple Steady States in Non-Adiabatic CSTR (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q_R[Heat Generation Curve Q_R versus T
rises then falls] -. Intersects .-> Q_C[Heat Removal Line Q_C versus T
UA T minus Ta] Q_C --> P1{Number of
Intersections} Q_R --> P1 P1 -- One --> S1[Single Steady State] P1 -- Two --> S2[Two Steady States
One stable, one unstable] P1 -- Three --> S3[Three Steady States
Typical for highly exothermic] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Industrial Multi-Bed Adiabatic Reactor with Interstage Cooling (SO₂ Oxidation) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BED1[Bed 1: Adiabatic] F1[Hot Feed T1] --> B1[Converter] B1 --> O1[Outlet: T2 higher than T1, XA1 partial] end O1 --> HX1[Heat Exchanger 1
Cool to T3] HX1 --> B2[Bed 2: Adiabatic] B2 --> O2[Outlet: T4 higher than T3, XA2 greater than XA1] O2 --> HX2[Heat Exchanger 2] HX2 --> B3[Bed 3] B3 --> PROD[Final Product:
T_final, XA_final maximized] HX1 -. Cooling medium .-> CM1[Cold Stream In] HX2 -. Cooling medium .-> CM2[Cold Stream In] note1[Note: Each bed operates adiabatically.
Interstage coolers approximate OTP
and push conversion toward equilibrium.] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Note:** Mermaid is used here for *architectural flow representation*. For precise P&ID-style process diagrams, students should refer to standard process-flowsheet conventions in Fogler/Levenspiel textbooks. ## 4.3 OTP Curve — Conceptual Plot The classic OTP plot has $T$ on the y-axis and $X_A$ on the x-axis. Three curves appear: 1. **Equilibrium curve** ($r_A = 0$): high at low $X_A$, decreasing to a low asymptote. 2. **OTP curve**: lies just below equilibrium; monotonically decreasing for exothermic reversible. 3. **Adiabatic operating line**: straight line from $(0, T_0)$ with slope $\Delta H_{Rx}/\sum \Theta_i C_{p_i}$. A non-adiabatic reactor can *depart* from the adiabatic line and approach the OTP curve, dramatically increasing equilibrium-allowed conversion. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP) for a single reversible exothermic reaction. With the help of a neat sketch, explain how the OTP varies with conversion.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP) is the locus of temperatures — one for each conversion — at which the instantaneous rate of a reversible reaction is maximized. **Behaviour (1 mark):** For a single reversible *exothermic* reaction, the OTP is a **monotonically decreasing function of conversion**. At low $X_A$, kinetics (rate) is the limiting factor, so a higher temperature is preferred. At high $X_A$, equilibrium becomes limiting (since $K_{eq}$ decreases with $T$), so a lower temperature is preferred. **Sketch (1 mark):** ``` T │ │\\ Equilibrium Curve │ \\___________ │ \\ OTP Curve │ \\_______ │ \\ / │ \\ / Adiabatic Operating Line │________\\/_________ │ / X_A ``` --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between adiabatic and non-adiabatic reactor operations. State the energy balance equation for a PFR in each case.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Adiabatic PFR | Non-Adiabatic PFR | |---|---|---| | Heat transfer term | $UA(T - T_a) = 0$ | $UA(T - T_a) \neq 0$ | | Energy balance | $\sum F_i C_{p_i} \, dT/dV = -r_A \Delta H_{Rx}$ | $\sum F_i C_{p_i} \, dT/dV = -r_A \Delta H_{Rx} - UA(T - T_a)$ | | T–X relationship | Linear | Non-linear ODE | | Conversion | Limited (adiabatic eq.) | Can approach equilibrium via OTP | (3 marks distributed: 1 mark for clear distinction + 1 mark for correct adiabatic equation + 1 mark for correct non-adiabatic equation.) --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice Based) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Optimum Temperature Progression** for the elementary reversible reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$ in a PFR operating at constant pressure. State clearly the conditions under which the OTP decreases monotonically with conversion. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The elementary liquid-phase reversible reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$ is carried out in a PFR. The inlet is pure $A$ at $C_{A0} = 2$ mol/L. The following data are given: $$k_1 = 4 \times 10^{4} \, \exp(-10000/T)\ \text{s}^{-1}, \quad k_2 = 2 \times 10^{4} \, \exp(-8000/T)\ \text{s}^{-1}$$ **(i)** Show that this reaction is exothermic. **(1 mark)** **(ii)** Determine $T_{opt}$ at $X_A = 0.5$. **(3 marks)** **(iii)** If the reactor is operated adiabatically with $T_0 = 350$ K and $\sum \Theta_i C_{p_i} = 200$ J/(mol·K), $\Delta H_{Rx} = -25000$ J/mol, estimate the **adiabatic equilibrium conversion** by graphical reasoning if the equilibrium curve in the $T$–$X_A$ plane passes through (T = 300 K, X = 0.95), (T = 350 K, X = 0.70), (T = 400 K, X = 0.45). **(3 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A** **(a) Derivation of OTP (7 marks):** See **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. The student is expected to write: 1. Starting rate expression: $r_A = k_1 C_{A0}(1 - X_A) - k_2 C_{A0} X_A$ (1 mark) 2. OTP condition: $(\partial r_A/\partial T)_{X_A} = 0$ (1 mark) 3. Differentiation: $\dfrac{\partial k_1}{\partial T}(1 - X_A) = \dfrac{\partial k_2}{\partial T} X_A$ (1 mark) 4. Arrhenius substitution: $k_1 E_1 (1 - X_A) = k_2 E_2 X_A$ (1 mark) 5. Rearrangement: $K_{eq}(T_{opt}) = (E_2/E_1) \cdot X_A/(1-X_A)$ (1 mark) 6. Final explicit formula: $T_{opt} = \dfrac{E_2 - E_1}{R \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A} \right]}$ (1 mark) 7. **Condition for monotonic decrease (1 mark):** Reaction is exothermic → $E_1 < E_2$ (so $E_2 - E_1 > 0$). As $X_A$ increases, the argument of the logarithm grows monotonically, so $T_{opt}$ decreases monotonically. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Exothermic check (1 mark):** The difference in activation energies: $E_1 - E_2 = 10000 - 8000 = 2000$ (in units of $R T$ form, but signs indicate the forward is more activated). For the equilibrium constant: $$K_{eq} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} = \frac{4 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times 10^{4}} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{10000 - 8000}{T} \right) = 2 \, \exp(-2000/T)$$ Since $K_{eq}$ **decreases** with $T$ (negative exponent in the form), $\Delta H_{Rx} < 0$. The reaction is **exothermic**. ✔ **(ii) $T_{opt}$ at $X_A = 0.5$ (3 marks):** Plug into the OTP formula: $$T_{opt} = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{R \ln\!\left[ \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A} \right]}$$ Substituting $A_1 = 4 \times 10^{4}$, $A_2 = 2 \times 10^{4}$, $E_1 = 10000$ K, $E_2 = 8000$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $X_A = 0.5$: $$\frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{X_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{2 \times 10^{4}}{4 \times 10^{4}} \cdot \frac{8000}{10000} \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.5}$$ $$= 0.5 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 1.0 = 0.4$$ $$\ln(0.4) = -0.9163$$ $$T_{opt} = \frac{8000 - 10000}{8.314 \times (-0.9163)} = \frac{-2000}{-7.617} = 262.6 \text{ K}$$ **Note:** The negative result here signals that the OTP is **physically unattainable** below 0 K for this particular parameter set, meaning the *maximum allowable* (not true OTP) temperature controls the design at $X_A = 0.5$. The student should comment on this. **(iii) Adiabatic equilibrium conversion (3 marks):** The adiabatic operating line: $$T = T_0 + \frac{\Delta H_{Rx} \cdot X_A}{\sum \Theta_i C_{p_i}} = 350 + \frac{(-25000) X_A}{200} = 350 - 125 X_A$$ Compute intersections with the equilibrium curve: - Try $X_A = 0.5$: $T = 350 - 62.5 = 287.5$ K. Equilibrium at $T = 287.5$ K: interpolate. From (300 K, 0.95) → (350 K, 0.70): at 287.5 K → $X_{eq} \approx 0.95 + (300-287.5)/50 \times (0.70 - 0.95) = 0.95 + 0.25 \times (-0.25) = 0.95 - 0.0625 = 0.8875$ - Try $X_A = 0.7$: $T = 350 - 87.5 = 262.5$ K. Equilibrium ≈ $0.95 + (37.5/50) \times (-0.25) = 0.95 - 0.1875 = 0.7625$ The **adiabatic equilibrium conversion** is the value where the operating line meets the equilibrium curve. Iterating: At $X_A = 0.6$: $T = 350 - 75 = 275$ K. Equilibrium at 275 K: - From (300 K, 0.95) → (350 K, 0.70): at 275 K (25 K below 300 K): $X_{eq} \approx 0.95 + (25/50)(0.70 - 0.95) = 0.95 - 0.125 = 0.825$ So at $T = 275$ K, equilibrium $X_A \approx 0.825$, but operating line gives only $X_A = 0.6$. **Operating line lies below equilibrium** — reaction is not yet at equilibrium. At $X_A = 0.85$: $T = 350 - 106.25 = 243.75$ K. Equilibrium at 243.75 K: extrapolating, $X_{eq} \approx 1.0$. So intersection occurs approximately at $X_A \approx 0.81$ to $0.85$. **Adiabatic equilibrium conversion $\approx 0.83$.** --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a)** For a PFR carrying out the exothermic reaction $A \to B$, derive the relationship between temperature and conversion under **adiabatic** operating conditions. Use this to define the **adiabatic temperature rise**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The gas-phase reaction $A + B \to C + D$ is conducted in an adiabatic PFR. Inlet conditions: $T_0 = 400$ K, $F_{A0} = 10$ mol/s, inerts absent, $\Delta H_{Rx} = -80000$ J/mol. Heat capacities (assumed constant): $C_{pA} = 30$, $C_{pB} = 25$, $C_{pC} = 35$, $C_{pD} = 40$ J/(mol·K). Inlet ratio: $F_{B0}/F_{A0} = 2$. **(All heat capacities in J/(mol·K).)** **(i)** Write the energy balance in differential form and derive the T–X_A relationship. **(2 marks)** **(ii)** Determine the **adiabatic temperature rise** $\Delta T_{ad}$ at $X_A = 1$. **(3 marks)** **(iii)** If the reactor is operated **non-adiabatically** with $UA = 2000$ J/(s·K) and $T_a = 350$ K, write the modified energy balance and discuss qualitatively how the operating line deviates from the linear adiabatic path. **(2 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B** **(a) Derivation of adiabatic T–X relationship (7 marks):** See **Section 3.2** of these notes. Step-by-step: 1. PFR mass balance: $dF_A/dV = r_A$ (0.5 mark) 2. PFR adiabatic energy balance: $\sum F_i C_{p_i} \, dT/dV = -r_A \Delta H_{Rx}$ (0.5 mark) 3. Division to eliminate $r_A$ and $dV$ (1 mark) 4. Introduce $\Theta_i = F_{i,0}/F_{A0}$ (1 mark) 5. Integration assuming constant $\Delta H_{Rx}$ (1 mark) 6. Final boxed result: $T = T_0 + \dfrac{\Delta H_{Rx} \, X_A}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}$ (1 mark) 7. Define **adiabatic temperature rise** $\Delta T_{ad}$: at $X_A = 1$, $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{Rx}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}$ (1 mark) 8. Physical interpretation: For exothermic $\Delta H_{Rx} < 0$, so $\Delta T_{ad} > 0$ (temperature rises linearly with conversion). (1 mark) **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Energy balance derivation (2 marks):** For $A + B \to C + D$ with $\Theta_B = F_{B0}/F_{A0} = 2$, and initially $F_C = F_D = 0$: $$F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A), \quad F_B = F_{A0}(2 - X_A), \quad F_C = F_{A0} X_A, \quad F_D = F_{A0} X_A$$ $$\sum_i F_i C_{p_i} = F_{A0}\!\left[ (1 - X_A)(30) + (2 - X_A)(25) + X_A(35) + X_A(40) \right]$$ $$= F_{A0}\!\left[ 30 - 30 X_A + 50 - 25 X_A + 35 X_A + 40 X_A \right]$$ $$= F_{A0}\!\left[ 80 + 20 X_A \right]$$ Dividing by $F_{A0}$: $$\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i} = 80 + 20 X_A \quad \text{J/(mol·K)}$$ Adiabatic energy balance in differential form (1 mark): $$\frac{dT}{dX_A} = \frac{\Delta H_{Rx}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}} = \frac{-80000}{80 + 20 X_A}$$ Integrating (1 mark): $$\int_{T_0}^{T} dT = \int_0^{X_A} \frac{-80000}{80 + 20 X_A}\, dX_A = -4000 \ln\!\left( \frac{80 + 20 X_A}{80} \right)$$ $$\boxed{\, T = T_0 - 4000 \ln\!\left( 1 + 0.25 X_A \right) \,}$$ **(ii) Adiabatic temperature rise at $X_A = 1$ (3 marks):** $$T(1) = 400 - 4000 \ln(1.25) = 400 - 4000 \times 0.2231 = 400 - 892.5 = -492.5 \text{ K}$$ This physically impossible value signals an inconsistency in the parameter set (in reality the adiabatic temperature rise is far too large; the reactor would explode in a real scenario). However, *algebraically*: $$\Delta T_{ad} = T(X_A = 1) - T_0 = -4000 \ln(1.25) = -892.5 \text{ K}$$ $$\boxed{\, \vert \Delta T_{ad} \vert = 892.5 \text{ K} \,}$$ (Student should note that the magnitude is impractically large for any real reactor — engineering judgment mark.) **(iii) Non-adiabatic energy balance (2 marks):** Modified energy balance (1 mark): $$\frac{dT}{dX_A} = \frac{\Delta H_{Rx} + \dfrac{UA(T - T_a)}{r_A}}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}} = \frac{-80000 + \dfrac{2000(T - 350)}{r_A}}{80 + 20 X_A}$$ Qualitative discussion (1 mark): The non-adiabatic operating line **curves downward** (or upward, depending on exo/endo) compared to the linear adiabatic path. The coolant removes part of the heat of reaction, so the temperature rises *more slowly* with conversion. This allows the reactor to track the OTP curve more closely, increasing the maximum attainable conversion before equilibrium limits. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make — and where you lose marks:** > > 1. **Sign Error in $\Delta H_{Rx}$:** For exothermic reactions, $\Delta H_{Rx}$ is *negative*. Many students write $T = T_0 + \frac{\Delta H_{Rx} X_A}{\sum \Theta_i C_{p_i}}$ and conclude that temperature *decreases* — wrong direction! Always check the sign. > > 2. **Forgetting the $E_2/E_1$ ratio in OTP:** The OTP formula has a subtle correction factor $\frac{E_2}{E_1}$ that students often omit. Drop it and you lose 1 full mark. > > 3. **Mixing up $K_{eq}$ definitions:** $K_{eq} = k_1/k_2$ (kinetic) vs $K_{eq} = C_{B,eq}/C_{A,eq}$ (thermodynamic). At equilibrium these are equal — but the student must *show* this. > > 4. **Adiabatic ≠ Isothermal:** Many students confuse these. *Adiabatic* means *no heat exchange with surroundings*. *Isothermal* means *temperature is held constant by external means*. Adiabatic PFR is **not** isothermal unless $\Delta H_{Rx} = 0$. > > 5. **Using $\sum \Theta_i C_{p_i}$ without the proper stoichiometric accounting:** The heat-capacity group depends on $X_A$ if the reaction involves a change in total moles ($\varepsilon \neq 0$). Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks. > > 6. **OTP only for reversible reactions:** For irreversible reactions, OTP reduces to the maximum allowable temperature. Examiners specifically test this understanding. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Optimum Temperature Progression & Heat Effects** ### Core Concepts - [x] **OTP** = locus of $T$ that maximizes $r_A$ at each $X_A$. - [x] For **reversible exothermic** reactions, OTP **decreases** with $X_A$. - [x] For **reversible endothermic** reactions, OTP **increases** with $X_A$ (or simply: use max $T$). - [x] For **irreversible** reactions, no OTP concept — use maximum allowable $T$. ### Key Formulas (must memorize) - [x] **Arrhenius:** $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ - [x] **Van't Hoff:** $d \ln K_{eq}/dT = \Delta H^\circ/(RT^2)$ - [x] **OTP:** $T_{opt} = \dfrac{E_2 - E_1}{R \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A} \right]}$ - [x] **Adiabatic T–X:** $T = T_0 + \dfrac{\Delta H_{Rx} \, X_A}{\sum_i \Theta_i C_{p_i}}$ - [x] **PFR energy balance:** $\sum F_i C_{p_i}\, dT/dV = -r_A \Delta H_{Rx} - UA(T - T_a)$ - [x] **CSTR energy balance:** $\sum F_{i,0} C_{p_i}(T - T_0) = -r_A V \Delta H_{Rx} - UA(T - T_a)$ ### Reactor Design Heuristics - [x] **Adiabatic PFR** → linear T–X line; conversion limited by where this line meets equilibrium. - [x] **Non-adiabatic PFR** → can follow OTP; higher conversion but more complex control. - [x] **Industrial practice:** Multi-bed adiabatic reactors with **interstage cooling** approximate OTP cheaply. - [x] **Highly exothermic reactions** in non-adiabatic CSTRs can exhibit **multiple steady states** — runaway risk. ### Engineering Judgment - [x] Always check if $\Delta T_{ad}$ is physically realizable (material limits). - [x] For $\Delta T_{ad} > 1000$ K, never use adiabatic operation — use heat exchange. - [x] Catalyst deactivation (not in Module 3 scope but adjacent) often demands intermediate cooling. ### Numerical Practice Priorities - [x] Compute $T_{opt}$ given kinetic parameters. - [x] Compute adiabatic equilibrium conversion by intersecting operating line with equilibrium curve. - [x] Modify energy balance for non-adiabatic case and identify the cooling/heating load. - [x] Compare reactor volume for adiabatic vs non-adiabatic for the same conversion (efficiency gain).

topics.title: Multiple Steady States in CSTR. Problem Solving in Non-Isothermal Reactors Using Excel/MATLAB/SCILAB/Python etc. - Only for Self Study/Microproject/Assignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiple Steady States in CSTR. Problem Solving in Non-Isothermal Reactors Using Excel/MATLAB/SCILAB/Python etc. - Only for Self Study/Microproject/Assignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Multiple Steady States (MSS)** in a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) refer to the existence of **more than one set of operating conditions (temperature, concentration, conversion) at which the material and energy balances are simultaneously satisfied** for the same set of input parameters (feed conditions, coolant temperature, volume, flow rate).

For a **non-isothermal CSTR** carrying out an exothermic reaction, the simultaneous solution of the **mole balance (design equation)** and the **energy balance** yields a non-linear algebraic equation in reactor temperature $T$ (or conversion $X$). Because of the **exponential dependence** of the rate constant on temperature (Arrhenius law) combined with the **linear dependence** of the heat-removal term, the resulting equation can have **up to three real roots** — corresponding to **three distinct steady-state operating points**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A steady state of a CSTR is said to be **multiplied** (MSS) when the coupled mole and energy balances admit **two or more physically valid solutions** for the same values of the operating parameters $(F, V, C_{A0}, T_0, T_c, \text{coolant flow rate})$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stove and the Saucepan"

Imagine heating a pan of water on a gas stove. The burner supplies heat ($Q_{\text{gen}}$) and the pan loses heat to the surroundings ($Q_{\text{rem}}$). As you slowly increase the flame:

- At **low flame**, the water temperature barely rises — the burner is just compensating for heat losses. This is the **lower steady state (LSS)**.
- As the flame increases, you suddenly notice a **jump** — the water temperature rises sharply to boiling. This is the **ignition phenomenon**.
- Now, if you **reduce** the flame slowly, the pan does **not** drop back to the low-temperature state at the same flame setting. It continues to boil until the flame is reduced further, and only then does the temperature drop. This is the **extinction phenomenon**.
- The plot of pan temperature vs flame setting forms a characteristic **"S-curve"** with three regions: a stable low-T branch, an unstable middle branch, and a stable high-T branch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Importance**
> - **Reactor Safety:** Unintended ignition can cause thermal runaway and catastrophic failure (e.g., the 1947 Texas City disaster).
> - **Polymer Reactors:** Operating at the upper steady state gives high monomer conversion, but small disturbances can drop conversion drastically.
> - **Bioreactors:** Microbial growth kinetics with substrate inhibition also produce MSS.
> - **Catalytic Converters & Catalytic Reactors:** Methanol-to-olefin, ammonia synthesis — all exhibit MSS.

## 1.3 The Three Steady States — Formal Naming Convention

| Symbol | Name | Conversion | Stability | Temperature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T₁ (LSS)** | Lower Steady State | Low | **Stable** | Low |
| **T₂ (USS)** | Upper/Upper-Lower Steady State | Intermediate | **Unstable** | Intermediate |
| **T₃ (USS)** | Upper Steady State | High | **Stable** | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students frequently confuse the naming. The **middle steady state is always unstable**, while the **lower and upper steady states are stable**. This is a high-frequency short-answer topic in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Governing Equations for a Non-Isothermal CSTR

For a single, irreversible, exothermic, liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow B$ with first-order kinetics in a well-mixed CSTR, the **three fundamental equations** that must be satisfied simultaneously at steady state are:

### 2.1.1 Mole Balance (Material Balance)

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V = \frac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A}$$

For first-order kinetics with $C_{A0} = F_{A0}/v_0$ and $\tau = V/v_0$:

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X}{k C_A} = \frac{X}{k(1-X)} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$$

### 2.1.2 Energy Balance (Thermal Balance)

At steady state, the rate of heat generation by reaction equals the rate of heat removal (through the coolant and through the sensible-heat difference of the streams):

$$Q_{\text{gen}}(T) = Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$$

$$V \cdot (-r_A) \cdot (-\Delta H_R) = F C_{p,\text{bulk}} (T - T_0) + UA(T - T_c)$$

For the case where the cooling coil dominates (or when the bulk sensible-heat term is absorbed into the formulation):

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{gen}}(T) = V \cdot k_0 e^{-E/RT} \cdot C_A \cdot (-\Delta H_R)}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{rem}}(T) = UA(T - T_c) \quad \text{or} \quad Q_{\text{rem}}(T) = (F \rho C_p + UA)(T - T_0)}$$

### 2.1.3 Rate Equation (Arrhenius Form)

$$-r_A = k C_A^n, \quad k(T) = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ **J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
> - **Activation Energy Range:** $E = 50{,}000 - 200{,}000$ **J mol⁻¹** for typical industrial reactions
> - **Typical Exothermic Heat:** $-\Delta H_R = 20{,}000 - 100{,}000$ **J mol⁻¹**

## 2.2 Why MSS Occurs — The S-Curve Mechanism

The two curves $Q_{\text{gen}}(T)$ and $Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$ behave very differently:

| Curve | Mathematical Form | Shape Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_{\text{gen}}(T)$ | Exponential (Arrhenius) × concentration | **S-shaped**, bounded above by $F C_{A0}(-\Delta H_R)$ |
| $Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$ | Linear in $T$ | **Straight line** with slope $UA$ |

When the slope of $Q_{\text{gen}}(T)$ at its inflection point **exceeds** the slope of $Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$ (i.e., $\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT}\big\vert_{T_{\text{inf}}} > UA$), the two curves can intersect at **three points** — yielding three steady states.

The critical slope condition is:

$$\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT}\bigg\vert_{\text{max}} = \frac{E}{RT_{\text{max}}^2} \cdot Q_{\text{gen}}(T_{\text{max}}) > UA$$

## 2.3 Stability Analysis — Why the Middle State is Unstable

Consider a small perturbation $\delta T$ from a steady state. Linearising the energy balance around $T_{ss}$:

$$\rho C_p V \frac{d(\delta T)}{dt} = Q_{\text{gen}}(T_{ss}) - Q_{\text{rem}}(T_{ss}) + \left(\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} - \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}\right)\delta T$$

Defining the **thermal time constant** $\tau_t = \rho C_p V / UA$, the perturbation evolves as:

$$\frac{d(\delta T)}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau_t}\left(\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} - \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}\right)\delta T$$

| Condition | Stability | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} < \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}$ | **STABLE** | Perturbation decays; system returns to steady state |
| $\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} > \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}$ | **UNSTABLE** | Perturbation grows exponentially |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** On the S-curve, the **lower branch** and the **upper branch** have heat-removal lines that are *steeper* than the heat-generation curve. The **middle branch** has the heat-generation curve *steeper* than the removal line, making it inherently unstable.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Print This Table for Exam Revision**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arrhenius Rate Constant | $k = k_0 \exp(-E/RT)$ | $k_0$ pre-exponential |
| 2 | CSTR Mole Balance (1st order) | $X = \dfrac{k\tau}{1+k\tau}$ | Dimensionless conversion |
| 3 | CSTR Material Balance (general) | $V = \dfrac{F_{A0}X}{-r_A}$ | Volume of reactor |
| 4 | Steady-State Energy Balance | $Q_{\text{gen}} = Q_{\text{rem}}$ | Heat generation = removal |
| 5 | Heat Generation Term | $Q_{\text{gen}} = V(-r_A)(-\Delta H_R)$ | J/s or W |
| 6 | Heat Removal (Cooling Jacket) | $Q_{\text{rem}} = UA(T - T_c)$ | $UA$ in W/K |
| 7 | Heat Removal (Stream + Coolant) | $Q_{\text{rem}} = F\rho C_p(T - T_0) + UA(T - T_c)$ | Combined sensible + jacket |
| 8 | Stability Criterion | $\dfrac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} \lessgtr \dfrac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}$ | Stable if LHS $<$ RHS |
| 9 | Max Heat Generation Slope | $\left(\dfrac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT}\right)_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{E}{RT_{\text{max}}^2} Q_{\text{gen}}(T_{\text{max}})$ | At inflection point |
| 10 | Conversion-to-Temperature (Adiabatic Rise) | $T = T_0 + \dfrac{(-\Delta H_R)C_{A0}X}{C_p \rho}$ | $X \in [0,1]$ |
| 11 | Ignition Temperature (Saddle Point) | $T_{i}$ at lower tangent point | Where MSS disappears |
| 12 | Extinction Temperature (Saddle Point) | $T_{e}$ at upper tangent point | Where MSS disappears |
| 13 | Hysteresis Width | $\Delta T_{h} = T_{i} - T_{e}$ | Operating margin |
| 14 | Damköhler Number (Energy) | $\beta = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_R)C_{A0}}{C_p T_0}$ | Dimensionless adiabatic rise |
| 15 | Arrhenius Number | $\gamma = \dfrac{E}{RT_0}$ | Dimensionless activation energy |

## 2.5 Why Computer Tools Are Essential — The Computational Challenge

The simultaneous non-linear algebraic equations:

$$X = \frac{k(T)\tau}{1+k(T)\tau} \quad \text{and} \quad Q_{\text{gen}}(T) = Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$$

**cannot be solved analytically** for $T$ in closed form because of the transcendental dependence on $T$ in the Arrhenius term. Therefore, **numerical root-finding** techniques (Newton-Raphson, bisection, secant method) are used with computational tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Tools Approved by KTU 2024 Syllabus:**
> - **Microsoft Excel** — `Goal Seek`, `Solver Add-in`, manual table construction
