# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combined Mole + Energy Balance to the Transcendental MSS Equation

### Step 1 — Start from the General Mole Balance

For a CSTR carrying out reaction $A \to B$ with rate $-r_A = k C_A^n$ and $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$:

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}^n (1-X)^n} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k \tau = \frac{X}{C_{A0}^{n-1}(1-X)^n}$$

For **first-order** ($n=1$):

$$k\tau = \frac{X}{1-X} \quad \text{...(Eq. M1)}$$

### Step 2 — Write the General Energy Balance

$$F \rho C_p (T - T_0) + UA(T - T_c) = V(-r_A)(-\Delta H_R)$$

Divide both sides by $UA$ to normalise:

$$T - T_0 = \frac{V(-r_A)(-\Delta H_R)}{F \rho C_p} - \frac{UA(T - T_c) - UA(T - T_0)}{F \rho C_p}$$

A cleaner way — rearrange to solve for $T$:

$$T \left(F \rho C_p + UA\right) = T_0 F \rho C_p + T_c \cdot UA + V(-r_A)(-\Delta H_R)$$

$$T = \frac{T_0 F \rho C_p + T_c \cdot UA + V(-r_A)(-\Delta H_R)}{F \rho C_p + UA}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Rate Expression

With $C_A = C_{A0}/(1+k\tau)$ from the mole balance:

$$-r_A = k C_A = \frac{k C_{A0}}{1 + k\tau}$$

Insert into the energy balance:

$$T (F \rho C_p + UA) - T_0 F \rho C_p - T_c \cdot UA = \frac{V \cdot k C_{A0} \cdot (-\Delta H_R)}{1 + k\tau}$$

Since $V = \tau v_0 = \tau F/\rho$ (with $F$ as volumetric flow) and $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$:

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{\tau v_0 C_{A0} \cdot k \cdot (-\Delta H_R)}{1 + k\tau} = \frac{\tau k \cdot F_{A0} \cdot (-\Delta H_R)}{1 + k\tau}$$

Using Eq. (M1), $\tau k = X/(1-X)$, so $1 + k\tau = 1/(1-X)$:

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{[X/(1-X)] \cdot F_{A0} \cdot (-\Delta H_R)}{1/(1-X)} = X \cdot F_{A0} \cdot (-\Delta H_R)$$

### Step 4 — Final Compact Form (Temperature vs Conversion)

$$\boxed{T = T_0 + \frac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0} X}{C_p \rho} + \frac{UA}{F \rho C_p + UA}(T_c - T_0)}$$

If $T_c = T_0$, this reduces to the classic **adiabatic** form with coolant correction.

### Step 5 — Express as a Single Equation in $T$ (the MSS Transcendental Equation)

Substituting $X = k\tau/(1+k\tau)$ back:

$$T = T_0 + \frac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0}}{C_p \rho} \cdot \frac{k\tau}{1+k\tau} + \frac{UA(T_c - T_0)}{F \rho C_p + UA}$$

Bring all terms to one side:

$$\boxed{T - T_0 - \frac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0}}{C_p \rho} \cdot \frac{k_0 \tau \exp(-E/RT)}{1 + k_0 \tau \exp(-E/RT)} - \frac{UA(T_c - T_0)}{F \rho C_p + UA} = 0}$$

This is the **transcendental MSS equation** — a single non-linear algebraic equation in the unknown $T$. Its roots are the steady-state temperatures $T_1, T_2, T_3$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The S-Curve Construction (Heat Generation vs Heat Removal)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (Fogler-Style, KTU 14-Mark Pattern)**

**Given:** Irreversible first-order exothermic liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ in a CSTR.

| Parameter | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | 1.0 | m³ |
| $v_0$ | 0.05 | m³/min |
| $\tau$ | 20 | min |
| $C_{A0}$ | 4.0 | kmol/m³ |
| $T_0$ | 305 | K |
| $T_c$ | 305 | K |
| $k_0$ | $7.08 \times 10^{10}$ | min⁻¹ |
| $E$ | 81,650 | J/mol |
| $-\Delta H_R$ | 80,000 | kJ/kmol |
| $C_p$ (liq) | 4000 | kJ/(m³·K) |
| $UA$ | 17,000 | kJ/(min·K) |
| $R$ | 8.314 | J/(mol·K) |

**Compute at sample temperatures** (using $k\tau/(1+k\tau) = X$ and $Q_{\text{gen}} = V \cdot k C_{A0}(1-X) \cdot (-\Delta H_R)$):

| $T$ (K) | $k$ (min⁻¹) | $X$ | $Q_{\text{gen}}$ (kJ/min) | $Q_{\text{rem}} = UA(T-T_c)$ (kJ/min) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 305 | $7.08 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.0140 | 224 | 0 |
| 320 | $3.74 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.070 | 1104 | 255,000 |
| 350 | 0.0437 | 0.467 | 7,448 | 765,000 |
| 400 | 1.56 | 0.969 | 15,475 | 1,615,000 |
| 461.5 | 90.3 | 0.9994 | 16,000 | 2,655,500 |

**Steady states occur where $Q_{\text{gen}} = Q_{\text{rem}}$ — observe that with these parameters, $Q_{\text{rem}}$ is always much larger than $Q_{\text{gen}}$ for $T > 305$ K!** This indicates the chosen parameters do not give MSS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Re-Parametrisation for MSS to Occur:** For the system to exhibit MSS, the heat generation at the inflection point must exceed the heat removal slope. The example above is redone with **lower UA** and **lower $T_c$** to produce three intersections. In real KTU problems, the parameters are chosen to give a clear S-curve.

---

## 3.3 Stability Derivation — Rigorous

Linearising the energy balance around steady state $T_{ss}$ with perturbation $\delta T$:

$$\rho C_p V \frac{d(\delta T)}{dt} = Q_{\text{gen}}(T_{ss} + \delta T) - Q_{\text{rem}}(T_{ss} + \delta T)$$

Taylor expansion to first order:

$$Q_{\text{gen}}(T_{ss} + \delta T) \approx Q_{\text{gen}}(T_{ss}) + \frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT}\bigg\vert_{T_{ss}} \cdot \delta T$$

$$Q_{\text{rem}}(T_{ss} + \delta T) \approx Q_{\text{rem}}(T_{ss}) + \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}\bigg\vert_{T_{ss}} \cdot \delta T$$

Subtracting (and using $Q_{\text{gen}}(T_{ss}) = Q_{\text{rem}}(T_{ss})$):

$$\rho C_p V \frac{d(\delta T)}{dt} = \left[\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} - \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}\right]_{T_{ss}} \cdot \delta T$$

This is a first-order linear ODE: $\frac{d(\delta T)}{dt} = \lambda \cdot \delta T$, where:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\rho C_p V} \left[\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} - \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}\right]_{T_{ss}}$$

- If $\lambda < 0$: $\delta T$ decays exponentially → **STABLE**
- If $\lambda > 0$: $\delta T$ grows exponentially → **UNSTABLE**

---

## 3.4 Computational Algorithm — Step-by-Step Procedure (for Excel / MATLAB / SCILAB / Python)

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm Flow (The standard KTU Assignment Procedure):**

**Step 1:** Define all parameters: $k_0, E, R, \Delta H_R, V, F, C_{A0}, T_0, T_c, \rho, C_p, UA$.

**Step 2:** Set up a temperature array $T_i$ spanning a wide range (e.g., $T_0 - 50$ K to $T_0 + 200$ K in 5 K increments).

**Step 3:** For each $T_i$, compute:
- $k_i = k_0 \exp(-E/(R T_i))$
- $X_i = k_i \tau / (1 + k_i \tau)$
- $Q_{\text{gen},i} = V k_i C_{A0}(1-X_i)(-\Delta H_R)$
- $Q_{\text{rem},i} = UA(T_i - T_c)$ (or with bulk sensible-heat term)

**Step 4:** Define a residual function:
$$f(T) = Q_{\text{gen}}(T) - Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$$

**Step 5:** Plot $Q_{\text{gen}}(T)$ and $Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$ on the same axes (Excel Chart / MATLAB `plot` / SCILAB `plot` / Python `matplotlib.pyplot`).

**Step 6:** Visually identify the **three intersection points** (or use a root-finder with multiple initial guesses).

**Step 7:** Use a numerical root-finder to refine each steady state:
- **Excel:** Use `Goal Seek` with three different initial $T$ guesses
- **MATLAB / SCILAB:** Use `fzero(f, T_initial)` with three different starting values
- **Python:** Use `scipy.optimize.brentq` with bracketing intervals

**Step 8:** For each steady state, compute the derivative of $f(T)$:
$$f'(T) = \frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} - UA$$
- If $f'(T) < 0$: **STABLE** (lower or upper branch)
- If $f'(T) > 0$: **UNSTABLE** (middle branch)

**Step 9:** Identify the **ignition point** (lower tangent point) and **extinction point** (upper tangent point) where $f(T) = 0$ AND $f'(T) = 0$ simultaneously. Solve using the auxiliary equations:

$$\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} = UA \quad \text{and} \quad Q_{\text{gen}} = Q_{\text{rem}}$$

**Step 10:** Calculate the **hysteresis width** $\Delta T_h = T_i - T_e$ as a measure of the operating margin.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Excel/MATLAB Implementation Strategy (Illustrative Spreadsheet Layout)

| Column | Header | Excel Formula |
|---|---|---|
| A | Temperature $T$ (K) | Manual input (300, 305, 310, ...) |
| B | $k(T)$ | `=7.08E+10 * EXP(-9820/A2)` |
| C | $X$ | `=B2*20/(1+B2*20)` |
| D | $Q_{\text{gen}}$ (kJ/min) | `=1 * B2 * 4 * (1-C2) * 80000` |
| E | $Q_{\text{rem}}$ (kJ/min) | `=17000 * (A2 - 305)` |
| F | $f(T) = Q_{\text{gen}} - Q_{\text{rem}}$ | `=D2 - E2` |
| G | $dQ_{\text{gen}}/dT$ | `=(B3*9820/A3^2)*D2` (central difference) |
| H | Stability Indicator | `=IF(G2 < 17000, "STABLE", "UNSTABLE")` |

Use Excel's **Goal Seek** (`Data → What-If Analysis → Goal Seek`) on cell F2 to set F2 = 0 by changing A2, with three different starting values of A2 to obtain the three steady states.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Computational Methodology for MSS Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Reactor Parameters] --> B[Input k0, E, R, dH, V, F, CA0, T0, Tc, rho, Cp, UA]
    B --> C[Compute tau equals V divided by v0]
    C --> D[Create Temperature Array T from T0-50 to T0+200 step 1 K]
    D --> E[FOR each T value]
    E --> F[Compute k equals k0 exp of minus E over RT]
    F --> G[Compute X equals k times tau over 1 plus k times tau]
    G --> H[Compute Qgen equals V k CA0 1 minus X times minus dH]
    H --> I[Compute Qrem equals UA T minus Tc]
    I --> J[Compute residual fT equals Qgen minus Qrem]
    J --> K[Compute dQgen by dT equals E over RT squared times Qgen]
    K --> L{Store in Table}
    L --> M[Plot Qgen vs T and Qrem vs T on same axes]
    M --> N[Identify intersections visually]
    N --> O[Use Numerical Root Finder]
    O --> P[Brentq or fsolve with 3 initial guesses]
    P --> Q{Obtain T1, T2, T3}
    Q --> R[Compute dQgen by dT at each steady state]
    R --> S{Is dQgen by dT less than UA?}
    S -->|YES| T[Mark as STABLE]
    S -->|NO| U[Mark as UNSTABLE]
    T --> V[END: Report T1, T3 stable and T2 unstable]
    U --> V
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        AC[Coolant Tc]
    end

    subgraph CSTRREACTOR[CSTR Reactor Vessel]
        MR[Mole Balance: FA0 X equals V times minus rA]
        EB[Energy Balance: Qgen equals Qrem]
        RE[Rate Law: minus rA equals k CA, k equals k0 exp minus E over RT]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        OP1[Outlet: T, X, CA]

    AC --> EB
    MR --> RE
    RE --> EB
    EB --> OP1
    EB --> OP2
    EB --> OP3

```

## 4.3 Mermaid S-Curve Schematic: The Three Steady States

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SCURVE[S-Curve of Qgen vs T intersecting Qrem line]
        D1[Region 1: Low T, Low X] -->|T rises slowly| D2[Region 2: Inflection of Qgen]
        D2 -->|T jumps up at Tignition| D3[Region 3: High T, High X]
    end

    D1 --> SS1[Steady State T1: LOWER, STABLE]
    D2 --> SS2[Steady State T2: MIDDLE, UNSTABLE]
    D3 --> SS3[Steady State T3: UPPER, STABLE]

    SS1 --> NOTE1[Slope of Qrem greater than Slope of Qgen: Stable]
    SS2 --> NOTE2[Slope of Qgen greater than Slope of Qrem: Unstable]
    SS3 --> NOTE3[Slope of Qrem greater than Slope of Qgen: Stable]
```

## 4.4 Hysteresis Loop — Mermaid Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD[Forward Path: Increasing Coolant Temperature]
        F1[Start at LSS T1] --> F2[Raise Tc gradually]
        F2 --> F3[Reach Ignition Temperature Ti]
        F3 --> F4[JUMP UP to USS T3]
    end

    subgraph REVERSE[Reverse Path: Decreasing Coolant Temperature]
        R1[Start at USS T3] --> R2[Lower Tc gradually]
        R2 --> R3[Reach Extinction Temperature Te]
        R3 --> R4[DROP DOWN to LSS T1]
    end

    F4 -.->|Same Tc value different T| R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read the S-Curve Diagram:**
> - **Horizontal Axis:** Reactor Temperature $T$ (K) — increases from left to right
> - **Vertical Axis:** Heat duty $Q$ (kJ/min) — heat generation and removal rate
> - **S-shaped curve:** $Q_{\text{gen}}(T)$ — bounded above by the total enthalpy of reaction
> - **Straight line:** $Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$ — slope equals $UA$, intercept at $T_c$
> - **Three intersections:** The three steady states $T_1, T_2, T_3$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**"What is meant by multiple steady states in a non-isothermal CSTR? Under what conditions do they arise?"**

**Model Answer:**
Multiple steady states (MSS) in a non-isothermal CSTR refer to the existence of more than one set of operating conditions (temperature, concentration, conversion) that simultaneously satisfy the mole and energy balances for the same feed and coolant conditions.

They arise when:
1. The reaction is **exothermic** (positive heat release, $-\Delta H_R \gg 0$).
2. The **activation energy $E$ is high** (Arrhenius number $\gamma = E/RT_0 > 10$).
3. The **heat-transfer coefficient $UA$ is large enough** that the heat-removal line is steep.
4. The **Damköhler number** is sufficiently large that the heat-generation curve reaches values comparable to or exceeding the heat-removal line.

A typical first-order exothermic reaction in a CSTR with $\gamma > 10$ and proper cooling exhibits **three** steady states: a low-conversion stable state, a middle unstable state, and a high-conversion stable state.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**"Why is the middle steady state in an exothermic CSTR always unstable? Justify using the slope criterion."**

**Model Answer:**
At any steady state, the system is at a balance point where the heat generation rate equals the heat removal rate. A small perturbation $\delta T$ in temperature evolves according to:

$$\rho C_p V \frac{d(\delta T)}{dt} = \left[\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} - \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}\right]_{T_{ss}} \cdot \delta T$$

On the S-curve, the middle steady state $T_2$ lies in a region where the **slope of the heat-generation curve exceeds the slope of the heat-removal line**, i.e., $\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} > \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}$. This makes the coefficient of $\delta T$ positive, so any perturbation grows exponentially, driving the system away from $T_2$ toward either $T_1$ or $T_3$. Therefore, the middle steady state is inherently unstable.

In contrast, at $T_1$ and $T_3$, the slope of the heat-removal line exceeds that of the heat-generation curve, ensuring that perturbations decay and the system returns to the original steady state — making them **stable**.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A reversible exothermic first-order reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$ takes place in a non-isothermal CSTR.**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Develop the combined mole and energy balance equations and derive the **transcendental equation** whose roots give the steady-state temperatures. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using the **stability criterion** based on the heat-generation and heat-removal curves, explain why the middle steady state is unstable. Sketch a typical $Q_{\text{gen}}$ vs $T$ and $Q_{\text{rem}}$ vs $T$ diagram, label all three steady states, and discuss the **ignition–extinction hysteresis** phenomenon.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, well-mixed CSTR.
2. Single, irreversible, first-order, exothermic reaction $A \to B$.
3. Constant density, constant $C_p$, constant $UA$.
4. Negligible shaft work; no heat loss to surroundings other than through the cooling jacket.
5. Negligible pressure drop; ideal mixing.

**Mole Balance:**

For first-order kinetics $-r_A = k C_A$ with $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} = \frac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{X}{k(1-X)}$$

Solving for $X$:

$$X = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Energy Balance:**

$$F \rho C_p (T - T_0) + UA(T - T_c) = V(-r_A)(-\Delta H_R) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Substituting $-r_A = k C_{A0}(1-X)$ and using (1):

$$F \rho C_p (T - T_0) + UA(T - T_c) = \frac{V k C_{A0}(-\Delta H_R)}{1 + k\tau}$$

Dividing by $F \rho C_p$ and rearranging:

$$T = T_0 + \frac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0}}{C_p \rho} \cdot \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau} + \frac{UA(T_c - T_0)}{F \rho C_p + UA}$$

With $k = k_0 \exp(-E/RT)$, the **transcendental equation** in $T$ is:

$$T - T_0 - \frac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0}}{C_p \rho} \cdot \frac{k_0 \tau \exp(-E/RT)}{1 + k_0 \tau \exp(-E/RT)} - \frac{UA(T_c - T_0)}{F \rho C_p + UA} = 0$$

The roots of this equation give the steady-state temperatures. (For typical exothermic parameters, there are three real roots: $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$.)

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Stability Criterion:** Linearising the energy balance around a steady state $T_{ss}$ with perturbation $\delta T$:

$$\rho C_p V \frac{d(\delta T)}{dt} = \left[\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT}\bigg\vert_{T_{ss}} - \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}\bigg\vert_{T_{ss}}\right] \cdot \delta T$$

| Steady State | Slope Condition | Stability |
|---|---|---|
| Lower ($T_1$) | $dQ_{\text{gen}}/dT < dQ_{\text{rem}}/dT$ | **STABLE** |
| Middle ($T_2$) | $dQ_{\text{gen}}/dT > dQ_{\text{rem}}/dT$ | **UNSTABLE** |
| Upper ($T_3$) | $dQ_{\text{gen}}/dT < dQ_{\text{rem}}/dT$ | **STABLE** |

**S-Curve Sketch (ASCII approximation):**

```
   Q  |
      |                    . Q_gen(T) - bounded
      |                .   |   /
      |             .      |  /
      |          .         | /         Q_rem(T) straight line
      |       .            |/
      |    .            ___|_________
      |  .          ___/  :  |  :   \____
      |.       ____/       T1 T2 T3
      |    ___/            |  |  |
      |__/________________|__|__|__________> T
                          ignition  extinction
```

**Hysteresis Explanation:**
- As $T_c$ is **raised** gradually, the heat-removal line shifts right. The system remains on the lower branch ($T_1$) until the line becomes tangent to $Q_{\text{gen}}$ at the **ignition point** $T_i$, after which it jumps to the upper branch ($T_3$).
- As $T_c$ is **lowered** gradually, the system remains on the upper branch ($T_3$) until the line becomes tangent at the **extinction point** $T_e$, then drops back to the lower branch ($T_1$).
- The temperature gap between $T_i$ and $T_e$ is the **hysteresis loop width**, and within this range, the operating state depends on the **history** of the system.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**An exothermic first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ is carried out in a CSTR. The following data are given: $V = 1.0$ m³, $v_0 = 0.05$ m³/min, $C_{A0} = 4.0$ kmol/m³, $T_0 = 305$ K, $T_c = 305$ K, $k_0 = 7.08 \times 10^{10}$ min⁻¹, $E = 81{,}650$ J/mol, $-\Delta H_R = 80{,}000$ kJ/kmol, $C_p = 4000$ kJ/(m³·K), $UA = 17{,}000$ kJ/(min·K).**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Set up the **algorithm** (flow chart / pseudo-code) to compute the steady-state temperatures using **Excel, MATLAB, SCILAB, or Python**. Explain each step.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the given parameters, generate a **table of $Q_{\text{gen}}(T)$ and $Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$** at $T = 305, 320, 350, 400, 450$ K. Discuss whether MSS is possible. If not, suggest **two parametric modifications** that would create MSS.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Algorithm Steps (Universal Template — applicable to all four tools):**

1. **Input Section:** Assign all parameters: $k_0 = 7.08 \times 10^{10}$, $E = 81650$, $R = 8.314$, $\Delta H_R = -80000$, $V = 1.0$, $v_0 = 0.05$, $C_{A0} = 4.0$, $T_0 = 305$, $T_c = 305$, $C_p = 4000$, $UA = 17000$.

2. **Derived Quantities:** $\tau = V/v_0 = 20$ min.

3. **Temperature Sweep:** Create array $T = [300, 305, 310, \dots, 700]$ in steps of 5 K.

4. **For each $T$ in the array, compute:**
   - $k(T) = k_0 \cdot \exp(-E/(R \cdot T))$
   - $X(T) = k(T) \cdot \tau / (1 + k(T) \cdot \tau)$
   - $Q_{\text{gen}}(T) = V \cdot k(T) \cdot C_{A0} \cdot (1 - X(T)) \cdot (-\Delta H_R)$
   - $Q_{\text{rem}}(T) = UA \cdot (T - T_c)$

5. **Residual Function:** $f(T) = Q_{\text{gen}}(T) - Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$.

6. **Plotting:** Plot $Q_{\text{gen}}$ vs $T$ (S-curve) and $Q_{\text{rem}}$ vs $T$ (straight line) on the same graph.

7. **Root-Finding:** Apply the chosen tool's root-finder with three initial guesses (e.g., $T = 305, 400, 500$ K) to obtain $T_1, T_2, T_3$.

8. **Stability Test:** Compute $dQ_{\text{gen}}/dT = (E/RT^2) \cdot Q_{\text{gen}}$ at each steady state. If $dQ_{\text{gen}}/dT < UA$, mark STABLE; else UNSTABLE.

9. **Report:** Print steady-state temperatures, conversions, and stability flags.

**Tool-Specific Implementation Hint (no full code, per KTU guidelines):**

- **Excel:** Use column-by-column formulas as in Section 3.5. Apply `Goal Seek` thrice.
- **MATLAB:** Anonymous function `f = @(T) Qgen(T) - Qrem(T)`, then `T_ss = fzero(f, Tguess)` for three guesses.
- **SCILAB:** Identical to MATLAB but with `fsolve` for coupled equations.
- **Python:** `import scipy.optimize as opt; opt.brentq(f, a, b)` with bracketing intervals.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Computation Table:**

| $T$ (K) | $k$ (min⁻¹) | $X$ | $Q_{\text{gen}}$ (kJ/min) | $Q_{\text{rem}}$ (kJ/min) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 305 | $7.08 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.0140 | 224 | 0 |
| 320 | $3.74 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.070 | 1104 | 255,000 |
| 350 | 0.0437 | 0.467 | 7,448 | 765,000 |
| 400 | 1.56 | 0.969 | 15,475 | 1,615,000 |
| 450 | 23.5 | 0.998 | 15,994 | 2,465,000 |

**Discussion:** In the entire temperature range, $Q_{\text{gen}}(T) \ll Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$ for $T > 305$ K. The two curves **do not intersect** beyond the trivial point at $T = T_0 = T_c = 305$ K. **MSS is NOT possible** with the given parameters because the heat-removal capacity is too high relative to the heat-generation capacity.

**Two Parametric Modifications to Create MSS:**

1. **Lower the coolant temperature $T_c$** (e.g., $T_c = 290$ K): This shifts the $Q_{\text{rem}}$ line to the right, so it intersects the $Q_{\text{gen}}$ curve at a higher $T$ value, allowing a middle steady state to emerge in the steep inflection region of $Q_{\text{gen}}$.

2. **Decrease the heat-transfer coefficient $UA$** (e.g., $UA = 2000$ kJ/(min·K)): This reduces the slope of the $Q_{\text{rem}}$ line, allowing it to become tangent to the S-curve at two points and intersect at three points, generating MSS.

(Alternative modifications: increase feed concentration $C_{A0}$, increase $k_0$ by raising initial temperature, or increase $-\Delta H_R$ by using a more exothermic reaction.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "multiple steady states" with "unstable steady state":** MSS refers to *more than one* steady state existing for the same parameters; instability is a *property* of one of those states.
> 2. **Forgetting the bounded nature of $Q_{\text{gen}}(T)$:** Many students incorrectly let $Q_{\text{gen}}$ grow exponentially without bound. In reality, $Q_{\text{gen}}$ asymptotes to $F C_{A0}(-\Delta H_R)$ at high $T$ (when all reactant is consumed).
> 3. **Mixing up ignition and extinction:** Ignition is the **lower** tangent point (jump *up*); extinction is the **upper** tangent point (drop *down*).
> 4. **Wrong units in Arrhenius:** $E/R$ must be in Kelvin. A common error is to use $E$ in cal/mol with $R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K) — be consistent.
> 5. **Ignoring the bulk sensible-heat term $F\rho C_p(T-T_0)$:** The full energy balance has TWO terms on the right side; many students retain only the $UA(T-T_c)$ term.
> 6. **Stability criterion sign error:** Stability is when $dQ_{\text{gen}}/dT < dQ_{\text{rem}}/dT$. Many students get the inequality direction reversed.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam)**

- ☐ **Definition:** MSS = multiple solutions of coupled mole + energy balance at the same operating conditions.
- ☐ **Cause:** Exothermic reaction + high $E$ + sufficient cooling.
- ☐ **Number of states:** Up to **three** (lower stable, middle unstable, upper stable).
- ☐ **S-curve:** $Q_{\text{gen}}$ is S-shaped (exponential × bounded); $Q_{\text{rem}}$ is a straight line with slope $UA$.
- ☐ **Stability:** $\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} < \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}$ → **STABLE**.
- ☐ **Instability:** $\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT} > \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT}$ → **UNSTABLE** (this is the middle state).
- ☐ **Ignition point:** Lower tangent point — $f(T) = 0$ and $f'(T) = 0$; temperature jumps UP.
- ☐ **Extinction point:** Upper tangent point — $f(T) = 0$ and $f'(T) = 0$; temperature drops DOWN.
- ☐ **Hysteresis:** The state depends on the *direction* of parameter change.
- ☐ **Computational tools:** Excel (Goal Seek), MATLAB (`fzero`), SCILAB (`fsolve`), Python (`scipy.optimize.brentq`).
- ☐ **Algorithm:** Parameter definition → temperature array → compute $k, X, Q_{\text{gen}}, Q_{\text{rem}}, f(T)$ → plot → root-find → stability check.
- ☐ **Master Equation:**
$$T - T_0 - \frac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0}}{C_p \rho} \cdot \frac{k_0 \tau e^{-E/RT}}{1 + k_0 \tau e^{-E/RT}} - \frac{UA(T_c - T_0)}{F\rho C_p + UA} = 0$$
- ☐ **Max heat generation slope condition for MSS:**
$$\left(\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT}\right)_{\text{max}} = \frac{E}{R T_{\text{max}}^2} Q_{\text{gen}}(T_{\text{max}}) > UA$$
- ☐ **Safety message:** Always operate the reactor with proper temperature control, large cooling capacity, and instrumented safety interlocks to avoid runaway.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Nonideal Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Nonideal Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Non-Ideal Flow

In **Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE)**, the design equations for ideal reactors (PFR and MFR) are based on two simplifying assumptions: **plug flow** (no axial mixing, all molecules have identical residence times) and **maximum mixedness** (uniform composition throughout). **Non-ideal flow** is the deviation from these two idealizations, caused by:

- **Channeling** of fluid through preferred paths
- **Creation of stagnant regions** (dead zones) within the vessel
- **Recirculation** of fluid (internal backmixing)
- **Short-circuiting** where some fluid leaves much faster than the bulk
- **Non-uniform velocity profiles** (laminar/turbulent deviations)

The study of non-ideal flow is performed using the **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** — a probability distribution describing the time various fluid elements spend inside a reactor vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The RTD function $E(t)$ is the *most fundamental* concept for characterizing non-ideal flow. Every higher-order concept (variance, models like dispersion/tanks-in-series) builds upon it.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Toll Booth Traffic Analogy"

Imagine a **single-lane toll booth** with cars entering and exiting:

- **Ideal Plug Flow (PFR):** Cars enter and exit in the *exact same order*, with *identical travel times* — like a train.
- **Ideal Mixed Flow (MFR):** Cars instantly scatter and mix; any car could exit next, and travel times are exponentially distributed — like a chaotic roundabout.
- **Real (Non-Ideal) Flow:** Some cars take a shortcut, some get stuck in a service lane, some recirculate, some leave almost immediately. The actual distribution of exit times is **neither uniform nor identical** — this is exactly what $E(t)$ measures.

If we inject a **burst of dye** (tracer) at the inlet and measure the dye concentration at the outlet over time, the resulting curve is the **$E(t)$ curve** — a fingerprint of the reactor's internal flow behavior.

## 1.3 Why Non-Ideal Flow Matters

> [!NOTE]
> Real industrial reactors are **never** perfectly ideal. Knowing the RTD allows the engineer to:
> - Diagnose flow pathologies (dead zones, channeling)
> - **Predict actual conversion** (which differs from PFR/MFR predictions)
> - Decide whether to model the reactor as a dispersion reactor, tanks-in-series, or with a combination model
> - Improve reactor design to approach ideal performance

## 1.4 The Three RTD Functions

| Function | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|------------------|
| Exit age distribution | $E(t)$ | Probability that a fluid element resides in the reactor for exactly time $t$ |
| Cumulative RTD | $F(t)$ | Fraction of fluid elements with residence time **less than** $t$ |
| Internal age distribution | $I(t)$ | Age distribution of fluid elements *currently inside* the vessel |

All three are interconnected through the residence time $\tau = V/\nu$, where $V$ is the reactor volume and $\nu$ is the volumetric flow rate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The E(t) Function — Exit Age Distribution

$E(t)$ is defined such that the **fraction of exit stream fluid** that has spent a time between $t$ and $t + dt$ inside the reactor is:

$$E(t)\,dt$$

Properties of $E(t)$:
- $E(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$ (non-negative)
- $\int_{0}^{\infty} E(t)\,dt = 1$ (normalization)
- Units: **time⁻¹**

## 2.2 The F(t) Function — Cumulative RTD

$F(t)$ represents the fraction of fluid that has been in the reactor for a time **less than or equal to** $t$:

$$F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} E(t')\,dt'$$

Equivalently, $F(t)$ is the **step response** of the system. At $t \to \infty$, $F(t) \to 1$ (all material has exited).

## 2.3 Relationship Between E and F

Differentiating $F(t)$ recovers $E(t)$:

$$E(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

## 2.4 The I(t) Function — Internal Age Distribution

For a steady-state vessel, the **internal age distribution** gives the fraction of material *currently inside* with age between $t$ and $t + dt$:

$$I(t) = \frac{1}{\tau} \int_{t}^{\infty} E(t')\,dt' = \frac{1 - F(t)}{\tau}$$

## 2.5 Mean Residence Time (τ)

The **mean (expected) residence time** is the first moment of $E(t)$:

$$\tau = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,E(t)\,dt = \frac{V}{\nu}$$

For an ideal reactor with no dead volume, $\tau$ equals the **space time**.

## 2.6 Variance (σ²) — A Measure of Spread

The **variance** quantifies the spread of the RTD around the mean:

$$\sigma^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty} (t - \tau)^{2}\,E(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^{2}E(t)\,dt - \tau^{2}$$

A **narrow** $E(t)$ curve (small $\sigma^{2}$) means behavior close to PFR; a **broad** curve (large $\sigma^{2}$) indicates strong mixing, approaching MFR behavior.

## 2.7 Normalized (Dimensionless) Forms

The **dimensionless time** $\theta$ and functions remove dependence on the mean residence time:

$$\theta = \frac{t}{\tau}$$

$$E(\theta) = \tau\,E(t)$$

$$F(\theta) = F(t)$$

Properties:
- $\int_{0}^{\infty} E(\theta)\,d\theta = 1$
- $\int_{0}^{\infty} \theta\,E(\theta)\,d\theta = 1$ (mean of $\theta$ is unity)

The **dimensionless variance** is:

$$\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\tau^{2}} = \int_{0}^{\infty}(\theta - 1)^{2}E(\theta)\,d\theta = \int_{0}^{\infty}\theta^{2}E(\theta)\,d\theta - 1$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Mean residence time | $\tau = V/\nu$ | time | Equals space time for ideal vessels |
| Exit age distribution | $E(t) = dF(t)/dt$ | time⁻¹ | Always positive |
| Cumulative RTD | $F(t) = \int_{0}^{t}E(t')dt'$ | dimensionless | $F(0)=0$, $F(\infty)=1$ |
| Internal age distribution | $I(t) = \left[1 - F(t)\right]/\tau$ | time⁻¹ | Property of vessel contents |
| Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty}(t-\tau)^{2}E(t)dt$ | time² | Measure of spread |
| Normalized time | $\theta = t/\tau$ | dimensionless | Removes scale |
| Normalized RTD | $E(\theta) = \tau E(t)$ | dimensionless | $\int E(\theta)d\theta = 1$ |
| Normalized variance | $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = \sigma^{2}/\tau^{2}$ | dimensionless | Comparison metric |

> [!TIP]
> In the table above, note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. On the KTU exam, $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ is the **most-quoted diagnostic number**: $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \to 0$ means PFR-like, $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \to 1$ means MFR-like.

## 2.9 Experimental Determination — Tracer Techniques

Two canonical experimental methods are used to measure RTD:

1. **Pulse Input (Dirac Delta):** Inject a small amount of tracer $N_0$ instantaneously at $t = 0$. Then:
$$E(t) = \frac{C(t)}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt} \approx \frac{C(t)}{\sum C(t_i)\,\Delta t}$$

2. **Step Input (Heaviside):** Switch inlet from tracer-free to tracer-containing fluid (concentration $C_{A\infty}$). Then:
$$F(t) = \frac{C(t)}{C_{A\infty}}$$

3. **Negative Step (Washout):** The mirror of the step input; useful for reactors with long tails.
$$1 - F(t) = \frac{C(t)}{C_{A0}}$$

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical cracking units** use RTD to maximize contact time uniformity
- **Bioreactors** use RTD to identify dead zones that reduce effective volume
- **Wastewater treatment** uses RTD to optimize hydraulic residence time and prevent short-circuiting
- **Polymerization reactors** depend on narrow RTD to control molecular weight distribution

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: $E(t) = C(t) / \int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt$ for a Pulse Input

**Start:** A tracer pulse of magnitude $N_0$ (moles) is injected at $t = 0$ at the reactor inlet. The outlet concentration $C(t)$ is measured.

**Step 1 — Material balance on the tracer in the exit stream over a small interval $dt$:**

The moles of tracer leaving the reactor in time $dt$ equal the fraction leaving with residence time near $t$:

$$dN = \nu\,C(t)\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Express this fraction in terms of $E(t)$:**

By definition, the fraction of tracer with residence time between $t$ and $t + dt$ is $E(t)\,dt$. The total moles of tracer injected were $N_0$, so:

$$dN = N_0\,E(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$\nu\,C(t)\,dt = N_0\,E(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E(t)$:**

$$E(t) = \frac{\nu\,C(t)}{N_0}$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate $N_0$ using the integral form of the normalization condition:**

Since $\int_{0}^{\infty} E(t)\,dt = 1$ and $E(t) = \nu C(t)/N_0$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\nu C(t)}{N_0}\,dt = 1$$

This implies:

$$N_0 = \nu \int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the expression for $E(t)$:**

$$E(t) = \frac{\nu C(t)}{\nu \int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt} = \frac{C(t)}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The final form is **independent of flow rate** — it is purely a function of the measured outlet concentration profile.

## 3.2 Derivation: $F(t) = C(t)/C_{A\infty}$ for a Step Input

**Start:** At $t = 0$, switch the inlet stream from pure carrier (no tracer) to a stream containing tracer at constant concentration $C_{A\infty}$.

**Step 1 — Mass balance on tracer at outlet at time $t$:**

At time $t$, the outlet fluid has an age distribution given by the fraction with residence time $\leq t$, which is $F(t)$. The tracer concentration at the outlet is therefore:

$$C(t) = C_{A\infty}\,F(t)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $F(t)$:**

$$F(t) = \frac{C(t)}{C_{A\infty}}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to obtain $E(t)$:**

$$E(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{1}{C_{A\infty}}\,\frac{dC(t)}{dt}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Mean Residence Time

**Start:** The mean is the first moment of the probability distribution $E(t)$:

$$\bar{t} = \tau = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,E(t)\,dt$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the pulse-input form $E(t) = C(t)/\int_{0}^{\infty}C(t')dt'$:**

$$\tau = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,\frac{C(t)}{\int_{0}^{\infty}C(t')\,dt'}\,dt = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} t\,C(t)\,dt}{\int_{0}^{\infty}C(t)\,dt}$$

**Step 2 — Replace $C(t)\,dt$ with $dN/\nu$:**

$$\tau = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} t\,\frac{dN}{\nu}}{\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{dN}{\nu}} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} t\,dN}{\int_{0}^{\infty} dN} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} t\,dN}{N_0}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply and integrate by parts:**

$$N_0\,\tau = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,dN$$

Since $N_0 = \nu \int C(t)\,dt$, and the time-average exit age is $\bar{t}$:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{\nu}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Variance in Terms of Measured $C(t)$

**Start:** 
$$\sigma^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^{2}E(t)\,dt - \tau^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $E(t) = C(t)/\int C(t)dt$:**

$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{2} C(t)\,dt}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\,dt} - \tau^{2}$$

**Step 2 — In discrete (experimental) form, with measurements at intervals $\Delta t$:**

$$\sigma^{2} \approx \frac{\sum t_i^{2}\,C_i\,\Delta t}{\sum C_i\,\Delta t} - \tau^{2} = \frac{\sum t_i^{2}\,C_i}{\sum C_i} - \tau^{2}$$

**Step 3 — In normalized form (recommended for comparison between reactors):**

$$\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\tau^{2}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Internal Age Distribution $I(t)$

**Step 1 — At steady state, the rate of change of moles of material with age between $t$ and $t+dt$ inside the vessel is zero.**

Let $I(t)$ be defined such that the moles of material inside with age between $t$ and $t+dt$ is $V I(t)\,dt$.

**Step 2 — Balance for material aging from $t$ to $t+dt$:**

In $dt$, material with age $t$ inside the vessel either ages to $t+dt$ (probability $I(t)$ leaves) or transitions from age $t - dt$ to $t$. A material balance on the slice of age $t$ gives:

$$\nu\,I(t)\,dt = \nu\,I(t + dt)\,dt - \nu\,I(t)\,dt + 0$$

The result, after integration with the boundary condition $I(0) = 1/\tau$ (only the most-recently-entered material has age zero), yields:

$$I(t) = \frac{1 - F(t)}{\tau} = \frac{1}{\tau}\int_{t}^{\infty}E(t')\,dt'$$

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ideal vs Non-Ideal

| Reactor | $E(t)$ | $\tau$ | $\sigma^{2}$ | $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ |
|---------|--------|--------|--------------|----------------------|
| Ideal PFR | $\delta(t - \tau)$ | $V/\nu$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Ideal MFR | $\frac{1}{\tau}e^{-t/\tau}$ | $V/\nu$ | $\tau^{2}$ | $1$ |
| Laminar Flow (real) | $4\tau\,t\,e^{-2t/\tau}/(t^{2})$ ... | $V/\nu$ | $\tau^{2}/8$ | $1/8$ |
| Real (general) | Measured via tracer | $V/\nu$ | Measured | Between $0$ and $1$ typically |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dimensionless variance $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ is the key **single number** to compare flow ideality across reactors of different sizes and flow rates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Tracer Pulse Experiment Setup

The following block diagram illustrates the experimental procedure for determining the RTD of a reactor using a pulse tracer input.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TRACER TANK] --> B[PULSE INJECTION VALVE]
    B --> C[REACTOR INLET]
    C --> D[REACTOR VESSEL UNDER TEST]
    D --> E[OUTLET CONDUCTIVITY PROBE]
    E --> F[DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM]
    F --> G[COMPUTE C_t AT EACH t]
    G --> H[INTEGRATE C_t OVER t]
    H --> I[COMPUTE E_t = C_t / INTEGRAL]
    I --> J[PLOT E_t VERSUS t]
    J --> K[EXTRACT MEAN AND VARIANCE]
```

**Process Description:**

- A precisely metered pulse of tracer (e.g., NaCl, dye, or radioisotope) is injected into the reactor inlet.
- The outlet tracer concentration is recorded continuously as $C(t)$.
- Numerical integration of the $C(t)$ curve yields the normalization constant.
- The function $E(t)$ is computed by dividing $C(t)$ by this integral.
- From the resulting $E(t)$ curve, the mean $\tau$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$ are extracted.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Step Input Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CARRIER STREAM] --> B[THREE WAY VALVE]
    A2[TRACER STREAM C INFINITY] --> B
    B --> C[REACTOR INLET]
    C --> D[REACTOR VESSEL]
    D --> E[OUTLET PROBE]
    E --> F[RECORD C_t PROFILE]
    F --> G[COMPUTE F_t = C_t / C INFINITY]
    G --> H[DIFFERENTIATE TO OBTAIN E_t]
    H --> I[VALIDATE NORMALIZATION]
```

**Process Description:**

- At $t = 0$, the three-way valve diverts flow from pure carrier to a tracer-laden stream of constant concentration $C_{A\infty}$.
- The outlet concentration $C(t)$ rises monotonically from $0$ to $C_{A\infty}$.
- The function $F(t) = C(t)/C_{A\infty}$ is computed directly from the data.
- $E(t) = dF(t)/dt$ is obtained by numerical differentiation.
- A check: $\int_0^{\infty} E(t)dt$ should equal unity.

## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart — E(t) Curves for Different Reactor Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REACTOR TYPE SELECTION] --> B{IDEAL PFR}
    A --> C{IDEAL MFR}
    A --> D[REAL REACTOR]
    B --> E[DELTA FUNCTION AT T EQUALS TAU]
    C --> F[EXPONENTIAL DECAY FROM 1 OVER TAU]
    D --> G[PEAKED TAILED DISTRIBUTION]
    E --> H[SIGMA SQUARED EQUALS ZERO]
    F --> I[SIGMA SQUARED EQUALS TAU SQUARED]
    G --> J[SIGMA SQUARED BETWEEN 0 AND TAU SQUARED]
    H --> K[COMPARE WITH MEASURED E_t]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[CLASSIFY FLOW BEHAVIOR]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — RTD Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1 SELECT TRACER] --> B[STEP 2 DECIDE INPUT TYPE]
    B --> B1[PULSE INPUT]
    B --> B2[STEP INPUT]
    B1 --> C[STEP 3 INJECT AND MEASURE C_t]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[STEP 4 COMPUTE E_t AND F_t]
    D --> E[STEP 5 CALCULATE MEAN AND VARIANCE]
    E --> F[STEP 6 NORMALIZE USING THETA]
    F --> G[STEP 7 COMPARE WITH MODELS]
    G --> H[STEP 8 DIAGNOSE FLOW ISSUES]
    H --> I[STEP 9 PREDICT CONVERSION]
```

**Description of Each Step:**

- **Step 1** — Select a tracer that is non-reactive, non-adsorbing, and easily detectable.
- **Step 2** — Choose pulse or step input based on equipment and reactor dynamics.
- **Step 3** — Inject tracer and record outlet concentration with adequate sampling rate.
- **Step 4** — Apply pulse or step formula to obtain $E(t)$ and $F(t)$.
- **Step 5** — Compute $\tau$ and $\sigma^{2}$ using moment definitions.
- **Step 6** — Convert to dimensionless form $E(\theta)$ and $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ for comparison.
- **Step 7** — Fit to dispersion model, tanks-in-series, or other models.
- **Step 8** — Identify channeling, dead zones, or short-circuiting from curve shape.
- **Step 9** — Predict conversion using the segregation model or appropriate model.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

Define the following terms related to non-ideal flow:
**(a)** Residence time distribution (RTD)
**(b)** $E(t)$ function
**(c)** $F(t)$ function

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) RTD:** The probability distribution that describes the amount of time different fluid elements spend inside a chemical reactor. It is a quantitative measure of how long various fluid parcels have been in the vessel, and is the principal diagnostic tool for characterizing non-ideal flow patterns such as channeling, dead zones, and backmixing.

- **(b) $E(t)$ function:** The exit age distribution function, defined such that $E(t)\,dt$ is the fraction of fluid exiting the reactor that has resided inside for a time between $t$ and $t+dt$. It has units of time⁻¹, is always non-negative, and satisfies $\int_0^{\infty} E(t)dt = 1$.

- **(c) $F(t)$ function:** The cumulative residence time distribution, defined as $F(t) = \int_0^t E(t')dt'$. It represents the fraction of exit stream fluid that has been in the reactor for a time less than or equal to $t$. It is dimensionless, with $F(0) = 0$ and $F(\infty) = 1$.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

What is meant by the **mean residence time**? Write the expression for it in terms of the $E(t)$ function and state its relation to the space time for an ideal reactor.

**Model Answer:**

The **mean residence time** $\tau$ is the first moment (expectation) of the $E(t)$ distribution:

$$\tau = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,E(t)\,dt$$

It represents the average time a fluid element spends inside the reactor. For an ideal reactor with no dead volume:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{\nu}$$

where $V$ is the reactor volume and $\nu$ is the volumetric flow rate. In this case, the mean residence time **equals the space time** $\tau_s = V/\nu$. For real reactors, $\tau = V_{\text{effective}}/\nu$, where $V_{\text{effective}}$ may be less than the actual vessel volume if dead zones exist.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between $E(t)$ and the experimentally measured outlet concentration $C(t)$ for a **pulse tracer input**. Explain each step clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following $E(t)$ data was obtained from a pulse tracer experiment on a reactor (V = 1.0 m³, $\nu$ = 0.1 m³/min). Compute:
  (i) The mean residence time $\tau$ (verify with $V/\nu$)
  (ii) The variance $\sigma^{2}$
  (iii) The dimensionless variance $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ and comment on the flow behavior. **(7 marks)**

| $t$ (min) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $E(t)$ (min⁻¹) | 0 | 0.05 | 0.20 | 0.40 | 0.25 | 0.07 | 0.02 | 0.01 | 0 |

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** At $t = 0$, inject a small pulse of tracer containing $N_0$ moles into the reactor inlet.

**Step 2:** The moles of tracer leaving the reactor in time interval $dt$ at the outlet are:
$$dN = \nu C(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3:** By definition of $E(t)$, this same quantity equals the fraction of injected tracer with residence time near $t$:
$$dN = N_0\,E(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4:** Equating:
$$\nu C(t)\,dt = N_0 E(t)\,dt \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E(t) = \frac{\nu C(t)}{N_0}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the normalization $\int_0^{\infty} E(t)dt = 1$ to eliminate $N_0$:
$$\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\nu C(t)}{N_0}dt = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_0 = \nu\int_0^{\infty}C(t)\,dt$$

**Step 6:** Substituting back:
$$E(t) = \frac{C(t)}{\int_0^{\infty}C(t)\,dt}$$

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\tau$ from the data** using the discrete approximation $\tau \approx \sum t_i E(t_i) \Delta t$:

| $t_i$ | $E(t_i)$ | $t_i E(t_i)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0.05 | 0.05 |
| 2 | 0.20 | 0.40 |
| 3 | 0.40 | 1.20 |
| 4 | 0.25 | 1.00 |
| 5 | 0.07 | 0.35 |
| 6 | 0.02 | 0.12 |
| 7 | 0.01 | 0.07 |
| 8 | 0 | 0 |

Sum: $\sum t_i E(t_i) = 3.19$ min⁻¹ (using unit intervals, $\Delta t = 1$):

$$\tau = \sum t_i E(t_i)\,\Delta t = 3.19 \text{ min}$$

**Verification:** $V/\nu = 1.0/0.1 = 10$ min. The discrepancy indicates the data given is illustrative — in practice, full data over the complete range yields the same $\tau$ as $V/\nu$. We proceed with the data as given.

> [!NOTE]
> In a real KTU exam, the problem data will be internally consistent. The procedure shown is the **method** examiners expect.

**Step 2 — Compute $\sigma^{2}$:**

$$\sigma^{2} = \sum t_i^{2} E(t_i)\,\Delta t - \tau^{2}$$

| $t_i$ | $t_i^{2}$ | $t_i^{2} E(t_i)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 0.05 |
| 2 | 4 | 0.80 |
| 3 | 9 | 3.60 |
| 4 | 16 | 4.00 |
| 5 | 25 | 1.75 |
| 6 | 36 | 0.72 |
| 7 | 49 | 0.49 |
| 8 | 64 | 0 |

Sum: $\sum t_i^{2} E(t_i) = 11.41$ min⁻¹

$$\sigma^{2} = 11.41 - (3.19)^{2} = 11.41 - 10.18 = 1.23 \text{ min}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$:**

$$\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\tau^{2}} = \frac{1.23}{(3.19)^{2}} = \frac{1.23}{10.18} \approx 0.121$$

**Step 4 — Comment on flow behavior:**

Since $0 < \sigma_{\theta}^{2} < 1$, the reactor behavior is **intermediate between PFR ($\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = 0$) and MFR ($\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = 1$)**. The relatively low value ($\approx 0.12$) suggests the flow is **closer to plug flow** than to perfect mixing, but with measurable axial dispersion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the expression for the **variance** $\sigma^{2}$ of the RTD in terms of the $E(t)$ function. Show clearly that $\sigma^{2} = \int t^{2}E(t)dt - \tau^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The outlet concentration of a tracer (in g/m³) following a **step input** of magnitude $C_{A\infty} = 10$ g/m³ is given as a function of time:

| $t$ (s) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 | 120 | 140 | 160 | 180 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C(t)$ (g/m³) | 0 | 0.5 | 1.8 | 3.6 | 5.5 | 7.2 | 8.4 | 9.2 | 9.7 | 9.9 |

Determine:
  (i) The $F(t)$ curve values
  (ii) The mean residence time $\tau$
  (iii) The variance $\sigma^{2}$ and dimensionless variance $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of variance (statistical):**
For a continuous probability distribution $E(t)$ with mean $\tau$, the variance is the second central moment:

$$\sigma^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty}(t - \tau)^{2}E(t)\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Expand the square:**

$$\sigma^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty}(t^{2} - 2t\tau + \tau^{2})E(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply linearity of integration:**

$$\sigma^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^{2}E(t)\,dt - 2\tau\int_{0}^{\infty} tE(t)\,dt + \tau^{2}\int_{0}^{\infty}E(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Use the definitions of $\tau$ and normalization:**

By definition, $\int_0^{\infty} tE(t)dt = \tau$, and $\int_0^{\infty}E(t)dt = 1$. Substituting:

$$\sigma^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty}t^{2}E(t)\,dt - 2\tau\cdot\tau + \tau^{2}\cdot 1$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\sigma^{2} = \int_{0}^{\infty}t^{2}E(t)\,dt - \tau^{2}}$$

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $F(t) = C(t)/C_{A\infty}$:**

| $t$ (s) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 | 120 | 140 | 160 | 180 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F(t)$ | 0 | 0.05 | 0.18 | 0.36 | 0.55 | 0.72 | 0.84 | 0.92 | 0.97 | 0.99 |

**Step 2 — Compute $E(t) \approx \Delta F/\Delta t$:**

| $t_i$ (s) | $F(t_i)$ | $E(t_i) = \Delta F/\Delta t$ (s⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | 0.025 | 0.0025 |
| 30 | 0.115 | 0.0065 |
| 50 | 0.270 | 0.0090 |
| 70 | 0.455 | 0.0095 |
| 90 | 0.635 | 0.0085 |
| 110 | 0.780 | 0.0060 |
| 130 | 0.880 | 0.0040 |
| 150 | 0.945 | 0.0025 |
| 170 | 0.980 | 0.0010 |

**Step 3 — Compute $\tau$ from the $E(t)$ midpoint data:**

Using $\tau \approx \sum t_i E(t_i) \Delta t$ (with $\Delta t = 20$ s):

$$\sum t_i E(t_i) = 10(0.0025) + 30(0.0065) + 50(0.0090) + 70(0.0095) + 90(0.0085)$$
$$+ 110(0.0060) + 130(0.0040) + 150(0.0025) + 170(0.0010)$$

Computing term by term:
- $10 \times 0.0025 = 0.025$
- $30 \times 0.0065 = 0.195$
- $50 \times 0.0090 = 0.450$
- $70 \times 0.0095 = 0.665$
- $90 \times 0.0085 = 0.765$
- $110 \times 0.0060 = 0.660$
- $130 \times 0.0040 = 0.520$
- $150 \times 0.0025 = 0.375$
- $170 \times 0.0010 = 0.170$

Sum $= 3.825$ s⁻¹

$$\tau = 3.825 \times 20 = 76.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\sigma^{2}$ from $\sum t_i^{2} E(t_i) \Delta t$:**

$$\sum t_i^{2} E(t_i) = 100(0.0025) + 900(0.0065) + 2500(0.0090) + 4900(0.0095) + 8100(0.0085)$$
$$+ 12100(0.0060) + 16900(0.0040) + 22500(0.0025) + 28900(0.0010)$$

Computing:
- $0.25 + 5.85 + 22.50 + 46.55 + 68.85 + 72.60 + 67.60 + 56.25 + 28.90 = 369.35$ s⁻¹

$$\sigma^{2} = 369.35 \times 20 - (76.5)^{2} = 7387 - 5852 = 1535 \text{ s}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$:**

$$\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = \frac{1535}{(76.5)^{2}} = \frac{1535}{5852} \approx 0.262$$

**Step 6 — Comment:** Since $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} \approx 0.26$, the reactor deviates from plug flow but is significantly more plug-flow-like than MFR-like.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Mistake 1 (2-mark loss):** Forgetting that for a **step input**, $F(t) = C(t)/C_{A\infty}$, NOT $E(t)$.
> - **Mistake 2 (3-mark loss):** Using $E(t)$ instead of $C(t)$ values in mean/variance integrals for step input data — these are conceptually different.
> - **Mistake 3 (1-mark loss):** Failing to verify $\sum E(t_i)\Delta t = 1$ before computing moments.
> - **Mistake 4 (2-mark loss):** Reporting $\sigma^{2}$ with the wrong units (should be time²) or computing $\sigma$ (standard deviation) instead of $\sigma^{2}$.
> - **Mistake 5 (3-mark loss):** Not explicitly stating the **boundary conditions** $E(0) \geq 0$, $E(\infty) \to 0$, $\int E dt = 1$ in derivation problems.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Module 4: Basics of Non-Ideal Flow**

### **Core Definitions**
- $E(t)$: Exit age distribution (units time⁻¹, integrates to 1)
- $F(t)$: Cumulative RTD (dimensionless, $0 \to 1$)
- $I(t) = [1 - F(t)]/\tau$: Internal age distribution
- $\tau = V/\nu$: Mean residence time
- $\sigma^{2} = \int(t-\tau)^{2}E(t)dt$: Variance
- $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = \sigma^{2}/\tau^{2}$: Dimensionless variance (PFR: 0, MFR: 1)

### **Key Relationships (memorize these)**
- $E(t) = dF(t)/dt$
- $F(t) = \int_0^{t}E(t')dt'$
- Pulse input: $E(t) = C(t)/\int_0^{\infty}C(t)dt$
- Step input: $F(t) = C(t)/C_{A\infty}$, then $E(t) = dF/dt$
- $\tau = \int_0^{\infty}tE(t)dt = V/\nu$

### **Ideal Reactor Limits to Remember**
- **PFR:** $E(t) = \delta(t - \tau)$, $\sigma^{2} = 0$, $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = 0$
- **MFR:** $E(t) = (1/\tau)\exp(-t/\tau)$, $\sigma^{2} = \tau^{2}$, $\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = 1$

### **Experimental Tips**
- **Pulse input** is preferred when inlet disturbance is short
- **Step input** is preferred when continuous switching is feasible
- **Tracer selection:** non-reactive, non-adsorbing, easily measured (e.g., NaCl conductivity, dye colorimetry)
- **Constraint:** $E(t) \geq 0$ always; verify normalization post-experiment

### **Practical Engineering Significance**
- Diagnose **channeling, dead zones, short-circuiting, recirculation**
- Predict **actual conversion** in real reactors (often lower than ideal PFR/MFR predictions)
- Enables **model selection**: dispersion model, tanks-in-series, recycle model, etc.
- Scale-up and scale-down decisions rely heavily on RTD comparisons

### **Common Mistake to Avoid**
Do not confuse the **mean residence time** with the **space time** for non-ideal reactors with dead volume. The relation $\tau = V/\nu$ holds for $V = V_{\text{effective}}$ only.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Residence Time Distribution Studies - C
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT503 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Residence Time Distribution Studies - C

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Residence Time Distribution (RTD) Studies – C** focuses on the **quantitative application of experimentally measured RTD data to predict the performance of non-ideal reactors**. While Parts A and B established *what* RTD is and *how* to measure it, Part C addresses the most critical engineering question: **"Given a real reactor's RTD, how do we predict its conversion?"**

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this module segment covers three principal modeling frameworks:
1. The **Tanks-in-Series (TIS) Model** (lumped parameter)
2. The **Axial Dispersion Model (ADM)** (distributed parameter)
3. The **Segregation Model** and the **Maximum Mixedness Model** (micromixing limits)

Mathematically, the foundation is the concept of an **isolated fluid element** or an **isolated microenvironment** that retains its identity long enough to react according to its specific residence time $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – RTD-C):**
> *"Diagnosis of non-ideal reactors using the Tanks-in-Series model and the Axial Dispersion model. Prediction of conversion in real reactors using the Segregation Model and the Maximum Mixedness Model."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water park with 5 connected slides in a row**. Water enters Slide 1, exits to Slide 2, and so on. If the water passed through **all 5 slides (large N)**, it would be thoroughly mixed by the end — almost like a single perfectly mixed tank. If it passed through only **1 slide (N = 1)**, it would behave like an ideal CSTR. If there were **infinitely many infinitesimally small slides**, it would behave like a plug flow reactor (PFR).

This is the essence of the **Tanks-in-Series model**: real reactors are modeled as $N$ identical CSTRs in series, where the value of $N$ (real, not necessarily integer) is a direct measure of how "plug-like" the reactor behaves.

For the **Segregation vs. Maximum Mixedness analogy**:
- **Segregation Model**: Think of fluid as **marbles of different colors** — each marble stays together and reacts independently. The reactor is just a "delayed exit" device.
- **Maximum Mixedness Model**: Think of fluid as a **potion that immediately mixes** with everything around it at the molecular level. The earliest-arriving fluid mixes with the latest-arriving fluid as if all possible mixing had already occurred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> Segregation and Maximum Mixedness give the **same conversion for a first-order reaction**, but **different conversions for orders other than one**. The true answer lies between these two bounds (with the actual conversion determined by the *degree of segregation*).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions

The key normalized parameters in this section are:
- **Dimensionless time:** $\theta = \dfrac{t}{\bar{t}}$ where $\bar{t}$ is the mean residence time (**seconds** or **dimensionless**).
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2$ measured in **seconds²** or as dimensionless $\sigma_\theta^2$.
- **Peclet Number:** $Pe = \dfrac{u \cdot L}{D_E}$ (dimensionless), where $u$ is superficial velocity, $L$ is reactor length, and $D_E$ is the axial dispersion coefficient.
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re$ is used to estimate $D_E$ via empirical correlations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tanks-in-Series (TIS) Model

### 2.1.1 Theoretical Basis
The TIS model approximates a non-ideal real reactor as a **cascade of $N$ equal-sized ideal CSTRs**. It uses a **lumped parameter** approach — the entire reactor is characterized by a single integer (or non-integer) $N$.

### 2.1.2 Derivation Logic
- For a single CSTR impulse response: $E(t) = \dfrac{1}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$
- For $N$ CSTRs in series, convolution of $N$ such exponentials gives the **Erlang distribution**:

$$
E(\theta) = \dfrac{N (N\theta)^{N-1}}{(N-1)!} \cdot e^{-N\theta}
$$

where $\theta = t/\bar{t}$ and $\tau = \bar{t}/N$ is the residence time of each sub-tank.

### 2.1.3 Variance Relationship
The key fitting equation connecting experimental variance to model parameter $N$:

$$
\sigma_\theta^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}
$$

This is the **most-asked TIS relationship in KTU exams**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Cases for N:**
> - $N = 1$ → Perfectly mixed CSTR (maximum variance, $\sigma_\theta^2 = 1$)
> - $N \to \infty$ → Ideal PFR (zero variance, $\sigma_\theta^2 = 0$)
> - $N$ between 1 and ∞ → Real reactor

## 2.2 The Axial Dispersion Model (ADM)

### 2.2.1 Theoretical Basis
The ADM is a **one-dimensional diffusion analogy** superimposed on plug flow. It assumes Fick's law of diffusion describes the deviation from ideal plug flow.

### 2.2.2 Governing PDE
For steady-state with constant density, the dimensionless form is:

$$
\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial \theta} = \left(\dfrac{D_E}{uL}\right) \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2} - \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} - k \tau C^n
$$

The dimensionless group $\dfrac{D_E}{uL}$ is the **reciprocal of the Peclet Number**: $\dfrac{1}{Pe}$.

### 2.2.3 Boundary Conditions (Danckwerts)
- **Open-Open vessel:**
  - At $z = 0$: $C(0^-, \theta) = C(0^+, \theta) - \dfrac{1}{Pe}\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z}\Big|_{z=0^+}$
  - At $z = 1$: $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z}\Big|_{z=1} = 0$

- **Closed-Closed vessel:** Used when the inlet and outlet are connected to well-mixed regions.

### 2.2.4 Variance Relationship (for small dispersion)
For $Pe \to \infty$ (small deviation from plug flow):

$$
\sigma_\theta^2 \approx \dfrac{2}{Pe} - \dfrac{2}{Pe^2}\left(1 - e^{-Pe}\right)
$$

A useful approximation: $\sigma_\theta^2 \approx \dfrac{2}{Pe}$ for $Pe > 50$.

## 2.3 Segregation Model (Complete Segregation)

### 2.3.1 Concept
The fluid is considered to consist of **discrete globules (or packets)** that do not exchange matter with their surroundings. Each globule behaves like a **batch reactor** with reaction time equal to its residence time in the reactor.

### 2.3.2 Conversion Formula (for an n-th order reaction)

$$
\bar{X}_A = \int_0^\infty X_A(t) \cdot E(t) \, dt
$$

where $X_A(t)$ is the conversion in a batch reactor at time $t$:

$$
t = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A} = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}^n (1-X_A)^n}
$$

## 2.4 Maximum Mixedness Model

### 2.4.1 Concept
All possible mixing has occurred — late-entering fluid mixes with early-entering fluid at every point. This gives an **upper bound on conversion for reactant-limiting reactions where $n > 1$**.

### 2.4.2 Concept of Expected Remaining Life
Each entering fluid element has an **expected remaining lifetime** $\lambda(t)$. The longer it has already spent in the reactor, the less time it has left.

For first-pass exit, the expected remaining life distribution function is:

$$
\Lambda(\lambda) = \dfrac{\int_0^{\lambda} (1 - F(\lambda')) \, d\lambda'}{\int_0^{\infty} (1 - F(\lambda')) \, d\lambda'}
$$

This is sometimes simplified using the concept of the **residual lifetime function**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Model | Key Equation | Variables | Limiting Cases |
|---|-------|--------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | TIS — E(θ) | $E(\theta) = \dfrac{N(N\theta)^{N-1}}{(N-1)!}e^{-N\theta}$ | $N$, $\theta$ | $N=1$: CSTR; $N\to\infty$: PFR |
| 2 | TIS — Variance | $\sigma_\theta^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | $N$ | Always positive |
| 3 | TIS — Conversion (1st order) | $\dfrac{C_A}{C_{A0}} = \dfrac{1}{(1+k\tau/N)^N}$ | $k$, $\tau$, $N$ | $N\to\infty \to e^{-k\tau}$ |
| 4 | ADM — Variance (small $D_E$) | $\sigma_\theta^2 = \dfrac{2}{Pe} - \dfrac{2}{Pe^2}(1-e^{-Pe})$ | $Pe$ | $Pe\to\infty$: PFR |
| 5 | ADM — Peclet Number | $Pe = \dfrac{uL}{D_E}$ | $u$, $L$, $D_E$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | ADM — Conversion (1st order) | $\dfrac{C_A}{C_{A0}} = 1 - X_A = \dfrac{4q\exp(Pe/2)}{(1+q)^2 e^{Pe q/2} - (1-q)^2 e^{-Pe q/2}}$ | $q = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{4k\tau}{Pe}}$ | Boundary-Dependent |
| 7 | Segregation | $\bar{X}_A = \int_0^\infty X_A(t) E(t) \, dt$ | $E(t)$ | Lower bound for $n>1$ |
| 8 | Max Mixedness | $\dfrac{dC_A}{d\lambda} = r_A - \dfrac{(C_{A0}-C_A)}{\Lambda}$ | $\lambda$ | Upper bound for $n>1$ |
| 9 | Mean Residence Time | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{V}{v_0} = \int_0^\infty t E(t) \, dt$ | $V$, $v_0$ | Reactor-specific |
| 10 | Micromixing Index | $J = \dfrac{\bar{X}_{seg} - \bar{X}_{actual}}{\bar{X}_{seg} - \bar{X}_{mm}}$ | $J \in [0,1]$ | $J=0$: Max Mixed; $J=1$: Segregated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention for KTU:** $D_E$ is in **m²/s**, $u$ in **m/s**, $L$ in **m**, $\tau$ in **seconds**, and $k$ per appropriate order. All integrals use **seconds** as the base unit.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | RTD-C Use |
|----------------------|-----------|
| **Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** | Predicting coke yield and product distribution in riser reactors using ADM |
| **Polymerization Tubular Reactors** | TIS model for predicting polydispersity index (PDI) deviation |
| **Wastewater Treatment** | Diagnosing short-circuiting in chlorination contact tanks using ADM |
| **Pharmaceutical Mixing** | Determining segregation index for tablet granulation uniformity |
| **Bioreactor Design** | Max mixedness model for substrate inhibition kinetics |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Variance-to-N Relationship for Tanks-in-Series Model

### Objective
Show that for $N$ equal CSTRs in series, the dimensionless variance $\sigma_\theta^2 = 1/N$.

### Step 1: Recall E(θ) for the TIS Model

The Erlang distribution for $N$ tanks in series is:

$$
E(\theta) = \dfrac{N (N\theta)^{N-1}}{(N-1)!} \cdot e^{-N\theta}
$$

where $\theta = t/\bar{t}$.

### Step 2: Definition of Moments

The $n$-th moment about the origin is:

$$
M_n = \int_0^\infty \theta^n E(\theta) \, d\theta
$$

We need $M_1$ (mean) and $M_2$ (second moment), then $\sigma_\theta^2 = M_2 - M_1^2$.

### Step 3: Compute $M_1$

Substitute $u = N\theta$, so $d\theta = du/N$ and $\theta = u/N$:

$$
M_1 = \int_0^\infty \theta \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot (N\theta)^{N-1}}{(N-1)!} e^{-N\theta} \, d\theta
$$

$$
M_1 = \dfrac{N}{N(N-1)!} \int_0^\infty \left(\dfrac{u}{N}\right) u^{N-1} e^{-u} \cdot \dfrac{du}{N}
$$

$$
M_1 = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot (N-1)!} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N^2} \int_0^\infty u^N e^{-u} \, du
$$

Using the Gamma function identity $\int_0^\infty u^N e^{-u} du = N!$:

$$
M_1 = \dfrac{N!}{N^3 \cdot (N-1)!} = \dfrac{N}{N^3} = \dfrac{1}{N^2}
$$

Wait — let me redo this more carefully. The integral form requires recognizing that the **mean residence time is normalized to 1** by definition of $\theta$. So $M_1 = 1$ by construction. This confirms that $\bar{\theta} = 1$.

### Step 4: Compute $M_2$

$$
M_2 = \int_0^\infty \theta^2 E(\theta) d\theta = \dfrac{N^N}{(N-1)!} \int_0^\infty \theta^{N+1} e^{-N\theta} d\theta
$$

Substitute $u = N\theta$:

$$
M_2 = \dfrac{N^N}{(N-1)!} \int_0^\infty \left(\dfrac{u}{N}\right)^{N+1} e^{-u} \cdot \dfrac{du}{N}
$$

$$
M_2 = \dfrac{N^N}{N \cdot (N-1)!} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N^{N+1}} \int_0^\infty u^{N+1} e^{-u} du
$$

$$
M_2 = \dfrac{1}{N^2 \cdot (N-1)!} \cdot (N+1)!
$$

$$
M_2 = \dfrac{(N+1) \cdot N!}{N^2 \cdot (N-1)!} = \dfrac{(N+1) \cdot N}{N^2} = \dfrac{N+1}{N}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Variance

$$
\sigma_\theta^2 = M_2 - M_1^2 = \dfrac{N+1}{N} - 1^2 = \dfrac{N+1 - N}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N}
$$

### Result

$$
\boxed{\sigma_\theta^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is exam-critical:** This single relation is the bridge between experimental RTD data and design parameters. Given $\sigma_\theta^2$ from a tracer study, $N$ is computed directly.

## 3.2 Derivation: Conversion in N CSTRs in Series (1st Order, $A \to Products$)

### Step 1: Mass Balance on Tank i

For steady state in the $i$-th tank of volume $V_i$:

$$
v_0 C_{A,i-1} = v_0 C_{A,i} + V_i k C_{A,i}
$$

Dividing by $v_0$ and defining $\tau_i = V_i/v_0$:

$$
C_{A,i-1} = C_{A,i}(1 + k\tau_i)
$$

### Step 2: Recursive Solution

For $N$ equal tanks, $\tau_i = \tau/N$. The product telescopes:

$$
C_{A,N} = \dfrac{C_{A,0}}{(1 + k\tau/N)^N}
$$

### Step 3: Conversion Expression

$$
\boxed{X_A = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(1 + k\tau/N)^N}}
$$

### Step 4: Limiting Case Check
- $N = 1$: $X_A = \dfrac{k\tau}{1+k\tau}$ → matches ideal CSTR ✓
- $N \to \infty$: $X_A \to 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ → matches ideal PFR ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: ADM Conversion for Closed Vessel (1st Order)

### Step 1: Governing ODE
For 1st order reaction and a closed vessel boundary, the analytical solution (Levenspiel) is:

$$
\dfrac{C_A}{C_{A0}} = 1 - X_A = \dfrac{4 q \exp(Pe/2)}{(1+q)^2 \exp(Pe \cdot q/2) - (1-q)^2 \exp(-Pe \cdot q/2)}
$$

where $q = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{4 k \tau}{Pe}}$.

### Step 2: Limiting Behavior

**Small dispersion** ($Pe \to \infty$): $q \to 1$, denominator terms collapse, and we recover the PFR solution:
$$
1 - X_A \to e^{-k\tau}
$$

**Large dispersion** ($Pe \to 0$): This is the CSTR limit, but the formula breaks down; instead, use the limiting form $1 - X_A \to \dfrac{1}{1 + k\tau}$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Segregation Model Conversion (n-th Order)

### Step 1: Batch Reactor Conversion Formula

For an $n$-th order reaction $A \to$ Products in a batch reactor of time $t$:

$$
t = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}^n (1 - X_A)^n}
$$

### Step 2: Solve the Integral

Let $\xi = 1 - X_A$, so $d\xi = -dX_A$. The integral becomes:

$$
k C_{A0}^{n-1} t = \int_{1-X_A}^{1} \xi^{-n} (-d\xi) = \int_{1-X_A}^{1} \xi^{-n} d\xi
$$

**Case 1: n = 1 (1st Order)**
$$
k t = -\ln(1 - X_A) \implies X_A(t) = 1 - e^{-kt}
$$

**Case 2: n ≠ 1 (General)**
$$
k C_{A0}^{n-1} t = \dfrac{1 - (1-X_A)^{1-n}}{1-n} = \dfrac{(1-X_A)^{1-n} - 1}{n-1}
$$

Solving for $X_A$:
$$
X_A(t) = 1 - \left[1 + (n-1) k C_{A0}^{n-1} t\right]^{\tfrac{1}{1-n}}
$$

### Step 3: Average Over the RTD

$$
\bar{X}_{seg} = \int_0^\infty X_A(t) \cdot E(t) \, dt
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For 1st order reactions, Segregation Model and Maximum Mixedness Model give the **same conversion** equal to that of an ideal PFR with the same $\bar{t}$:
> $$\bar{X}_{1st} = 1 - \int_0^\infty e^{-kt} E(t) \, dt$$
> This is a frequent "show that" question.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Model

| Criterion | Tanks-in-Series | Axial Dispersion | Segregation | Max Mixedness |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Type** | Lumped | Distributed | Micromixing | Micromixing |
| **Best for** | Baffled tanks | Long tubes/pipes | Slow reaction | Fast reaction |
| **Parameter** | $N$ (integer-like) | $Pe$ | None | None |
| **Output** | $C_A$ vs. $t$ | $C_A$ vs. $z,t$ | Conversion bounds | Conversion bounds |
| **Order Sensitivity** | All orders | All orders (linear) | All orders | All orders |
| **Bounds conversion?** | No | No | Lower for $n>1$ | Upper for $n>1$ |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: RTD-C Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Conduct Tracer Experiment"] --> B["Step 2: Compute E curve and Moments"]
    B --> C{"Step 3: Choose Model"}
    C --> D["Tanks in Series Model"]
    C --> E["Axial Dispersion Model"]
    C --> F["Segregation or Max Mixedness"]
    D --> D1["Fit N from variance"]
    D1 --> D2["Compute Conversion via N-CSTR cascade"]
    E --> E1["Fit Pe from variance"]
    E1 --> E2["Solve ADM PDE for Conversion"]
    F --> F1["Compute lower bound via Segregation"]
    F --> F2["Compute upper bound via Max Mixedness"]
    D2 --> G["Step 4: Compare with Experimental Conversion"]
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H{"Match?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Model Validated"]
    H -- "No" --> J["Refit or Try Different Model"]
    J --> C
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Tanks-in-Series Cascade Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CSTR_Cascade["Real Reactor Approximation"]
        T1["Tank 1\nVolume V/N\ntau_i = tau/N"] --> T2["Tank 2\nVolume V/N\ntau_i = tau/N"] --> T3["Tank 3\nVolume V/N\ntau_i = tau/N"] --> TN["Tank N\nVolume V/N\ntau_i = tau/N"]
    end
    Feed["Feed Inlet\nC_A0, v0"] --> T1
    TN --> Prod["Outlet\nC_A,N, X_A"]
    style CSTR_Cascade fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style Feed fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400
    style Prod fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#8b0000
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Segregation vs. Maximum Mixedness Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Seg_Model["Segregation Model Fluid Behavior"]
        S1["Globule enters at t=0"] --> S2["Ages in isolation as if in a batch reactor"]
        S2 --> S3["Exits and is collected"]
        S3 --> S4["Converted amount depends only on its own residence time"]
    end
    subgraph MM_Model["Max Mixedness Model Fluid Behavior"]
        M1["All possible fluid entries mix with each other"] --> M2["A molecule entering late meets one exiting soon"]
        M2 --> M3["Environment pre-loaded with reactive history"]
        M3 --> M4["Highest possible conversion for n greater than 1"]
    end
    style Seg_Model fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style MM_Model fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#7c2d12
```

```mermaid
    A["Experimental RTD Curve E(t)"] --> B["Variance Calculation sigma squared"]
    B --> C["Models Available"]

    C --> D2["Two Parameter Models:\nTIS with bypass\nADM with dead zone"]
    C --> D3["Multi-parameter:\nGamma, Beta, Lagged Tank Models"]
    D1 --> E1["Quick Estimates"]
    D2 --> E2["Better Fit for Complex RTD"]
    D3 --> E3["Highest Accuracy with More Data"]

    style D3 fill:#f4cccc
```
